### Ben1983 — 2014-02-11T03:40:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-intake.9704/post-109549>

How much protein should we be having each day? I read on one of Tim Jackson's posts that he recommends around one gram per kilogram of body weight. I weigh 70kg so If I'm eating 50 grams for my BAB that means I'd be having 10 grams each at my other two meals or 20 grams at my second meal if I'm only eating twice a day (which I am starting to do). Anyway, I'm just wondering if Jack recommends around the same amount of protein per day as Tim? Is there a context for protein intake?

### margor_j — 2014-02-11T03:52:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-intake.9704/post-109552>

you know TJ never talked to me about amounts of protein (im thinking that we can get the bulk of our help on this site, but TJ refines it all again..). i wondered the same thing.. this morning i would have been slightly shy of 50gm of protein, and i didnt have a heap of fat and hey presto - 3pm and still no hunger (and i didnt have the best sleep, which is usually my danger zone). i know were have lamb for dinner so il get some of that into me. i reckon keep budging the fat up to. like you say, eat most of it at b'fast, and then when youre down to two meals a day, some form of protein at nite is probably not gonna take you anywhere dangerous re health!

I know i didnt 'answer' your question per se, but the above is what im playing around with (ie higher fat). im only like a week into it now, but feeling good and steady. im gonna flick even a few greens at nite cos im finding im almost 'too full' after dinner, and im not even eating a lot! haha oh and my energy has done a back flip and i have more of it now - though sort of creeping in the sleep stakes again. but TJ got me off the whey protein powder -he is a fan of whey, but he said it mite not be a fan of me.. fair call! i said i dont feel dodgy after eating it, but as he pointed out, that doesnt mean its not having a possible negative effect. i found my energy was just horrendous a couple of hours after my smoothie - now im flying!

### Ben1983 — 2014-02-11T04:12:40+0200

Then my work here is done.....

See - the thing is .....you aren't going to see immediate results ..... repeat after to me.....this is going to - Take - a - while.

Let me just tell you a few things that I see people doing ...... and this is just my opinion .....Dr. K. might want to smack me.....

If you are going to do this - you got to be all in and 100% committed Details matter HUGE..... make no mistake about that. This is really a very simple protocol if you follow exactly what Dr. K. is saying.

Is it hard? - I don't think so ....but that is me. I was so grateful to find something that worked that I stuck like glue.
Did I do everything exactly right - nope - I am sure I slipped up .... but never intentionally.

All the time I see people pick and choose and don't want to do it !00% - they think 50% will be okay - or 80% will be okay ... and maybe for some it might just work somewhat. But - I wanted what Jack was talking about - an optimal life ... and I am more than prepared to work hard at it - and two and a half years later ... I can't believe the changes! Did it happen overnight? absolutely not! I am still a work in progress. But I can tell you one thing for sure - this is the first time in my entire life that I feel comfortable in my own skin and can actually look in the mirror and like what I see - Martin taught me that! I am older than you and regret not getting here sooner ...but I am here now. Now that I know better - I am doing so much better!

Dr. K. isn't holding a gun to anyone's head - he is telling it like it is. We can chose to follow the protocols - or not .... entirely our call.

Now , are the blogs hard? heck ya! Do I wish I was a lot smarter? you betcha! but Jack has never, ever not helped us when we have asked for it. He has asked us over and over again to tell him what we don't know so he can maybe say it another way or whatever it takes. Does he want us to work for it ...yep! How else are we going to learn and ask better questions and how else are we going to help our own Docs to get dialed in and be better.

Dr. K.has always been positive and supportive and he has definitely known where he is going the last ten years.

I am so glad to be along for the ride

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-11T09:24:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-intake.9704/post-109607>

> [Ben1983 said:](/goto/post?id=109549)
>
> How much protein should we be having each day? I read on one of Tim Jackson's posts that he recommends around one gram per kilogram of body weight. I weigh 70kg so If I'm eating 50 grams for my BAB that means I'd be having 10 grams each at my other two meals or 20 grams at my second meal if I'm only eating twice a day (which I am starting to do). Anyway, I'm just wondering if Jack recommends around the same amount of protein per day as Tim? Is there a context for protein intake?

I don't believe Jack has given a formula anywhere. He more says things like, "Eat like a Great White shark." But as you say, he does give a figure for breakfast.

I don't know of Tim Jackson, but that is a figure you see around. Many Paleo people go a lot a higher than that. I think Rob Wolf has suggested 1g per _pound_ of bodyweight. The low-carb camp tend to advise lower figures than the Paleo people. Diets from Kwasniewski and Ron Rosedale cut protein right down to the kind of figures you're giving; others, such as Volek and Phinney are more generous:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-Art-Science-Carbohydrate-Living/dp/0983490708/](http://www.amazon.com/The-Art-Science-Carbohydrate-Living/dp/0983490708/)

Mark Sisson, who's kind of low-carb Paleo sits somewhere in between. He says take your _lean_ bodymass (not total bodymass) in pounds and times that by around 0.7 or 0.8 or by 1.0 if you're very active.

You pays your money and you takes your choice …

I'd guess your lean bodymass would be around 130 - 135 lbs so that Sisson's lower figures would allow you around 100 to 110 g per day. That'd be roughly in line with Volek and Phinney's recommendations, too. That's a fair bit more than Kwasniewski and Rosedale allow you, but it's not massive. Some people when trying to get into ketosis seem to need to go very low on protein, but eating down at around 60 or 70 grams a day of protein would be a bit of a pain. If you look at what Peter at Hyperlipid eats, it begins not to look like normal meals at all.

I _think_ the large dose of protein at breakfast in the Leptin Reset is partly there as a deliberate confuser to your body. As I understand it what we all do over the years in many different ways can keep throwing our biological systems, in particular our leptin sensitivity, off so that they get set wrong. Your body isn't expecting all that protein at breakfast, so it tends to give up and, as it were, revert to factory setting. IOW, it helps the reset to take place.

Dr. Kruse seems to be doing something more complex and deeper than someone like Kwasnieski is. It's not just about getting into ketosis.

IIRC, Jack has said that as you progress you tend to gravitate to less protein and more fat.

I'd not sweat it too much. Great White sharks don't count macronutrients, I guess. I should think you can allow yourself a little more protein than you originally suggested.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-02-11T12:17:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-intake.9704/post-109625>

I think it depends on personal context matters.

For the leptin reset, the minimum effective dose is 25g to provide that "jolt" to the vagal nerve. The recommended range is 25g to 75g of protein during a Leptin Reset breakfast.

Dinner time protein doses should probably be around 25g to 75g as well.

The critical timing for protein will be 30 minutes upon waking if doing the Leptin Reset, and then 4pm to 6pm when protein synthesis is highest.

\---

Given those minimums, you should then cater protein intake to your goals.

I weigh about 84kg at maybe 13-14% body fat. And personally, I aim to be in ketosis most of the time, while aiming to lose some body fat.

For consistent ketosis, you probably want protein below 120g a day, and ideally at 1kg per kilogram bodyweight. For me, that puts the range at 85g to 120g

So I've been testing my blood ketone levels for the last maybe 3 months, and I've found that eating 40-50g of protein during breakfast (200g meat weighed raw), and then 40-80g protein during dinner (200-350g meat weighed raw), for a total of. This results in steady blood ketones above 1.0mM for essentially the entire day (even after meals). Carb intake is pretty low (0g), and satiety is controlled pretty well at those levels of protein intke.

Lately, I've been experimenting with having one day a week at 200g protein (motivation in the protein refeed thread -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/protein-refeeds-instead-of-carb-refeeds.9295/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/protein-refeeds-instead-of-carb-refeeds.9295/)). On such days, blood ketones are pretty low (0.4 to 0.8mM), but seem to recover back to their +1.0mM range the next day.

\---

I've also experimented with having almost no protein during breakfast, but found that I don't feel as satiated through the day, and also tend to throw off less body heat (which I view as a bad clinical sign).

\---

I wouldn't be adding in the protein refeed day if I were obese or had a really bad redox potential, and would instead stick to that 80-130g a day range consistently.

> so, i hope my efforts in the sea can be classified as CT - its nowhere near as cold as the lake was, but for now, its the easiest option for me. and the bonus is, the grounding / earthing! and when im in there, im do breathing exercises..
>
> somethings not sitting well in me since dinner. i did have half an egg as well, and iv been off eggs, but i also tried them a couple of days ago, and no effect.. hmmm more bloody detective work needed! im gonna go for an evening walk.. im sO into romanian pop music haha! i stick those sounds on and off i go!

I no longer track my food or macro breakdown, as I did it for almost 10 years and it lead me do develop and unhealthy obsession plus it did not produce any weight loss. I have come to realize that my excess weight is the outward manifestation of how screwed up my hormones are and until I get them fixed, trying to control the macro nutrients of my diet is just going to mess with my head again.

For years I was if I eat this amount of calories with this breakdown it says that I should lose weight but it NEVER worked out that way. There were even days that I burned more calories in exercise then I ate (stupid, stupid, stupid).

I had a session with a counselor and she really opened my eyes to what I was doing to myself **_(see page 1 of my blog for a video with her)_** Now, besides the BAB (within 1 hours as I have meds that need at least 30 minutes before food), I only eat when hungry and stop when full. I have also not stepped on the scale since March 12, 2013 when I was at the lowest weight in my adult life (165). I am not that weight now since the pants I wore then I cannot get on so I know I have gained but part of my mental healing it to not base my happiness in life on what the scale says or what size pants I am in. It has NOT been easy but it has to be done.

Once I get the hormones balanced a bit more then I might look back into seeing about tweeking the macronutrient but for now I eat my fats and protein with just a little bit of carbs from veggies and listen to what the body wants. Right now, the body is telling me pork, pork and pork for breakfast and I am happy to oblige.

> For consistent ketosis, you probably want protein below 120g a day, and ideally at 1kg per kilogram bodyweight. For me, that puts the range at 85g to 120g
>
> So I've been testing my blood ketone levels for the last maybe 3 months, and I've found that eating 40-50g of protein during breakfast (200g meat weighed raw), and then 40-80g protein during dinner (200-350g meat weighed raw), for a total of. This results in steady blood ketones above 1.0mM for essentially the entire day (even after meals). Carb intake is pretty low (0g), and satiety is controlled pretty well at those levels of protein intke.
>
> Lately, I've been experimenting with having one day a week at 200g protein (motivation in the protein refeed thread -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/protein-refeeds-instead-of-carb-refeeds.9295/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/protein-refeeds-instead-of-carb-refeeds.9295/)). On such days, blood ketones are pretty low (0.4 to 0.8mM), but seem to recover back to their +1.0mM range the next day.
>
> \---
>
> I've also experimented with having almost no protein during breakfast, but found that I don't feel as satiated through the day, and also tend to throw off less body heat (which I view as a bad clinical sign).
>
> \---
>
> I wouldn't be adding in the protein refeed day if I were obese or had a really bad redox potential, and would instead stick to that 80-130g a day range consistently.

Thanks for this post, Yew! It really helped me today


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Any advice on taking antibiotics please?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-advice-on-taking-antibiotics-please.9708/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sandra — 2014-02-11T10:06:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-advice-on-taking-antibiotics-please.9708/post-109616>

I cannot remember the last time I had to take antibiotics and avoid them if at all possible.

However, I've had a few more tests back to try to get to the bottom of my tiredness and one has come back positive for a parasite (dientamoeba fragilis). I don't know how I got it or how long I've had it and the whole idea of it totally grosses me out!! So, I want it gone ASAP.

The doctor prescribed antibiotics which contains doxycycline monohydrade - to take for 10 days, with breakfast and with dinner.

Is there anything I can also do while I am taking the antibiotics or after it ... especially regarding the gut flora (and anything else for that matter)?

I am currently 2 weeks into a 2 month course of pro-biotics - the first month to rid the gut of bad bacteria, and then the 2nd month to build up the good flora again.

In the first month is:
\- saccharomyes cerevisiae (boulardi) 7.5 billion CFU
\- bifidobacterium lactis 5.0 Billion

In the second month is:
\- lactobacillus acidophilus 11.5 billion
\- bifodobacterium lactis 11.5 billion
\- lactobacillus rhamnosus 2 billion

Three weeks ago, a naturopath suggested I might possible have a parasite and gave me tablets called 'Cina 6' to take for a month ... I did take these for about 10 days and noticed no difference to any symptoms, so stopped them.

The doctor said it wouldn't really matter if I stop this while on the antibiotics and then start it up again, or if I continue on with this while taking the antibiotics.

If anyone has had a similar experience or can offer any suggestions, that would be great.

Thanks so much

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-11T10:51:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-advice-on-taking-antibiotics-please.9708/post-109621>

You'd need better comment than I can give, but to start things off ...

Dr. Art Ayers at Cooling Inflammation is interesting on gut microbiota. I sceptical of some of what he says, as I think anyone here (or anyone in Paleo) would be, on some topics -- for example, he seems to suggest that if your gut bacteria are good enough it doesn't really matter much what you eat. But on the bacteria themselves he is enlightening. I guess that's his area.

He says restoring the gut bacteria is the crucial thing -- and also what most practitioners give absolutely no thought to. (He gives Dr. Oz as an example.)

But he goes much further than that, suggesting that the common idea that regular consumption of fermented dairy -- yoghurt or kefir -- will put things right is a hopeless pipe-dream. The problem here is that there are only three or four strains of bacteria in yoghurt, but there are hundreds in your gut. (I heard a dentist say that there are 700 in your mouth alone.) Furthermore, Ayers asserts that dairy probiotics don't find permanent lodgment in the human gut; so while taking them may be beneficial as getting them to occupy space something nastier might encroach on, they don't stay there and you have to keep taking them. The capsules are, he says, not a lot better -- again there are few strains in them. (They tend, Ayers says, to be bacteria that have been already used in other contexts, since those are safety-tested, so they might, for example, be ones that have been used in the past for making enzymes for washing powders!)

Best, he says, is fermented food that you make yourself. I guess this would mean things like sauerkraut and beet kvass. Because you make them yourself, the cultures tend to pick up bacteria that are in your own local environment, and this is supposed to be helpful. He's also a fan of eating locally-grown organic veg that isn't washed too much, and of getting out in the countryside and getting dirty. Associating with healthy people (and avoiding sick ones) is another of his suggestions -- since you tend to swap bacteria when shaking hands, using doorknobs, or even breathing near others. Oh, and with pets.

But on the whole, he seems to think the problem of restoration is so deep that the radical solution of faecal transplant may often be the only solution.

I suspect most of us have compromised gut bacteria. One thing I learnt from Jack's book, which I recently read, is that vitamin K2 can actually be made by a healthy gut microbiome. He says tartar on the teeth is a sign that's not happening -- well, I've had that for years, and no-one ever told me that. I've just had dentists say things to me like: "Your teeth tend to scale up a lot: some people's do," as if it were a weird inexplicable fact of nature. (isn't this medicine all over?) I'm now taking K2. I guess we're all somewhat compromised in one way or another -- unless we're Hadza tribesmen or something.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-11T20:17:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-advice-on-taking-antibiotics-please.9708/post-109666>

An extra note:

Here's Dr. Ayers writing specifically about milk and gut flora. It's interesting stuff:

[http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.co.uk/2014/01/milk-kefir-and-gut-flora.html](http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.co.uk/2014/01/milk-kefir-and-gut-flora.html)

It's also shows that there's a fair amount of controversy in this area. The regimen you outline starts with _saccharomyes boulardii._ As you probably know, that's a yeast. The word cerevisiae comes from the Latin for beer and the word for beer in several Romance languages is similar. Similarly, the word _boulardii_ might remind you of _boulanger_ the French for baker. I assume it's a strain of yeast used in baking (and those yeasts are basically the same as beer yeasts). Apparently, French doctors have favoured using it for some time. I think the theory is that it can compete with and displace other yeasts that are harmful. I believe they don't usually give it for long, however, because it can itself get out of control.

You then go onto a bifidobacterium. This is quite controversial. This is the one that is there in high concentrations in the guts of breastfeeding babies. Here's what Ayers says:

> When I taught microbiology, I had students spike raw cow’s milk with _E. coli_ and then measure the decreasing survival of these common gut bacteria that are actively excluded from dairy fermentation. One of the lessons here is that milk stops the growth of adult gut bacteria and supports the growth of lactic acid bacteria found in baby diapers and used to make fermented dairy products.

(These would be beneficial _E. coli_ not the harmful strain.)

It's a point isn't it? Do we really want to be stopping the growth of adult gut bacteria?

Elaine Gottschall, who devised the SCD (Specific Carbohydrate Diet), considered bifidus was not helpful for the people with disturbed digestive systems that she was trying to help:

[http://www.breakingtheviciouscycle.info/knowledge\_base/detail/bifidus/](http://www.breakingtheviciouscycle.info/knowledge_base/detail/bifidus/)

However, Dr. Natasha Campbell-McBride (deviser of the GAPS Diet, brought out as an "updated version" of the SCD) thinks they are beneficial and they're in the Bio-Kult product she and several other doctors have devised:

[http://www.gapsdiet.com/Bio-Kult\_Probiotic.html](http://www.gapsdiet.com/Bio-Kult_Probiotic.html)

It's unfortunate, but there's controversy rather than agreement out there.

### Sandra — 2014-02-12T03:37:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-advice-on-taking-antibiotics-please.9708/post-109731>

Thank you! I'll check out all those links

### cantweight — 2014-02-12T05:19:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-advice-on-taking-antibiotics-please.9708/post-109741>

Ive had good results with Dia Verm by desbio. Had all kinds of parasites a few years back...freaked me out! The one I remember most was some really long named thing that is responsible for African Sleeping Sickness...lol
Have also used supplements called Paracidin and Parastat. They contain a lot of clove and cinnamon and such.

In addition to fermented foods, raw milk has some amazing probiotic properties. Whatever you decide to do just make sure your bowels are moving at least twice a day.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-12T15:45:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-advice-on-taking-antibiotics-please.9708/post-109786>

> [Sandra said:](/goto/post?id=109731)
>
> Thank you! I'll check out all those links

I wonder if this Underground Wellness podcast with Paul Chek being interviewed about parasite and fungal infections might be of interest. I've had it around and only just got round to listening to it:

[http://undergroundwellness.com/podcast-229-how-to-heal-fungal-and-parasite-infections/](http://undergroundwellness.com/podcast-229-how-to-heal-fungal-and-parasite-infections/)

I found it interesting that he says that these tend not to come back if people follow a few simple lifestyle practices. These turn out to be pretty much what anyone following Epi-paleo would be doing -- care about sleep, not eating a lot of sugar, and so on.

The one that particularly pleased me was using spices -- I see cantweight (above) mentions clove and cinnamon for actual therapeutic use. Paul Chek pointed out that people have been using spices for a _very_ long time, as I pointed out in another post here recently, and one of the reasons is that they tend to kill these things -- particularly important in hot climates, where very spicy food is often eaten, of course. Some herbs it seems, too. Paul mentions oregano. As he relates in the interview/phone-in he had the unfortunate experience of getting oil of oregano on his privates, which made him dance somewhat.

Since Epi-paleo cooking doesn't eschew tastiness, and does make use of herbs and spices, we should all be well set up on this front.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Gut microbiota
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microbiota.9713/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-11T17:23:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microbiota.9713/post-109645>

A new piece from the _Guardian_ on work being done at University College, Cork.

There's a certain amount of nonsense in it:

> The western diet, by which we tend to mean the North American diet, is high in fat and protein. In this diet bacteroidetes usually make up more than 55% of the gut microbiota, and sometimes, in North America itself, as much as 80%. In Europe, the average numbers vary from country to country. In my case I had 34%.

Yeah, right ... not much carbohydrate in the North American diet. Even _JAMA_ is now admitting that the U.S. carb intake is now frightfully high.

They continue:

> The opposite to a North American diet is what O'Toole calls a "natural diet". "Our antecedents on the plains of Africa weren't chewing on burgers," he explains. "They were running around eating plant foods and leaves and occasionally eating a squirrel if they were lucky."

Where do they get this nonsense from? Plant foods and leaves and an "occasional" squirrel? Before hypothesizing in a vaccuum, they might look at data. Plant foods and leaves and an "occasional" squirrel? That would keep a six-foot man going! That would fuel human encephalization! Have they ever actually read _any_ anthropology? Have they bothered to check static isotope analysis data on prehistoric bones? Have they ... ? ... oh, never mind.

But some of this is interesting:

[http://www.theguardian.com/science/2014/feb/11/gut-biology-health-bacteria-future-medicine](http://www.theguardian.com/science/2014/feb/11/gut-biology-health-bacteria-future-medicine)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-11T20:13:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insects.9715/post-109664>

best bugs for eating - ants (just make sure you don't have fire ants in your neck of the woods)

put a dab of honey at the bottom of a wide mouth jar - put it near where you've seen ants - when the swarm into the jar put the lid on - pop them in the freezer. once frozen - put on a baking sheet and broil then grind them up.

In survival school they had cookies out on the front table - made from insect flour. No wheat flour in the recipe. they told us after we'd eaten the cookies. 1/2 the class tossed their cookies. I thought they were pretty tasty. they had a lemon flavor

# Thread: National Geographic catches up with Jack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/national-geographic-catches-up-with-jack.9717/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-11T21:24:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/national-geographic-catches-up-with-jack.9717/post-109678>

> White fat—the blobby kind that bulges hips, thighs, and bellies—stores energy. [Brown fat](http://www.nytimes.com/2012/01/25/health/brown-fat-burns-ordinary-fat-study-finds.html)—prevalent in infants but less abundant in adults—generates heat and burns calories when stimulated. ...

[http://news.nationalgeographic.com/...-fat-white-fat-irisin-metabolism-weight-loss/](http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2014/02/140211-shivering-cold-exercise-brown-fat-white-fat-irisin-metabolism-weight-loss/)

Nah, it's only a "hormetic effect" ;-)

OT, but as for "hormesis", I recently read this:

> I am a molecular biologist and that means that I think in the equivalent of mechanical terms at the atomic and molecular level. I can't think in the physiological terms of the body wants to do something. Hormesis is a physiological interpretation to indicate that, for example, plant defense molecules will kill us at high levels, but will turn on our immune system at low levels. But that is like saying that the body wants to prepare itself for future stresses. That is not what happens at the molecular level and most of the physiological explanations don't happen at the molecular level. It is just a contrived coincidence that the demonstrations that Dr. Oz provides of biological processes give the right answers, because nature doesn't work that way. Enzymes aren't attracted to substrates, they bump into them and stick. There is a huge difference. Hormesis gives the wrong answers.

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-11T21:27:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/national-geographic-catches-up-with-jack.9717/post-109680>

Jack's been leading this wave for a while now....

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-11T21:47:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/national-geographic-catches-up-with-jack.9717/post-109685>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=109680)
>
> Jack's been leading this wave for a while now....

I think that's becoming clearer and clearer as time goes on. There's no-one else in the movement who's even close to putting all this stuff together let alone understanding it all.

I mean you get Paleo folk saying silly stuff like "No-one used spices much before the 20th century" in order to justify that ludicrous "food reward" theory. That's ridiculously ill-informed. Spice trading disappears into a "dark backward and abysm of time". Vasco da Gama, Magellan, and Columbus were all in search in new spice routes. One historian has said that the exploration of the planet was driven not by gold or conquest (as one might assume) but principally by the search for spices.

If Jack says something I'm not expecting I know by now that there's something I don't yet know that I need to grasp.

# Thread: f.lux
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux.9718/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-11T21:36:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux.9718/post-109682>

What are people setting for this in its preferences?

I know I'd be best off not using the computer at all at this time in the evening, and I'll shut down in just a mo … but …

It's been on my mind. I've had it installed for some years now, I think, but left the settings as default. I meant to look into what would be best, but forgot and didn't get round to it.

If I right-click on its icon in the toolbar (this is on a Mac) and pull up "Preferences" then I see the "At night" setting is set by default to a colour temperature of 3400 Kelvin, which is equivalent to a halogen bulb. That's actually still a pretty high temperature.

Checking the f.lux website, I see they also have presets for Candle (2300K) and Tungsten (2700K).

Well, tungsten would presumably be the equivalent of a normal incandescent bulb with a tungsten filament, and, historically, that's what's caused all the trouble. Maybe it should be dialled right back to "candle" colour temperature. I just tried that, and it seemed fine.

What are others here doing?

### Tanya — 2014-02-11T21:46:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux.9718/post-109684>

Mine is set at Candle (2300K)

### sooperb — 2014-02-17T14:58:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux.9718/post-110784>

Mine is set to candle too  Just wish I could have it on the ipad without jail breaking it.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-17T15:49:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux.9718/post-110797>

You can get a blueblocker cover to fit over the screen. There's a company in the U.S. That sells them -- Sleepshield. They'll post overseas, because I emailed them to ask. They're sending me an iPod cover at the moment. Unfortunately, no-one seems to import them.

[http://www.sleepshield.com/](http://www.sleepshield.com/)

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-17T17:48:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux.9718/post-110828>

i have mine set to candle all day.... love it... and if I must be on the computer at night I actually turn down the monitor brightness as well as wearing my blueblockers...

### Infinusss — 2014-03-25T05:17:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux.9718/post-117781>

Mine is set a 1200K at night. Works great. Here is a great site for products that lower/eliminate blue light: [https://www.lowbluelights.com/index.asp](https://www.lowbluelights.com/index.asp)

They sell everything from low blue light nightlights, to eyeglasses, TV screen and computer/tablet covers to ceiling light fixture covers that go over fluorescent lights in the office. The company was started by an ex-GE scientist and a physics professor. Good stuff there.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T22:22:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux.9718/post-117900>

> [Infinusss said:](/goto/post?id=117781)
>
> Mine is set a 1200K at night. Works great. Here is a great site for products that lower/eliminate blue light: [https://www.lowbluelights.com/index.asp](https://www.lowbluelights.com/index.asp)
>
> They sell everything from low blue light nightlights, to eyeglasses, TV screen and computer/tablet covers to ceiling light fixture covers that go over fluorescent lights in the office. The company was started by an ex-GE scientist and a physics professor. Good stuff there.

Oh, yeah. I heard Richard Hansler on Evan Brand's podcast -- very interesting.

I don't know about the Windows or Linux versions, but there's been a recent update to the Mac version of f.lux. I knew, because I took the RSS feed which informs of updates.

People may want to hold fire on it.

I say that because, like a lot of software, it's got more complex and added more functionality over time, but whether the additional functions do what you want may be a question. What it does now is set up colour-teperature settings for _three_ periods instead of two. So you no longer have daytime and nighttime, but:

Daytime
Sunset
Bedtime

During the period that it calls "Sunset" it automatically sets the colour-temeprature to Tungsten (2700 Kelvin) and you _can't_ go lower than that. You can set "Bedtime" to Candle (1900 K) but you have to run through the "Sunset" cycle, which lasts for quite some time -- some hours by default -- first.

By the looks, one can probably fudge this by pushing "Bedtime" back by setting the "this is when I wake up" time early enough to push "Bedtime" back till it coincides with "Sunset". But I haven't fiddled yet.

IOW, we seem to be moving into the "feature creep" stage of this software:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feature\_creep](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feature_creep)

There's also a funky new "Darkroom" setting where there's a lot of black and red and kind of film-negative feel with text in light-grey. How useful that really is I don't know ...

### sooperb — 2014-03-26T00:53:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux.9718/post-117936>

I will have to check, I don't often use my computer in the evening, I use my, erm, cough, iPad.

# Thread: Trying to buy a cup of coffee
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-buy-a-cup-of-coffee.9725/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-12T12:53:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-buy-a-cup-of-coffee.9725/post-109769>

I'm in the local garden centre and I go into the coffee shop. There's a bill of fare on the counter, and it has two types of coffee, capuccino and something called "Douwe-Egberts filter coffee". I say to the woman behind the counter, "Do you sell espresso?"

She puts her reading glasses on and stares at the machine. "Well," she says, "there is a button on the machine that says 'espresso', but we don't sell it. I think we used to, but I don't think people want it."
I contemplate suggesting that she push the button and see what happens, and if she gets an espresso ring it up as 'capuccino'. I decide not to complicate matters for her and suggest a somebody-whatdyecallum filter coffee. I'm given something very thin-looking in a cup about the size of a half-pint beer mug. The good old Anglo-American approach: Never mind the quality; look at the serving size!

It tastes like something flushed from a radiator.

(I got that line off Harry Palmer in _The Ipcress File_.)

I take two or three sips and leave the rest. As I leave the area, I notice a poster for chai. I'd understood this as being tea with a great deal of hot milk and some spices. This poster is advertising it as not containing trans-fats. _What!_ If you need to tell people that there aren't trans-fats in a cup of tea, then you're selling a product they need to beware of anyway. This immediately tells you that this is going to be some kind of product reconstituted from dried powder and that it's likely to contain other things that they haven't been told are not there.

No, just no. I really don't care how much the supplier spent on hiring commerical artists and making a nice colourful poster. That's not what it's about. Just no.

### Lahelada — 2014-02-12T13:10:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-buy-a-cup-of-coffee.9725/post-109773>

Douw Egberts is actually a nice coffee, if the beans are not put into the mug on a string so as to be retractible at the first opportunity.
Re quality and quantity a worldwide phenomenon now I am afraid.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-12T20:02:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-buy-a-cup-of-coffee.9725/post-109812>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=109773)
>
> Douw Egberts is actually a nice coffee ...

They'd probably not be pleased to have their name plastered around by this garden centre then. LOL

I guess the garden centre was using the name to lend cachet … it does sort of sound exotic to an English ear being Dutch or something. But how much cachet it lends I really don't know as the only time I see that brand in supermarkets it's on pretty heavy discount.

Of course, if you start with pre-ground coffee that you've probably opened and then left to sit around till it goes stale, and you make it pretty weak, it's not going to taste of much whoever's beans you start with.

I haven't ground coffee myself for years, but I did recently buy a grinder again. I was surprised to find all over again just how aromatic a cup of coffee can be if you grind it fresh yourself and make it carefully using a good method. I use a cafetiere which gives something with plenty of body. The beans were organic ones from a small supplier that are carried by a local supermarket.

I'm thinking of getting some direct from a supplier, though. Some of these look enticing:

[http://www.unionroasted.com/coffees/estates.html](http://www.unionroasted.com/coffees/estates.html)

### caroline — 2014-02-12T23:53:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-buy-a-cup-of-coffee.9725/post-109852>

Mike - you have such an enquiring mind - about everything ..... I love it!

### margor_j — 2014-02-13T00:22:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-buy-a-cup-of-coffee.9725/post-109857>

personally, i dont rate DE coffee either.. and after spending time in italy and reading bullet proof exec stuff, im even more horrified as to what 'passes' in NZ as a barista. Being able to pour a shot from a machine is not a measure of 'coffee talent'. most of them fall horribly wrong with that abhorrent (haha, warning, i really hate this!) act of tamping, then bashing the sides of the basket with the tamp and then re-tamping the coffee! WTF! well, in a competition, youd be straight out the door for that, because its going to mess up the extraction some what. i refuse to pay for lousy grinds, that are just as lousily tamped, in a crappy extraction... but thats good,cos im off the stuff anyway  hehe
as for chai - yes, in india, it is a spiced tea. in cafes, its a syrup. very rare that it would be anything other (like the different nut syrups and stuff as well that ppl have in their massive, milky coffees that dont taste like coffee (hello starbucks).
if i sound dark about this well... theres a lot to learn about coffee, and to find the baristas who are true baristas... thats a push! its just as complex as cocoa and that complexity could easily be compared to wines..
DONT PAY FOR CRAP IN A CUP!
 rant over

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-13T10:33:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-buy-a-cup-of-coffee.9725/post-109946>

I don't think those do people's digestion any good.

I think I could probably enjoy French-style (or new Orleans-style) cafe-au-lait with a fair amount of strong black coffee to scalded milk, though I don't suppose I'd feel comfortable in my stomach afterwards. But these coffees that taste like milkshakes ...

### caroline — 2014-02-13T10:43:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trying-to-buy-a-cup-of-coffee.9725/post-109947>

It was in a large milkshake glass with icecream and loads of whipping cream and chocolate!!! I still don't know how I kept myself from getting one ... that was so yummy looking I was beside myself! Quantum desire and quantum leering going on

oh my ..... I am losing my touch if I am leering at coffee .......not a good sign on so many levels!

# Thread: "The Americans were pounding the bread"
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-americans-were-pounding-the-bread.9728/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-12T20:23:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-americans-were-pounding-the-bread.9728/post-109816>

This is a quote from the Caveman Doctor.

I was listening to an old podcast of his. He'd been on holiday in southern Italy, and what he had to say was pretty interesting.

He said there were plenty of tourists around, and he began thinking that many of his fellow Americans were overweight, while simultaneously noticing that obesity was rare among the local populace. So he started to count samples. He counted 30 Americans and found that 60% of them looked obese. He counted off 30 Italians and he says only "a handful" of them were.

He also noticed how much fitter the Italians were. It was very hilly where he was, and some of these buildings have over 300 steps up to them. The Italians just take these in their stride. Now the Caveman Doctor himself is a young and fit man who lifts weights, but he said even his thighs began to ache, and most of the American tourists were just nowhere. In fact, they were mostly using taxis between the different tourist sights (and doubtless missing out some they couldn't get up the steps to see).

He said it would be no exaggeration to say that some of the tourists that he saw in their 30s would need to use "five points" to get up off the ground from a sitting position (a test originally devised for 80 year olds). It's sad to think of people who should be still in their prime in this state. He also commented that matters in the States being how they are now he'd bet that many of these people will have been given statins, too, which will further impair and degrade their physical capacity and health.

Later on in the podcast he talked about the local food. He said it was superb. He also said it was not at all like the mythical "Mediterranean Diet" of the low-fat faddists -- a point that's already been made some years ago (June 2000) by the ladies at the WAPF:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/traditional-diets/mediterranean-diet](http://www.westonaprice.org/traditional-diets/mediterranean-diet)

There was masses of seafood, including some dishes like calamari turning up every day. There was also, what he hadn't expected, a lot of meat, much of it "preserved meat". (I guess this means air-dried ham, salami, sausages, and so on.) There was quite a bit of good-quality full-fat cheese. While you hear a lot about mediterranean monounsaturates and olive oil and nuts; he says he got the sense of more saturated fat that's generally said to be "Mediterranean". There was certainly olive oil, but he saw no nuts. He says most local people were not eating many vegetables, which surprised him.

In the restaurants they put baskets of bread on the tables. He said the locals would generally just take a small piece to mop their oil up with. However, it was different with the American tourists.

He said: "The Americans were pounding the bread."

### caroline — 2014-02-13T12:11:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-americans-were-pounding-the-bread.9728/post-109964>

That was really interesting ..... no surprises tho....

I am Thinking that I could get away with a bit of bread if I wanted to - I probably wouldn't be so hyper vigilant.

As Dr Kruse is saying .... when our redox is great we can get away with little misdemeanors.

Some Canadian friends were just here in Sydney for a few days - I keep thinking of them now that they have gone home. Ever since I have known them they have always been slender and healthy. They don't have cell phones. They devour books by the ton. They Golf, fish, hunt, garden, and love wine. They eat very healthy homecooked meals most of the time.
Altho Brian is a Doc - he really controlled the hours he worked because he adores his family - and he is a GP - so no EMF hell I suppose. They radiate kindness and love and energy and an interest in everybody and everything. I think this is an example of a life well lived.

# Thread: Food Timing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-timing.9733/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-02-12T22:30:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-timing.9733/post-109837>

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413114000059](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413114000059)

> **Summary**
> Circadian clocks play a major role in orchestrating daily physiology, and their disruption can evoke metabolic diseases such as fatty liver and obesity. To study the role of circadian clocks in lipid homeostasis, we performed an extensive lipidomic analysis of liver tissues from wild-type and clock-disrupted mice either fed ad libitum or night fed. To our surprise, a similar fraction of lipids (∼17%) oscillated in both mouse strains, most notably triglycerides, but with completely different phases. Moreover, several master lipid regulators (e.g., PPARα) and enzymes involved in triglyceride metabolism retained their circadian expression in clock-disrupted mice. Nighttime restricted feeding shifted the phase of triglyceride accumulation and resulted in ∼50% decrease in hepatic triglyceride levels in wild-type mice. Our findings suggest that circadian clocks and feeding time dictate the phase and levels of hepatic triglyceride accumulation; however, oscillations in triglycerides can persist in the absence of a functional clock.

> In studying the role of circadian rhythm in the accumulation of lipids in the liver, postdoctoral fellow Yaarit Adamovich and the team in the lab of [Dr. Gad Asher](http://www.weizmann.ac.il/Biological_Chemistry/scientist/Asher/) of the Weizmann Institute’s Biological Chemistry Department, together with scientists from Dr. Xianlin Han’s lab in the Sanford-Burnham Medical Research Institute, Orlando, US, quantified hundreds of different lipids present in the mouse liver. They discovered that a certain group of lipids, namely the triglycerides (TAG), exhibit circadian behavior, with levels peaking about eight hours after sunrise. The scientists were astonished to find, however, that daily fluctuations in this group of lipids persist even in mice lacking a functional biological clock, albeit with levels cresting at a completely different time – 12 hours later than the natural schedule. “These results came as a complete surprise: One would expect that if the inherent clock mechanism is ‘dead,’ TAG could not accumulate in a time-dependent fashion,” says Adamovich. So what was making the fluctuating lipid levels “tick” if not the clocks? “One thing that came to mind was that, since food is a major source of lipids – particularly TAG – the eating habits of these mice might play a role.” Usually, mice consume 20% of their food during the day and 80% at night. However, in mice lacking a functional clock, the team noted that they ingest food constantly throughout the day. This observation excluded the possibility that food is responsible for the fluctuating patterns seen in TAG levels in these mice.

[http://wis-wander.weizmann.ac.il/time-is-of-the-essence#.UvvbDvldVAI](http://wis-wander.weizmann.ac.il/time-is-of-the-essence#.UvvbDvldVAI)

# Thread: Dr. Rodney Ford on gluten
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rodney-ford-on-gluten.9739/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-13T13:59:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rodney-ford-on-gluten.9739/post-109969>

Evan Brand, who comes across as a very enthusiastic and nice podcaster (and who incidentally has an interview with Dr. Kruse), has an interview with New Zealand gluten-expert Dr. Rodney Ford.

I got it off iTunes, but here's the direct link:

[http://notjustpaleo.com/drford/](http://notjustpaleo.com/drford/)

There's some good stuff in there. Here's one interesting snippet: apparently, some skin conditions can take six months to a year to clear up _after_ the patient has been put on a gluten-free diet. Apparently "the gluten complexes stay around in the skin".

He also says that improvemenst from taking gluten out of the diet sometimes show up in days or weeks with children; with adolescents, it can take months; with adults, years. He makes the comparison with smoking: if you're a smoker your risk for lung cancer doesn't drop for some years after you give up. If you do damage it has to heal, and if you've done it for years, and you're an adult and heal slower anyway, it can take that long.

Actually, Dr. Ford seems to regard gluten as pretty much the new smoking. He says he believes it is implicated in a third of all chronic disease conditions.

He also says, what I hadn't heard, that global harvests of wheat are now down by quite a sizeable amount. It seems that many people worldwide are getting the message about gluten and simply turning away from wheat.

New Zealand sounds like something of a paradise. Not only is there all that "Lord of Rings" scenery. Most of the livestock is grassfed, of course; and, according to Dr. Ford, the seas are clean, the people are fairly health-conscious (and alert to threats to them), and GM crops aren't allowed.

### sooperb — 2014-02-13T18:54:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rodney-ford-on-gluten.9739/post-109992>

Just to add to the good news about New Zealand, where once you had to like lamb a lot  they have now reduced the number of sheep they have and have increased the beef herds. As you have pointed out, they have no trouble finding the grass so yep, they all tend to be grass fed. For now. I am trying not to be pessimistic but how long before they discover they can fatten them up on grain, especially if the world price is dropping because of demand?

### freesia — 2014-02-14T01:51:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-rodney-ford-on-gluten.9739/post-110077>

Recently I encountered some Americans confused when they couldn't find the grass fed beef in the Supermarket. I explained it's all grass fed therefore not labelled as such.
There's big opposition here to anything resembling CAFOs (yuck yuck yuck), but with so many farms being sold to buyers from overseas where industrial farming is more the norm I have a few worries too.
I think our seas are reasonably clean - my health has only improved since I've been getting in the ocean more.
But we do have that, infamous around here, environmental stressor....the \*cue jaws music\* ...donut hole.

### Evan Brand — 2016-09-03T04:03:29+0300

so, i guess im not answering ur question at all - im just saying.. hell to the yeah, chocolate rocks! cocoa / cocoa beans i mite make an exception for but def watch the amounts cos how sure can we be that its not affecting us... lol

# Thread: Nicole, would you share your CO/Cocoa bark from last night?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nicole-would-you-share-your-co-cocoa-bark-from-last-night.9755/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2014-02-14T16:43:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nicole-would-you-share-your-co-cocoa-bark-from-last-night.9755/post-110189>

Nicole, if you read this, would you mind sharing your legal CO/cocoa bark recipe that you mentioned in the chat last night?

I would love to try it....

Thanks

### nicld — 2014-02-15T23:32:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nicole-would-you-share-your-co-cocoa-bark-from-last-night.9755/post-110481>

I didn't have coconut bark per say but I do make coconut "crack" and bullet proof hot cocoa.

My coconut crack is nutriva coconut oil, unsweetened coconut, stevia and dark cocoa powder. Just mix it all together and enjoy. No real amounts just what you feel like having.

I did mention that I make Bullet proof hot cocoa. It is unsweetened coconut milk (I use So Delicious coconut milk in the green container).
I pour what I want to drink in a cup to measure then heat it up in my hot pot (not the microwave).
In my ninja blender I add about 1 tsp dark chocolate cocoa powder and 1 tsp cocoa powder. I also add about 1-2 tbsp. coconut oil and some MCT oil (now that I have it). Also add some stevia to taste and sometimes heavy cream. Pour the hot coconut milk in the blender and blend, then enjoy.

### amberge — 2015-02-10T13:53:40+0200

# Thread: Tuna, Oil Spills and of Course Us Humans...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tuna-oil-spills-and-of-course-us-humans.9760/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-02-14T18:40:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tuna-oil-spills-and-of-course-us-humans.9760/post-110232>

> Human implications?
> Because the mechanisms involved operate in the same way in the hearts of all vertebrates, it is highly likely, the team says, that other animals swimming in waters around the crippled rig would have been exposed to similar cardiac risks.
>
>
> And the questions also reach across to human health - because vehicle engines put PAHs into the air in our cities.
>
> “Impressively, the cardiac excitation-contraction coupling pathways are the most conserved pathways in all of animals,” said Prof Block.
>
> “It means that the same ion channels present in tuna to make its heart beat are present in humans.
>
> "So we’re interested in the impact of oil petroleum products on our own excitation-contraction coupling, and we’re interested in linking air pollution, for example – a place where petroleum products are often found, volatiles from our exhausts - to the problems of cardiac morbidity that are seen across the planet on a very smoggy day.”

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-26184116](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-26184116)

>

# Thread: Underground power lines as bad as above?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/underground-power-lines-as-bad-as-above.9785/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2014-02-16T02:22:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/underground-power-lines-as-bad-as-above.9785/post-110534>

Does anyone here know anything about underground power lines? I ask bc we are looking for places to live and my fav area has no above ground lines as far as I can tell, and it must come from somewhere.

While it's nice not to see some of the ugly towers that seem ubiquitous here, I am not sure if this is worse, better or the same. Anyone know anything or have a source? I can't seem to figure it out and my brain is feeling fried.

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-16T03:12:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/underground-power-lines-as-bad-as-above.9785/post-110546>

I spoke to Michael (the EMF guy) about power lines as we could bury ours...we are the end of the line on a back road. This turns out to be a good reason not to bother...we are not getting a lot of crap from surrounding houses AND there is a risk that the magnetic part of the EMF package actually comes closer to you by being in the ground. If you do it with super shielded cables etc. it can diminish the electric field, but not the magnetic one. Dr. K mentioned the same thing in a webinar when I asked him a question about it.....there isn't a lot of specific advise out there about burying cables. The best thing to do is to measure with a good devise. If they are already in the ground, then don't worry about it and keep away from the area if it registers a high field.

### Hope — 2014-02-16T21:10:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/underground-power-lines-as-bad-as-above.9785/post-110653>

SeaHorse, thank you SO much. Great thought. They would have to be in-ground already...and we couldn't change a thing as it's an established townhouse community.....

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20-minute-pieces-of-music.9792/post-110615>

Sometimes I like to take time out to sit or lie quietly for 20 minutes and listen to music. So something that lasts about 20 minutes is useful. Here's some I've currently got:

En Saga, Jean Sibelius
The Warriors, Percy Grainger
Billy the Kid, Aaron Copland
Romeo and Juliet - Fantasy Overture, Tchaikovsky
In the South (Alassio), Edward Elgar

Any other suggestions?

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-17T10:02:50+0200

Thanks. I don't know him, but I can find and sample this in iTunes.

Apparently, if you lie down 20 mins is just long enough for your intervertebral discs to swell again (they tend to get the fluid forced out a little through the day just by the weight coming down the spinal column) as well as generally to quieten down.

I did find a CD of recordings of the sea the other day. A whole hour! It's supposed to be soothing. I did buy it, as it was pretty cheap -- it ought to be!

It is kind of soothing. Most people would probably think I'm eccentric if they knew I'd bought a CD of the sea. Still, I'm sure they think we're mad already. They probably see avoiding cake as a sign of insanity. LOL

I heard that there was a famous violinist doing a recital somewhere and an audience member's mobile phone went off. This was the well-known Nokia ring-tune -- back in the days when people had those. Without pausing the violinist played the ring-tune back and then played a set of variations on it, then going on with his piece again. What a dramatic way of showing someone up for disturbing the performance! It must've been quite funny.

# Thread: Ethiopian spiced coffee
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-16T18:26:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-110620>

I know the Ethiopians have been cited as the inventors of "bulletproof coffee" -- probably by the Bulletproof Man himself.

Looking around you do come across vague references to Ethipioans putting "butter and spices" in their coffee. Looking closer it looks like what they're doing is adding "spiced butter" -- which turns out to be clarified butter with spices in. Makes sense: clarifying the butter would help it keep, specially important in a hot climate before refrigeration. The spices might help that, too.

I wonder _which_ ones. All the recipes I found for "Ethiopian Spiced Butter" included stuff like garlic that would not exactly go well with coffee.

So has anyone any idea?

I don't often make this stuff, preferring my coffee straight up black, but it would be interesting to know what people who've been doing this for a long time choose to use.

\----------------
By the way, I came across a Heston recipe for "Rich chilli con carne with spiced butter". Sounds interesting (and suitably LCHF). I haven't tried it yet, but I'll post it, if anyone's interested.

### Inger — 2014-02-16T18:56:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-110624>

I sometimes add ground vanilla too... very good too but cardamom is my favorite

Strangely enough, I had wondered if cardamom would work well. I'll have to try that.

### nonchalant — 2014-02-17T16:31:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-110809>

I bought some tea a few days ago that also has cardamom and cinnamon. Must be cardamom season!

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-17T16:43:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-110814>

I would say cardamom, turmeric, cumin, cinnamon, and vanilla.... that would be awesome

### sooperb — 2014-03-04T15:37:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-113340>

Spiced ghee sounds wonderful, the Indian company has an amazing range, shame they are all out of reach. Found this recipe if anyone's interested:

1.  -
        for the Spiced Ghee
    -   3/4 lb butter
    -   6 cardamom pods
    -   2 cinnamon sticks
    -   1 tsp whole coriander seeds
    -   1/2 tsp powdered turmeric
    -   1/2 tsp fenugreek seeds
    -   1/2 tsp cumin seeds

    Preparing the Spiced Ghee
2.  If you wish to prepare your own spiced ghee instead of purchasing traditional Niter Kibbeh ([available here](http://nourishedkitchen.com/where-to-buy/#fats)), simple begin by placing the butter in a wide stainless steel skillet over low heat. Toss in the spices. As the butter melts, it will release a fine foam. Skim off the foam and discard it. Continue melting the butter until it releases all its foam, about 30 minutes, and the remaining milk solids settle to the bottom of the pan.
3.  Place a fine mesh strainer over a bowl, and line the strainer with butter muslin ([available here](http://nourishedkitchen.com/recommends/butter-muslin/)) and pour the melted butter through the muslin-lined strainer. Transfer the the spiced ghee to individual jars and store at room temperature in a dark cupboard for up to 1 year.

### Lahelada — 2014-03-04T15:51:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-113344>

Think about need rather than taste. The cardamom is probably spot on as it used widely as a as an anti stomach upset remedy when drinking water that may not be be pure. Cardamom is still sprinkled into well water in India,essentially using it as a flocculant.
Another example is Mexican streetfood people get sick from it. Avoidable if you do not eschew the cilantro and lemon served with it.

### Lahelada — 2014-03-04T16:16:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-113350>

A bit of investigation in other languages tells me cardamom and cloves but also that it is not a national but a regional thing. They have an interesting coffee ceremony.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-04T16:55:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-113356>

One more to add to your spiced coffee Garam Masala! incase anyone is wondering... I'm on my countdown to coffee. only 29 days until Coffee returns to my life....

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-137331>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=113356)
>
> One more to add to your spiced coffee Garam Masala! incase anyone is wondering... I'm on my countdown to coffee. only 29 days until Coffee returns to my life....

Never would have thought of that - thanks - I LOVE garam masala...

I do the Moroccan coffee: vanilla, black pepper, turmeric, coconut oil, cacao powder, cardomom, cinammon, ginger - the black pepper (bioperine) activates the turmeric so it's more bioactive -

### Penny — 2014-08-09T01:36:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ethiopian-spiced-coffee.9793/post-137332>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=113344)

# Thread: Interpreting raw data 23and me
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interpreting-raw-data-23and-me.9801/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2014-02-17T15:38:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interpreting-raw-data-23and-me.9801/post-110790>

I have received my raw data and geneology info from 23and me. Man am I white. I thought there might be some African or Native American in the mix, but no except for some Neanderthal and a bit of Ashkenazi I am all European. Anyway, does anyone know how to get started with the raw data for health?

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-17T15:49:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interpreting-raw-data-23and-me.9801/post-110798>

One of the best programs is through Sterling's app on MTHFR support. You buy the program, upload your date and get a very good selection of snps pertaining to detox, methylation, allergies etc.

I don't know of any quick way of extracting info. for a lot of the disease risks that they used to include. It there were particular ones you were interested in, somebody could probably tell you the specific gene or genes they looked at. Then you could plug in that to your raw data and see what comes up. I didn't print mine out before they changed the site, so I'm in the same boat.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-17T15:56:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interpreting-raw-data-23and-me.9801/post-110802>

This always sounds interesting -- wish we had one in this country.

According to the Caveman Doctor the Federal government in the U.S. seems to be trying to shut 23andme down, though it's not clear what they have against it. IIRC, it was privacy concerns that they kept throwing at its procedures, though there's nothing about the procedures that should trigger that, as they're pretty much the same as everyone else's -- and at this late stage ...

### nicld — 2014-02-17T16:09:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interpreting-raw-data-23and-me.9801/post-110804>

I am 99.2% northern European, .5% southern European and .3% not assigned. I was hoping for a little more variation too but knowing that my great grandparents on my fathers side were from Germany it is no shock that I was 14.2% German/French.

The Sterling app works great but it is still alot of data to go through.

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-17T16:37:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interpreting-raw-data-23and-me.9801/post-110812>

Dali
here's Sterling's app [http://www.mthfrsupport.com/reports-consults/](http://www.mthfrsupport.com/reports-consults/)
you can do this [http://geneticgenie.org/](http://geneticgenie.org/)

### nonchalant — 2014-02-17T16:58:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interpreting-raw-data-23and-me.9801/post-110819>

I imagine some crony owns a competing business, Mike.

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=110802)
>
> This always sounds interesting -- wish we had one in this country.
>
> According to the Caveman Doctor the Federal government in the U.S. seems to be trying to shut 23andme down, though it's not clear what they have against it. IIRC, it was privacy concerns that they kept throwing at its procedures, though there's nothing about the procedures that should trigger that, as they're pretty much the same as everyone else's -- and at this late stage ...

### Embyr42 — 2014-02-19T17:08:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interpreting-raw-data-23and-me.9801/post-111181>

I have a spreadsheet that you can put the data that you ran through sterling's app (or just type in from your 23 and me raw data if you want) and I've listed many of what the gene mutations relate to off to the side.
If you PM me I'll send it to you. Anything missing can easily be looked up on snpedia.com. Prometheus is another good tool to run your results through and get some interpretation. It interprets things sterling doesn't look at that are intriguing.

### Gracelind — 2014-02-19T17:37:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interpreting-raw-data-23and-me.9801/post-111189>

For ancestry upload your raw 23 and Me data in txt format at [http://Gedmatch.com](http://Gedmatch.com). It runs your info past more detailed databases and produces pretty chromosome paintings, among other things.

Also, look at Stanford Interpretome: [http://www.interpretome.com/](http://www.interpretome.com/)

# Thread: Acupressure Mat - I got one!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/acupressure-mat-i-got-one.9812/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### margor_j — 2014-02-17T22:20:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/acupressure-mat-i-got-one.9812/post-110861>

After talking to you caroline! lol i relented and got one, because i found them in NZ..

[http://www.shantimat.co.nz/](http://www.shantimat.co.nz/)

Im onto day 3. I still have a thin layer of material between me and the mat, but you still feel the prickles and im amazed at how much they dont seem to hurt.. i guess im up to ten minutes or so? i put it in my bed and lie on it when im going to bed - seems to be a lot of people found they fall asleep a lot easier if they do that, so il try that as well!

what have i noticed? nothing that blew me out of the water, as of yet! def havent NOT gone to sleep after using it, has it helped my sleep? not sure - knocking back on CO later in the day made an instant effect on sleep!
i do like the idea of it!! so il keep on with it..

### sooperb — 2014-02-17T23:41:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/acupressure-mat-i-got-one.9812/post-110875>

### Josh — 2014-02-19T05:12:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qu-wave.9819/post-111120>

I have one of these:

[http://www.biophotonanalyzer.com/](http://www.biophotonanalyzer.com/)

ran it continuously for about a year except at night because my wife said she could feel it and did not like it. It has a protection/cocoon setting and the feedback I got from sensitive people is that it interferes with the natural subtle energies that nourish us as well. I did notice that it always felt a little stimulating. I stopped using it and do not regret it. I use it to clone things resonantly which it does well. I imagine the QuWave is some version of this and may have unintended and less beneficial side effects, but I could be wrong.

### Danco3636 — 2014-02-19T06:29:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qu-wave.9819/post-111129>

### turism0remix — 2014-04-17T07:01:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qu-wave.9819/post-122062>

I have been using something similar called the Blushield [http://blushield-usa.com/](http://blushield-usa.com/) I believe that this is closer to the Earthpulse that your talking about (78.83Hz). I have been doing some research and I understand that QuWave uses coils to recreate that frequency which is an outdated technology. Whereas the Blushield is more up to date and uses a microprocessor to create the EMF instead, Which also uses less energy to produce. I have been using it around the house and by my electronics for the benefit that it would give me a piece of mind whenever I turn on the power. I haven't tried any of the other products but I think that the Blushield would be far more effective and more natural than the others.

### sooperb — 2014-04-17T18:57:46+0300

# Thread: Labs / Help me biohack my drive/ libido??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-help-me-biohack-my-drive-libido.9824/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pine_Cone — 2014-02-19T04:08:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-help-me-biohack-my-drive-libido.9824/post-111114>

Hello, I'm new to the forum (I posted a similar post in Optimal Journal)

**I'm attaching my wellness fx from this year and the previous year**. I'll also include a bit more about myself for context/ introduction. Any feedback on the labs, general comments/ questions are welcome. I look forward to interacting with the community and getting to know you.

I'm a 36 yr old male, 189 lbs with an athletic/muscular build although not 'ripped'. In the right light, I have a 4 pack  I would take healthy over ripped any day of the week, just trying to give you an idea of body composition.
Diet wise, I'm pretty strict with paleo and fall towards the low carb side of the spectrum. In January (the 30 days preceding my most recent labs, I was in ketosis for nearly 3 of the 4 weeks. I had some red wine 5 days before my lab work, which I think explains the whacky triglyceride markers. I also lost about 10 pounds in the 30 days preceding lab work. Also worth mentioning, I got blood work done in the morning (to see my testosterone at its best), fasted and I was in ketosis.

**One of my motivations for posting this is to find ways to optimize my drive. My libido is very low, and I feel like my drive in general is not what it used to be. I sell software for a living, so competitive drive is essential and my wife is too hot for me to be lacking drive in the bedroom**

It looks like I had some T3 down regulation during January due to the low carbohydrates and continuing to workout (short intense HIIT types of workouts).

I'm getting wordy, so I'll leave it there for now. I'm happy to answer additional questions if you have any. Thanks in advance for taking a look at my labs and helping me biohack my drive!

### JanSz — 2014-02-22T02:42:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-help-me-biohack-my-drive-libido.9824/post-111638>

> Diet wise, I'm pretty strict with paleo and fall towards the low carb side of the spectrum. In January (the 30 days preceding my most recent labs, I was in ketosis for nearly 3 of the 4 weeks. I had some red wine 5 days before my lab work, which I think explains the whacky triglyceride markers. I also lost about 10 pounds in the 30 days preceding lab work. Also worth mentioning, I got blood work done in the morning (to see my testosterone at its best), fasted and I was in ketosis.
>
> **One of my motivations for posting this is to find ways to optimize my drive. My libido is very low, and I feel like my drive in general is not what it used to be. I sell software for a living, so competitive drive is essential and my wife is too hot for me to be lacking drive in the bedroom**
>
> It looks like I had some T3 down regulation during January due to the low carbohydrates and continuing to workout (short intense HIIT types of workouts).
>
> I'm getting wordy, so I'll leave it there for now. I'm happy to answer additional questions if you have any. Thanks in advance for taking a look at my labs and helping me biohack my drive!

Consider talking with dr Tim Jackson here.

I am not a doctor, but note the following:

Get scale that measure body fat, you want 15% or less.

Per Epi-Paleo-Rx you should not eat carbohydrates with high or medium GI (ever).
Yes to the other carbohydrates but in season.

Post all Rx medicines and OTC supplements that you are taking.

Your pituitary is responding only partially to your low Total Testosterone.
high FSH (should be high)
low LH (should be high)
Consider pituitary MRI.
Have you been hit in the head, specially forehead?

If LH will stay as is you may consider trial of
HCG-shot=300iu/EOD (BD Ultra-Fine II U-100 Insulin Syringes Short Needle 31g 3/10cc 5/16in)
HCG mimic LH, may induce your testicles to produce more testosterone (check your testicles for possible varicocele, hydrocelle or something else)
You can buy HCG over internet (women use it for weight loss)

Your E2 looks low, that may change when you get more testosterone.
**To asses libido you should know your DHT** think of (75-115)ng/dL as good range

You want Total testosterone=>700ng/dL and no more than 1200
Your SHBG=35.4 is highish, good (15-20), I do not know specific item that would influence SHBG but good thyroid is must.

Your DHEAs=303.6 is not bad and not good, you want
350-490 μg/dL
get some DHEA pills, stick to one brand. I use Allergy Research Micronized Lipid Matrix 50mg.

To start working on thyroid
eat Epi-Paleo-Rx
Brazilian nuts
Selenium
Iodine/iodide (50-100)mg/day

TSH=0.857, 1.41, that is lowish, if that is due to eating thyroid hormones,........ I do not see much except for high RT3
if you do not eat thyroid hormones--------- that would be a second strike against your pituitary gland.
please clarify
Next time you test thyroid add antibodies.

\-----------------------
Cortisol AM ---- low
progesterone--- low
pregnenolone---- likely low

I see you taking
pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix from Nutricology, 150mg tabs scored

I use that and it works well for me.
I eat enough preg pills until my cortisol,serum(7:30AM)=(20-22)
You must figure your dose testing from top to bottom (at least preg, prog, cortisol).
Many things can happen, we are all different.
There is also transient involved. Specially with me, I started with undetectable preg & prog levels. My max dose was 900mg/day
but now
I am now taking two of those pills (300mg/day) (74yo man).

There are two goals with cortisol.
#1--To have enough of it (and no more)
#2--To have it distributed per Circadian Rhythm. (Study dr Kruses writings on how to tend to good Circadian Rhythm)
\=============================
If you have a spare $350, good idea to have Spectracell Micronutrients test done (every 6 months).
That covers almost 40 vitamins and micronutrients.

Look at my posts here:
[http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508)

\======================
IGF-1=134(117-300) lowish
next time do also
IGFBP3
[Dr Hertoghe
The Hormone Handbook 2Ed 2010
page 69

GH Growth Hormone
Excess, lowers SHBG
\-----
page 58

IGF-1 men
220-300 optimal
0-180 GH probably deficient

IGF-BP3
3000 optimal
\>4000 GH probably deficient](http://www.beckmancoulter.com/CustomerSupport/IFU/ivdd/6600.pdf)

\-----
Growth Hormone
per Tierry Hertoghe MD Hormone Solution
supplementation required when
IGF-1<150mcg/L
IGFBP-3>4000mcg/L=4mg/L=140(nmol/L)
\-------------
dose 0.02 - 0.4mg/day
1 mg. of powder GH = 3 iu
0.02-0.4mg/day=0.06-1.2iu/day
\--------------
Note.
Hertoghe, top p267 advices HGH at bed time,
Suzan is takig her's in the morning.

Signs of over dosage:
swollen feet, pins and needles in the fingers
carpal tunnel syndrome
pain in the joints (real serious)
\------------------------------------------
\========================================
