Thank you for the detailed reply - there's a TON here for me to consider and research.

You asked about RX and supplements. I don't take any prescription meds. As for supplements, I take Vit D (5 - 10,000 iu daily)... if that seems high it's because I live in the rainy Pac NW. I also take an Omega 3 supplement and a multi vitamin with selenium. I have also been trying Zinc/ Magnesium (ZMA) before bed. Other than those supplements I try to get most of what I need from my diet.

Thank you again for the help - I will take a look at the vitamin/ mineral labs and strongly consider getting that done in the next 30 days.

### JanSz — 2014-02-22T21:57:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-help-me-biohack-my-drive-libido.9824/post-111729>

> [Pine\_Cone said:](/goto/post?id=111721)
>
> Thank you for the detailed reply - there's a TON here for me to consider and research.
>
> You asked about RX and supplements. I don't take any prescription meds. As for supplements, I take Vit D (5 - 10,000 iu daily)... if that seems high it's because I live in the rainy Pac NW. I also take an Omega 3 supplement and a multi vitamin with selenium. I have also been trying Zinc/ Magnesium (ZMA) before bed. Other than those supplements I try to get most of what I need from my diet.
>
> Thank you again for the help - I will take a look at the vitamin/ mineral labs and strongly consider getting that done in the next 30 days.

I suggest not eating Omega3 supplements or any other fatty acid type of supplements unless supported by Fatty Acids Analysis.

The big deal about Fish oils or Krill oils is that they have megadoses of EPA which will influence negatively your Omega6 profile.

It is so bad that even if you do Fatty Acids Analysis you usually will not get clear picture.
There is proliferation of laboratories that do Fatty Acids Analysis and they are not worth dealing with.
Look at my post here
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-2](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-2)
for best analysis that I know about.
but
Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum
at Mayo Clinic or LabCorp is pretty good.

In the mean time eat Epi-Paleo-Rx.

Hopefully in some future blogs dr Kruse will address this in detail.

////////////////////////////////////

Vit D, start with 10000-12000iu and then you will likely need more.

////

# Thread: First cold bath
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Julie5 — 2014-02-19T05:22:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111122>

My hubby and I have been preparing ourselves for CT over the last few weeks. A trip over the weekend derailed him, but I kept at it. We got home today, and after taking care of essentials like unpacking, I began to run some water in the tub. Taking someone's advice, I got in and let it fill up around me. I hadto keep reminding myself of what Jack said on FB a while back: Don't let fear dictate your actions. Well, I didn't quite get the water into the 50s. But I id get it to 60 degrees up to my arm pits. I was surprised that it didn't feel any colder...until I put my upper arms in the water about 15 minutes into it. Then the shivering began. Iheld out for 30 minutes. Yoohoo! CT was my greatest fear. I conquered it!

### caroline — 2014-02-19T12:02:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111155>

good girl! You rocked it! We are often our own biggest enemy - at least I know I am. Once you are in it doesn't even seem that cold does it.......

### nicld — 2014-02-19T16:42:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111178>

> [Julie5 said:](/goto/post?id=111122)
>
> My hubby and I have been preparing ourselves for CT over the last few weeks. A trip over the weekend derailed him, but I kept at it. We got home today, and after taking care of essentials like unpacking, I began to run some water in the tub. Taking someone's advice, I got in and let it fill up around me. I hadto keep reminding myself of what Jack said on FB a while back: Don't let fear dictate your actions. Well, I didn't quite get the water into the 50s. But I id get it to 60 degrees up to my arm pits. I was surprised that it didn't feel any colder...until I put my upper arms in the water about 15 minutes into it. Then the shivering began. Iheld out for 30 minutes. Yoohoo! CT was my greatest fear. I conquered it!

WHOOOHOOOOO!!!!!! Don't worry about getting the water down to between 50-55 degrees right away. Do what you can and eventually you will be able to get it colder. Any CT is great CT.

It will become quite addictive and hopefully the hubby will let you rewarm with him unless he does CT right after you. Then you might need some oxy release to rewarm the both of you

### nonchalant — 2014-02-19T16:47:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111179>

Heh, this reminds me of my first time. Yes, fear is a big factor beforehand, but surprise is a big factor a couple minutes into the bath. Hey, I can actually do this!

### Julie5 — 2014-02-20T03:39:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111307>

Sooo...second bath today. Same temp, same length of time. Today I felt colder. But I do have a question: Am I supposed to immerse my arms but leave my hands out? When my arms are submerged I start shivering like crazy. I know the shivering is fine, but it makes me want to get out earlier. My tub is very deep, so if I put my arms up on top of the tub, it gets uncomfortable very fast. I will continue with 60-65 deg. water for the next couple of weeks before making it colder. Or is that too slow?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111314>

### caroline — 2014-02-20T05:30:24+0200

When my arms are submerged I start shivering like crazy. I know the shivering is fine, but it makes me want to get out earlier. My tub is very deep, so if I put my arms up on top of the tub, it gets uncomfortable very fast. I will continue with 60-65 deg. water for the next couple of weeks before making it colder. Or is that too slow?

I have a deep tub too and for the most part I keep my arms out too.

Just do a temp that works for you. Eventually you will find you want it colder.

### sooperb — 2014-02-20T22:59:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111412>

I started with 60 and found that quite easy. Next I went down to about 57 and that wasn't too bad, I stayed in 30 minutes. Today I tried it at 54, it was still ok getting in and staying in and I managed 45 minutes. I was feeling a bit cold toward the end and I was cold for a fair while after I got out and dried.

I'm not clear about whether it's beneficial to stay in any longer or whether you should try to CT everyday or to space it out a bit.

### nicld — 2014-02-21T01:36:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111430>

From what I have heard Jack say in various webinars, the worse your exposure to non-native EMF's the more CT you need to do to improve yourself. That's kinda why I do really cold and long CT sessions. My battery is just not recharging enough. But, just do what you can since any CT is good CT.

### caroline — 2014-02-21T01:40:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111431>

~you have it nailed Nicole!~

### freesia — 2014-02-21T02:54:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111460>

You're doing GREAT sooperb! Just do it as often as feels good/fits in with what else you want to do. Feel good about 10 minutes and feel good about 45 mins.

My first winter I tried ever day then had to space it a bit because it stimulated some (I think estrogen) detox. Second winter I did it mostly every day over the coldest months then didn't stress when I had to take a week away from it. Now, in summertime, I'm managing 2 or 3 times a week. My 9 year old is nagging me to make more ice, because if he had his way he'd CT every day. (Funny kid.)

### Lahelada — 2014-02-21T03:04:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111465>

I hope I am right on this, but at this stage of the game as often, as cold and as long as you want and don´t want...

### Julie5 — 2014-02-21T07:23:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-cold-bath.9825/post-111484>

I am so glad for all your combined experience! With all my issues, I don't quite know every avenue to explore, but I will follow Jack by doing the 3 legged stool. I"ve got the water down. Next the light and, hopefully along with it, the magnetism. Plus eating the RX.

# Thread: Sea Salt in foods
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sea-salt-in-foods.9827/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2014-02-19T07:00:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sea-salt-in-foods.9827/post-111134>

I've heard that many times when Sea Salt is mentioned its referencing coarsely ground refined salt, and not unrefined sea salt that contains trace minerals.

I sent a question to the people who make On the Border Chips because I was curious if the sea salt listed was good or not.

Dear Jonathan,

Thank you for your inquiry regarding our On The Border Tortilla Chips.

In regards to your question, our sea salt is produced using vacuum evaporation of purified brine, which is obtained from salt ponds near the San Francisco bay. It contains up to 1% Tricalcium Phosphate as a flow agent.

I hope this information helps to answer your question. Should you have any additional questions or comments, please feel free to contact us.

I got this response.

Tricalcium Phosphate first of all I'm wondering how bad that is as far as anti-caking ie flow agents go, and I'm wondering if their description of how they acquire the salt would mean it includes trace minerals or if its just a typical refined salt devoid of everything but sodium and chloride.

I should stay away from these chips anyway for multiple reasons, but since I still haven't conquered my cravings I get curious about random stuff that I notice. I know there are plenty of reasons not to eat the chips besides the salt even if it is good salt....curious anyway.

I've noticed a few things like certain chocolate mentioning Fleur De Sel specifically but most just say Sea Salt. I'm wondering if most of these are just refined salt but they call it sea salt for marketing or if many are actually salt with trace minerals included.

Thanks

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-19T10:28:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sea-salt-in-foods.9827/post-111146>

> [Raiken3712 said:](/goto/post?id=111134)
>
> I've heard that many times when Sea Salt is mentioned its referencing coarsely ground refined salt, and not unrefined sea salt that contains trace minerals.
>
> I sent a question to the people who make On the Border Chips because I was curious if the sea salt listed was good or not.
>
> Dear Jonathan,
>
>
> Thank you for your inquiry regarding our On The Border Tortilla Chips.
>
>
> In regards to your question, our sea salt is produced using vacuum evaporation of purified brine, which is obtained from salt ponds near the San Francisco bay. It contains up to 1% Tricalcium Phosphate as a flow agent.
>
>
> I hope this information helps to answer your question. Should you have any additional questions or comments, please feel free to contact us.
>
> I got this response.
>
> Tricalcium Phosphate first of all I'm wondering how bad that is as far as anti-caking ie flow agents go, and I'm wondering if their description of how they acquire the salt would mean it includes trace minerals or if its just a typical refined salt devoid of everything but sodium and chloride.
>
> I should stay away from these chips anyway for multiple reasons, but since I still haven't conquered my cravings I get curious about random stuff that I notice. I know there are plenty of reasons not to eat the chips besides the salt even if it is good salt....curious anyway.
>
> I've noticed a few things like certain chocolate mentioning Fleur De Sel specifically but most just say Sea Salt. I'm wondering if most of these are just refined salt but they call it sea salt for marketing or if many are actually salt with trace minerals included.
>
> Thanks

Seasalt refers to its being got from the sea. Otherwise, there's rocksalt, which is mined. But. AFAIK rocksalt deposits are simply where the sea once was.

An example of seasalt would be Maldon salt, which is still got by evaporating seawater in pans at Maldon. That comes in large white flakes. An example of rocksalt would be the Himalayan salt, which is pink, because of trace minerals in it, and comes in lumps or ready-ground.

Salt's generally fairly refined these days. I suppose that's because in the past people have identified its degree of whiteness with its purity.

I guess the flow agent stops the salt clumping up and possibly jamming the machinery -- I'd suppose they spray it over the food to get an even coating. I don't know what else they could do -- besides provide a paper sachet of salt and let you add your own.

### nonchalant — 2014-02-19T17:22:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sea-salt-in-foods.9827/post-111186>

Fleur de Sel salt is harvested from sea brine ponds in France, similar to how the San Francisco salt is. It has a snowflake-like texture and is exquisite.
But France has joined with other nations that now fluoridate their salt. I'm not positive the salt is fluoridated. Perhaps they put fluoride in the brine ponds before the salt is formed? I'm not buying it anymore.

### Tanya — 2014-02-19T22:31:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sea-salt-in-foods.9827/post-111240>

I love taranka - dried and salted fish that is packaged in Russia. I eat it by itself or cut it up and use it in my broth. I wonder now what kind of salt they use. Have some investigating to do…..

# Thread: Renee's Lab's - What does it all mean??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/renees-labs-what-does-it-all-mean.9828/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 8

### Renee — 2014-02-19T07:13:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/renees-labs-what-does-it-all-mean.9828/post-111136>

Hi Jack and Forum members ..
My name is Renée and I 'm new to all this and participating in the Optimal Reset 2014 - Learning so much!
I've posted this in the Optimal Journal section but have a bunch of lab tests that someone suggested I repost in this section so that many I can get some advice.. Below is a bit of my story summary along with the labs.. they are a bit out of date but give some history..

I am a 48 yrs old female - post menopausal.. I went into menopause suddenly when I was 42 - like overnight it seemed.. everything changed! Lost libido, became extremely fatigued, chronically fatigued actually, adrenal burnout and an emotional train reck.. Also my disordered eating behaviour came back with a vengeance and it hadn't really been an issue for several years. Reynauds showed up as well and low thyroid - cold intolerance and hair loss as well as brittle hair / nails, dry skin and very dry eyes and painfully so. I have multiple food sensitivities and they seem to be getting more - I also have severe candida / fungal overgrowth issues due the mercury toxicity issue. I also have a weird thing that happens in the afternoon.. my entire nose burns and turns red .. it's really odd and it started last year doing that - I think maybe it's a histamine reaction or some sort of imbalance but again I have all these symptoms that are popping up more and more it seems.

I have seen a myriad of natural and traditional alopathic medical practitioners to help me try to figure out what was going on with my health.. It's been a looooong journey - and it seems like I just keep getting more and more symptoms and that I'm chasing around a red herring, spending all my savings on tests and protocols to the point that I'm in debt .. and not getting any better! I have chronic fatigue and no energy for life at all - I have a hard time getting through my day and often find myself by 10am wanting to go home and to bed.. I am often in bed by 7:30-8pm and wake up dragged out and fatigued. I sleep in a pitch black room with NO electronics etc.. and limit my blue light exposure before bed etc. Been doing that for over a year/ half.. I've done IF lots ( 16/8) as well as 2x 24hrs fasts /week to see if that helps..
I have found that if I eat fat I gain weight immediately! I keep my carbs very low.. No fruit or stachy carbs of any kind.. and seem not be to able to handle fat at all.. Maybe because of my redox potential?? Not sure why but seems like I have to keep my carbs extremely low and low fat to avoid not balloooning up in weight..
I really struggle with maintaining weight and have lower body stubborn fat on my thighs especially - it has been impossible to loose this fat - even when I've done super keto diet and lost weight - my thighs remained heavy with fat stores.
I have attached many of the lab tests that I've done over the past few years for a bit of a history. Honestly OMG I've spent soo much money on all this and all I got was a battery of supplements which did nothing to change my health in the right direction. I live in Canada an I've gone over the borer to the US to do many of the blood and other tests because our labs don't do many of the specific tests here.. I've never had a Reverse T3 test and asked the lab here and they said they don't use that at all  .. I've love to know if I could order this myself on line somehow ? I don't mind going down to Bellingham WA to a lab to get the test if required.. or any other tests too to help me determine what's really going on with me.

I also suffer from Reynauds as I mentioned and CT scare the crap out of me because my fingers and toes become so painful when I'm cold which is pretty much almost every day! If I get anything out of the freezer or it's slightly cold out - they freeze up instantly and turn white /blue and it's really painful.

Just wondering how to start to be able to do CT ? Should I start in the sink ? How does one get into the tub without getting feet and hands too cold?? .. I follow an ancestral diet as well .. eat really clean - paleo and ordered Jack's epiPaleo book it's on it's way. Bought some oysters today and typically eat lots of seafood / shrimp and wild BC and Alaskan salmon. I have Mercury and Lead toxicity as well and just started chelation therapy.. the magnetico matress pad seems like it might really help me .. I also think that I'm very Leptin resistant.. eating disorders all my life.. still struggle with binge eating.. .. Frankly I feel like a mess.. NO energy and through these past 6-7 yrs my health has been one struggle after another .. My redox sucks - I'm sure of it based on what Jack talked about in all of the webinars so far..

I just started the Leptin RX on Monday Feb 17th to help me kick this binge eating that's started up this week again.. Starting my day with lots of protein has helped with the cravings already.
I would love any suggestions and support Dr Kruse or others could provide.. I know I've found the right place to start to really heal.. my journey has lead me here and I know this is the place that's going to really make the difference - I love all this incredible information that Dr Kruse is sharing and looking forward to my healing journey this year..

I have to started reading the blogs now.. Just don't know where to start first!!!
Leptin Reset and CT I think

Looking forward to the Hormones webinar this Thursday as well
thank you !
Renée

### nonchalant — 2014-02-19T17:43:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/renees-labs-what-does-it-all-mean.9828/post-111190>

Hi Renee, welcome! Glad to have you here.
Yes, there is a LOT to read here. I would start with the two latest blogs, E&E12 and the Redox Rx. Your redox is low, as you suspect.

> I have chronic fatigue and no energy for life at all - I have a hard time getting through my day and often find myself by 10am wanting to go home and to bed.. I am often in bed by 7:30-8pm and wake up dragged out and fatigued. I sleep in a pitch black room with NO electronics etc.. and limit my blue light exposure before bed etc. Been doing that for over a year/ half.. I've done IF lots ( 16/8) as well as 2x 24hrs fasts /week to see if that helps..
> I have found that if I eat fat I gain weight immediately! I keep my carbs very low.. No fruit or stachy carbs of any kind.. and seem not be to able to handle fat at all.. Maybe because of my redox potential?? Not sure why but seems like I have to keep my carbs extremely low and low fat to avoid not balloooning up in weight..

The wanting to go back to bed at 10am sounds just like I was, years ago. It is frustrating and confusing and all-consuming.
Sounds like you are doing things right, however, with the reduced artificial EMF and blue light. You should probably not IF, because of fatigue.
If you eat fat and gain weight immediately, I would think that your body is not able to pull all the energy that your mitochondria are making away to other parts of the cell. The energy stays around the mitochondria, creating oxidation and havoc in general. So you may be making plenty of energy, you just can't use it. Water is needed to transfer energy to places that need it. And to get water inside the cell it needs to be good water, and it needs to attach to cysteine. Once you have good hydration, you should be able to start using energy more efficiently. You may find that your ability to tolerate temperature variations improves as you get better hydrated. I remember I used to be comfortable in a narrower and narrower range of temps, as my health declined.

Your chelation protocol might need to wait until your body has the energy to eliminate the metals/toxins. Dr. Kruse also mentions that in the Redox blog. Renee, please focus on your health first, instead of your fat. Your body is fat because it is trying to survive a low energy crisis while remaining in the best possible health.

Do you get much sunlight? I suppose this isn't a good time of the year for that, but it is free energy in its purest form.

# Thread: Buttermints
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buttermints.9832/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2014-02-19T16:17:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buttermints.9832/post-111174>

Came across this recipe that I'd like to try. I like little fat bombs.

[http://empoweredsustenance.com/stop-sugar-cravings/](http://empoweredsustenance.com/stop-sugar-cravings/)

**Buttermints
Ingredients**

-   1 cup butter, organic pasture butter is the healthiest (If using ghee, which is an oil, it will not emulsify with the honey. See the note on the recipe below)
-   10-12 drops therapeutic grade peppermint essential oil OR 1/8 - ¼ tsp. peppermint extract(I use the oil) Taste and adjust amount as necessary.
-   3 Tbs. raw honey(You can increase to 4 Tbs. if desired)
-   Small pinch of unrefined salt
-   Optional: 2 Tbs. cocoa powder for a chocolate version

**Instructions**
1\. Have the butter at room temperature. Stir together all ingredients then scoop it into a
disposable pastry bag or zip-top bag. You can use a metal pastry tiplike I did, I just stuck the
metal tip into the end of a zip-top bag and then cut off a corner of the bag . If you don't want to use a tip, just snip of the corner of the bag.
2\. Squeeze bite-sized buttons onto a baking sheet lined with unbleached parchment paper. Place in the fridge until firm, about 2 hours, then transfer to a storage container and store in
the fridge.

**Option #2 (with coconut oil and butter)
Ingredients**

-   1/2 cup butter (pasture butter is best!)
-   1/2 cup organic coconut oil
-   3 Tbs. raw honey (You can increase to 4 Tbs. if desired)
-   10-12 drops peppermint essential oil OR 1/8 - 1/4 tsp. peppermint extract. Taste and adjust amount as necessary.

**Instructions**
1\. This variation includes both butter and coconut oil. Because the coconut oil is softer than
butter, it will be too soft to pipe through a pastry bag. Instead, mix together all ingredients
and then spread in an even layer onto a baking sheet lined with unbleached parchment
paper. The mixture will be thick enough to spread evenly and stay put.

2\. Place in the fridge until firm, about 2 hours, then cut into little squares and transfer to a
storage container. Store in the fridge.

**Notes
Important note regarding making the recipe with straight coconut oil or ghee:** The butter is needed to emulsify the oil and the honey. If you use straight coconut oil (or straight ghee, which is an oil), the oil and honey will not emulsify. You can use an immersion blender and blend the mixture for a minute, and this will help emulsify it.

# Thread: The Sixth Extinction
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-sixth-extinction.9834/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-02-19T23:48:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-sixth-extinction.9834/post-111256>

Elizabeth Kolbert's new book:
[http://www.amazon.com/The-Sixth-Extinction-Unnatural-History/dp/0805092994](http://www.amazon.com/The-Sixth-Extinction-Unnatural-History/dp/0805092994)

from National Geographic Interview:

> **Aren't we part of the natural world? Won't evolution allow organisms to adapt to us and our impact on the world?**
>
> Well, that is the $64 trillion question. If we were doing just one of these things, we could precipitate a mass extinction. It turns out we're doing several at the same time. We're not just warming the world, we're cutting down the rain forest. We're not just cutting down the rain forest, we're moving invasive species into the rain forest. So you just add these all up, and you say, that's a lot, and that's how you get to saying: We are the asteroid now. The asteroid also had a lot of different effects, and it didn't end too well.
>
> **Our ancestors did okay, though.**
>
> It ended okay for our relative, whatever that was—some little shrewlike creature who crawled through the end of the Cretaceous. So then the question is, What is going to crawl through this moment? That's the big question.
>
> And to say that we are part of the natural world, or not—I think that is absolutely the subject of the book. What are we? To the extent that the asteroid is a part of the natural world, we are. And to the extent that the asteroid is not part of the natural world, then I guess you could say we're not. We occupy a very, very unusual position in the whole history of the planet. That is really the point of the book.
>
> **You don't write much here about the effort to conserve habitat—but your next assignment for _National Geographic_ magazine is to write about the 50th anniversary of the [Wilderness Act](https://www.fws.gov/laws/lawsdigest/wildrns.html). Is there any chance that wilderness will be the preservation of the world?**
>
> In a period of rapid change, one of the few things we know how to do is to try to leave as many places alone as possible. Big places, so that if things need to move they can, so that evolution can take its course. If these things can adapt, they will—but the point would be to give as many organisms as possible a chance to make it through this moment, by leaving food webs as intact as they still are. Many people said the same thing to me: That's our best shot.

BOOM..."we are the asteroid now...."

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-20T00:23:00+0200

# Thread: 31 yr old Male
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/31-yr-old-male.9840/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Acorn — 2014-02-20T05:10:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/31-yr-old-male.9840/post-111317>

Hi Everyone, I’m new to this forum.

I had my blood work done recently and would love to get some thoughts on it. Test was done in the morning, after 12 hr fast.

I am a 31 yr old Male, steady weight of 132 lbs with slim build (I guess a bit underweight for my height ~6ft). I eat a fairly clean diet, not strict paleo or low carb but mainly meat and veg, dairy, little bit of wheat but very little, if any processed foods. My sleep is ok, but could be better. I get 7-8hrs most nights but tend to wake up once around 4-5am.

Overall I would consider myself reasonably healthy - but I do feel a bit low on energy at times and have periods of fatigue and low motivation. I have also developed (mild) seasonal allergies the last few years with irritating eyes and sneezing etc.

Appreciate any insights on the numbers and happy to answer any additional questions - Thanks in advance!

——

**Plasma Zinc Chl
PLASMA ZINC:
**
25 Hydroxy Vitamin D: 57 nmol/L ( 50 - 150 )

Reference range:
<25 Moderate to severe vitamin D deficiency
25 - 50 Mild deficiency
50 - 150 Optimal target range for bone health
\>250 Vitamin D intoxication has been reported at this level,
but is rare at levels <500nmol/L

**Dhea Sulphate**
DHEAS: 5.9 umol/L ( 3.3 - 17.4 )

**
Cortisol**

Serum Cortisol: 898 nmol/L HH

Comment: High cortisol can be due to stress, illness, on hydrocortisone or Cushing's syndrome. If Cushing's syndrome is suspected, suggest an overnight 1mg dexamethasone suppression test.

Cortisol (Morning Peak) 200 - 700 nmol/L

Serum B12 And Folate
B12: 256 pmol/L ( 170 - 600 )
Folate: 22.0 nmol/L ( > 7.0 )
Comment: Borderline range B12 110 - 170
Borderline range Folate 7.0 - 10.0

**Thyroid Antibodies**
Thyroperoxidase Ab: 30 U/mL ( < 60 )

**Liver Function Tests**
Total Bilirubin: 11 umol/L ( < 25 )
Alk. Phosphatase: 90 U/L ( 40 - 110 )
GGT: 7 U/L ( < 60 )
ALT: 13 U/L ( < 45 )
Total Protein: 74 g/L ( 66 - 84 )
Albumin: 49 g/L ( 38 - 52 )
Globulin: 25 g/L ( 18 - 38 )

**Renal Function Tests**
Creatinine: 85 umol/L ( 60 - 105 )
Urate: 0.31 mmol/L ( 0.20 - 0.42 )
eGFR: > 90 mL/min/1.73m2 ( > 90 )

**Iron Studies**
Ferritin: 120 ug/L ( 20 - 400 )

**Thyroid Function Tests**
Free T4: 18.0 pmol/L ( 10.0 - 20.0 )
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L ( 0.30 - 4.00 )
Free T3: 5.3 pmol/L ( 3.0 - 6.5 )

Comment: Consistent with euthyroidism. FT3 is not indicated when screening for thyroid disease or monitoring thyroxine replacement.

**Calcium / Phosphate**
Calcium: 2.52 mmol/L ( 2.10 - 2.55 )
Phosphate: 1.22 mmol/L ( 0.70 - 1.50 )
Adjusted Calcium: 2.32 mmol/L ( 2.10 - 2.55 )
Albumin: 49 g/L ( 38 - 52 )

Comment: When the serum albumin is outside 20-45 g/L, the calculation of adjusted calcium may not be reliable. Please interpret with caution.

**Lipid Tests**
Fasting status: Fasting
Cholesterol: 7.4 mmol/L ( < 5.0 ) H
Triglyceride: 0.7 mmol/L ( < 2.0 )
HDL Cholesterol: 2.25 mmol/L ( > 1.00 )
LDL cholesterol: 4.8 mmol/L ( < 3.4 ) H
Chol/HDL Ratio: 3.3 ( < 4.5 )

Comment: For most patients non-fasting lipids are acceptable for CVD risk assessment, as Chol/HDL ratio is not affected.
For established CVD risk (including diabetes) NZGG optimal levels are Cholesterol < 4.0, LDL < 2.0 and Chol/HDL ratio < 4.0.
Raised cholesterol, consider acquired causes: hypothyroidism, diabetes, nephrotic syndrome, liver disease.

**Magnesium**
Magnesium: 0.81 mmol/L ( 0.70 - 1.00 )

**Ige**
IgE: 28 kIU/L ( < 100 )

**C-Reactive Protein**
CRP: < 1 mg/L ( < 5 )

**Diabetic Profile**
HbA1c: 35 mmol/mol ( < 41 )

Comment: If used as a screening test, HbA1c result likely excludes diabetes. No need to repeat until next scheduled CVD risk assessment.

If diabetic and treated with insulin/sulphonylureas, the risk for hypoglycaemia is increased. Suggest repeat HbA1c in 6 - 12 months.

**BLOOD GROUP**
Group: O Rh(D) Positive

**Complete Blood Count**
Haemoglobin: 161 g/L ( 130 - 175 )
RBC: 5.63 x10e12/L ( 4.30 - 6.00 )
HCT: 0.47 L/L ( 0.40 - 0.52 )
MCV: 83 fL ( 80 - 99 )
MCH: 28.6 pg ( 27.0 - 33.0 )
Platelets: 234 x10e9/L ( 150 - 400 )
WBC: 6.5 x10e9/L ( 4.0 - 11.0 )
Neutrophils: 2.47 x10e9/L ( 1.90 - 7.50 )
Lymphocytes: 3.00 x10e9/L ( 1.00 - 4.00 )
Monocytes: 0.79 x10e9/L ( 0.20 - 1.00 )
Eosinophils: 0.17 x10e9/L ( < 0.51 )
Basophils: 0.04 x10e9/L ( 0.00 - 0.20 )

**Testosterone**
Testosterone: 35.2 nmol/L ( 8.0 - 29.0 ) HH

# Thread: OK Beverages
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### madamechickendance — 2014-02-20T05:42:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111323>

For the leptin reset, I know that "drink pure spring water" is a foundational principle, but please clarify... does this mean, "Drink ONLY pure spring water to the exclusion of all else for the rest of your life"?

I gave up soda long ago, but I am fond of seltzer water with flavor added (no sweetener added). Also, are herbal teas ok? I read the ingredient list on my herbal tea (Celestial Seasons), and it lists natural flavor as containing soy! Is a tea bag going to ruin everything?! What about lemon juice in water, or apple cider vinegar? Do these "count" as in-between-meal snacks?

I drink coffee in the mornings, but I am trying to get away from drinking it in the afternoons. I feel emotionally fragile in the afternoons, and a hot drink really hits the spot.

Also, a friend of mine makes kombucha. She swears that the scoby "eats" all the sugar, and that it's suitable for my low carb routine (and now epi-paleo). Since it's homemade there's no way I can test that claim. Is kombucha ok if you are leptin sensitive? I'd love to hear from someone who makes it now or has in the past.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-20T09:38:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111333>

> [madamechickendance said:](/goto/post?id=111323)
>
> For the leptin reset, I know that "drink pure spring water" is a foundational principle, but please clarify... does this mean, "Drink ONLY pure spring water to the exclusion of all else for the rest of your life"?
>
> I gave up soda long ago, but I am fond of seltzer water with flavor added (no sweetener added). Also, are herbal teas ok? I read the ingredient list on my herbal tea (Celestial Seasons), and it lists natural flavor as containing soy! Is a tea bag going to ruin everything?! What about lemon juice in water, or apple cider vinegar? Do these "count" as in-between-meal snacks?
>
> I drink coffee in the mornings, but I am trying to get away from drinking it in the afternoons. I feel emotionally fragile in the afternoons, and a hot drink really hits the spot.
>
> Also, a friend of mine makes kombucha. She swears that the scoby "eats" all the sugar, and that it's suitable for my low carb routine (and now epi-paleo). Since it's homemade there's no way I can test that claim. Is kombucha ok if you are leptin sensitive? I'd love to hear from someone who makes it now or has in the past.

No, you don't have to stick to water. In fact, Jack gives a recipe for Optimal Coffee in the eBook -- and can be seen drinking a glass of red wine on one of the videos.

As for "spring water" -- what this means depends on your jurisdiction. In the UK, for example, the term, unlike "mineral water" is not controlled at law, so can mean anything the seller wants. One university specialist in the chemistry of water, whose site Jack linked in a blog post, has the story of "Dasani" spring water marketed by the multinational company Danone. Now it turned out that "Dasani" was actually London tap water ... Except that it was contaminated with bromates, which London tap water is not.

Best is reverse osmosis water. Water that's been through a really good filter system might be as good or nearly as good. Bought mineral water of good quality that's bottled in glass (the BPA from plastic bottles leaches into the water) is also good. Choose a reliable source, like Perrier. I guess "spring water" is OK if that has a guranteed minimum level of purity where you're living -- depends on your local law.

In general water is prteferred to other drinks and to hot drinks. Dr. Kruse also says to drink it very cold if you can. Other drinks aren't cut out, though. Coffee is OK. I think green tea is preferred to black, because IIRC the latter tends to be a little higher in fluoride. But I'm not sure on that one -- essentially the difference between the two is that the latter is fermented. But I guess just a cup or two a day is neither here nor there.

Herbal teas? Yes, "natural flavouring" is another of these terms that isn't controlled at law in many jurisdictions, so that effectively it has _no_ meaning. However, it makes the public feel comfortable, because of the associations of the word "natural". It can often mean monosodium glutamate. It could also mean, as one Paleo wag pointed out, "a pungent flavoring synthesized from what's extracted from a gland up a civet cat's ass". IOW, it means what the seller wants.

I don't know what Jack's opinion on Kombucha is. I think you're right in saying there's little or no sugar left. I think the mushroom culture _might_ be an issue. Apparently, they can pick up spores of other fungi, not all of which may be benificent. It's certainly been suggested that there could be issues with mycotoxins. But, as I say, I don't know if Dr. Kruse has ever even mentioned kombucha.

### caroline — 2014-02-20T11:41:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111339>

Yep - he has mentioned Kombucha ......and he used to drink it. I don't know if that is still the case tho. I do remember that of the flavors you could buy some had more sugar than others. I think it kinda falls in the category of fermented stuff and is really good for us. Maybe don't go overboard?

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-20T12:11:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111342>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=111339)
>
> Yep - he has mentioned Kombucha ......and he used to drink it. I don't know if that is still the case tho. I do remember that of the flavors you could buy some had more sugar than others. I think it kinda falls in the category of fermented stuff and is really good for us. Maybe don't go overboard?

Ah, that is interesting. I used to make it and had a book on it. The Russians rate it very highly. I think the medical profession over there can be a bit more open-minded than in the West.

Speaking of which, I just got Robert O. Becker's book this morning, and isn't this dead right?

> The philosophical result of chemical medicine's success \[he's referring to the advent of penicillin and the sulpha-drugs\] has been belief in the Technological Fix. Drugs became the best or only valid treatment for all ailments.

There's a historical reason why doctors are so drugs-oriented (besides advertising and suchlike). Not a good thing, though.

Anyway, the Russians claim improvements in many conditions and even that it's something of a protection against radioactive exposure.

The question of what might happen to the culture over time, depending on what fungi are around is an interesting one, though. I have seen this topic raised in Paleo circles. And I don't think Jack is dismissive of worries about mycotoxins: he does use Upgraded Coffee, and he has mentioned mycotoxins in respect of both coconut oil and chocolate.

I'd gladly see kombucha given a clean bill of health, but I don't think I'll ever brew it again -- too much fuss.

### Lahelada — 2014-02-20T13:24:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111347>

Jack´s opinions on Kombucha

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/jack-kombucha.8692/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/jack-kombucha.8692/)

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/kombucha-risky.3436/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/kombucha-risky.3436/)

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...e-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/so-you-want-to-now-why-progesterone-and-cortisol-are-huge-for-a-leaky-gut.2864/)

and the last one is a whopping 6 pages

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/kombucha-tea.1224/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/kombucha-tea.1224/)

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-20T14:43:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111354>

Thanks. I couldn't actually get through the 6 pages to anything Jack himself had said. Too much for the handheld I'm using to cope with.

Can anyone actually quote his opinion?

It does seem to be agreed in the kombucha community that mycotoxins can be a problem. See, for example:

[http://users.bestweb.net/~om/~kombu/konnection/mold.html](http://users.bestweb.net/~om/~kombu/konnection/mold.html)

It was strongly recommended by Sally Fallon and the WAPF at one time, and their influence on the movement has been very great. On the whole, I think that's a good thing. Still, she, and they, have been wrong before ...

### Lahelada — 2014-02-20T16:49:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111372>

Paraphrasing: He drinks it in spring / summer ,sometimes in autumn/fall but never in winter
Beneficial or risky ? Depends on your context .
That is the summary of the first two links I posted.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-21T12:37:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111501>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=111372)
>
> Paraphrasing: He drinks it in spring / summer ,sometimes in autumn/fall but never in winter
> Beneficial or risky ? Depends on your context .
> That is the summary of the first two links I posted.

Thanks. I'm on a desktop and can look at these threads now. The last is rather long and rambling; the first two, short and to the point. His answer to the first thread is:

> I only use this in spring or summer.......Rarely in autumn and never in winter.

I take it that's another way of saying that he avoids anything that may have excess carbohydrate in it except in long light-cycles. Unsurprising: he's always said that.

The answer to the second question is more ambiguous. He says, in answer to the question, "Should we be at all concerned?" posed by someone who does, indeed, raise the mycotoxin issue:

> depends upon personal context.

But I'm not sure whether this is a general comment about consuming foodstuffs that may be a little high in carbohydrate or a direct comment specifically on the hazards of mycotoxins. He doesn't use the term mycotoxin, and the article the poster linked is a long one that only mentions this issue briefly at the end of some very general chit-chat. Jack **didn't** actually say: "Even if mycotoxins are present, they're not a problem for everyone". That might be so, but one could only get that out of the comment by rather free interpretation.

Kombucha seems to me to be potentially useful, but infection of the culture does appear to be an issue. Anyway, as I say, it is fuss making it, so I shan't do it again. Interesting, though.

### caroline — 2014-02-21T13:20:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111510>

Don't we get back to our redox? If our redox potential is good we can handle stuff.............

### madamechickendance — 2014-02-21T16:05:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-beverages.9841/post-111530>

I am going to stay away from the kombucha for now. Maybe in the summer once I become leptin sensitive. All I know is that when I tasted my friend's kombucha for the first time, I got an immediate adrenaline surge--it was really sweet. Even doling it out a few ounces at a time, I was immediately obsessed with it and finished almost half a gallon in 2 days. Whether that was psychology or biology, I can't say. All I know is that I can't control myself around it yet. If it has that much power over me, well, I best avoid it.

Any thoughts on non-sweet acids like lemon juice and acv?

# Thread: Fluor in water.. what is revivable and how
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluor-in-water-what-is-revivable-and-how.9843/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2014-02-20T16:03:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluor-in-water-what-is-revivable-and-how.9843/post-111361>

Our water is Rio de la Plata water , fed by the Parana river . There might be some tidal influx from the Atlantic but rarely , only under severe storm conditions .We do have pronounced tide and ebb periods. The very muddy water gets pumped to the water works from a river bed intake , purified , chlorinated , fluoridated and then sent on its merry way to our tap. It is soft water to start with. The fluoridation is 1.5 mg per l I believe. The RO water I buy is municipal water filtered in a 5 filter system, ozonised and one of the filters being RO.
Revivable or should I go with "spring water" which all have a slightly higher fluor content of 0.7 mg per litre .

This brings up the question that was touched upon in the Himalaya salt thread. Calcium fluoride is present in almost all waters and by default therefore in salt. Until the big business of water purification systems it was generally accepted that calcium fluoride was not absorbed or only to a small extent. However, as there is a feeding hysteria with many people that have an axe to grind in the process, all over sudden fluor in any form is damaging, absorbable while previously this only happened with iodine and magnesium deficient waters. Some inland waters here are high fluor , mineral poor and have historically produced goitre clusters. The iodine supplementation of salt and mineralisation of the water reduced this to a minimum.
So my questions from this are .
1\. Is the " calcium fluoride is not absorbable" pure convenient and misleading dogma or are we being misled by the new water purification craze to believe that we need to do more than we need to ? Note I do not doubt the need for purification, filtration but sense a very strong element of panic making. Not being a chemist or so minded I do not know which side to come down on.

2\. Does adding iodine (lugol) to your water do anything in terms of dispersing /reducing the absorption of calcium fluoride? I would think sodium fluoride is another kettle of fish and would remain unaffected but that is a gut feeling.

Any chemists,biochemists ,would be aforementioned or simply tarot readers who would like to wade in on the subject ?

Edit : I am asking about my water but others should should be able to extrapolate what to do with their waters from the answers received.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-21T09:35:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluor-in-water-what-is-revivable-and-how.9843/post-111492>

I thought Jack had said he thought reverse osmosis water the best bet. In fact, I'm sure he did.

Surely, if you're worried about any particular mineral that might have got in the water, the question would be: "Is it water soluble?" If it'll dissolve in water, then it's present as ions (charged particles) not as a compound. This is why a saline solution will conduct electricity: because you have Na+ and Cl- ions. Of course, beyond the saturation limit, they'll crystallise out as sodium chloride crystals.

### Jude — 2014-02-21T11:51:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluor-in-water-what-is-revivable-and-how.9843/post-111499>

Lah, think the problem with fluoride stems from the field effect...... emf allows the F to cross the Blood BB and as a dialectic inhibits hydration. Pre emf times it wasn't really a prob I believe, but with emf's and manmade inclusions of F in food, agriculture and who knows what else, it has multiplied our exposure.
So if your emf exposure is high, I reckon you stick with RO

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-21T12:19:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluor-in-water-what-is-revivable-and-how.9843/post-111500>

As far as I can tell, she's worried that fluorspar might be getting into her drinking water. It isn't highly soluble:

1.6 mg/L at 18 C, apparently. Here's a factsheet on the mineral:

[http://www.chemicalbook.com/ProductMSDSDetailCB5225741\_EN.htm](http://www.chemicalbook.com/ProductMSDSDetailCB5225741_EN.htm)

Here's U.S. government information on the toxicology of fluorides:

[http://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/toxprofiles/tp.asp?id=212&tid=38](http://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/toxprofiles/tp.asp?id=212&tid=38)

### Lahelada — 2014-02-21T13:17:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluor-in-water-what-is-revivable-and-how.9843/post-111509>

I think I have given too much info which muddies the waters.
I have the choice between RO water with 0.4 mg /residual fluor of some manmade added variety of fluor and spring water that is unfluoridated but has 0.4mg /l of naturally occurring fluor.
If calcium fluoride is less absorbable by the body why would I eschew spring water for RO water that has a "manmade" fluor compound that is highly bioavailable?

What Jude said about EMF making any fluor more bioavailable , enabling it to cross the blood barrier makes most sense to me but leaves me to think that it is six of one versus half a dozen of the other,in other words no difference.

I went into deeper detail so as tthat other readers may relate in their circumstnces but apparently I have made it as clear as mud.

### Jude — 2014-02-22T02:03:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fluor-in-water-what-is-revivable-and-how.9843/post-111630>

LOL...probably me off on a tangent...but amounts F being equal...emf conditions the same.....spring water, why not!

# Thread: Tying it all together

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tying-it-all-together.9847/post-111402>

In a nutshell I feel as though I've made huge progress in working through general health issues with over the past year or so. Gut issues have substantially decreased as I feel my diet has cleared up the majority of those issues with the exception of a lesser degree of bloat, fullness, bad breath. I'm feeling way better than I ever have with a few exceptions. However, it seems as though the last 20% of the equation is the most tricky. I need help in deciphering my labs to see if there is a common thread in relation to my brain fog, low libido and mood swings. So without further adieu:

### kirk- — 2014-02-21T02:04:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-optimizing-labs.9849/post-111439>

I'm a 32 year old male, 6' 202 lbs I have been eating paleo type diet since jan 1, 2014 lost about 13 pounds to get down to 202. I also have hypertension about 135-150/80-90. From the optimal lab worksheet a few of my labs are quite a bit below optimal. Namely my BUN/Creat ratio, Vitamin D, Testosterone, SHBG, CO2.

Now where to go from here. Thanks for any and all help

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### JanSz — 2014-02-22T04:25:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-optimizing-labs.9849/post-111651>

Get scale that checks body fat. You want 15% or less.
Stop eating high GI carbohydrate (grains, potatoes, sugar)
eat all other but only in season.

Eat vit D untill you have 100
try at first 6000iu/day

check your LH & FSH to see if pituitary is asking your testiscles for more testosterone.

SHBG little high.
Work on your cortisol first then on thyroid hormones, then you have a chance that SHBG may get lower.

Drink lots of good water.

.

### kirk- — 2014-02-25T22:31:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-optimizing-labs.9849/post-112158>

Thanks for your help,

I'm at about 14% body fat right now. I just got back my Adrenal stress index.

Any thoughts about that.

I drink a ton of water (4L/day) but I never feel hydrated. I feel like I just pee more. I have been drinking Costco bottled water. I'm guessing that's not good. I have started with vitamin d. I haven't done any weight lifting do you think that could be affecting my testosterone?

Where do you get water?

>
> Where do you get water?

14% body fat right now ---- I would kill for that. Look at my picture.

I get water at Costco.
For drinking Pellegrino
For cooking Poland Spring

Your overall cortisol is low.
Your night cortisol is high. ----->Circadian Rhythm alert, do all that dr Kruse recomends
additionally:
I would do following labs at LabCorp:
[http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508)

I would do following labsat LabCorp:
1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry--Test Number: 140707 CPT Code: 84140
4 ------- Progesterone--Test Number: 004317 CPT Code: 84144
5 ------- Cortisol, Free, Serum, With CBG (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500440 CPT Code: 82533; 84449
6 ------- Cortisol, AM & PM Test Number: 104000 CPT Code: 82533(x2) (7:30AM, 12PM)
7 ------- Corticotropin Releasing Hormone (CRH)
8 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate--Test Number: 004020 CPT Code: 82627
9 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA)--Test Number: 004100 CPT Code: 82626

\========================================
Then I would use two type of tablets from (Allergy Research/Nutricology)
pregnenolone Micronized Lipid matrix
DHES Micronized Lipid Matrix

\-----
Take in the morning as many pregnenolone pills as it takes to:
get Cortisol(7:30AM)~(20-22)ug/dL
but not to exceed pregnenolone~<151)ng/dL
preferably get progesterone~1.4 ng/mL or little higher, but I found that up to 5ng/mL may be ok
(note, you want high prog because you want to have high prog/E2 ratio and good hydration
but you are afraid that progesterone's 5aR activity may decrease enough of your DHT and kill sex)
When sex is lagging, add DHT, E1 & E2 to your labs, per my list of tests within link above.

I have been thru this and process my self, the only way to get control over it is to do relatively freequent blood tests.
Pregnenolone= neurosteroid. Some people easily go over their limit when supplementing, you will know if you are there, if you are there, stop supplementing then resume at lower dose.
\------

Pregnenolone supplementation have better chance at being successful if you adjust your DHEAs first.
My self, I can do that at will, but I am still low with DHEA. Looking for suggestions how to raise.
Many guys may overload DHEA,serum while DHEAs is till low.
Watch both.
Aim at
DHEAs(350-490) μg/dL
DHEA(31-701)ng/dL (get closer to high on range)
You do not want to exceed either one.
I have used max of 400mg/day DHEA from LEF.org
dr Kruse reported using 500mg/day during his exhausting hacks (so, rather short term).

\---------

\========================================

To raise low night cortisol I use
Doctor's Best Natural Brain Enhancers Featuring GPC & PS

\========================================

I drink a ton of water (4L/day) but I never feel hydrated.
BUN=20
BUN/creatinine=22

DHEAs=406-----not bad
progesterone=1.1 ----->lowish
ferritin=325--------high
Hgb=14 ------------(low) check testosterone
E2= 9.8 Low
Testosterone=421 low better 1200 (but holding it for long time should lower SHBG and up E2
ideal SHBG~20
SHBG= High, testosterone at better levels would lower SHBG and raise Hgb & E2
Vit D=33 low get it up to 100, Start with 12000iu/day
sodium=137 ---- low
HDL=40.2 could be better, eat more seafood

If you are high into sports (and hopefully recuperate quickly)(your likely redox potential is still high).
I am not clear about what course of action dr Kruse or dr Tim Jackson would recommend.
1\. easing up on exercises
2\. supplementing with testosterone

Just in case, get your
LH
FSH
PSA

When in doubt, think about it while fully submerged in icy water or hot/cold combination.

\----------------
Your Cortisol is low, your TSH is high
You are missing thyroid panel.
You will need thyroid supplementation (after you raise your cortisol)
do this panel (per my list under link above):
27 ------- Thyroid-stimulating Hormone (TSH)- Test Number: 004259 CPT Code: 84443
28 ------- Thyroxine (T4)- Test Number: 001149 CPT Code: 84436
29 ------- Thyroxine (T4), Free, Direct, Serum-Test Number: 001974 CPT Code: 84439
30 ------- Triiodothyronine (T3)- Test Number: 002188 CPT Code: 84480
31 ------- Triiodothyronine (T3), Free, Serum- Test Number: 010389 CPT Code: 84481
32 ------- Reverse T3-Test Number: 070104 CPT Code: 84482
33 ------- Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) Antibodies
34 ------- Thyroid Antithyroglobulin Antibody
35 ------- Prolactin Test Number: 004465 CPT Cod

Check your
(BUN)BloodUreaNitrogen----->very high
Creatinine------->unknown
BUN/Creatinine Ratio--------->very high

You want
BUN/Creatinine Ratio<10

if BUN is high it may help to use:
carnitine
zinc
Phosphatidylcholine (in water and alcohol)(from BodyBio.com)

last time I posted that dr Kruse said that better carnitine form is ALCAR
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...-labs-pam-m-53-yr-old-woman.9869/#post-111796](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/please-biohack-labs-pam-m-53-yr-old-woman.9869/#post-111796)"\]Please biohack labs -- Pam M. 53 yr old woman

L-carnitine
Acetyl-L-Carnitine
ALCAR is superior to L-carnitine in terms of bioavailability
He (dr Kruse) 1-2 grams/day Acetyl-L-Carnitine ALCAR (600mg 4x/day) (he takes when stressed, spring, summer)

**I am not a doctor, just doodling here.**

..

# Thread: Intermittent fasting questions.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intermittent-fasting-questions.9850/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Emily Sullivan — 2014-02-21T02:48:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intermittent-fasting-questions.9850/post-111459>

I'm planning on doing intermittent fasting to help with my weight loss after I'm LS. I've been trying to read up on the best way to do this. Some places on this forum I see people saying to NEVER skip breakfast if you're IF'ing, other places don't state as much. Could anyone clear this up for me? If I fast should I ALWAYS eat breakfast no matter what? Or should wait till dinner if I'm not hungry?

### caroline — 2014-02-21T02:54:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intermittent-fasting-questions.9850/post-111461>

In my opinion - you should get well first ...... IF is not good if someone has eating disorders etc.

The most important thing is to fix your redox potential ...and then other things will fall into place.

I do it because I feel fantastic and it feels right for me - and I have been doing this almost three years.

This is my n=1

fixing your redox should be your priority .... in my opinion of course!

### Lahelada — 2014-02-21T02:59:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intermittent-fasting-questions.9850/post-111464>

I agree with caroline.If is great but mu gut tells me that you need to be sure that you are leptin resistant.One of the tells that you are is just that you eat breakfast and can go until dinner without being hungry that is without forcing the issue. Once you are you cqn experiment whether you do better with a higher fat lower protein breakfast or whether you get along better with the BAB.

### Emily Sullivan — 2014-02-21T05:22:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intermittent-fasting-questions.9850/post-111479>

I was going to wait until I knew that I was leptin sensitive. I guess I just don't really believe that I'll eventually lose weight while eating as much as I do now, so I'm anticipating the need to put in some extra measures.

### AnnieW — 2014-02-21T18:52:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intermittent-fasting-questions.9850/post-111566>

Give it a chance first. I think I mentioned on your other thread that when I started my reset in November I lost almost 20 lbs in two weeks. That was eating huge amounts of coconut oil, three eggs for breakfast with two hamburgers and bacon. You will lose weight, but it might be slower for you than for someone else. Each person will have a different experience.

# Thread: Labs just came in and I need some advice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-just-came-in-and-i-need-some-advice.9852/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Vinje — 2014-02-21T06:55:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-just-came-in-and-i-need-some-advice.9852/post-111482>

What's up everybody?! I just got my blood panel back from Wellness FX and they're "ok" at best. A little background about me. I'm 21 years old,5' 11", 180lbs, about 10% body fat, and I grew up eating the SAD: cereals, poptarts, candy, grains, margarine, every type of damaging grain possible, and anything else that's not for human consumption, I stuffed down my mouth. I've always been involved in sports and I'm decently fit, but I've always been lathargic and always wanting to nap. I just assumed that's what every teenager goes through. About a year and a half ago, I started taking over my health, due to a very low sex drive and having very little energy. I started eating a diet mostly consisting of things that grow from the earth, but it still included many inflammatory foods. About 6 month ago I started eating a primal type diet, high in fats, quality proteins, and carbs mainly from veggies and a little fruit(mostly berries) and have gone about 2 months without any "cheat days". I get depressed almost for no reason sometimes, I occasionally get anxiety, although its been getting better, and I haven't woken up with a hard on in almost two years.

Now to my labs, there are some things that are very alarming. My hormones are all out of wack, my thyroid is trashed, my inflammation is a little high(I did have a jiu jitsu practice the night before the labs which could have an effect on this), and my liver has a few things that need work.

I train brazilian jiu jitsu and I try to go 3 times a week but I find it hard sometimes since I'm always tired after 5. I also try to get one full body interval training lift in every week. If this amount of exercise has anything to do with these levels I'm glad to accept any recommendations.

Any advice is appreciated! Thanks in advance for every things you guys do, especially Dr. Kruse.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-just-came-in-and-i-need-some-advice.9852/post-111655>

Eat Epi-Paleo-Rx
You cant eat to many oysters.
