# Thread: Sleeping orientation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-21T10:59:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111496>

I remember reading something about this years ago. I can't recall exactly what -- maybe that it was best to sleep with your head pointing north or something. I think the theory was that you'd then be in alignment with the Earth's magnetic field.

I'm not sure people could always do anything much about this, anyway. Many modern buildings tend to have small and odd-shaped bedrooms, so that furniture will only go in one way. And perhaps it's a bogus notion anyway. Still, maybe it's worth airing.

Does anyone find they sleep any better when oriented in any particular direction?

Does anyone know of any lore among hunter-gatherers suggesting it's important to sleep in any particular direction?

I've heard of Australian Aborigines sleeping around a fire -- presumably, here the point is to be oriented with respect to a heat source on a cold night. Apparently, many tribal Aborigines had scars on their shins from getting too close to fires.

Again I've heard of huts in Africa being built to catch the dawn-light. Therefore, they'd have east-facing entrances.

Nothing in either of those examples. But perhaps someone else knows of other customs that could fit ...

### caroline — 2014-02-21T13:23:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111511>

Mike - there was a little bit of chatter on the forum .......but I don't really remember the outcome - must not have been memorable.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-21T14:38:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111517>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=111511)
>
> Mike - there was a little bit of chatter on the forum .......but I don't really remember the outcome - must not have been memorable.

Now I think about this, I can remember being shown something in a health and beauty book about 20 years ago by, if I get the name right, Susanna Kenton. Turned out she was the daughter of Stan Kenton, who was a big band leader back in the 40s.

I don't automatically discount stuff like that, even if it's not mainstream and has celebrity/Hollywood associations (sometimes a bad sign I know!). IIRC Miss Kenton was coming from an earlier West-Coast outlook -- people like the guy with the delightful name Gaylord Hauser and Renee Something-or-other who was greatly impressed with the Hunzakut.

Hovering in the background is quite serious stuff like McCarrison's work in India. IIRC, McCarrison found that people from Northern India (including Hunza) who had abundant dairy produce and vegetables and some meat were very much larger and healthier than people from southern India, many of whom ate little more than polished rice and pickles.

But the Hunza diet was _still_ basically a grain-based diet -- lots of chapattis (unleavened wholewheat cakes). Sometimes I think McCarrison and Weston Price did us all an unintentional bad turn by talking up whole foods, including wholegrains, as if wholeness was all.

Anyway, it seems Hunza may not have been quite the Shangri-la that Hollywood believed. There's also suggestions that Renee Whatshername, and other "health tourists" may have been exploited and misled by the Mir (ruler) of Hunza ... and that the stated ages of robustly healthy peasants they were shown were ... err ... stretched. As the population increased, the foods _other than_ wholewheat, which were perhaps where what health there was had come from, were, it's been said, getting in short supply. In conclusion, maybe Renee was sold a pup.

Stan Kenton's daughter, IIRC, had clocked this and started shying away from grain-based diets, recommending salads and fried liver. She was also talking about the need for moderate sunbathing (at the time seen as dangerous) and sleeping "aligned" and on magnets. Well, I guess we now have the Magnetico pads, so maybe she wasn't that crazy after all.

Dunno whether notions of sleeping "aligned" are valid or not, though.

### Tanya — 2014-02-21T17:01:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111550>

Mike, I found this in my notes: a simple way to realign your own energetic field each day is to sleep with your head toward magnetic north and your feet facing south.

### nicld — 2014-02-21T18:07:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111560>

Well, when I buy/build our next house I think I want the main living space to be facing west to have the evening sun to allow the most natural light in and the bedroom on the south side. I also want a sun porch that gets the most light all day as possible.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-21T19:02:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111569>

Thanks, Ladies. Googling obvious things like "sleep orientation" only brings up questionable unreferenced comments like "in Ayurveda ... " or "in Feng Shui ..." with just about any point of the compass that takes the poster's fancy.

I'm not convinced there's any effect here. But I suppose there might be. One doesn't want to be too sceptical -- obviously, there are practices like dowsing that work whether one would like them to or not. (Same for acupuncture, which even, apparently, works on zoo animals, who can't be accused of responding to the placebo effect!) But at the same time rampant irrationalists who'll claim just about anything without exercising judgment and with no checks are the bane of the internet.

I would be interested to know whether people in hunter-gatherer societies tend to do one thing or another. Australian Aborigines, by the looks, prefer to have the head pointing to the East. This is probably in order to get the first light of dawn coming up. One source indicates that they believed this "facilitate\[d\] good dreams and prevent\[ed\] illness":

[http://books.google.co.uk/books?id=...ge&q=aboriginal sleep "head pointing"&f=false](http://books.google.co.uk/books?id=xNhsTkT6MZ8C&pg=PA292&lpg=PA292&dq=aboriginal+sleep+%22head+pointing%22&source=bl&ots=yJmtLzdsl8&sig=2L9xDfcE-Jt923qFiCY1E45TXx4&hl=en&sa=X&ei=NIIHU5TfMPHn7Aa3wIDQDw&ved=0CDMQ6AEwAQ#v=onepage&q=aboriginal%20sleep%20%22head%20pointing%22&f=false)

Interestingly, one aboriginal people in India buries its dead with their head facing south:

[http://www.bradshawfoundation.com/india/pachmarhi/aboriginals\_tribal\_art.php](http://www.bradshawfoundation.com/india/pachmarhi/aboriginals_tribal_art.php)

One wonders what's behind that choice.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-21T19:52:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111574>

I should leave this one alone ...

Here's another thought, though: what do animals do?

How about cats and dogs, for example. My guess would be that they orientate themselves with respect to the environment -- so that they can see who or what is approaching, for example. So landscape features (or walls, furniture, heat sources, supplied basketsn etc at home) would be the cues. Maybe not, though.

### Josh — 2014-02-21T20:21:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111578>

As I understand it, there is a coherence created in the electrons coming from the Earth by the photons from the sun as they move in a wave over the Earth. The effect is similar to the EZ zone for water. There is also a polarity shift in the dielectrics at dawn and dusk. I have heard both North and East as preferred directions. I sleep with my head in the East. I am not sure if it switches in the Southern Hemisphere?

Consider Earth's Electrical Activity:

[http://74.63.154.231/here/wp-content/uploads/2013/06/Chevalier\_electrical\_surface\_potential-2007.pdf](http://74.63.154.231/here/wp-content/uploads/2013/06/Chevalier_electrical_surface_potential-2007.pdf)

This site is a mix of serious science and some "magical" extrapolation. My take away is that there is a lot going on with the magnetic field and on average in a given location, being oriented properly to maximize things such as the spintronics of the electrons that is dependent on field orientation is a good thing. How to figure that out where you are is another question. Sooner or later someone here will have to dive into it or maybe Dr. Kruse will be kind enough to give us a hint.....

[http://sedonanomalies.weebly.com/geomagnetics.html](http://sedonanomalies.weebly.com/geomagnetics.html)

### caroline — 2014-02-22T00:51:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111604>

Mike - I like how your mind works! this is all so interesting ..... how could we possibly discount anything after a couple of years with JK.
Last nite I went to a thing with an empath/medium ........I still can't even begin to process it all.....

talk about vibrational energy .......

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-22T10:21:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111671>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=111604)
>
> Mike - I like how your mind works! this is all so interesting ..... how could we possibly discount anything after a couple of years with JK.

Sure. I don't off the bat. Becker seems to think that "the body electrical" could explain most esp phenomena. But I'd keep an open mind on that sort of thing, anyway, on the basis that there's a lot of convincing personal testimony regardless of how you explain them.

Becker suggests all thoughts and feeling probably involve a very weak EMF signal, and it's all floating around out there like a soup. This, he says, would be "the collective unconscious". I can see that someone like Edgar Cayce, The Sleeping Prophet, might be drawing on that in trance without knowing how he did it.

[http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edgar\_Cayce](http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edgar_Cayce)

I can see one could say that "the cures" were drawn out of "the collective unconscious" -- i.e. from the minds of doctors and pharmacists without Cayce consciously knowing what it was all about. It still remains mysterious, though, doesn't it? How would he be unconsciously judging between what's in the minds of different doctors with no medical knowledge himself?

> Last nite I went to a thing with an empath/medium ........I still can't even begin to process it all......

You're teasing us all now with such a small sample. Are you going to post an account later? I hope so.

I think I said to you I used to work with a girl who did that. I think she does it full time now. She believed she was being spoken to and guided by the dead. Whether she was or whether she was just reading her sitter's mind. ...

Of course, there's also the more worrying possibility that there may be entities out there we know little about that might choose to masquerade as people's dead loved ones. I read an interesting tale of a choirboy who told his priest his dead grandma had spoken to him at a seance. Interestingly, he didn't tell the boy not to dabble but simply gave him a question to ask "her". When he did "she" responded with a torrent of foul language.

But then again maybe it's all coming out of our own unconscious.

### Lahelada — 2014-02-22T10:46:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111672>

Czechoslovakian scientists have found that deer sleep North to South. There is also an analysis of cows from Google photos around the world that show the same thing,additionally they eat like that too. We sleep towards the East .
Interesting correlation though that jet lag is more pronounced when flying East. More interesting still that our spare bed is orientated north which is where I retire to when I can´t sleep and there I am more likely to fall asleep again . I need to find a picture of cows sleeping from the the Northern hemisphere.
Apparently humans are more difficult to assess but one of the scientists was considering evaluating fotos from music festivals around the world.

### Lahelada — 2014-02-22T11:42:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111676>

Found the original study and the answer is in it:
"Taken the pastures separately, cattle show significant axial body orientation with a mean vector of 1.2°/181.2° in Europe, 3.7°/183.7° in Asia, 12.1°/192.1° in Australia, 30.9°/210.9° in Africa, 32.0°/212.0° in South America, and 347.5°/167.5° in North America"
From : [http://www.pnas.org/content/105/36/13451.long](http://www.pnas.org/content/105/36/13451.long)

### sooperb — 2014-02-22T14:07:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111679>

I remember that advice too Mike though I have no idea whether it was any more than folk lore. Often folk lore seems to have a basis in a little scientific fact.

I was a bit of a fan of Lesley Kenton and her daughter Susannah although I never knew she was the daughter of Stan. I still have her first book, "Raw Beauty" in which she advocated a raw diet. I was light years away from regarding her ideas as something I should try but I have since bought another book of hers. I well remember my husband's face when he picked it up, eyes raised, shake head, sigh and pronounce it claptrap without even opening the cover. Here's a link to an article from about 4 years ago : [http://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/2010/feb/20/leslie-kenton-child-sexual-abuse](http://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/2010/feb/20/leslie-kenton-child-sexual-abuse).

Look at this extract though, how spot on was she:
What should do so, she says, is the gut, and it is upon that instinct that she herself operates. "The mind serves the very deepest levels of our being, yes, but it should not necessarily rule our lives. When you are able to truly connect with the gut, then – wow, basically. I love watching people find that out for themselves. It's like walking into a garden and seeing flowers you have never seen before. It's breathtaking. It turns me on."

Regarding the way dogs sleep, I'm sure there was something about this recently that suggested one of the reasons a dog turns around and around before settling down is because he is orientations himself, I just can't remember what with, might have been magnetic North.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-22T14:50:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-orientation.9853/post-111681>

Ah, yes, Lesley. Susannah was her daughter. Sure, she probably wasn't so far out on some things, and this was years ago. I think she mentions Weston Price. He seems to have had a continuing influence in the U.S. in alternative circles after Academia forgot him. I do like people who are prepared to think for themselves and look into all these things and take even some more unlikely stuff seriously, even if some of it turns out to be a bit wacky in the end. I don't really buy Lesley Kenton's notion of salads and vegetable and fruit juices as a panacea. You get this in the naturopath tradition, too. I quite like salads from the taste point of view, but I'll pass on the juices, and I bet my blood-sugar's glad of that. But I bet those books have got some interesting stuff in them, and it's got to be better than the mainstream approach of chow down on 7 to 11 servings of wholegrains and run till you damage your knees. IIRC, she also recommended various forms of bodywork, which again was ahead of the curve 20 years ago -- still is a little now.

On sleeping dogs and cats. I'd like to check their orientations (head and body) with a compass. Cats would be good, as they kip just about anywhere, beds, chairs, rugs, dropped clothing, airing cupboards. But always the same orientation ... or not?

I wonder about the sleeping chambers in badgers' dens and so on -- all manner of wild animals. I guess zoologists might know.

I'm not sure it's an important issue. If Australian Aborigines like their heads pointing east, then I guess north probably isn't a "must" even if deer do it. Interesting, though.

# Thread: Doc's protocols vs. chelation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/docs-protocols-vs-chelation.9863/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 6

### Julie5 — 2014-02-21T20:08:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/docs-protocols-vs-chelation.9863/post-111576>

After listening to Doc Jackson last night, I looked up chelation therapy. It seems there may some significant improvement of heart conditions, but studies don't definitively prove it. I had 7 cardiac stents put in over the course of 2 hospital visits and 9 months. I don't want to repeat the experience, so I am motivated to take action. I realize that labs tell a big piece of the story, so here are my cardiac numbers:

homocysteine 14.5 um01/L ( normal 5.0 -12.0)
crp, highly sens. 2.44 mg/L (normal 0 -3.0)
fibrinogen 488 mg/dl (normal 190-420)
lipoprotein (a) 136.0 mg/dL (normal 0)

I can't find the results of my heavy metals hair test, but I was high in several metals.

I also have the C677 and A1298 gene mutations. My doc and further research show that these predispose me to heart disease, addictions, cancer, depression, difficulty clearing heavy metals, high homocysteine levels. As I look at my family (I have outlived dad, mom and both siblings), they died from cancer, heart disease and drug overdose, and suffered from depression/anxiety.

So, to my question, will the protocols be sufficient for my heart disease, or should I do the protocols AND the chelation therapy?

By the way, you all are AWESOME!

### tellmisty — 2014-02-21T22:21:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/docs-protocols-vs-chelation.9863/post-111587>

I've been researching chelation therapy and ... I'm a bit nervous about it, to be honest. I don't think it's smart to just dive into it because stirring up metals can be dangerous, especially if you're not detoxing well. It seems you'd benefit by doing some gut healing first, make sure you've got good bowel movements, your liver phase 2 detox is working well (I, for example, barely methylate ... been working on that) ... I'm asking Dr. Kruse about this when we chat today and I'm going to see if I can get him to do a blog posts on it.

### Hope — 2014-02-21T22:54:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/docs-protocols-vs-chelation.9863/post-111590>

I had looked into it too...this is just my N-1 but I did use DMSA for a bit, a protocol for mercury...very different than what Tim mentioned. It's the Andy Cutler protocol based on hair analysis, which of course Tim didn't agree with. I couldn't handle DMSA, too powerful for me. Instead I did a special supplement program which very slowly pulls things out, which has been good. It's taken a long, long time (been at it since 2009) but I can see the progress in the hair analysis over time. The best help has been the magnetico and 50mg iodine for a long time....over several months, my Hg went really high and now it's on the way way down, along with other toxins like Al, Ni, lead too. It's really cool to see....even if Tim, or Dr Bonlie, don't like it. I still believe in it.

The other drawback to chelation is...it pulls good stuff (minerals you need) along with the bad. But I know Sterling has said she has heard of good success with it with EDTA....also an MD showed me some amazing pics of a heart before and after....with his own patient! So it can work well for some people.

Good luck with the decision...

### Julie5 — 2014-02-22T01:50:15+0200

Hope, my doc has me on a lot of supplements too. After doing some more reading,, i think some of them are for the purpose of chelating, albeit slowly. What things did you take for it?

### functionalmed28 — 2014-03-02T03:36:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/docs-protocols-vs-chelation.9863/post-112988>

IV chelation can be very helpful especially in those with cardiac issues, narrowing of the arteries, etc. I recommend the book Bypassing Bypass Surgery. If you're just attempting to get metals out for other reasons, optimizing the efficiency of the excretory organs should be first. Glutathione and methylation also help with metal elimination. Sometimes further chelation is needed. In my opinion, it should be based on the half life of the chelator.

# Thread: Missed my mealtime...hungry
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/missed-my-mealtime-hungry.9864/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### madamechickendance — 2014-02-21T22:16:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/missed-my-mealtime-hungry.9864/post-111586>

It's been 7 hours since my BAB. It's just after 3pm, and I am hungry. Not crazy hungry, but my stomach is definitely growling. If I eat now and follow the five hour rule, I would be eating dinner after sunset, which is a no-no. Sunset (all twilight gone?) is at 6:45pm now.

BTW, is there an epi-paleo definition of sunset? When sun is below horizon? What about twilight?

Which is more optimal? "Fasting" between BAB and dinner (11 hours w/o eating) OR eat now, skip dinner, and get to bed early (14 hours-including 8 asleep-before next BAB)?

I am going to drink a lot of water and wait for dinner, but what do y'all do when you've missed a meal?

### nicld — 2014-02-21T22:33:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/missed-my-mealtime-hungry.9864/post-111589>

This happens to me alot. Sometimes I eat a little something to just make the hungry subside a while until supper if I know I need to make supper. Other times, I eat my "dinner" @ 3 then that's it, but I make sure to eat enough to hopefully not be hungry after 7 (which is my cut off).

I have been working on eating with hunger and this seems to work for me. For the longest time I ate when i was "suppose" to and up to 6X a day since that is what one is "suppose" to do. Now I follow what my body want and eat when it is hungry but staying within the light cycles.

### madamechickendance — 2014-02-22T00:27:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/missed-my-mealtime-hungry.9864/post-111599>

I guess the heart of my question, with regard to developing leptin sensitivity, is the relative importance of digestive emptying (5-hour rule) to the importance of the circadian factor.

The longer I go between meals during the day, as I have today, the more massive my dinner is. The more massive my dinner (total calories), the greater the impact on insulin (I assume), even with minimal non-starchy carbs. The "stuffing" factor is a psychological trigger for me. When I'm "stuffed," I'm mentally primed to keep eating.

Anyone else?

### nicld — 2014-02-22T01:13:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/missed-my-mealtime-hungry.9864/post-111609>

I see what you mean.

I try to lean for the most food in the morning, hence the BAB, a medium second meal and then if I need supper something small. Sometimes when I get home after eating lunch and I am just not hungry enough for a meal I might just have some coconut oil with coconut and cocoa.

# Thread: Body vibrating (invisible tremor?) after sundown?

### Marie — 2014-02-22T03:48:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-vibrating-invisible-tremor-after-sundown.9868/post-111646>

I've noticed a distinct difference in my circadian clock after purchasing a Magnetico pad. I am sleepy about an hour after sundown, regardless of light influences, which has been very interesting. (I'm also dreaming everynight and sooo rested, thank you very much!)

However, I've also noticed something a bit troubling... I seem to have a small tremor that starts at about sundown. (It is so small, I notice it off and on and doubt anyone else notices.) It is a bit like vibrating from head to toe. And when I wake in the night, I feel my whole body vibrating and I'm only noticing it at night.

Since I'm in the EMF capital of the world, although my place seems to have escaped a lot of it, I'm wondering if I'm feeling degradation, due to EMF and being at a computer all day, and then repair at night.

Just wondering if anyone else has experienced this?

Marie

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-22T04:32:59+0200

I also went through a start up phase with my magnetico which was a kind of over stimulation of the nervous system feeling....buzzy throughout the body. I don't have that anymore and the only time I get it is when I'm exposed to too much emf, stimulents or high stress.

I think it is part of your magnetico response...just keep track of it. I sleep really well now, but it did take about a month to adjust to each layer of the sleep system.

# Thread: Supermarkets bowdlerizing meat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supermarkets-bowdlerizing-meat.9881/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-23T13:15:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supermarkets-bowdlerizing-meat.9881/post-111786>

I'm getting sick of this ...

I've noticed recently that the supermarkets are interfering with the meat they sell more and more. It's noticeable with bacon: first, it lost its rind -- that happened years ago. Now they've started trimming almost all the fat off. The same is happening with cold beef and ham: they're trimming it close around the muscle, so that it doesn't have any fat on it.

Sainsburys, much the worst supermarket over here in many different ways, is the worst. Even better ones like Waitrose have started doing it, though.

The rind is also missing from joints like belly pork. What I suspect is that they're fliching it off the tradtional cut and selling it off to people who're making "pork scratchings" with it. That's likely more profitable, and the customer won't notice ...

I don't know what's going on with the fat. It's particularly obvious with the cold meat. There's almost no fat left on it.

Did they read Rob Wolf's book -- which bores on about "lean meat" or something?

That's a in-joke, of course. But seriously, what is going on? Are they selling the fat off at a better price for some other use? Have they perhaps decided that people have been so panicked by the "health" lobby about fat _even as it becomes clearer with every passing month and each new piece of data that such worries are bogus and always were groundless._

Now, while the intellectual position is crashing around them, they decide to flich the fat. What gives?

Today I noticed that the fat on the Italian air-dried ham in Sainsburys "antipasta" packets was trimmed right off. That's new. I suppose having taken these packets from an Italian supplier for long enough, they now have the commercial position to dictate to the Italian supplier and tell them to mutilate their traditional product.

Now livestock in England (and probably Italy) is considerably less intensively farmed than in the U.S. Nevertheless, the omega-3/ -6 balance is probably off. But it's better than nothing, and my seafood consumption probably straightens things out. As for saturated fat: no health issue there at all. I don't want to manufacture my own by stuffing bread. I want that fat.

I think this is only one of a (probably uncountable) number of issues that happen when people abandon their butchers, fishmongers, egg suppliers, greengrocers, etc. Pretty soon you find the quality of a foodstuff has been compromised bit by bit, a little every year, until it's hardly worth eating. You then look round for the butcher, or whoever, and find he's been driven out of business. Now you have no choice.

The only _really_ good bacon I've seen in the past couple of decades has been either bought at a butcher's or from a farm-shop or directly off a farmer. Most of the stuff in the supermarkets is horrible: fairly small rashers, anyway, but thin as a piece of paper. No rind, almost no fat. And even its pathetic weight is in large part water, held in with polyphosphates. It's _token_ bacon. The eating of it alnost ritualistic: you eat it, perhaps at the weekend having been scared off it entirely in the week, and think you've had bacon but actually fill up on toast.

Supermarkets ... I've no language bad enough.

### sooperb — 2014-02-24T17:41:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supermarkets-bowdlerizing-meat.9881/post-111941>

I can only agree Mike, the supermarkets have long dictated how we eat. They would say it's in response to the consumer and what they ask for, I believe it's profit driven. I've hated big supermarkets for years and have tried to avoid shopping in them unless I have no choice, which isn't that often thank goodness. I found an organic farm the other day that is experimenting with white bacon, cured only with salt and molasses. Hmm, molasses. We buy a lot of Gloucester Old spot bacon from the butcher in Ulverston, it's more fat than anything else and has the rind. You know what? I don't like the taste! I feel bad about it but there's no joy in eating something that you don't enjoy. He makes sausages and has various GO joints. We have a slow roast belly pork and it's always oozing with fat and totally wonderful.

Waitrose has been my choice since it opened a couple of years ago but I find myself questioning things like "Outdoor bred pork" when I'm sure that all that took place outdoors was the copulation. On the other hand "Outdoor reared pork" is I'm sure just that, I take issue with the first statement as being very misleading. They sell Duchy bacon, I like to think that Charles wouldn't allow any mis-selling but maybe I'm being naive! I just know that I will be taking at look at your observations next time I'm there lol. I have to agree that food manufacturers are still misguidedly producing fat reduced food because they haven't caught up with the changes yet and also because neither have the majority of their buyers. Where profit's concerned they won't be slaying the golden goose anytime soon.

### Hope — 2014-02-24T22:22:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supermarkets-bowdlerizing-meat.9881/post-111974>

waitrose...isn't that where Kate M goes to shop? LOL

BTW Soo, your signature is a riot! You had me LOL literally! I still am. I adore your humor....

### sjoshua — 2014-02-25T18:59:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supermarkets-bowdlerizing-meat.9881/post-112131>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=111786)
>
> What I suspect is that they're fliching it off the tradtional cut and selling it off to people who're making "pork scratchings" with it. That's likely more profitable, and the customer won't notice ...

This seems counter-intuitive, unless it is what the customers are requesting/demanding.... I doubt the store would be able to wholesale fat trimmings for the same prices that they would get by just leaving them on the (now) lean cuts for customers and charging them the increased per pound rate of the fat portion.

The system supplies what the people demand...

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-25T20:51:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supermarkets-bowdlerizing-meat.9881/post-112144>

> [sjoshua said:](/goto/post?id=112131)
>
> > What I suspect is that they're fliching it off the tradtional cut and selling it off to people who're making "pork scratchings" with it. That's likely more profitable, and the customer won't notice …
>
>
> This seems counter-intuitive, unless it is what the customers are requesting/demanding.... I doubt the store would be able to wholesale fat trimmings for the same prices that they would get by just leaving them on the (now) lean cuts for customers and charging them the increased per pound rate of the fat portion.

This referred to the **rind**, not the fat -- though some fat would come with it. They're stripping the rind off cuts like pork belly. That's a fact. I can only suppose they have _some_ commercial use for it. I can't imagine they're using labour to remove it, if they're just throwing it away. My guess would be selling it to make "pork scratchings". Bags of these are standard sellers in pubs in the UK:

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/art...--time-food-police-laid-pork-scratchings.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-1302329/Theyre-heart-attack-bag--time-food-police-laid-pork-scratchings.html)

I don't know what's going on with the **fat** -- increasingly doing a disappearing act, particularly in the worst supermarket, Sainsburys. As I said:

> But seriously, what is going on? Are they selling the fat off at a better price for some other use? Have they perhaps decided that people have been so panicked by the "health" lobby … ?

Then you say:

> The system supplies what the people demand...

Not always, I think. That's a major theoretical problem in "the dismal science" (economics). The purpose of much law in this area is to try to bring about a situation where the balance of interests is served.

In fact, the original post does open out something of this problem. People begin to use supermarkets, and then use them for more and more, because of the convenience. Eventually, they find they've been suckered, because the near monopoly the supermarkets obtain then allows them to put undue pressure on suppliers and to offer customers what it suits them to offer. By then, what one might wish to "demand" may not then be easily available. As the theorists of the market pointed out long ago (back in the 18th century) markets "work" (which is another way of saying produce a just outcome) when there are _many_ actors.

Farmers Markets are perhaps one of the ways of trying to "route around" the supermarkets, the specialist outlets -- butchers, bakers, greengrocers, fishmongers, etc. -- having, in the meantime, being forced off the streets. It takes awhile for these things to get off the ground, though, and they're not always available to everyone everywhere.

### sjoshua — 2014-02-26T01:52:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supermarkets-bowdlerizing-meat.9881/post-112195>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=112144)
>
> This referred to the **rind**, not the fat -- though some fat would come with it. They're stripping the rind off cuts like pork belly. That's a fact. I can only suppose they have _some_ commercial use for it. I can't imagine they're using labour to remove it, if they're just throwing it away. My guess would be selling it to make "pork scratchings". Bags of these are standard sellers in pubs in the UK:
>
> [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/art...--time-food-police-laid-pork-scratchings.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/article-1302329/Theyre-heart-attack-bag--time-food-police-laid-pork-scratchings.html)
>
> I don't know what's going on with the **fat** -- increasingly doing a disappearing act, particularly in the worst supermarket, Sainsburys.

True, sorry for missing the nomeclature and thank you for clarifying.

I think my point would still stand though: the supermarket would make more money selling meat cuts **with** those items included to the consumer, since their per/lb cost to customers is going to be more than they'd be able to wholesale the individual parts. As far as how it works in the U.S. and where I have shopped meats are packaged, weighed, and sold by the lb/kg so keeping the rind and the fat is increasing product weight and therefore cost irregardless of whether or not the end user decides to utilize it.

If the reduction in rinds/fat has something to do with the supply, it must be higher up the chain than the supermarkets. Otherwise, I'd think it must be driven by what consumers purchase at the supermarket level.

My quote: The system supplies what the people demand...

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=112144)
>
> Not always, I think. That's a major theoretical problem in "the dismal science" (economics). The purpose of much law in this area is to try to bring about a situation where the balance of interests is served.
>
> In fact, the original post does open out something of this problem. People begin to use supermarkets, and then use them for more and more, because of the convenience. Eventually, they find they've been suckered, because the near monopoly the supermarkets obtain then allows them to put undue pressure on suppliers and to offer customers what it suits them to offer. By then, what one might wish to "demand" may not then be easily available. As the theorists of the market pointed out long ago (back in the 18th century) markets "work" (which is another way of saying produce a just outcome) when there are _many_ actors.
>
> Farmers Markets are perhaps one of the ways of trying to "route around" the supermarkets, the specialist outlets -- butchers, bakers, greengrocers, fishmongers, etc. -- having, in the meantime, being forced off the streets. It takes awhile for these things to get off the ground, though, and they're not always available to everyone everywhere.

Also, my statement still holds true here, and always will. That is the foundation of economics. In your example, the people are "demanding" a supermarket by shopping there, therefore it is able to and must exist. They have chosen the convenience and chosen to get "suckered". Let's say one day 50% of the population of the U.S. decided to no longer shop at supermarkets, what do you think would happen? Suddenly, farmers markets and the like would be necessary, and local butchers and bakers would be needed since producers still have product that they want to get to the end users. In this case, people would be "demanding" local farmers markets and small economies instead of supermarkets.

### sooperb — 2014-02-27T18:48:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supermarkets-bowdlerizing-meat.9881/post-112496>

"I think my point would still stand though: the supermarket would make more money selling meat cuts **with** those items included to the consumer, since their per/lb cost to customers is going to be more than they'd be able to wholesale the individual parts."

They wouldn't make extra money if they aren't selling the cuts with the rind and fat attached, which they likely aren't because governments have sold the idea to consumers that fat especially is bad and supermarkets have picked that mantra up and the products offered reflect this. Therefore they are stripping out the fat to make the meat more attractive. As supermarket CEOs, or whoever is responsible for putting goods on shelves, play both ends in order to ensure they don't miss sales, they are also targeting the changing market for fat as more people become aware of its benefits, evidenced by the pots of goose fat, duck fat and dripping, the first two products were not on the shelves 2 years ago but everyone is selling them now and the second had all but vanished but is now getting shelf space.

Where's the removed fat going? My guess is that it goes to bulk out or be used as filler in the cheaper products, the value ranges in the pies, mince, ready meals and possibly as Mike asserts, into pork scratchings and snack foods. In a strange sort of way, it may even be the case that value products are becoming nutritionally better because of having the extra fat although I suspect it goes through some sort of rendering process that does it no good at all.

Not all shoppers are fools either, I can see where two organic chicken breast fillets cost almost as much as buying the whole chicken and where trimmed meat is substantially more per lb than its fatty neighbour  I loathe supermarkets for their lack of morals when they deal with suppliers, they have ruined many businesses who can't fulfil the big 4s constant lust for profit by squeezing them for a price they can't deliver for. Suddenly the plug is pulled and they're left with a shedload of produce, that is time limited and no market for it. It's heinous.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-27T19:57:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supermarkets-bowdlerizing-meat.9881/post-112510>

> [sjoshua said:](/goto/post?id=112195)
>
> I think my point would still stand though: the supermarket would make more money selling meat cuts **with** those items included to the consumer, since ….

If they could, that's what they'd do. It's _not_ what they're doing.

But it's really not worth quibbling over what they're doing with what they're choosing to remove. The point is that they are. I wanted to make that point. These things can even go unnoticed.

> Also, my statement still holds true here, and always will.

And everything is always for the best in the best of all possible worlds, Dr. Pangloss. No! For the reason I already explained it doesn't. It's there in the economists if you go and read them. To repeat: the theorists of the market say that markets work when there are many actors -- many sellers and many buyers. They don't function properly when that condition doesn't obtain. And justice, a major concern of the market theorists, doesn't obtain when there's that condition. Here's one important example: many small suppliers get gradually squeezed more and more each year by supermarket chains, having blithely thought when they first got a contract that they're made owing to the size of the contract even though the conditions are poor. Never mind, it'll all work out, they think. But what they have to do and what they get out of deals gets gradually ratcheted up and they can't step off the treadmill, because they're find they're already in debt. Eventually it breaks them. That's a common pattern.

Markets do not work and do not result in just outcomes when the number of sellers -- or buyers -- is not large enough. That's _basic_ to the theory of the market. The public has got itself into a situation where it can't go to Mr. Jones the butcher instead of Mr. Smith the butcher because they're both gone and the supermarket is the only game in town -- for meat … and vegetables and bread and virtually everything else.

Too few sellers or combinations among sellers are a recognised problem in economics. This is why there are laws again cartels; this is why countries have trust-busting laws. But, again, there's no point in my keeping repeating myself.

I only hope that farmers markets help to break their stranglehold, as I said. But I'll stop repeating myself. Finis.

# Thread: Gaining weight on ketosis?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gaining-weight-on-ketosis.9891/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lazydog — 2014-02-24T23:31:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gaining-weight-on-ketosis.9891/post-111980>

I'm new here so please forgive me if I've asked questions that have already been addressed. I have searched but did not find anyone with a similar situation.

Is it possible for a female to GAIN weight while trying to adapt to ketosis? And if so, what do I do about that? I ask because I've been trying to adapt since end of October but I do not feel any of the after effects that everyone speaks of - able to go long periods without eating (my stomach still rumbles a few hours after bfast), increased energy and no afternoon fatigue. My diet is 80% fat, 15% protein and 5% carb. I workout intensely 5-6x/week for 1-1.5 hours and walk the dog but I still have intense afternoon fatigue along with not sleeping through the night. I'm not using the scale but use my clothes as a guideline and ALL my clothes that used to fit me before I started ketosis do not fit me any longer. I would guess I've gained about 5-8lbs. I started the ketogenic diet because I wanted to drop a few lbs! I will say that my blood markers are all in excellent shape so I know I'm on the right path. My monthly cycle is dead on the nose at 28 days and I'm 44 (when PMS used to be pretty bad).

If anyone has any insights or comments, I would LOVE to hear them. I found Jack through Mark's Daily Apple.

Thank you so much!!

### caroline — 2014-02-25T00:05:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gaining-weight-on-ketosis.9891/post-111983>

Hi and welcome! First of all, I am going to choose not to call you by your forum handle ......I wish you would pick something positive .... surely you don't want us calling you that? Please pick something positive and life affirming!

Please go to the home page and become very familiar with the easy start guides - they are excellent.

It would be great if you would start a journal in the journal section ...go to the top of this page and click on forum and then go down 1 category to journals and start a new thread. Please tell us about yourself ... some health history and where you live and what you do etc. .... Dr. kruse needs context if he were to offer you some suggestions.

You speak of Ketosis but I am not sure what your are doing....

Please read several times ... The Leptin reset and then fully commit - 100%

Do you have Dr. K's book? The Epi-Paleo RX - available at amazon in kindle or paper. This book is a great overview of all of this and about what Dr. kruse is trying to accomplish.

Please listen to JK's podcasts - you will find links on the home page. Once again they are a great way to get fast and easy info.

You need to start reading and learning and asking questions .......it isn't easy but it is doable! We are all living, breathing proof of that.....

Start a journal today and we will come back and help you .....

### caroline — 2014-02-25T00:08:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gaining-weight-on-ketosis.9891/post-111984>

I forgot to say .... You need to STOP working out intensely ASAP .....

you said you have intense fatigue and don't sleep .... but your blood markers are good. That doesn't make sense.

What you are doing is a recipe for disaster ....... you are putting yourself at risk.

In Jack's book there is a great chapter on labs -

Please stop and re focus .....and read the Epi-Paleo RX .... and then we will talk ....

I've been eating only 2 meals a day for a couple of years, have no cravings, am never hungry, have very few carbs and don't exercise excessively, dog walking is mostly it. I have thought often about whether the ideal calorie intake for men and women is little more than just a bit of a guess tweaked with a few statistics and some not ideal math. Do we eat way too much? I only seem to be able to lose weight if I cut back on quantity and when I do, I still don't experience hunger. It seems odd to me that if we are referencing back to Paleo and the hunter/gatherer model, there must have been days when they didn't catch much and made do with bones and any available greenery, nuts etc. Maybe when they had a decent kill they gorged but that may have not been often and in poor weather conditions they would surely have to ration in some way to ensure they didn't go more than a few days without some kind of meat?

# Thread: Scott of the Antarctic
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scott-of-the-antarctic.9896/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-25T09:20:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scott-of-the-antarctic.9896/post-112073>

There's been another analysis of their rations -- this time by the University of Roehampton.

Instead of concentrating on the issue of scurvy (long thought to have been important, and written about by Stefansson among others) this one talks about the sheer energy value.

> Each of the polar explorers were burning nearly 7,000 calories a day hauling their sledges, it is estimated. At the same time, they were consuming only around 4,400 calories.

… which does sound like a recipe for disaster …

As the short article points out they were unlucky in striking particularly bad conditions -- weather that was apparently unusually harsh even for that environment.

I think, what that article doesn't touch on, they were also unwise to try to man-haul their sledges. That's where a lot of that energy was being burnt up. Amundsen, learning from Nansen and from the Eskimo, used dogs. They gradually ate 'em on the way back to base, as the sledges got lighter.

Scott seems to have been unsure as regards motive power, taking a motorised vehicle -- which, IIRC, sank through the ice, ponies (which couldn't cope with the conditions), and a few dogs. Mostly they man-hauled, though.

The investigators also pointed out that:

> The balance was 24% fat and 29% protein.

(That leaves about 47% carbohydrate.)

Actually, that's around about what Kuipers et al. reckoned on for an East African Paleolithic diet:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20860883](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20860883)

But this was the Antarctic!

The investigators don't seem to have commented on the carbohydrate, but they did correctly point out that if Scott had taken more fat and less protein, he could have carried more total calories for the weight of food:

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/wor...killed-by-slimming-diet-scientists-claim.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/antarctica/robert-falcon-scott/9362396/Captain-Scotts-team-were-killed-by-slimming-diet-scientists-claim.html)

### caroline — 2014-02-25T09:50:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scott-of-the-antarctic.9896/post-112079>

Mike - you post the most interesting stuff .......

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-25T19:00:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scott-of-the-antarctic.9896/post-112132>

That's some really good info..... great find!!!!

### Tanya — 2014-02-25T19:15:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scott-of-the-antarctic.9896/post-112135>

No Jack here, but it gets a "BOOM" from me!

### Optimalbound — 2014-02-28T21:31:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scott-of-the-antarctic.9896/post-112705>

Aside from the caloric differences and potential vitamin-C issues, Scott seems to have made a couple of poor food choices, compared to Amundsen (a relative of mine, by the way), who had learned a lot from the Innuit in that regard.

> In his incredible raid on the South pole Amundsen had sleighing diets made up of only four ingredients, [pemmican](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pemmican), chocolate, biscuits and powdered milk (and dog, I guess).

> Biscuits are basically double-baked bread (Zweiback), and vary, depending on which explorer you read. Amundsen had a wholemeal, rolled oat, yeast-raised biscuit. Scott used white flour biscuits, raised with baking powder (and died of malnutrition).

> Amundsen and his men were eating fresh seal and penguin meat which, unknown to anyone at that time, contained enough vitamin C to prevent scurvy. The Norwegians, of course, had got this idea from indigenous peoples in the Arctic who ate an almost exclusively meat diet. The British palate preferred a less fishy taste and their penguin and seal meat was often overcooked, destroying the vitamin C.
>
> A biscuit found in Scott's tent ©
> Another difference between the two teams was the flour used for 'sledging' biscuits, a staple part of any explorer's diet. The British biscuits were made with white flour and sodium bicarbonate. The Norwegian biscuits were made with oatmeal and yeast, which provided the essential B vitamins needed to keep the nervous system healthy. Pemmican, a cake made by mixing pounded dried beef with beef fat, was also eaten every day. Although it was nutritious and compact, it was unappetising. On sledging journeys, pemmican was mixed with melted snow to make a hot stew or 'hoosh'. Scott's pemmican lacked the oatmeal and peas of Amundsen's recipe, depriving his men of essential roughage.

Sources:
[http://www.bbc.co.uk/history/british/britain\_wwone/south\_approaches\_01.shtml#four](http://www.bbc.co.uk/history/british/britain_wwone/south_approaches_01.shtml#four)
[http://www.scandinavian-hiking.com/2012/03/en-route-to-amundsen-diet-recipes-for.html](http://www.scandinavian-hiking.com/2012/03/en-route-to-amundsen-diet-recipes-for.html)

### sooperb — 2014-03-02T15:14:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scott-of-the-antarctic.9896/post-113056>

And yet, Scott and his men probably wouldn't have died of malnutrition had they not missed their path on the way back and stopped short of the camp where they left some rations.

### margor_j — 2014-03-02T15:40:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scott-of-the-antarctic.9896/post-113059>

When I did my summer with NZ in antarctica, the team who come and go through belongings left in the 4 surrounding huts, had a heap of food articles, amongst other things, that needed recording / tagging.. We got to go to the labs and see the stuff (but also went to the huts!) and il never forget the dried parsnip - it still smelt of parsnip and looked just fine, some 80 odd years later!

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-02T16:22:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scott-of-the-antarctic.9896/post-113062>

> [Optimalbound said:](/goto/post?id=112705)
>
> Aside from the caloric differences and potential vitamin-C issues, Scott seems to have made a couple of poor food choices, compared to Amundsen (a relative of mine, by the way), who had learned a lot from the Innuit in that regard.

Yes, Stefansson wrote extensively about this, didn't he? The fresh meat in particular. He pointed out that Shackleton, who was far less organised than Scott, took much the same kind of food as Scott but nowhere near enough. Consequently, his men were forced to shoot seal, which, unknown to them, is probably what saved them from scurvy.

I do think some of those ideas people have suggested that you quote, are hilarious though. The notion that Scott would have been in trouble, because he didn't have enough peas and oatmeal and therefore deprived his "men of essential roughage" is so delightfully silly I literally did laugh out loud.

If your rations are 2600 kcals per day short of what you need and there's probably little vitamin C in them, owing to the fact that they're no longer fresh, "roughage" is the least of your worries. The "conventional wisdom" on diet strikes again. Sometimes one wishes these people would do a little basic maths.

# Thread: Scientists "unlock a microbial Pompeii"
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientists-unlock-a-microbial-pompeii.9897/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-25T09:22:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientists-unlock-a-microbial-pompeii.9897/post-112075>

> An international team of researchers have discovered a 'microbial Pompeii' preserved on the teeth of skeletons around 1,000 years old. The key to the discovery is the dental calculus (plaque) which preserves bacteria and microscopic particles of food on the surfaces of teeth, effectively creating a mineral tomb for microbiomes.
>
> The research team discovered that the ancient human oral cavity carries numerous opportunistic pathogens and that periodontal disease is caused by the same bacteria today as in the past, despite major changes in human diet and hygiene. ...

[http://www.eurekalert.org/pub\_releases/2014-02/uoy-sua022114.php](http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2014-02/uoy-sua022114.php)

Interesting -- another doctor asks the Caveman Doctor:

> where do I find an easy read for Paleo lowcarb for cancer. ..

[https://twitter.com/IMWHorvitz/status/437434652413132800](https://twitter.com/IMWHorvitz/status/437434652413132800)

The Caveman Doctor responds that there isn't one.

In fact, I thought there was a Cancer ℞ in Jack's book, but can't remember for sure now. I suppose it comes to the same thing -- there certainly _hasn't been_ although Paleo has been around for quite some time now.

Few of the paleo books seem to have been written by doctors and with specific illnesses in mind. And the original books are mediumish on the carbs anyway, although VLC seems to be necessary for some cancers. The popular books are Rob Wolf's and Mark Sisson's, which are great as far as they go but basically written for the fitness market by fitness enthusiasts.

# Thread: Harming the gut microbiome by changing diets
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/harming-the-gut-microbiome-by-changing-diets.9903/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-25T15:49:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/harming-the-gut-microbiome-by-changing-diets.9903/post-112102>

There's a couple of interesting posts by Dr. Art Ayers on the gut microbiome again.

[http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com](http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/)

These two posts are addressing this from the point of the view of the resistant starch fad that's -- apparently -- going through the paleosphere. (I say "apparently": I hadn't known it was: I've only come across it in regards to Jack's biohack and these two articles of Dr. Ayers'.)

Anyway, Dr. Ayers reckons that reports that it can help people with some GI issues are accurate and that there's a simple enough explanation. He thinks that some people's gut flora have been flattened by antibiotics and their dietary habits. But he postulates that sometimes people have just enough bacteria of the sort that resistant starch can feed left for a dose of that to get them to multiply. (Past a certain point, when someone's actually lost the bacteria, he says, it's not going to help.)

Those it seems to be working for, Ayers says, are individuals who are on close to all-meat diets. He said that wasn't what he expected to find in the paleosphere, because that's not what he'd understood "Paleo" to mean. I agree: I always think of Cordain's books and ones that follow in those footsteps in this connection. That may not reflect what the actual Paleolithic diet was like, of course, but that's what I take the name Paleo (no lithic) Diet to mean. (Actually, there seems to be reasonable evidence that the actual Paleolithic Diet may often have been highly carnivorous: I think Dr. Eades for one would give anyone an argument on that; so I understand would some of the scientists doing static isotope anlysis on prehistoric bones.)

Here's the nub of the matter in Dr. Ayers' own words:

> It is very important to remember that feeding your flora means matching the soluble fiber with the existing flora. Most people make the mistake of assuming that if they change their diet, then their flora will also change. Their flora will adapt with each of their hundred different species of gut bacteria increasing or decreasing in numbers, but no new genes will be present to digest new soluble fiber. Eating a meat diet will eventually eliminate gut bacteria needed to digest some plant materials and produce intolerances. The missing bacteria will not be regained upon return to eating plants again. ...

This sounds highly plausible to me. We know that it's possible to maintain good health on a diet with low to no plants. It's been done -- by explorers such as Nansen, by French-Canadian _voyageurs_, as well as by peoples indigenous to the Far North.

However, I guess in those days someone like Nansen wasn't starting with a compromised gut microbiome -- antibiotics, antibacterial soaps and whatnot being in future. Secondly, the hypothesis seems to suggest that someone may need to _acquire_ new gut bacteria to support a changed diet. Thinking about that, probably meat hanging around longer that we'd perhapsleave it -- as sometimes happens to people living off the land -- would pick up bacteria from the environment. And an explorer or anthropologist mixing with indigenous hunters while working with them would probably pick up some of their gut bacteria off them directly.

Chopping and changing between diets, if Ayers is right, sounds like it could be a dangerous practice. And it seems that yoghurt and probiotic capsules don't help with re-seeding missing bacteria, as he comments. They can provide temporary relief, but if they actually did what's suggested, you wouldn't have to keep eating/taking them.

Home-made ferments that are able to incorporate varied bacteria from your own environment are Dr. Ayers' solution of choice. I guess this is what Inger has chosen to do?

### nonchalant — 2014-02-25T16:14:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/harming-the-gut-microbiome-by-changing-diets.9903/post-112108>

I would think eating raw foods (meat as well as other) would change the gut biome quite a bit. And what about inhaling pollen from blooms that later turn into berries? I would think that you might get the perfect microbes for digesting berries from their blooms several weeks before.

### 400lbbenchpress — 2014-02-27T15:51:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/harming-the-gut-microbiome-by-changing-diets.9903/post-112457>

There were some people talking about taking resistant starch on a nootropics forum. I asked them if it all made them fart constantly and they said yes. No thanks.

# Thread: Mr Pinkies (and family) journey with Magneticos!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mr-pinkies-and-family-journey-with-magneticos.9908/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MrPinkies — 2014-02-25T20:03:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mr-pinkies-and-family-journey-with-magneticos.9908/post-112141>

So.. Our magnetico mattress pads came yesterday!!! YAY!!!!!!

Last night.. Chris and I got those heavy bad-boys up the stairs and on the beds... Kids got in their beds and BOOM- lights out! I'll admit that we got ours on our bed and BOOM- we were OUT cold.. My little legs itched and itched and itched.. but.. I got over that after a while... Well, 12:35 am, Lizzie (our 4 year old) got out of bed to go potty and asked for some water.. Naturally, we had turned off the breakers, so, I had to find my way down the stairs and in the kitchen and back.. It was a fun time.. I was thinking how mad that my sleep was interrupted and was going to mess up my thoughts about how my first night went.. but... I got back into bed.. and the next thing I know.. my eyes opened and that was it.. I woke up.. I had no idea what time it was.. I feared that I had woken up at 3 am and.. well.. two seconds later my alarm went off.. Sense then, I have felt like I have had a whole pot of coffee myself- not jittery, but.. awake.. My daughter woke up happy and hungry (for the past few months, it has been like pulling teeth to get her to eat breakfast with a side of crabby whiny pants).. Ryan woke up calm and quiet.. He said that he liked his new bed.. I am in the routine of weighing myself every morning.. I almost can tell you with a 95% success rate if I will have lost or gained weight. Because my sleep was interrupted, I was sure that I was either going to stay the same weight or gain a pound back.. I was very wrong... I lost nearly two pounds from the day before...

For years.. I have been tired... very very tired... even two years ago.. tired.. well... yes.. I weighed around 320lbs two years ago.. but.. I had to have two mandatory naps.. even dropping the weight has lifted my energy level considerably.. but.. the way I feel awake today has been like no other.. Somehow... everything that I have ever read or learned from JK makes sense to me now... I woke up this morning with ideas connected to each other.. I feel that I am fully understanding the leptin reset and the three leg stool.. yes.. what a beautiful way to put it... I am awake...

I am very excited for the future!

# Thread: abbykadi's labs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/abbykadis-labs.9911/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1

### lazydog — 2014-02-25T21:38:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/abbykadis-labs.9911/post-112151>

This is a VAP cholesterol profile that Caroline asked me to post:

I used to be on Armour thyroid 90mg/day but worked with my physician to get off these meds and am now taking 12.5 mg of iodorall/daily.

LDL chol = 142
HDL chol = 104

# Thread: Bulletproof coffee instead of Big protein breakfast

### La_Femme_Russe — 2014-02-25T22:42:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112159>

I wonder - can bulletproof coffee (coffee with grass-fed butter and MCT oil) be substituted for a big protein breakfast in the leptin reset protocol without affecting the results? Or does it have to be protein?
I sort of grew to like bulletproof coffee, and it easily keeps me full until lunch. But if I am to start the leptin reset, should I forego it and eat protein high breakfast instead? or can I do both together? what are pros and cons?

### Lahelada — 2014-02-26T00:15:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112171>

Skipping the protein is not recommendable. You can however have a bulletproof coffee with it ,if indeed you tolerate coffee well.

### caroline — 2014-02-26T01:07:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112185>

yep .... what Lah said^^^^^^^^^

The BAB isn't about the food as much as resetting your internal clock .......hence the timing of meals - really, really important.
I always have a BPC. Have you tried more fat?
I make pate which is half fat and munch on other thinks too.

### La_Femme_Russe — 2014-02-27T16:18:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112468>

Oh, I use plenty of fat in my coffee (both butter and MCT, and sometimes use even a little cocoa butter, too), and it does keep me full well past lunchtime. So hunger is not a problem. I just know it'll be difficult for me to eat protein in addition to this coffee. But I guess, without protein (with BPC alone) - the reset will not happen...

### Lahelada — 2014-02-27T16:28:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112470>

Just a thought,if it is hard to give up the BPC, maybe you should give it up for a little while. You might be automedicating with it without realising.

### nicld — 2014-02-27T16:58:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112479>

My suggestion, if you are doing the leptin reset, eat BAB with protein and fat and then have BPC at the end.

### caroline — 2014-02-28T06:54:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112600>

### margor_j — 2014-02-28T07:29:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112606>

### Lahelada — 2014-02-28T11:29:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112619>

I listened to the podcast with Ewan Brand ( Why EMF is dehydrating) and your answer is in it,directly from the doc. Have a liste,it is good

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-28T12:02:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112623>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=112619)
>
> I listened to the podcast with Ewan Brand ( Why EMF is dehydrating) and your answer is in it,directly from the doc. Have a liste,it is good

As I recall, Lahelada, what he says there is that protein will "kill cravings". That's fairly standard Paleo advice. It's in Cordain; it's also one of the reasons why Robb Wolf used to, perhaps still does, recommend a large proportion of protein in meals. "It's very satiating," Rob used to say. (Rob's nutritional advice, and also his explanations of "why we get fat", to coin a phrase, seem to have fluctuated a fair bit, though.)

This _isn't_ why I'd understood in the past that Jack was recommending around 50g of protein at breakfast. But Jack's not always easy to understand, and maybe he has more than one reason. And, of course, he's said it depends on who you are ...

AFAIK, Dave Asprey was using the bulletproof coffee as a meal replacement for himself, and, I think, specifically for breakfast, then maybe for lunch, too. It's building on older ideas of the "fat fast" (which I think go back to experimental diets used at a hospital back in the 50s). Dave, like many people who do "intermittent fasting" seems to be relying on exploiting the fact that one should already, so long as one's metabolism isn't too disrupted, be in fat-burning mode overnight. Let's not eat anything that'll raise insulin and just keep going with that, seems to be the thinking. It's a different _rationale_.

If the point of the protein is that it "kills cravings" then I don't see that La femme rusee has anything to worry about. She doesn't report any cravings, at any rate.

Personally, I think I'd sooner eat a full breakfast and maybe exchange the bulletproof coffee for lunch, but I don't think I'd be able to offer any very secure grounds for recommending that to anyone else.

I think on the whole, if someone thinks they need to follow the Leptin Reset plan, they'd probably be best to give it a go just as it's laid out -- at least at first. Give it a try. But first check if they fall within the criteria Jack has given for needing it. I can recall another post where someone wondered about Tim Jackson's advice. I didn't know at the time, but it seems that Tim works with Jack, giving consultations here. Well, then, I'd say to anyone if you've taken a consultation with him, why not take his advice?

### Albert83BCN — 2014-02-28T17:14:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112659>

If I understood correctly, the high protein content is supposed to trigger special mechanisms on the brain-gut axis via the vagal nerve so it can re-train your brain that due to the epigenetic terror brought by our modern world and lifestyle has lost the newer-evolved mechamisms to account for food energy. With the reset, you switch to a more ancestral way to account for food income that relies on the gut, the light cycles and weather. So, intuitively, having a -liquid- breakfast with only some added fat like the bulletproof coffee should not achieve the same goal... that does not mean that it cannot make for a good plus when fasting but my bet is not when one is leptin resistant, has bad sensing of his/her circadian clock and has low redox potential?

### La_Femme_Russe — 2014-02-28T17:46:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112664>

However, having only BPC in the morning and not eating until lunch or later (it does keep me full, no cravings, and I love the taste - it's almost like super treat for me) would constitute IF. So it all defies the process of leptin reset.
I guess I'll try to do as several of you recommended - have a protein breakfast and finish it with BPC.
thank you all for your help!

### Albert83BCN — 2014-02-28T18:02:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112669>

Be curious like that and keep trying to understand things, not just default to some other's strategy because most people tell you. Yep, regarding the leptin reset that would be the typical advice. Depending on your situation you might go with your BPC like that, consider it fine, border line or be a no-no depending on a lot of things... You say hunger is not a problem. Isn't it also during the night? What's the ultimate goal for you to be on the leptin reset? Which issues do you carry? What is your typical day? That might give us some insight to give better advice. Well, maybe you stated these on another post, sorry I don't follow all the threads

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-28T18:46:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-112676>

> [La\_Femme\_Russe said:](/goto/post?id=112664)
>
> My main problem is this: I know Jack said that you should not start IF until you are leptin sensitive.

But do you know you're not? Or badly out perhaps? AFAIK, he says this you know either from labs or from the fact that you're 30 lbs or more overweight.

I'm not sure that "IF" is necessarily equivalent to this, or any other form, of a fat fast. I doubt there's any reliable data that could settle that either way.

From what I understand fasting (certainly meaning going without anything, but perhaps extensibly) seems to work for many men, but is counterproductive for many women. This is perhaps because women are hormonally more complex. But you could be an exception to the norm for whatever reason.

> having only BPC in the morning and not eating until lunch or later (it does keep me full, no cravings, and I love the taste - it's almost like super treat for me) would constitute IF. So it all defies the process of leptin reset.
> I guess I'll try to do as several of you recommended - have a protein breakfast and finish it with BPC.

I'd do what you feel comfortable with. As I said, I'd tend to give any particular plan a fair chance. If you don't try it for 30 days (or whatever) how can you judge it? You can't. But if you like the fat-loaded coffee and it seems to work for you, why not?

### Ed Warnicke — 2014-03-16T16:41:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee-instead-of-big-protein-breakfast.9913/post-115785>

Lahelada... wrt to it being hard to give up BPC... I've experimented a bit with it, and what I found was this:

Giving up the coffee part is not so hard.
Giving up the butter+MCT part is \*very\* hard.

I can titrate off the coffee (by continuously halving the amount of coffee) in a few days with \*zero\* adverse symptoms as long as I replace it with hot water and keep drinking the blended butter and MCT oil (and adding vanilla and cinnamon for flavor  ).

That said... I do \*like\* the coffee

### nonchalant — 2014-02-26T17:40:35+0200

Dr. K said "The K+ being low is a sure sign of low ATP and bad intracellular water dynamics."
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/blood-work-after-a-cramp-seizure.9799/#post-112092](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/blood-work-after-a-cramp-seizure.9799/#post-112092)

### NeilBB — 2014-02-26T19:58:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-seizure.9923/post-112316>

I was asked to comment. Can't practice medicine here, but can state a few basic principles about new-onset seizures that I would tell any medical student.

A single seizure does not constitute epilepsy (the condition in which one has recurrent seizures).
Possible provoking factors or underlying causes should be investigated in a patient who has a seizure.
A single seizure does not necessarily need to be treated with anti-epileptic medication depending on specifics.
Labs following a seizure can be altered to do the seizure itself, muscle breakdown, and dehydration, and should be repeated later for better baselines.
MRI of brain is far superior to CT in looking for possible underlying structural issues after a seizure.

### fitness@home — 2014-02-26T20:01:05+0200

### nicld — 2014-02-27T04:30:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-seizure.9923/post-112406>

Hey Dawn,

That just sucks, I know, been there. My hubby has had 2-3 of them, the tonic-clonic kind. It was kinda scary since I saw each one of them. Do they think that there might be something that could have triggered them?

For my hubby, each time it was lack of sleep, after 3am, probably overheated and a little booze. His last one was after our daugher had a major one and went to the hospital. He was up till almost 1, went to work the next morning by 5 am and had a glass of wine when he came home and guppied 3 am that next morning (it was a crappy weekend). He has been on meds for a while but his doc is probably going to start weening him off. He NEVER had one during the day so we were never concerned about driving. If he did have it during the day, then we would be concerned.

Now we know how crucial sleep is, especially to him and if he is lacking a bit of sleep, no wine.

Hope you find some answers soon.
