### JanSz — 2014-03-01T19:43:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-seizure.9923/post-112861>

Shortly after my Green Laser prostate surgery I had a bout with high Hemoglobin.

When I went to Red Cross to give blood they turned me down because of cancer I have.
I had to come back to them with script, so I did.
My blood is not given to anybody.

The process is that they first check my Hgb level then ask if I want to have a blood drawn.
This is via pin prick similar to checking glucose level. Next minute Hgb is known.
So, I know my Hgb level right before I make decision about blood draw.
That decision also depends on what is written on script.
There is another decision point, frequency, frequency of checking.
I got once a month on my script, but it may be weekly when starting that high as you have.
The script is valid for one year. When or how often you come should mostly be your decision.
After a while you will learn your body response and come as often as you want/need.

I think that ideal Hgb is Hgb=~16 (for men)
With all that in mind, I discussed all that with nurse at Red Cross (about 20 min) and came up with text for script that would have all acceptable information that she need. In retrospect I could have put "check weekly" rather than monthly.
My doctor had no problem giving me script with all those words.

My highest readings
7/21/2010 Blood Ctr NJ Hgb=17.5

Overall, to stabilize at better Hgb level, I have given 3 pints of blood and stopped eating UNILIVER (10-15 tabs/day)

Note;
Those 3 pints are misleading. Sometime after third pint I had prostate biopsy that went unfinished because I started bleeding. Bleeding continued and I ended in hospital. There they did nothing except watched me for 3 days (while I was bleeding). Eventually bleeding stopped. Latter I learned that I was real close in need of transfusion.
After all that for some time I had low Hgb, but I am ok now. Hgb=16.5, HCT=49.2 10/29/2013
\----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Script for therapeutic phlebotomy

John Doe DOB-xx/yy/19zz
elevated Hgb levels
check once a month,\* -----------check weekly
perform therapeutic phlebotomy
if Hgb>=15.8
repeat 12 months
289.0 Secondary Polycythemia

\----------------------------------------

...

### fitness@home — 2014-03-04T22:22:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-seizure.9923/post-113427>

DH just came from appt with Hematologist. It was at MD Anderson.

She reviewed everything from ER and current & past labs. Said it is not Polcythemia Vera. At this time she does not recommend anything as treatment. Said the elvated numbers can be related to his Testosterone therapy (good call JanSz) but she sees no reason for him to stop at this time.

He must continue with his quarterly labs through Primary doctor. If there is any extreme fluctions up in them, he is to see her agin. She also indicated he needs to keep up with good water intake. Dehydration is an issue.

He hasn't had MRI or EEG yet. Hospital has not called to schedule these yet.

### fitness@home — 2014-06-26T16:57:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-seizure.9923/post-132338>

Night before last DH had another seizure. Again, it was while he was **asleep**.

It occurred about 10 minutes after he went to bed. It woke me up and the seizure did not last as long this time (maybe 2-3 minutes). I put a leather belt in his mouth so he wouldn't bite his tongue and waited it out. He sat up, laid back down, answered some questions from me and then fell back asleep snoring heavily. Within five minutes he was sleeping quietly.

He does not remember anything. His first clue was trying to get out of bed the next morning and his calves crying out in pain. Stayed home from work yesterday.

Stubborn man needs to go back to doctor and he is fighting it. I think he should be tested for sleep apnea ASAP.

I know when my apnea occurs my inflammation is very high across the board and likewise when my body is in a healthier state I dont have apnea episodes. It all seems like a big brain issue to me. I have been using the [www.snorerx.com](http://www.snorerx.com) for a few months now with awesome results. Sleeping like baby, hardly moving all night, vivid dreams, and not even a peep of a snore to disturb my light sleeper of a husband.

How scary, I know. Did his doc put him on any meds? My DH only had his seizures at night but his doc still had him go on meds. How hot was the bedroom, was he a bit sleep deprived? Those along with a little alcohol were my DH's triggers.

I just got wedge pillowd for the bed to see if that would help the DH with his snoring and it has helped tremendously. If your hubby is really resistant to a sleep study, try a wedge pillow.

Good luck.

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=132345)
>
> Dawn I'm sorry you are dealing with this again, its so scary to see a loved one in that state.
>
> I know when my apnea occurs my inflammation is very high across the board and likewise when my body is in a healthier state I dont have apnea episodes. It all seems like a big brain issue to me. I have been using the [www.snorerx.com](http://www.snorerx.com) for a few months now with awesome results. Sleeping like baby, hardly moving all night, vivid dreams, and not even a peep of a snore to disturb my light sleeper of a husband.
>
> Would he consider cooling the head before bed? Not sure if this would help but icing my head and thyroid before sleep was very soothing to me when my inflammation was high.
>
> [http://www.mycoolingstore.com/enduracool-instant-cooling-helmet-liner.html#.U6xA1rEhsYU](http://www.mycoolingstore.com/enduracool-instant-cooling-helmet-liner.html#.U6xA1rEhsYU)

Oooh, I need to look into some of this stuff. My head has been feeling hot lately plus we are going to an outside music festival in 3 weeks and odd are it is going to be HOT and I NEED to stay cool. Thanks for sharing.

### caroline — 2014-06-29T02:30:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-seizure.9923/post-132549>

Dawn - Thinking of you and Chuck ...xo

### fitness@home — 2014-06-29T04:05:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-seizure.9923/post-132568>

Thanks for the words of support and the links too. No, he is not on meds yet.

Has an appt scheduled for Tuesday with his primary, an internist. Needs to see the neurologist again to, IMHO.

He's a stubborn man. For example, he won't even consider that it might be time to give up the Harley. I have not been on it since he had the first seizure. I did let his riding buddies know that he had another one though. I know they've only happened at night so far, but one never knows.

### nicld — 2014-06-29T05:15:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-after-seizure.9923/post-132574>

Wow, he still has his license. In our state, you have one and they can pull your license for 6 months. The hubby's doc has said that if he does not have any in a year he would consider taking him off the meds but he still might have to report it and take the license away. He NEVER had one during the day and all of his scans and tests showed fine (DD has had them too and even with meds her scans are abnormal). I am not as worried about his since the were at night but still nothing to mess with.

Good luck getting him to figure out why he is having them at night. If it is situational he can learn to avoid the situations that cause it. DH KNOWS that he needs his sleep and if he is lacking one night no alcohol, not even a small glass of wine the next night. I am doing all I can to make sure he gets a good night sleep. It is no fun when they guppy in the middle of the night.

Hang in there.

# Thread: Neat kit from Down Under
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neat-kit-from-down-under.9924/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-26T18:11:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neat-kit-from-down-under.9924/post-112292>

I'm envious of the spectacular scenery and the sunshine the Australians get, but they have some good kit there, too.

I like these -- SADF (South African Defence Force) Canteens. The South African Army uses 2 litre bottles unlike the 1 litre bottles everyone else has. I seems they're actually made in Australia, though, and it's about the only place you can obtain them:

[http://www.wellingtonsurplus.com.au...n-Military-Style-Plastic-Canteen-Model-009522](http://www.wellingtonsurplus.com.au/showProduct/militaria/canteens/CA9522/TAS-2-Litre-South-African-Military-Style-Plastic-Canteen-Model-009522)

I just like the idea of toting around a shedload of structured water ...

Still, my Klean Kanteens are very nice.

### Tanya — 2014-02-26T21:31:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neat-kit-from-down-under.9924/post-112332>

Mike, I wonder what happenes to all that structured water when you put it in those plastic canteens? I like my glass bottles for that…..

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-27T09:22:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neat-kit-from-down-under.9924/post-112419>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=112332)
>
> Mike, I wonder what happenes to all that structured water when you put it in those plastic canteens? I like my glass bottles for that…..

Dunno. I suppose these are BPA-free at any rate. I'm using an 800ml Klean Kanteen at the moment and a British Army 1 litre waterbottle when I need to carry more. Sometimes I buy mineral water in glass, and then it's got its own container with it.

I don't actually have structured water as yet. It seems I need to buy one of the wine things to swirl it about. Some time, I guess. We're not fluoridated at least, so I suppose I can get away without fitting a reverse osmosis filter in the house, which would be beyond my means. I use a Brita filter, and that's as far as I go.

# Thread: Forgiveness for wartime wrongs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forgiveness-for-wartime-wrongs.9928/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-26T20:39:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/forgiveness-for-wartime-wrongs.9928/post-112321>

Here's another of those interesting stories that seem to challenge our picture of what the world is like. I read it some time ago, and it came back into my mind recently.

In the story a man has a vision of a Japanese soldier who had mistreated him when he was a POW. Some kind of reconciliation seems to take place. The vision took place at the cell of the medieval mystic and anchoress Julian of Norwich. (No-one knows her real name -- she's sometimes known as Lady Julian, perhaps to make it clear she was a woman -- and probably adopted the name from the name of the church of St. Julian where her cell was.) AFAIK, the church was damaged in WWII and the current shrine is a reconstruction.

Of course, exactly what took place no-one can know, and maybe it was "all in his mind". But maybe not. And either way, it's a significant story for the effect it has on the man (and perhaps, who knows, on the departed Japanese soldier). The tale is told by the Anglican priest Robert Llewelyn who was the chaplain of the Julian Shrine in his book _With Pity Not With Blame_:

> In the early summer of 1974, a blind man, assisted by his companion and driven by some strange compulsion, made his way to Norwich to visit the Julian cell. Many years before he had been a prisoner of war in the hands of the Japanese. He had been severely treated and his present blindness was one of the scars carried from those days. As he and his friend knelt together in the silence of the cell he became aware of the reason for his strange journey. Julian herself appeared and with her she brought the Japanese soldier responsible for his suffering. The dead man had come to seek forgiveness of his wartime victim, who himself had not been able to overcome the bitterness felt against his captor. What then took place none can tell, only that the blind man was heard to speak to his visitor in Japanese; and, released from his resentment in the reconciliation which followed, he lapsed into an ecstasy of joy. The two made their way to All Hallows convent guest house next door, where they were given tea by one of the sisters. During tea in her presence the vision was repeated, and the blind man poured out his heart in great animation once more in Japanese. The vision was for him only, and not for his companions who saw nothing; though in any case the fact of his blindness indicates that we are not speaking of the ordinary channels of seeing. Understanding now the reason for his journey and knowing that reconciliation had been effected, the blind man left in great happiness.

books.google.co.uk/books?isbn=1848252870

Julian is now acknowledged as an important medieval thinker, who had obviously grappled with some difficult theological problems and come up with some interesting attempts to solve them. She's also the first woman writer in Middle English. She seems to have been a down-to-earth woman, who makes an interesting contrast to the rather unbalanced and hysterical Margery Kempe another Norwich woman of a slightly later time who left what is said to be the earliest surviving autobiography in English. Norwich was the second city in the kingdom at this time, in terms of wealth and populousness. Julian's language has some features that show that she had moved there from farther North.

Julian is quoted several times by T. S. Eliot in "Little Gidding", one of the _Four Quartets_:

[http://www.columbia.edu/itc/history/winter/w3206/edit/tseliotlittlegidding.html](http://www.columbia.edu/itc/history/winter/w3206/edit/tseliotlittlegidding.html)

# Thread: For Anyone Who Thinks "Traditional" Medicine Is "Simple"
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/for-anyone-who-thinks-traditional-medicine-is-simple.9936/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-02-27T02:36:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/for-anyone-who-thinks-traditional-medicine-is-simple.9936/post-112373>

> This set of six volumes provides a systematic and standardized description of 23,033 chemical components isolated from 6,926 medicinal plants, collected from 5,535 books/articles published in Chinese and international journals. A chemical structure with stereo-chemistry bonds is provided for each chemical component, in addition to conventional information, such as Chinese and English names, physical and chemical properties. It includes a name list of medicinal plants from which the chemical component was isolated. Furthermore, abundant pharmacological data for nearly 8,000 chemical components are presented, including experimental method, experimental animal, cell type, quantitative data, as well as control compound data. The seven indexes allow for complete cross-indexing. Regardless whether one searches for the molecular formula of a compound, the pharmacological activity of a compound, or the English name of a plant, the information in the book can be retrieved in multiple ways.

[http://www.amazon.com/Encyclopedia-...m\_sbs\_b\_1?ie=UTF8&refRID=1AZWCDNMXKK4ZQRTWNWN](http://www.amazon.com/Encyclopedia-Traditional-Chinese-Medicines-Pharmacological/dp/3642167349/ref=pd_sim_sbs_b_1?ie=UTF8&refRID=1AZWCDNMXKK4ZQRTWNWN)

So imagine that all formulas contain 5-35 herbs in combination and one begins to wonder who and how they figured all of this out without "modern" science???

Michio Kaku (Author)

............

Amazing.
Brain uses 20 w of power.
Memory can be put on CD, then my great grandson can have conversation with me while I am long gone.
Shove in CD and person with Alzheimer's gains access to the rest of his brain (that is still intact).
Shove in CD and you are expert on QED, Quantum electrodynamics.
Shove in another CD and you are black belt carate master.

Too bad that they currently are working on the mice only.

I have to buy this book.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-27T19:36:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bumblebees-in-trouble-too.9947/post-112506>

> Honeybees have gotten a lot of sympathy for their mysterious declines. But a recent study has shown that the honey-makers are spreading their worst diseases to their cousins the bumblebees.
>
> [A study published online this week in _Nature_](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v506/n7488/full/nature12977.html) clearly revealed that two pathogens that infect honeybees have crossed over into populations of wild bumblebees. ...

This is really bad news for agriculture, of course:

> The loss of honeybees and other pollinators like wild bumblebees could devastate agriculture. [Pollinators are estimated to do nearly $200 billion in "work"](http://scholar.google.com/scholar_url?hl=en&q=http://www.researchgate.net/publication/41621021_Global_pollinator_declines_trends_impacts_and_drivers/file/32bfe5113880d88a8d.pdf&sa=X&scisig=AAGBfm14FbcqY1LMnZClTBFGqeQK24Iiww&oi=scholarr) around the world each year.

[http://news.nationalgeographic.com/...disease-declines-science-pollinators-disease/](http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2014/02/140227-bumblebee-honeybee-disease-declines-science-pollinators-disease/)

# Thread: Ppl with mold issues....check out this C4a story
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ppl-with-mold-issues-check-out-this-c4a-story.9952/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2014-02-28T02:49:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ppl-with-mold-issues-check-out-this-c4a-story.9952/post-112561>

Discovered this online...amazing. I bet the way better redox potential in this environment plays a huge role in this. How I wish I could do this too...

Here goes...oh and it's not me of course. But wow. Wish it were.

My c4a a little over a year ago was 21,000 and I was actually feeling better at that point than I had a year previously. I"m sure I was still dumping toxin at a pretty good rate. The last c4a was 451 at the beginning of this year, and I have also regained an immense amount of mental function in the last year. I attribute a lot of it to living in pure mountain air at 8000 feet last summer. Not so much to medications, but we are all different. I consider pure air at 8000 feet to be mold-safe housing, keeping the posts on topic for the group

It was done almost entirely with extreme avoidance. I wish more doctors would get on board with that; they continue to let people spend money on treatment without taking any action at all to help them with what they really need, which is mold-safe housing.

Moldies actually "emanate toxins" for a while after they get out of exposure, and they can actually contaminate their own clothing and bed clothing. If the toxins can be washed out (most can, but not all) that stuff can just be repeatedly washed, but the key is to not own anything you can't get in the washing machine.

### Lahelada — 2014-02-28T02:52:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ppl-with-mold-issues-check-out-this-c4a-story.9952/post-112562>

We were flooded pretty badly and I always wonder what things are like under our lovely pseudo parquet floor....

### Hope — 2014-02-28T02:55:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ppl-with-mold-issues-check-out-this-c4a-story.9952/post-112563>

OH, if you had flooding, I'd get it checked out somehow. I can tell you, surviving mold.com has some great info on this, though. They just changed the site so i don't know where it is, but check it out...there are great ideas on how to see if you have mold. If anything, can you get a moisture meter? Do you smell anything? I am SO SO sensitive, I will smell anything. But there can be mold even if you don't smell it. Of course RP is everything..

### Lahelada — 2014-02-28T02:58:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ppl-with-mold-issues-check-out-this-c4a-story.9952/post-112565>

It happened in April last year and only recntly we have got rid of the smell withthe hot weather and aid of the air conditioning.We shall see what happens when the cooler weather comes in.If the smell returns...

### Hope — 2014-02-28T03:00:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ppl-with-mold-issues-check-out-this-c4a-story.9952/post-112568>

Lahe, wow. You are fortunate not to be affected. I got home tonight and bam, brain fog, stuffy nose...ugh. I wanna live outside now too. But some stuff I don't know how to have the energy for. Unless I am at the beach. See this:

Extreme avoidance is one way of living mold-safe. People who are severe reactors often end up practicing extreme avoidance. Tenting, cargo trailer, small travel trailer, even some living out of their cars. It means avoiding mold in an extreme sense, staying out of housing/buildings/situations that are not pristine. Most of us cannot find housing that is free enough of mold that it doesn't continue to make us sick. So in my case I bought the Camplite. I bought metal kitchen tools and new clothing and bedding. I do not use the cushions that came with it but put my own pads down on the benches, so I can replace them if they develop a problem. I have mostly new clothing. I have "inside" clothing and "outside" clothing, and once any clothing has been inside my car or inside a building, I don't wear it inside the trailer except to take it off and bag it prior to a shower. After having been in the car or in a building, when I get home, I remove all clothing, bag it for washing, and shower and wash hair. After that I put on clean "inside" clothing and proceed from there. If I forget something at the grocery, I will generally do without it rather than go through the whole routine again. I try to only do one shower a day, and sometimes that means I spend quite a few hours outside between appointments.

### Lahelada — 2014-02-28T03:08:56+0200

# Thread: ketosis, thirst
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Julie5 — 2014-02-28T15:53:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-112639>

I understand that I need to be in ketosis. I am eating the 50 g of protein in my BAB and maybe 30 or so g in my afternoon meal. When I test for ketones, the ketostix show that I have trace ketones. Is that being sufficiently ketotic, or do I need to further tweak my diet?

I do use a bit of hard cheese now and then and organic cream. I am not strictly tracking my carbs, but I do eat a serving of veggies every day: combos of onion, mushrooms, cauliflower, broccoli, cabbage,brussels sprouts. Not all at the same time, however. Also I eat coconut fat bombs, which have flaked unsweetened coconut and coconut cream, as well as coconut oil.

My other issue is thirst. I am drinking a LOT of water, but I am thirsty all the time. Not just a little, but very. I am controlling my EMF to the degree hubby will let me, which really only amounts to dimmer lighting at night, using goggles, no power or devices in our bedroom at night. So the question for me is, besides water, what more do I need to do to quench the thirst?

### nonchalant — 2014-02-28T16:10:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-112640>

Hi Julie5, have you read the Redox Rx blogpost? EMF is a big issue for trying to get hydrated, unfortunately. But also you could eat lots of seafood, egg yolks, and gently cooked pork for cysteine. Cysteine is needed to make collagen, which is needed to hang onto water. Your sulfurous veggies also have some cysteine.

Ketosis and hydration are linked. Finding trace ketones is good, however, and I believe you are doing fine on your diet otherwise. Are you drinking RO water? The only other thing I can think of is potassium, which is needed to attach to proteins at the same time water attaches to the protein. You also need ATP, to unfold that protein in the first place. Sunlight would help the unfolding process by providing direct energy.

### nicld — 2014-02-28T16:59:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-112653>

My N=1 is that I too eat a pretty ketogenic diet but I can hardly get the strips to read. I have found that it all has to do with how hydrated I am. The one day I did not drink that much water (I know, bad) my strips really showed good ketosis. I have realized that if I am drinking the massive amounts of water that one needs, the pee will be just so diluted that the strips may not show anything.

### Inger — 2014-02-28T20:18:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-112696>

Do you measure blood ketones or urine? I guess the urine does not tell much.... from what I have read it could as well be you are just good in using them so you do not pee them out. Or you eat too much carbs / protein. But I would do the blood ketone test if any...

Is your water good quality?

I was much thirsty in the beginning of my ketotic diet many years ago... the increased thirst disappeared after a while. At least it did for me.. I just drank a lot! I now too feel it if I have forgot to drink - I get dryish mouth and feel dehydrated (especially if I am inside or get hot - I have a pretty high temp so I guess that is why I need much water too). So I guess it is good the body reminds us of drinking

Cooked meat/fish makes me way thirstier than raw - no idea why?!

Somewhere I read some people might be able to hold on water better if drinking a small glass of salt water in the morning followed by lots of fresh water. IDK tho if this is real You could experiment. I sometimes do this if I take Iodine (salt flush) Do not add salt to your water otherwise tho! Jack has said not a good idea.

You should count your carbs once in a while? At least in the beginning. Veggies can have quite a bit of carbs... at least certain veggies like cooked carrots etc. so if you have lots of them it could get above 20 grams / day easily...

### Julie5 — 2014-03-01T04:00:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-112775>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=112696)
>
> Do you measure blood ketones or urine? I guess the urine does not tell much.... from what I have read it could as well be you are just good in using them so you do not pee them out. Or you eat too much carbs / protein. But I would do the blood ketone test if any...
>
> Is your water good quality?
>
> You should count your carbs once in a while? At least in the beginning. Veggies can have quite a bit of carbs... at least certain veggies like cooked carrots etc. so if you have lots of them it could get above 20 grams / day easily...

Inger, yes, our water quality is excellent. We have a country well AND an RO. No fluoride issues here. I know that most days I am doing well with the carbs. Other days I think I am pushing it, but haven't counted. It's interesting that drinking more water dilutes the ketones. I know I am eating lots of protein, but Jack told me not to worry about it converting to glucose. I get about 80 g. of protein a day.

### Linz — 2014-03-01T11:12:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-112810>

Perhaps it is the structured water in the raw food that works.

Julie, do you drink the water cold. It has optimal structure at 4 degrees. I don't like the taste of room temp water any more but just a little icy water stops me feeling thirsty.

### Da-mo — 2014-03-01T21:02:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-112877>

> [Linz said:](/goto/post?id=112810)
>
> It has optimal structure at 4 degrees.

Now that rings a bell . . . . I remember learning long ago that water reaches maximum density at 4degC. Above or below that density decreases.

### QiGuy1997 — 2014-03-02T03:47:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-112993>

I never understood this. Doesn't the water quickly just warm-up in the stomach?

### Linz — 2014-03-04T11:12:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-113308>

Qi, i think structured water = more energy. The water charge separates to form a molecular arrangement that can absorb more energy and actually excludes some toxins. Cold water, bone broth, fermented veg, raw are all ways to increase structured water I think, and perhaps it is stable enough to withstand warming.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-04T18:34:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketosis-thirst.9956/post-113377>

What Linz said, although I think that not all of the water actually reaches the stomach...

# Thread: Theory on top-end performance and the PPP
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/theory-on-top-end-performance-and-the-ppp.9960/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sjoshua — 2014-02-28T23:02:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/theory-on-top-end-performance-and-the-ppp.9960/post-112726>

I've been browsing the interwebs and it seems the research surrounding "fat burning" individuals always tends to find that humans lose their "top end" sprint performance when they forgo carbs. It seems fairly universal among the n=1 testing and the scholarly findings.

The PPP's evolutionary intent was to function in cold climates. With that in mind, I believe that a "proper" test of fat adapted transitions would mandate environmental control.

My theory is a keto human with efficient redox _in the right environment_ (i.e. cold & ideally natural - such as artic circle adventures) would be able to match or beat their warm adapted/carb sprint performance hands down.

Anyone have some $$ around to sponsor a research study??

### Albert83BCN — 2014-02-28T23:27:59+0200

>

# Thread: A Little Perspective On How "New" This All Is or Is Not.....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-little-perspective-on-how-new-this-all-is-or-is-not.9962/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-02-28T23:58:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-little-perspective-on-how-new-this-all-is-or-is-not.9962/post-112735>

There was this doctor named Sun Si Miao born in 581 AD who lived 99 years....

He noted that thyroid/goiter was treated well with seaweeds and the thyroids of animals:

> Sun Simiao is credited with recognizing that goiter occurred in mountainous regions and could be cured by prescribing both seaweeds (which contain iodine) and thyroid glands (which contain thyroid hormone) from deer and sheep. He successfully treated night blindness with livers from oxen and sheep, which contain vitamin A, and treated beriberi (leg edema due to vitamin B1 deficiency) by using unpolished rice (the outer layer of rice and other grains are rich in B vitamins), based on Buddhist practices from India. Sun also contributed to the utilization of placenta for treatment of weakness as well as for regulating menstruation and relieving difficult labor. He presented 25 formulas for treatment of malaria, 17 of them containing _changshan_ (dichroa). Some Chinese authorities have suggested that Sun Simiao authored the ophthalmology classic _Yinhai Jingwei_, but the evidence supports a much later date for the work, at least during the Song Dynasty, perhaps afterward (7). Nonetheless, Sun devoted an entire volume of his work to ophthalmology and is credited with being the first to elaborate the causes and treatments for ophthalmic disorders.

[http://www.itmonline.org/arts/sunsimiao.htm](http://www.itmonline.org/arts/sunsimiao.htm)

He wrote a textbook on Medicine that included peventive health and wellness as well as Women prominently and noted that treating them was a bit more difficult...

> A few points are worth mentioning: First, one of the greatest innovations in Sun Simiao’s encyclopaedia was the eminent position accorded to gynaecology. Earlier texts like the Essentials from the Golden Coffer (Jin Gui Yao Lüe 金匱要略, composed in the Eastern Han period by Zhang Zhongjing 張仲景) or On the Origins and Symptoms of the Various Diseases (Zhu Bing Yuan Hou Lun 諸病源候論, composed in 610 by Chao Yuanfang 巢元方) contained brief sections on disorders and treatments specifically targeting the female body, but these were always placed, like an afterthought, at the very end of the text, to be followed only by paediatrics. Information on diagnosing or treating the female body in the other Han dynasty classics (Inner Classic of the Yellow Emperor \[Huang Di Nei Jing\], Classic of Difficult Issues \[Nan Jing\], Treatise on Cold Damage \[Shang Han Lun\]) was minimal and limited to fleeting comments. By contrast, Sun Simiao placed his section on _**‘Prescriptions for Women’ at the very beginning, after the introductory scroll. Moreover, he began his gynaecological section with an essay that emphasised the need for any ‘gentleman engaged in the art of nurturing life’ to devote particular attention to this topic because ‘women are ten times more difficult to treat than men’, and yet are the mainstay of the family because of their pivotal role in reproduction. As a consequence, he devoted a full three scrolls, or roughly ten percent of his Essential Prescriptions to this topic and eloquently argued for the need for ‘separate prescriptions’ and special care for women’s bodies and minds.**_ He thereby set the stage for the fully-fledged emergence of gynaecology as a respected medical speciality in the official, government-sponsored system of medical training, research and publications in the Song dynasty several centuries later.
> Related to this, I would argue that Sun Simiao radically altered the meaning of a key term in Chinese medicine, namely ‘nurturing life’ (yang sheng 養生). In the early history of Chinese medicine, the notion of health and longevity, achieved and maintained by personal cultivation, was the overarching goal, the pivot around which elite medical authors contemplated and composed medical literature, especially in the context of materia medica and theoretical literature. This concept included such practices as dietetics, alchemy, a reclusive lifestyle, gymnastic and breathing exercises to stimulate the circulation of qi throughout the body, sexual cultivation (which, while emphasising the female orgasm as an essential component of complete intercourse, aimed at the male practitioner’s health benefits) and visualisation meditation. By reinterpreting the meaning of ‘life’ in a social, moral and cosmological context, Sun Simiao was, to my knowledge, the first author to widen this circumscribed set of individual practices, centred on improving the individual practitioner’s health and prolonging life. He included the female body in the larger perspective of life beyond just the individual body, as well as the survival of the family for generations to come, and ultimately the altruistic ideal of benefiting society and the macrocosm at large.

[http://www.jcm.co.uk/product/catalo...ugs-transforming-bodies-and-cultivating-life/](http://www.jcm.co.uk/product/catalog/product/view/10293/nurturing-life-in-classical-chinese-medicine-sun-simiao-on-healing-without-drugs-transforming-bodies-and-cultivating-life/)

Just thought I would mention it....No EMF that we know of.....

# Thread: Leptin as the topic for this week's homework on my university
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-01T17:18:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/post-112836>

Wow, I couldn't be happier today when I saw that the topic for this week's homework is to write a short essay on leptin and it's role on obesity.

The assignment goes like this:

_In this forum we are going to do critical thinking and documented work on Related Topics with lessons 3, 4, 5 and 6.

Leptin And Obesity

Obesity has been declared by the WHO as "the epidemic of the XXI century". This pathology is associated with multiple factors, including the genetic ellos. One of the lines of research in the boarding of this disease focuses on leptin.

One. What is Obesity? ¿And leptin?

Two. leptin impact on metabolism.

Three. Hypothetical beneficial effects of treatment leptin on obese subjects. Reference at least two studies that manifest the Role of Leptin in Obesity.

4\. Your own conclusions on the subject._

Considering that this is an online,distance university that has just starting operating this year and that, until now, the provided material hasn't been top quality -to say the least... I'm pretty surprised to find such a hot topic on this subject. Well, I guess it depends on the teacher, some of them suck big time but as far as it seem, the biochemistry one is onto something. Maybe he follows this blog also? hehe

### caroline — 2014-03-02T06:07:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/post-113013>

We will be expecting big things fom you ......

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-02T09:00:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/post-113023>

Well, remember I'm a software engineeer, I'm not actually sure what I'll make about this degree on Nutrition (I think it's RD on the USA) and Sports Science... when I enrolled I made it with the idea in mind of continuing living as a software developer and keep this as my biggest passion, just to know more and more (and in fact as a great way to understand better a lot of the thing I read here and everywhere about physiology, neuroscicence, etc)... but since I became unemployed and got so deep into my blog I really started considering changing professions... well I really don't know where I'm going, just living today and doing what I want without questioning too much but although it troubles me a little bit. The entropy in me is really high right now...

### caroline — 2014-03-02T10:21:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/post-113027>

Albert - are you in Spain? not sure I remember that right.... I will go and read your blog!
So - that isn't so bad \[unemployed\]....you can get out of EMF hell?

### caroline — 2014-03-02T10:22:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/post-113028>

nice blog ........I think........

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-02T10:54:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/post-113030>

Thanks caroline!

Yep I'm in Spain. No, I have quite a trouble getting out of the EMF hell and, in fact, this is the area where I still have lots of work to do, its my Achiles heel. This is my context regarding my environtment: I live in an 8th floor in Barcelona with a lot of WiFi signals as well as high electrification, light contamination and technology. I have computers, tablets and mobile devices all around, WiFI at home, almost no windows, poor sunlight exposure, high artificial light all day long and I'm hooked to my own device as I cannot scape the convenience of it on this world I live... etc so you guess, this is the EMF hell at the Nth level, and I'm pretty sure that if I have two problems that suck up my life energy and redox potential right now are a high messed environtment and psychological stress.

BTW, as my girlfriend went to the doc this week I went with her and took the chance to ask for some labs as it has been quite some time since I took the last ones. I asked for Vitamin D testing (really looking towards to it since long time) and guess what my doctor told me? That a deficiency would be strange on someone of my age range and that asking for it 'without a need' was unnecessary... I told him 'look I spend a lot of time indoors and I'm curious about it' (not planning to explain to him how it could be used as a proxy for hormonal status and redox potential, he might have looked to me like ?) but it made no difference at all. He only asked for a RBC with basic lipid profile testing and liver enzymes since he's worried because my cholesterol levels are high... Lots of things went through my mind in a second but finally the only thing I could say to not enter a discussion that would have been futile was "ehhr... ok".

This has been a little bit offtopic, maybe I should have used it as a good excuse to update [my optimal journal](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/alberts-story-and-journey-to-optimal.7216/) instead, hehe

### caroline — 2014-03-03T02:15:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/post-113129>

Albert ..... please have a look at Peter's posts today .....all you young people drive me crazy! sorry to say......

You are young and delicious ......the world is your oyster.....why aren't you out travelling the world and improving your redox potential - the way mother nature intended? You could be sleeping under the stars, swimming and fishing in streams ...... getting to know people who have very little, but, in reality have everything.

Take your girlfriend and throw a clean tshirt and some shorts in a backpack and have the time of your life........

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-03T10:24:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/post-113147>

That's what we should be doing from a health standpoint, no doubt about it. The problem is that we're tied by the expectations that this crazy world has put on us, from our family, etc.. I'm working on my startup company while studying a double degree in Nutrition and Sports Science and trying to get my message to the world about this topics on my blog (a little bit ironical, isn't it?). These are 3 things that each of them would requiere that I dedicate the full day on them, yet I have to split the time and also find some more to exercise, meet ocasionally my friends (everytime less and less), search the net for information (and this site consumes a lot of time also, LOL), and a lot more things...

I also have to think about how will I make money from something of this, as I'm still at my parents home at 31 and unemployed... here the Barcelona live is so expensive that you either accept an 8h job that is a shit and team up with someone or you cannot be afford to rent a flat. There's almost no help to enterpreneurs and even the normal jobs are scarce with the current crisis and very bad paid. My girlfriend also has a shit of a job but she's not willing to leave it because, well, she needs the money to live.

The good thing at least is that the startup I'm working on, if it turns out well, will be an online tool that will not only allow you to track your diet and exercise but also all the biomarkers that you want. For example, you could create the biomarker 'redox potential' if it still does not exist, as well as many others and then compare them with graphics, share it here, etc. It will be some kind of a mix between WellnessFX with SuperTracker with social media capabilities. I think it will be very helpful to every one of us that love to do our N = 1's, that my hope at least if I get engulged by this crazy world, that I'll leave something good for the ones that come after, let it be this tool or my blog! That's why I cannot afford to leave just for my sake, I want to change this world in some way or another.

BTW, I'll have a look at that post, and you remind me that I must consider giving priority to plan some weekend breaks in nature to try to bring some calm to my world, thank you caroline

PD: Today I slept almost 8h and feel pretty refreshed, It seems that at least I could recharge batteries a little bit!

### caroline — 2014-03-03T10:43:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-as-the-topic-for-this-weeks-homework-on-my-university.9967/post-113148>

Albert - I hear what you are saying. of course.......but - you are letting the world put it's expectations on you.......I think that is the wrong way around.

What do you want out of your life?

Please do read Peter's posts - He is an amazing young man - who has figured out what is important to him .... and also take a look at Ricardo's posts on lipids and ketones.

Please let me know what you think.....

no regrets......

# Thread: Japanese Ama
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-ama.9981/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-02T10:26:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-ama.9981/post-113029>

This from Ranulph Fiennes' new book _Cold_:

> But although in theory humans, like other animals, may acclimatize to cold on an individual level, proof of such an adaptation process is lacking, except in the case of Japanese _ama_, women pearl divers who have a unique tolerance of cold water. Their bodies show a marked ability to shut down their surface blood vessels so that the blood flow to the skin, and even to the underlying muscles, is limited. This reduces heat loss.

Interesting. I can't recall hearing of these women before.

He continues:

> ... growing up in a cold climate will also have a direct influence on cold-protective mechanisms. Studies of laboratory piglets raised in cold rooms have shown that their legs will be shorter than the norm, due to less blood circulating in their limb growing points. A similar effect may underlie the general stature of the Eskimo.

I recalled at this point that the Mongolians (who, incidentally have a fairly paleo diet) also tend to be stocky and strong but not particularly tall. They may also have limbs that are relatively short in proportion to their size: that I don't know.

One sometimes hears people in Paleo with very schematic notions making large claims about food and stature. Contrary to what they claim, populations that have cereal grains in the diet havee not always been short -- I'll forbear to give examples: I'm sure everyone here already knows that -- so long as the rest of the diet is good.

(I'm not, of course, recommending that anyone eat cereal grains -- I'd recommend the contrary! -- merely pointing to a physiological fact in the interests of truth and accuracy.)

Now it seems that cold may affect stature and build, too.

These things are complex. Everything is not always about the food.

# Thread: Small volume, zero carb, 70g protein, rest fat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 62
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2014-03-02T12:46:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113040>

Hi guys,

I'm doing an experiment to see if really high fat and bare minimum carbs helps me. My stomach cannot handle much stuff in it, so my theory goes that if I increase the fat and decrease the protein I will get more energy, from fat ATP, and more water, due to not as much protein to use up water and more fat to liberate water from, for the same volume of food.

Problem is I'm not really sure what to eat to achieve this. So far the things I'm doing seem to be lacking in micro nutrients (particularly magnesium and potassium). I also cannot eat large quantities of butter, neat, without feeling sick.

What I have been doing so far is 125g butter + 4 eggs for breakfast. Then chizo(???) sausage for tea, with about a litre of water as well. Nutrients aren't great from this but I get about 75g protein and rest fat.

Any other cheap idea's for recipe?

### seanb4 — 2014-03-02T12:48:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113042>

Also does anyone know if my theory has any weight to it?

Does higher fat/ketosis mean you can get away with less water?

### sooperb — 2014-03-04T15:31:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113338>

I wouldn't cut down on water, from reading Jack's posts etc. water is important and that we get plenty. I'm currently trying to up mine as I'm sure I don't get enough. Maybe you could add cream to the eggs if you're scrambling them? I'm not sure I could eat great lumps of butter on its own either. I'm sure Audrey said she made butter and cheese sandwiches once over, butter as the "bread" lol. Could you mix it with pure cocoa powder, then maybe freeze it in cubes and just munch straight from the freezer? Might be more palatable. Have you tried coconut butter? If you buy creamed coconut, warm it so that it melts down, add butter, maybe cocoa powder, mix well and refrigerate to set. Cut into small squares. You can't each much at once but you'll keep going back for it  You could add in chopped nuts to make it more interesting? If you need to keep your intake to small bites, maybe that's the way to do it.

### Lahelada — 2014-03-04T15:42:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113342>

Butter and cheese sandwiches are great. Cheese rollups with liver pate rolled into it.
If you have cocoa and want to use it you could make it mexican style preparing it with water and cinnamon.I think with everything you eat though that is in some way prepared like liver pate or cheese you will get carb creep,ie inherent carbs in the product.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-04T15:57:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113346>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=113040)
>
> I'm doing an experiment to see if really high fat and bare minimum carbs helps me. My stomach cannot handle much stuff in it ...

> Problem is I'm not really sure what to eat to achieve this. So far the things I'm doing seem to be lacking in micro nutrients (particularly magnesium and potassium). I also cannot eat large quantities of butter, neat, without feeling sick.

I think micronutrient density can be arguable anyway. The RDAs aren't all necessarily correct, and how much you need of each seems actually to depend on the overall balance of your diet. For example, it appears that once the amount of carbohydrate goes up, vitamin C needs to go up, too: it's not clear why. (I think it's been suggested that the reason may be that glucose uses the same transport mechanism and so excess carbohydrate may swamp the ability of the vit. C to get round.) Magnesium it's reasonable to supplement anyway -- it's probably one of the few nutrients everyone could take as a supplement. It's depleted in soil, and it gets burnt up by stress (which is endemic in modern life). I'd take some magnesium malate before bed every night. Potassium -- it is fairly high in much veg. but it's another you could supplement: some low-carb doctors automatically prescribe it to patients they put on low-carb diets. Most above-ground veg. doesn't equal much in the way of calories, so most low-carb people don't even count it ... but I suppose it is bulky and if your stomach can't take a lot in it. ...

General comments -- I don't know enough to advise.

I don't know why you feel sick. I did used to eat lumps of butter. I think it should be possible. I wonder if it could be a signal that you're not going to be able to digest it. I'd think it possible that you need to back off with the amount of fat, or take digestive enzymes ... or perhaps you need some other strategy anyway.

> What I have been doing so far is 125g butter + 4 eggs for breakfast. Then chizo(???) sausage for tea, with about a litre of water as well. Nutrients aren't great from this but I get about 75g protein and rest fat.

Chorizo (Spanish sausage pronounced choreetho)? Fairly high fat, but often has a lot of additives.

> Any other cheap idea's for recipe?

A standard thing back in the day when ketogenic diets were used therapeutically by hospitals was to give people eggs beaten into double cream. You'd just keep drinking that till you went into ketosis.

You're UK-based, aren't you? Waitrose has French pork rilletes -- those'll be pretty high fat. Then there's really dark chocolate -- you can even get 100% cocoa chocolate now:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Zotter-Organic-Percent-Peru-Chocolate/dp/B0069KVKI6/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/Zotter-Organic-Percent-Peru-Chocolate/dp/B0069KVKI6/)

More on what people on very high-fat diets tend to eat here:

[http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.co.uk/2008/03/kwasniewski-praise-lard.html](http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.co.uk/2008/03/kwasniewski-praise-lard.html)

But I'd say a bit more about what your problem is. Maybe you need a different strategy and the more detail the better.

### sooperb — 2014-03-04T16:03:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113349>

Sean has a journal Mike where he details his problems with his gut/stomach. He's struggling with quantity of anything.

### seanb4 — 2014-03-04T21:20:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113411>

Thanks for the responses guys, some great information there.

@[sooperb](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/8412/) I'm not talking about reducing water intake, im talking about getting more water for the same volume of food due to higher fat liberating more water when metabolised. I like the idea of coconut butter, will definitely try that. Not so sure about the cocoa powder or the cheese sandwhiches, the former is chocolate which gives me worse sickly feeling and palpitations, and the latter has cheese in which makes bloating and heaviness worse.

@[Gagnrad](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9746/) I hear you about the micronutrients though they still look low even when you take into account the things you said.
Yeah I don't eat much veg at all due to bulk meaning potassium and mag are hard to get, dark chocolate seems like a great source for these but, as said above, causes problems.
Eating really high fat is a new thing I'm trying, I've tried all other macronutrient combo's and specific diets and non of it seems to help, only reducing volume of food/water does.
I have tried digestive enzymes and various other things with no success.
The chorizo I buy has fairly decent ingredients but I don't want to have to rely on it.
Might check out the rillets and eggs with double cream, are those eggs raw?
Thanks for the link, very interesting. Seems I will have to keep at it until my tolerance increases.

Brief overview of my problems. Whenever I eat or drink ANYTHING (including just water) stomach massively bloats, feels uncomfortable/heavy, heart palpitates, feel like stomach contents come back up through esoughagus, stomach feels full mouth does not. Long term problems are massive fatigue, constipation, tiredness, and a whole host of other smaller stuff.

### sooperb — 2014-03-04T21:37:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113414>

### seanb4 — 2014-03-04T21:45:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113415>

It's not a bad idea though my stomach is constantly bloated so they may think that's normal. Last time I showed them my abdomen pulsating and now I'm getting my blood pressure taken a couple of times a week because it was high. I think the only reason it's high is because my heart is beating harder because my stomach is stressed but no doubt they will chalk it up to the high fat diet... I see the GP again this friday.

### kovita — 2014-03-05T10:33:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113535>

have you looked at GAPS diet? i did mkdified intro GAPS diet and it helped me i. gre t way to start and mostly to identify my sensitivities. I did IgG test later on and I discovered almost all my sensitivities through GAPS intro correctly (missed only ginger). I am pretty sure that the large amount of bone broths I consumed and still do consume quite a lot did me a great service. For me larticulary resistant starch proved to be very good. You may have poor vagal nerve tone, there are breathing exercises to improve this. The GAPS intro was not eaxtly smooth, I had some major crisis two weeks into it but it was worth it. ### Gagnrad — 2014-03-05T12:36:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113554>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=113411)
>
> @[Gagnrad](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9746/) I hear you about the micronutrients though they still look low even when you take into account the things you said.
> Yeah I don't eat much veg at all due to bulk meaning potassium and mag are hard to get, dark chocolate seems like a great source for these but, as said above, causes problems.

Right -- allergic reactions or something of the sort to the chocolate.

I would supplement magnesium anyway -- something like this would do:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Source-Naturals-Magnesium-Malate-Tablets/dp/B0013RGUK8/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/Source-Naturals-Magnesium-Malate-Tablets/dp/B0013RGUK8/)

Possibly you could do the same with pottasium. Dr. Mike Eades recommends it for people starting on a low-carb diet. This is quite an interesting article in which he mentions that among other things:

[http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/...ks-for-starting-or-restarting-low-carb-pt-ii/](http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/saturated-fat/tips-tricks-for-starting-or-restarting-low-carb-pt-ii/)

> Eating really high fat is a new thing I'm trying, I've tried all other macronutrient combo's and specific diets and non of it seems to help, only reducing volume of food/water does.

What about meal frequency and timing? Do you have the water away from the food or together with it?

> I have tried digestive enzymes and various other things with no success.

Right.

> The chorizo I buy has fairly decent ingredients but I don't want to have to rely on it.
> Might check out the rillets and eggs with double cream, are those eggs raw?

The rilletes are fairly dear, but I thik they're probably a fairly good option for LCHF diets. It's really just shredded pork mixed with lard. Waitrose also do a goose rilletes, but that has additives. It comes in little four ounces tubs. I find that half a tub with dressed some salad makes a good light lunch. This morning I had a spoonful with some eggs and some ham. They put the tubs with the pates on a shelf near the salads, if you're interested to try it -- they look like this:

[http://www.waitrose.com/webapp/wcs/...000-Unearthed+pork+rillets.html?storeId=10317](http://www.waitrose.com/webapp/wcs/stores/servlet/ProductView-10317-10001-97000-Unearthed+pork+rillets.html?storeId=10317)

Yes, the hospitals use (or used?) raw egg yolks for that non-alcoholic egg-nog thing.

Have you tried Jack's Optimal Coffee? There's another high-fat drink.

> Brief overview of my problems. Whenever I eat or drink ANYTHING (including just water) stomach massively bloats, feels uncomfortable/heavy, heart palpitates, feel like stomach contents come back up through esoughagus, stomach feels full mouth does not. Long term problems are massive fatigue, constipation, tiredness, and a whole host of other smaller stuff.

What has your doctor said? Have you been sent for tests or an endoscopy?

### seanb4 — 2014-03-05T13:10:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113558>

@[kovita](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9805/) yea I've had endoscopy, barium meal, hpylori, cat scan which have all come back fine.
Might try acupuncture when I get some money though doesn't it work by stealing current from the brain or something, is this bad?
What are the symptoms from deep internal tissue tension?
Yea I'm not too concerned about what I eat, as long as it doesn't give additional symptoms, I'm more concerned with how much I eat (volume). I figure this, high fat, may reduce overall volume of food and water I need to consume which will then lessen my stomach symptoms. I used to read a lot of chris kressers web site. He has some good idea's but none of which seem to specifically help me.

@[Gagnrad](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9746/) I should probably supplement magnesium, etc again. I have done in the past but noticed not much difference, constipation was slightly less, I just ran out and forgot to buy again. It would be more convenient to get it from food however.
Yeah I've tried various thing involving meal frequency, timing, and content. I've sort of settled for half a litre of water spaced out over the hour I wake up, breakfast, 3hrish gap then take mouthfulls of water every 10 mins or so until tea time.
Might check out the rillets next time I pass a waitrose. Will also try the egg nog.
Not sure about the coffee, I find it better to space my liquid consumption out.
I have listed the tests in the paragraph above. I have been up and down the doctors for the last 2.5 years. Tried differen't medication etc. It always ends with them saying they have no idea what it is and I should get on with my life.
I think they think that because my tests have come back fine, it's not life threatening and I'm probably being overdramatic or a hypochondriac. I have in the past accepted this as there diagnosis because I don't have much faith in the NHS and I had things I wanted to try myself to fix me.
I am now seeing the GP again and this time however I cannot accept that as a diagnosis as I have pretty much run out of things to try and am getting worse. Although having said that I really don't know what else they can do for me, run the stomach emptying test again maybe...

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-05T13:46:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-113563>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=113558)
>
> @[Gagnrad](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9746/) I think they think that because my tests have come back fine, it's not life threatening and I'm probably being overdramatic or a hypochondriac.

Well, maybe, but it's easy to misinterpret what others are thinking.

> I am now seeing the GP again and this time however I cannot accept that as a diagnosis as I have pretty much run out of things to try and am getting worse. Although having said that I really don't know what else they can do for me, run the stomach emptying test again maybe...

This being that they've said "they have no idea what it is and I should get on with my life" or something like it.

I suppose if this means that _because_ they don't know what it is, you should conclude there is no problem, it's a dodgy "diagnosis". As you say, they may not be able to think of anything else, but it doesn't follow that you haven't got a problem.

I wonder if your gut flora is disrupted. Have they ever tried to find out what it looks like?

As you're open to acupuncture, perhaps you might look at neurofeedback. That's not cheap, unfortunately. It does seem to have been used for GI issues with some success, though. Nora Gedgaudas has a podcast on that:

> **Your Gut Health and YouA person can eat the best quality diet and still be faced with tremendous health challenges unless digestion is optimally functioning. All nutrients responsible for every single metabolic function in the body require digestive processes in order for the body to make use of them. Nora explores many of the most common issues, including hydrochloric acid deficiency, GERD (Gastro-Esophageal Reflux Disorder), gallbladder issues, IBS (irritable bowel syndrome) and others. Joining Nora in this discussion is Nutritional Therapist, Colon Hydrotherapist and the Educational Director for the Nutritional Therapy Association (NTA), Colleen Dunseth, NTP, to discuss various aspects of these issues…and what you can do to naturally restore healthy digestion today! In the second half hour, Nora talks about the role of the nervous system and neurofeedback in addressing many digestive issues. Nora welcomes a former client to discuss their dramatic recovery from GERD using neurofeedback.**

If you scroll down it's the one from Aug 12, 2009 here:

[http://www.primalbody-primalmind.com/lectures-and-workshops/](http://www.primalbody-primalmind.com/lectures-and-workshops/)

These things are so complex. There's a suggestion that GERD could "result from a disordered myoelectric activity of the esophagus":

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC526194/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC526194/)

### Andrea — 2014-03-09T11:41:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-114298>

Mackerel and coconut?  AND tomato juice?!  Uhmmm... I'm not sure I would finish that either...

As for a curry, I sear some onions and garlic in suet/butter/coconut oil; add a chopped chilli pepper; then add coconut milk; in with herbs/spices like turmeric, ginger, lemon grass, cardamom, chilli powder, paprika, saffron, cinnemon etc. depending on what I feel up to...
For a meat curry I would sear my meat next to the onions and let the whole pot simmer very gently for an hour or two, depending on which muscle I'm cooking. For a seafood curry I would add pieces of white fish and/or shrimps and other creatures just a couple minutes before serving.

### sooperb — 2014-03-21T14:23:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-116940>

Sorry Sean, still chuckling here. Sign up to your local library and browse the cookery book section, find one that appeals to your taste buds, try a simple recipe and once you get that right, adapt it a little, more spices, different meat etc. Creamed coconut is great, I love it, but I just cut a little bit off the block and eat it, it's too claggy to eat a lot in one go, at least unless you've got plenty of water to wash it down. Everyone has to start somewhere learning to cook, once you master a few basics it's much easier to experiment.

It seems to me that you are trying to eat more fat than anything else, this is always going to be tricky as it's not the most appealing substance. I love butter but eating it straight off the block, nope, it just doesn't appeal. I can easily drink a coffee with two tablespoons of butter in it though  Boiled egg mashed with butter, not bad, scrambled eggs with loads of butter, fine. Omelette filled with cream coconut grated with maybe spring onion or sprinkle on some spices, should be fine. Start small, it's no good throwing the contents of your fridge into a pan, if it tastes horrid, you've wasted food.

Best time to shop in most supermarkets is around 8pm, they mark the fresh stuff down to silly prices to get rid. As the sell by/eat by dates are usually rubbish (except maybe fish) then you can buy knowing that it will last several days longer than they suggest. Makes shopping cheaper so that if you do make something inedible, binning it won't hurt your pocket so much.

### seanb4 — 2014-03-21T21:04:07+0200

Woke at 2 with stomach cramps and had a diahrea-y BM. This was probably from taking some of the olive oil straight off the spoon before tea. Been on water all day today until now when having my tea.

I think to counter this sickly feeling I'm getting with the oils I will eat more avocado.

### sooperb — 2014-03-21T23:22:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-117019>

Are you able to raise the head of your bed at all, this is apparently very helpful for reflux problems and allowing the whole body to stretch a bit.

Have you tried belly pork? You can buy it in strips and just put it in the oven till it's crispy, lowish heat and then leave it until you re ready to eat. It's very fatty  to jazz it up put sliced onion under the strips, sprinkle with herbs and black pepper and eat with cabbage which will help soak up the fat.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-22T12:06:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-117124>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=117112)
>
> I usually end up having to sit bolt upright but then stack my pillows and slowly lay down as it gets less bad.

I wonder if you might try sitting in a meditation stool for a while rather than sitting upright in bed or a chair. That opens the angle at the hip and allows more room for your internal organs. They're cheap enough -- just google "meditation stool", if you're interested.

With one of these, you're as it were kneeling, but the "sitting-bones" are resting on the seat of the stool; and your back can be nice and straight without effort, because when the angle at the hip is greater than 90 degrees the forces on both sides of the spine are more balanced. Put a pad under it, if the floor is hard on your knees.

Interestingly, I recently learnt that medieval choir pews sometimes had flip-up seats. When the seat was down, you could sit in the 90 degree angle; flipped up, there was a little ledge on top where you could perch, so that the angle at the hip could be about 135 degrees. That was probably for when the person was actually singing -- you apparently can't even inflate your lungs fully, hence can't sing properly, when sitting in the 90 degree angle.

### Penny — 2014-07-26T02:46:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-135580>

Spend $99.0 and get an American Gut project DNA of your gut flora... it will tell you what microbes you have... including any urease producers which kill stomach acid such as h pylori... if you don't have $99.00, then try taking a couple of mastic gum pills before you eat which will prevent the little darlings from neutralising your stomach acid... if that helps, chances are good you have h pylori or some other urease producer... acid reflux is the hallmark of this particular stomach pathogen...

To digest fat, you need bile - so eat something bitter before you eat - if desperately poor, even ear wax will work... the bitterness (dandelion greens are also bitter as are Angostura bitters) will stimulate bile production which is necessary to digest fat - so get a bottle of ox bile extract:
[http://www.amazon.com/Nutricology-Bile-500-Vegetarian-Capsules/dp/B0002JIUDS/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1406331143&sr=8-1&keywords=ox bile](http://www.amazon.com/Nutricology-Bile-500-Vegetarian-Capsules/dp/B0002JIUDS/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1406331143&sr=8-1&keywords=ox+bile)

and it will augment your own and help you digest fat - ox bile is all the rage with the gall bladder surgery community... bitters before you eat, digestive enzymes and ox bile pretty much cover it, IMHO...

You can also eat cabbage, oregano, thyme, turmeric, ginger, raw honey (bad for keto so maybe skip that one) bay leaves, garlic, sunflower seeds, shredded coconut, pumpkin seeds and cilantro - trust me, bugs will die... if all else fails, stick the fat in your coffee: coconut oil, butter, seeds, turmeric, ginger, black pepper to make the turmeric more bio-active, d-ribose, pumpkin/sunflower seeds and any nuts you want and chocolate powder (resveretrol), avocado, aloe vera - blast it in a blender with ice and any raw green veggies like kale/parsely etc to feed the distal flora and trust me, you won't be hungry for hours... plus the greens are very refreshing and have liquid... the ginger will help cut the fat and with digestion - fresh is always best... it's Morrocan coffee BTW Also, maybe get some ground beef and pork, mix in all the savory stuff, bake/saute it and have the coffee with it... if the coffee spikes insulin, just do it with chocolate...

Careful to not mix your acids and bases...

Hi Sean
Sounds as though you've really been through it--courage.
2 thoughts:
1) Have you been tested for SIBO?
2) How is your invisible environment (PARTICULARLY WHERE YOU SLEEP) with respect to EMF's? Have you done any measurements to test for a) Electro Magnetic fields; b) Low level radiation from WIFI, Cell Phone, Smart Meter; or, c) Body Voltage?

### seanb4 — 2014-07-26T22:35:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-135654>

> [Alex97232 said:](/goto/post?id=135651)
>
> Hi Sean
> Sounds as though you've really been through it--courage.
> 2 thoughts:
> 1) Have you been tested for SIBO?
> 2) How is your invisible environment (PARTICULARLY WHERE YOU SLEEP) with respect to EMF's? Have you done any measurements to test for a) Electro Magnetic fields; b) Low level radiation from WIFI, Cell Phone, Smart Meter; or, c) Body Voltage?

Hi Alex,
Not been tested for SIBO however have tried various SIBO diets and supplements with no effect. If I get the DNA gut flora test then hopefully that would say if it is SIBO.
I don't think it is though to be honest as my symptoms don't fit. For example today, I had 3 cans of mackerel at 8am then was slowly sipping my cold spring water afterwards. At 2pm I drank too much at once for me (still a real small amount) and my stomach shut down, felt real sick and heavy and I had to try and sleep it off, didn't go away for 3hrs. I have to assume only water was in my stomach at this time so it's like if too much is in it causes my symptoms to flair up like gasteroparesis. I have had a stomach emptying test and it came back normal however. Makes me think it is a mechanical problem, every time there is distention from food or water in my stomach/small intestines my body reads it as an emergency.

As for the EMF. In my room I am very good, magnetico to sleep on, grounded computer, sit 70 cms away, wired everything apart from internet, phone on airplane mode, etc.
Outside my house there is a tram system and about half a mile away there are big power lines.
As for measuring I have a voltmeter but nothing to detect EMF's. No smart meter.

At 2pm I drank too much at once for me (still a real small amount) and my stomach shut down, felt real sick and heavy and I had to try and sleep it off, didn't go away for 3hrs. I have to assume only water was in my stomach at this time so it's like if too much is in it causes my symptoms to flair up like gasteroparesis. I have had a stomach emptying test and it came back normal however. Makes me think it is a mechanical problem, every time there is distention from food or water in my stomach/small intestines my body reads it as an emergency.
>
> As for the EMF. In my room I am very good, magnetico to sleep on, grounded computer, sit 70 cms away, wired everything apart from internet, phone on airplane mode, etc.
> Outside my house there is a tram system and about half a mile away there are big power lines.
> As for measuring I have a voltmeter but nothing to detect EMF's. No smart meter.

Hi Sean,
Sounds as though you have been very dilligent about doing the things that are proven to help us get better. What are the measurements you have taken to show how effective your measurements have been?

IMHO, There are at least 3 different types of EMF's that could be fuxxing up your gut system causing it to malfunction mechanically--unable to handle the load you are putting on it.
1) Magnetic fields coming from improper wiring in your home, or even from close proximity to a power station. A Trifield meter (available from lessemf.com) can measure the milligaus (mG) in your home--could be high because of your proximity to the power station.
2) Radio Frequency fields from lots of things, including: WIFI, cell phones, cell towers, plasma tv's, anything wireless, et al. You can use an HF35 c meter to test the levels where you live. Your neighbors could be passing on their wireless signals to you.
You can alsochose hardwire cable, no WIFI. That should help to bring down the stress on your body's electrical-mechanical system system.
3) Body voltage you may be picking up from anything electrical around you--less emf or Michael Neuert could be good resources to help you understand what your body voltage meter is telling you.

MHO: The power station and tram.

I will remember this post if I get the money for the Trifield/HF35c.
As for the body voltage I have a voltmeter that measures this, I think it was above 3V until I made the adjustments to my room then it dropped to <0.5V.

@[caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/)
Hi! Yea although I've not noticed any difference going from 10 to 20 guass.
My energy has been mostly better (although still crap). I have been getting in lots of water (4+ Litres per day). I have also been eating a lot of salami however I am going to stop as the salt content is extremely high when I do this and is counterproductive to hydration.

### caroline — 2014-07-27T14:27:33+0300
