Are you eating canned salmon?

### seanb4 — 2014-07-28T19:18:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-135843>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=135707)
>
> Hi Sean .... you aren't drinking too much water are you?
>
> Are you eating canned salmon?

No I'm eating canned mackerel.
Before I was eating 2 cans of mackerel then 450g of salami. I have stopped this now due to salt.
I'm currently eating lots of canned mackerel and other meats + lard. Wondering about the can metal toxins though.

About the too much water. If you think this is what is causing my stomach to shut up and feel sick then I don't think so. I think it is the speed I drink it as well as other seemingly random factors. If I'm good, I can get upwards of 4L (2 quarts?) per day. However yesterday and the day before my stomach shut up at under 2L. The only noticable reason was I was drinking slightly faster than usual, this probably lead to a higher build up than usual and at a certain point my stomach shuts off.

I was reading on a forum about some guy who had similar problems drinking water and someone mentioned it could be a psychosomatic response. I think this could be similar for me. My stomach learned to protect itself, when I had the stomach ulcer, by shutting off my stomach, making me feel nauseous, etc. It then adapted to this so that even when my ulcer went away it still automatically does it.
The guy on the forum says he has it were he got food poisoning from a certain type of fish and now he cant eat it without being sick due to his bodies reaction.
I think Gagnrand mentioned something about biofeedback. Could be worth looking into in the future.

### Penny — 2014-07-29T00:58:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-135875>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=135620)
>
> Thanks for all the information Penny. Unfortunately for me, I have tried all these suggestions at one point or another and none have made any difference.
> At one point I was convinced I had H Pylori/Low stomach acid, so I tried every trick in the book.
>
> I need to get a DNA project of the gut flora. I have saved up so money from work as a back up if I can't convince the NHS to do it for me. I should have it done either way in the next few months. Hopefully this will shine a light on some things...

I don't get why you're eating such massive quantities of food - 2 cans of mackeral and a pound of salami? I mean, I'm no rocket scientist, but even I would have had trouble digesting that - and trust me, that's sayin' something...

I drink 16-32 ounces of water before breakfast and the same before bed - a few sips before meals so I don't choke on the food - weird but that's me - and then let it digest - if I have troubles I take either an enzyme or a probiotic - I think there are enough glutamates in salami to start 15 consecutive migraines... maybe add some raw greens/spices to feed the gut flora? Especially something bitter...

As far as nausea goes and a psychosomatic reaction - I can buy into that... my dad made me a homemade pina colada once while the band "Heart" was playing and it made me want to throw up... I literally felt nauseous every time I would hear that band for years...

Or you could do what my grandmother who lived to be 97 did every morning to help her digestion - she would drink warm water with some lemon in it - I've also heard people do apple cider vinegar - throw some acid down the hatch and maybe whatever is down there won't see it coming... ?

If you are nauseous, eat small quantities of food, lots of ginger or chamomile tea until you can handle it - you don't look to me like you have a leptin sensitivity problem...

I can drink more ice cold water than regular water...

Another resource, as odd a site as it is is "The Woo"... she eats keto, and has some really good examples of what that actually means... like a couple pieces of smoked salmon with some cream cheese - she is not going out of her way to eat volumes of fat/protein...

[http://itsthewooo.blogspot.co.uk/2014/06/myth-meat-is-not-very-ketogenic-plants.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.co.uk/2014/06/myth-meat-is-not-very-ketogenic-plants.html)

Of course , your CF presents its own challenges, but that Jeremy guy did it and I have no doubt you can too - hang in there...

### Penny — 2014-07-29T01:32:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-135880>

You know - and I can't believe it took me this long to think of it - if you eat a lot of protein, it creates a lot of ammonia for your body to get rid of - that could be why you feel so ill... you could do the ol' molybdenum/B12/manganes/boron/yucca/phospatidyl serine/beets and methylated B vitamin complex like Thorne #12 and see if it helps? Some people have enzyme issues - they are either blocked from genetics or blocked from aluminum etc... you also might want to try magnesium malate and some kind of horsetail/silica - propolis helps with aluminum toxicity and is actually amazing on so many levels:
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19425234](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19425234)
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19425229](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19425229)

This guy had some of the same issues digesting protein and he solved it with a mild keto diet - interesting:
[http://defyingagewithfood.com/12-9-body-fat-mild-ketosis-meat-protein-june-6-2014/](http://defyingagewithfood.com/12-9-body-fat-mild-ketosis-meat-protein-june-6-2014/)
[http://defyingagewithfood.com/ketos...otein-clarity-or-protein-coma-day-152-112413/](http://defyingagewithfood.com/ketosis-n1-protein-experiment-protein-clarity-or-protein-coma-day-152-112413/)
And this is really good - about the ammonia/urease producing bugs and resultant nausea - we are certainly experts here on ammonia producing bugs leading to nausea:
[http://caloriesproper.com/?p=3882](http://caloriesproper.com/?p=3882)

From what I can tell, the clostridia family puts out 20 times more ammonia than any good flora would - add a couple of urease producers and you have even more...
And you might have some kind of desulfobrio bacteria which eats any sulphur you eat thus depriving you of sulfate...

My kid soaks in Epsom salts for nausea - it's the only thing that has helped him - another tid bit - hormones are processed in the liver by "glucoronidation" - this is a phase 2 liver detoxification pathway where the body slaps a glucose onto the toxin/hormone to get rid of it - clostridia will actually cleave the glucose using glucoronidase enzymes... so taking calcium d-glucarate will help this...

So, soak in Epsom salts, take calcium d-glucarate, a b complex with some trace minerals and maybe some yucca when you eat protein and eat beets/dandelion greens/liver to support methylation - liver would be so much better for you than salami IMHO and it is cheap - chicken liver with eggs is divine - add a little bit of bacon - yum!

Green smoothies would also maybe help you as well as being hydrating - good gut flora loves greens:
[www.unblindmymind.org](http://www.unblindmymind.org)

>
> Of course , your CF presents its own challenges, but that Jeremy guy did it and I have no doubt you can too - hang in there...

I don't get why you think that is such a large quantity of food. Maybe in America the can portions are bigger? A can is 88g here and about 200kcals, I did have 2 of these for breakfast and then 3 x 150g Salami for tea. This totalled 2000kcals, and ~150g Protein. The weight was 600g total and the volume pretty small.

Anyway it is not a protein issue. I have tried all differen't combinations of food, even a dreaded all fruit diet and it doesn't seem to make a difference. My stomach reacts to everything.

Another example is just the other day I had only 2 cans of mackerel (170g) the entire day and my stomach still shut off.

Thanks for the info on the glutamates but I have dropped the salami now anyway. Also tried apple cider vinegar/lemon, methycoloblamin B12 and anything else I could read about that would help acid production. The wierd thing is, when I was taking Betaine HCL (increases acidity) you are meant to stop when you feel burning, the higher you go the less stomach acid you have, I went ridiculously high and never felt burning...

I tried lots of raw ginger before, all my water is ice cold.

I'm not sure ammonia is the problem. I drank just water for 30days last year and had the same problems throughout. The ammonia levels would have surely died down in this time.

Thanks for the links, I will read them soon.

### Penny — 2014-07-30T01:59:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-volume-zero-carb-70g-protein-rest-fat.9983/post-135980>

Try soaking in Epsom salt foot soak in hot water - if it helps => you R toxic and that will give us more info... still think you're guessing unless you see what your gut flora is...

You also might want to try feeding the distal flora a variety of plant based green foods like they talk about in [www.unblingmymind.org](http://www.unblingmymind.org) -

Did you try the mastic gum?

As far as betaine HCL goes, if you have a urease producing bug, they use stomach acid as a substrate... this is why Yasko doesn't go for betaine HCL - Klinghardt doesn't go for it either as the stomach will produce 6 oz of stomach acid on its own and betaine HCL is a drop in the bucket... he recommends bitters for that reason -

The mastic gum will inhibit the urease and your stomach will then have acid again, thus doing its job of keeping out a whole host of pathogens...

that is what it sounds like to me - you could get a urease test if they pay for it, but I would still go to American Gut...

### sooperb — 2014-03-02T12:46:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-a-warm-shower.9984/post-113041>

After a cold bath ok or is it preferable to warm up naturally?

### Lahelada — 2014-03-02T14:06:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-a-warm-shower.9984/post-113049>

My intuitive answer would be to warm up naturally as it draws out the process of rewarming. By the way have you realised yet that that you feel colder when you put on clothes after CT than if you walk around naked ?

### sooperb — 2014-03-02T18:11:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-a-warm-shower.9984/post-113071>

I haven't La, interesting. I guess my usual process is to wash and dress as I usually shower in the morning. I might give it a try though, once I've drawn all the curtains, wouldn't want to make sideshow Bob faint in shock  I thought that maybe not having a warm shower and warming up naturally would burn more fat.

### nicld — 2014-03-02T21:46:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-a-warm-shower.9984/post-113083>

Jack has said many times to walk around naked and rewarm or even better, get some oxy release afterwards (wink, wink). I think that it does take more energy to keep the body warm so it would make sense that one would burn more fat by being cold longer. I agree with La, I seem colder the more clothes I put on after CT.

### caroline — 2014-03-03T02:19:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-a-warm-shower.9984/post-113130>

ditto from me too ^^^^^^ jumping around naked is way more fun .....

**Subforum:** The Cave

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-02T16:35:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-snippets-from-fiennes.9985/post-113063>

I'm still enjoying Ranulph Fiennes new book _Cold_. Here it is:

[http://www.amazon.com/Cold-Extreme-Adventures-Lowest-Temperatures/dp/1471127826/](http://www.amazon.com/Cold-Extreme-Adventures-Lowest-Temperatures/dp/1471127826/)

Here's one of many things that struck my eye -- an excerpt from a speech given by Prince Charles on Fiennes' expedition in 1979. Praising the ambition and courage of the participants, he says:

> … Above all the human risks are still the same. They're all there today, the frostbite, **the loss of body fat because of the cold** and the protracted bouts of shivering, especially at night ...

There we are -- it's even in a public speech from the 1970s. So much for "hormetic effects".

On page 100 he mentions the lowest survival body temperature ever recorded. It was "Swedish skier Anna Bågenholm who, in 1999, was trapped in water, and under the ice, for eighty minutes. Her body temperature dropped to 13.7 ℃".

A few pages further on I learnt that orcas (killer whales), which are about 30ft long, will attack a blue whale. A sealer in 1923 saw five attack. What they did was to fasten on the jaws, pull them open, and eat its tongue. That was all they touched.

It turns out that the tongue of a blue whale could yield "up to 2,000 kilograms of oil".

Has no-one warned these orcas about the dangers of high-fat diets? … LOL

### sooperb — 2014-03-02T18:04:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-snippets-from-fiennes.9985/post-113070>

Ah whale wisdom, a new one for me. What I find fascinating is that in the early days of Antarctic exploration, the men apparently had little to nothing in the way of cold weather wear, they set off in hats, tweed jackets, plus fours and long socks with stout shoes. Curious that any would survive more than half an hour in that attire yet some came back to tell the tale.

### caroline — 2014-03-04T09:14:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-snippets-from-fiennes.9985/post-113299>

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-04T10:39:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-snippets-from-fiennes.9985/post-113303>

Yeah. That's a photograph from Mallory's expedition.

From the accounts I've read, the Shepas tend not to wear very much. I think that's partly poverty -- AFAIK, the conditions are so extreme, that even cold-adapted people would want to wear more if they had it. At the "lower" altitudes, it seems they wear fairly flimsy clothes and light shoes or sandals. If they go higher, the expeditions kit them out.

I don't know that the Sherpas' diet is particularly helpful. Sure, there used to be a lot of butter used in Tibet -- I think people would drink literally dozens of cups of tea a day with butter added to them -- but I'm not sure whether that's true of Nepal. I got the impression that they ate the kind of thing that was fairly standard in much of Asia: high carb -- noodles, that kind of thing. But I may be quite wrong.

They would also eat a lot of meat in Tibet. This apparently used to be "shocking" to Westerners with an interest in Buddhism but not much knowledge about it. There were taboos against killing and butchering animals in Tibet but not against _eating_ meat. There was a special ritually unclean caste who did the butchery. Tibetans even trained their horses to eat meat -- sometimes the grass would be under snow.

### lubylouUK — 2014-03-03T11:50:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adrenal-exhaustion-new-here.9990/post-113152>

Hello, I'm new here. I have had adrenal fatigue for years and years which escalated into adrenal exhaustion after the birth of my daughter (now aged 2). I have been taking lots of Mg, Ca, Zn, fish oils, enzymes, have tried all sorts of amino acids for my sleep-htp/tryptophan (htp makes me groggy and angry and palpitations)/theanine, melatonin, zopiclone (ughhhhh). , antihistamines, Valerian, hops, passiflora (these do work, but the hops make me so depressed). I've become a raving carnivore, decreased my carbs loads, no sugar, no dairy, no gluten, no caffeine. I've lost loads of weight (not a great thing-feeling quite skinny and cold).

I need some help getting started please??

1\. I don't understand the COld therapy? Is there a simple and quick explanation/procedure I can get started with? Just dunk my face?

2\. I wake up at 3.30 every morning. I don't wake up with a jolt anymore thank god but I still cant get back to sleep even though I really need to! I need to reset my circadian rhythm? How?

3\. How do I know if I'm leptin resistant? I am under weight.

4.Since I cut out refined carbs and increased my meat my sleep has got worse?! I don't seem to be able to get through the fast at night. Waking up due to blood sugar drop/cortisol increase? I know that sounds like I have that the wrong way round but this is whats happening.

5Should I be eating BAB breakfast once I wake up or get up?

6.Phosphatidylserine? At night for high cortisol? But my cortisol is low in the day (I assume). Anyone had any good experience with this?

7\. I have used Zeolite to detox heavy metals and it has helped my mood a lot!

8\. I had an ASI done last year. Cortisol was HIgh am but low all day. It may have changed since then.

Any advice will be gratefully received.

PS What do I need to do to be able to post on ASK JACK thread?

### nicld — 2014-03-03T17:09:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adrenal-exhaustion-new-here.9990/post-113171>

Hi Lubylou and welcome,

I can offer some suggestions to a couple of these.

Read Cold Thermogenisis protocol. That is a great way to start plus there is a really great CT thread on the forum (How was your CT today). Jack says that CT is huge for everyone and I can vouch for that.

You bet that your circadian rhythm is off. Do you have blue blocking glasses? It is crucial to wear these as soon as the sun goes down and try to eliminate as much artificial light at night. I was just like you waking up in the middle of the night and not being able to fall back asleep and this has helped my tremendously. Amazon had the orange safety glasses for about $9.00. Taking sleep supplements is not recommended as it messes with your natural rhythm.

It is also crucial to take a look at your environment to see what non-native EMF’s you have. Are you signed up for the optimal reset? Michael Neuert will be the guest tomorrow and he is the expert on EMF’s. I suggest making sure to join.

My suggesting is to start adding more fats to the diet and what kinds of protein are you eating? Jack really like people to eat seafood and a lot of it. That should help with the saeity that you seems to be having issues with. Good for you for eliminating all of the other crap in the diet.

As for the BAB, when I would wake up early I would stay in bed till at lest the time I wanted to get normally get up then work on eating my BAB.

Also check into the biohacking and hormones 101 email series. That will be a good start to look into getting more labs done to get a better picture of what is going on.

To do an “Ask Jack” question you must be a gold member (highly recommended) and if you got to the main page (not the forum page) there should be a spot there to upgrade to memberships.

As for your other questions, hopefully others can comment on.

There is SO much information here that is can get over whelming but just take it one day at a time and realize that you will not fix yourself in 6 weeks, women are WAY too complex for that.

Nicole

### Sammy — 2014-03-07T11:18:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adrenal-exhaustion-new-here.9990/post-113961>

Hi Lubylou,
You can use these links as a starting point if you haven't done so :
[http://jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/](http://jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/)
[http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/)

1)[http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/)
[http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-easy-start-guide/](http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-easy-start-guide/)
[http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-13-the-faqs/](http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-13-the-faqs/)

My take on cold thermogenesis: Chronic cold exposure acts like a reset button on your biology. Cold decreases entropy(=disorder). When there is inflammation in the body = there is alot of disorder/chaos in your cells. Every disease has an inflammatory component to it, that's why Dr. Kruse recommends cold thermogenesis for everyone who is battling disease/inflammation.

What else? You must be always tired with adrenal fatigue right? This means you are losing energy, lots of it. Cold slows down your loss of energy. (like putting your laptop battery in your freezer makes it last longer).

2)Is your bedroom dark? If not, are you wearing an eye mask? I used to wake up at around 3:30-5 am....way before sunrise. When I woke up that early I just stayed in bed, with the eye mask on(as to make sure body senses dark), and just lie there and rest. I also try to consciously breathe in and out(don't know whether this counts as meditation or not). Sometimes I am able to fall back asleep, maybe 30 minutes to an hour. Sometimes I don't. Initially I was very frustrated and annoyed when I woke up that early, I'd also stress about it(NOT GOOD).
I learnt to just accept it.

I would try to have my BAB when the sun is up.
Make sure avoid any exposure to blue light after sunset. Blue-blocker glasses do help.

3) I think if you have adrenal fatigue you have leptin resistance...plus your ASI is upside down. You can check with HS-CRP and rT3 as well. They will be high.

4) I have heard alot of people say that carbs help them sleep. I can't remember why...but I know this is not a good thing.

5) You need to change the time you wake up first. Once you do that, you will be able to eat breakfast when the sun is up AND when you wake up. I am waking up later now...around 6:30. Sunrise at around 6:50am. Getting there....

No idea about supplements. Don't use any.

Make sure you drink enough water.

### Hope — 2014-03-03T17:24:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-camping-cure.9991/post-113173>

For all my JKF friends...and especially those of us with mold/biotoxin illness...this is one incredible article. Makes me want to go camping. Especially if there are places where you can camp AND bring/use your CPAP machine......

[http://aeon.co/magazine/being-human/environmental-illness-cured-by-camping/](http://aeon.co/magazine/being-human/environmental-illness-cured-by-camping/)

She gets the light thing too:

Yes, research from the University of Colorado confirms this effect; camping for one week, away from electric light, resets even the most stalwart night owl’s circadian rhythms.

Now I wonder if she is a JK member.....

I become cold-adapted in winter, cooking in a hoodie and sandals, in temperatures that might have formerly made me shiver – and research backs me up there, too (cold thermogenesis, as it’s called, burns brown fat and raises antioxidant levels. It’s the reason Finns like to jump in the snow after a sauna).

### kovita — 2014-03-03T18:35:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-camping-cure.9991/post-113177>

Martin, i just spent a weekend in my pare ts house in a tiny village without wifi etc on the shore of a lake. I did not camp but spent two whole days outdoors, took a few very cold baths, drank water from our well in th sunshine and had great pfilosophical talks with my mother and sisters. I feel so incredibly refreshed, taht. j cannot believe it. My painns disappeared for whole 2 days, this never happened ever since I got sick. I have always spent a lot of time camping and outdoors but it disappeared from my life few years back. I know that this year's holiday on atlantic coast in the camp will make me great service. Maybe, after all, all we really need is to be outside in the nature most of the days and this will eventually cure us. Sad, it is very difficult to organize it since our lifes are constantly bringing us indoors and out of the nature.

### Martin — 2014-03-03T18:39:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-camping-cure.9991/post-113178>

Yes, very true words from you. I'm going out soon to my garden plots and just "be" there for a while. I have to make time to do more of that . Responsibilities, yes, but Nature is calling me.

### Hope — 2014-03-03T18:46:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-camping-cure.9991/post-113182>

Kovita that is great. Your response sounds like proof that your environment (inflammatory) is a big part of your story. Need nature more than supplements  And the more I learn, the more I realize, we just have to change our minds around how we want to live....and then change our lives.

Martin...that is great. You are so blessed. I will miss my 48 square foot garden in our townhouse community here, a great source of joy. But I hope to do the most of a small ground spot and a container garden.

I heard an amazing talk this weekend by Christiane Northrup, talking about The Anastasia Series. Never heard of it but she mentioned that if you, at the end of the day, water some plants with water that you first poured over your feet, the soil watered with that water will produce homeopathic compounds that are good/healing for you. Wild. No idea if it's true....but sounded amazing.

# Thread: Giant Virus Resurrected From Permafrost After 30,000 Years
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/giant-virus-resurrected-from-permafrost-after-30-000-years.9997/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2014-03-04T02:59:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/giant-virus-resurrected-from-permafrost-after-30-000-years.9997/post-113269>

[http://m.disclose.tv/news/Giant\_virus\_resurrected\_from\_permafrost\_after\_30000\_years/100542#DTV](http://m.disclose.tv/news/Giant_virus_resurrected_from_permafrost_after_30000_years/100542#DTV)

[March 3, 2014 - A mysterious giant virus buried for 30,000 years in Siberian permafrost has been resurrected.
The virus only infects single-celled organisms and doesn't closely resemble any known pathogens that harm humans.

Even so, the new discovery raises the possibility that as the climate warms and exploration expands in long-untouched regions of Siberia, humans could release ancient or eradicated viruses. These could include Neanderthal \[URL='http://www.disclose.tv/search/desktop?title=Viruses'\]viruses](http://m.disclose.tv/news/Giant_virus_resurrected_from_permafrost_after_30000_years/100542#DTV) or even smallpox that have lain dormant in the ice for thousands of years.

"There is now a non-zero probability that the pathogenic microbes that bothered \[ancient human populations\] could be revived, and most likely infect us as well," study co-author Jean-Michel Claverie, a bioinformatics researcher at [Aix-Marseille University](http://www.disclose.tv/url/Tm9DaGFuY2VBbm9ueW1vdXNodHRwOi8vd3d3LnVuaXYtYW11LmZyLw..) in France, wrote in an email.

"Those pathogens could be banal bacteria (curable with antibiotics) or resistant bacteria or nasty viruses. If they have been extinct for a long time, then our immune system is no longer prepared to respond to them."

(A "non-zero" probability just means the chances of the event happening are not "impossible.")

In recent years, Claverie and his colleagues have discovered a host of giant viruses, which are as big as bacteria but lack characteristic cellular machinery and metabolism of those microorganisms. At least one family of these viruses likely evolved from single-celled parasites after losing essential genes, although the origins of other giant viruses remain a mystery, Claverie said.

In the researchers' hunt for more unknown pathogens, they took a second look at permafrost samples collected from [Kolyma](http://www.disclose.tv/url/Tm9DaGFuY2VBbm9ueW1vdXNodHRwOi8vbWFwcy5nb29nbGUuY29tL21hcHM_bGw9NjUuMCwxNTIuMCZzcG49MC4xLDAuMSZxPTY1LjAsMTUyLjAgKEtvbHltYSkmdD1o) in the [Russian Far East](http://www.disclose.tv/url/Tm9DaGFuY2VBbm9ueW1vdXNodHRwOi8vZW4ud2lraXBlZGlhLm9yZy93aWtpL1J1c3NpYW5fRmFyX0Vhc3Q.) in 2000. Because the permafrost was layered along steep cliffs, drillers could extract samples from 30,000 years ago by drilling horizontally into the ice, thereby avoiding contamination from newer samples.

The team then took samples of this permafrost and put them in contact with amoebas (blob-like single-celled organisms) in Petri dishes. The researchers then waited to see what happened.

Some of the amoebas burst open and died. When the scientists investigated further, they found a virus had killed the amoebas.

The ancient virus infects only amoebas, not humans or other animals. This pathogen belongs to a previously unknown family of viruses, now dubbed Pithovirus, which shares only a third of its genes with any known organisms and only 11 percent of its genes with other viruses. Though the new virus resembles the largest viruses ever found, Pandoraviruses, in shape, it is more closely related to classical viruses, which have an isocahedral shape (with 20 triangular-shaped faces), Claverie said.

The findings raise the possibility that other long-dormant or eradicated viruses could be resurrected from the Arctic. As the climate warms and sea ice and permafrost melt, oil and mining companies are drilling many formerly off-limit areas in Russia, raising the possibility that ancient human viruses could be released.

For instance, Neanderthals and humans both lived in [Siberia](http://www.disclose.tv/url/Tm9DaGFuY2VBbm9ueW1vdXNodHRwOi8vbWFwcy5nb29nbGUuY29tL21hcHM_bGw9NjAuMCwxMDUuMCZzcG49MC4xLDAuMSZxPTYwLjAsMTA1LjAgKFNpYmVyaWEpJnQ9aA..) as recently as 28,000 years ago, and some of the diseases that plagued both species may still be around.

"If viable virions are still there, this is a good recipe for disaster," Claverie said. "Virions" is the term used for the virus particles when they are in their inert or dormant form.

But not everyone thinks these viruses spell potential doom.

"We are inundated by millions of viruses as we move through our everyday life," said Curtis Suttle, a marine virologist at the [University of British Columbia](http://www.disclose.tv/url/Tm9DaGFuY2VBbm9ueW1vdXNodHRwOi8vbWFwcy5nb29nbGUuY29tL21hcHM_bGw9NDkuMjYxMTExMTExMSwtMTIzLjI1MzA1NTU1NiZzcG49MC4wMSwwLjAxJnE9NDkuMjYxMTExMTExMSwtMTIzLjI1MzA1NTU1NiAoVW5pdmVyc2l0eSUyMG9mJTIwQnJpdGlzaCUyMENvbHVtYmlhKSZ0PWg.) in Canada, who was not involved in the study. "Every time we swim in the sea, we swallow about a billion viruses and inhale many thousands every day. It is true that viruses will be archived in permafrost and glacial ice, but the probability that viral pathogens of humans are abundant enough, and would circulate extensively enough to affect human health, stretches scientific rationality to the breaking point."

"I would be much more concerned about the hundreds of millions of people that will be displaced by rising sea levels than the risk of being exposed to pathogens from melting permafrost."
\[/URL\]

### nicld — 2014-03-04T03:24:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/giant-virus-resurrected-from-permafrost-after-30-000-years.9997/post-113274>

Very interesting, I was just thinking of this very thing today. I have a post that I am working on about this.

# Thread: The Role of the Pituitary
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-role-of-the-pituitary.10000/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### QiGuy1997 — 2014-03-04T06:07:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-role-of-the-pituitary.10000/post-113287>

Something that I've seen often is the argument that growth hormone is the youth hormone and the pituitary is the "master gland". However, some things seem to challenge this thought. First of all, reduced igf-1 (hence, lowered growth hormone) is repeatedly associated with longer life-spans. In fact, the way that calorie restriction is postulated to lengthen life-span is by lowering igf-1 pathway signalling. This effect is well documented and supported. This makes me wonder, why do we designate growth hormone as the "youth hormone" and attempt to actuallly boost it? This seems slightly backwards.
Even more shocking are the effects shown from hypophysectomy, the complete removal of the pituitary gland (I'm not actually suggesting this). A scientist named Deneckla found that the pituitary "may be responsible for a considerable part of the total 75% decline in the MOC (Minimum 02 consumption AKA metabolic rate) with age".
"Response curves to injected thyroxine indicated that immature rats were three times more responsive to thyroxine than adult rats. All the major endocrine ablations were performed in this and earlier work, and only pituitary ablation (a) restored in adults part of the responsiveness to thyroxine found in immature rats and (b) arrested the normal age-associated decrease in responsiveness to thyroxine in immature rats." [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC301501/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC301501/)

So, there appears to be a substance in the pituitary which characterizes aging by reducing the body's sensitivity to thyroid hormone and reducing the metabolic rate. This was clarified when the ablated pituitary resulted in a partial reversal of the thyroxine insensitivity and prevented the otherwise expected further reduction in thyroxine sensitivity. Deneckla went so far as to call this the DECO hormone (reducing consumption of oxygen hormone) and that "a newly described pituitary factor is used as an example of a possible endocrine mechanism by which the body might control it's own demise".

Furthering on this research, a scientist named Everitt performed a series of full pituitary removals on rats. He found that pituitary removal reduced age and obesity related diseases such as proteinuria and renal histopathology. It also prevented the atrophication of the hind legs of old male Wistar rats.

My point here is not to make any particular claims. I simply want to open up a discussion on the broader role of the pituitary and perhaps growth hormone/igf-1 with relation to aging. Perhaps classifying growth hormone, and the pituitary gland, as simple youth promoters is a mistake. Is it necessarily the DECO, death orchestrating hormone? Maybe, I'm not sure. I'm certainly not going to pursue increasing my pituitary function in light of some of this unresolved and contradictory information. I'm just interested in opening up this discussion.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: NFC phones
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nfc-phones.10001/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-04T11:34:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nfc-phones.10001/post-113311>

I noticed on both trains I used this morning panels where passengers could buy goods and so on. There were those images that open URLs by means of scanning them with their phone's camera. What I hadn't seen before was anything quite like these: they also had large white circles. "Tap here with your NFC phone," it said. My conclusion: here's another technology.

I googled "NFC phone":

> You've been the victim not of theft, but of the future – a future where the wallet, purse, paper ticket and pocket have all gone digital and live solely on your phone.
>
> Welcome to Near Field Communications (NFC), a contactless, Wi-Fi-lite style tech that could already be in your smartphone, and could soon be a regular feature of your commute. ...
>
> The technology is simple. It's a short-range, low power wireless link evolved from radio-frequency identification (RFID) tech that can transfer small amounts of data between two devices held a few centimeters from each other.

[http://www.techradar.com/news/phone...hat-is-nfc-and-why-is-it-in-your-phone-948410](http://www.techradar.com/news/phone-and-communications/what-is-nfc-and-why-is-it-in-your-phone-948410)

It's an interesting illustration of the times we live in, isn't it? I'm not suggesting this particular technology is dangerous: I don't know enough about it. But--suddenly--it's there will-nilly. And if it should turn out not to be a good idea ... well, there's no opt-out. I hadn't even heard of it before today. This is the speed new technologies are appearing at.

### Lahelada — 2014-03-04T13:50:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nfc-phones.10001/post-113329>

What Spinergie said. I have a smartphone but it really is a dumb generic not capable of more than internet and what a phone is supposed to do. Even if I can trick it out I am surely not going to. All over sudden I see virtue in queuing...

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-04T15:07:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nfc-phones.10001/post-113336>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=113315)
>
> You can buy wallets that block signals ...

I've seen those wallets -- I saw one with aluminium threads woven into the material. The claim against which they're sold is that there are already people out there with RFID readers scanning people's credit and debit cards to steal their IDs. I wonder if there is, and if so how much of it is going on. Passports have contained RFID chips for some time.

### sooperb — 2014-03-04T15:58:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nfc-phones.10001/post-113348>

From here: [http://www.cba.ca/en/consumer-infor.../622-contactless-payment-card-security-an-faq](http://www.cba.ca/en/consumer-information/42-safeguarding-your-money/622-contactless-payment-card-security-an-faq)

**Should I be concerned about security of contactless payment cards?**
No. Contactless card transactions are processed through the same secure networks used for all other Visa, MasterCard and Interac transactions. Your card never leaves your hand and each transaction has a unique, encrypted code that changes every time the card is used.

There have been news reports recently about electronic pick-pocketing, where a criminal with a card reader or smartphone can read the information on contactless cards and commit fraud. It’s important to know that contactless cards are embedded with multiple layers of security to protect you, so the chances of you becoming the victim of this type of fraud are extremely unlikely. These security features include:

-   **Short range** – Contactless cards can only work within short range of a retail terminal, which makes it difficult for criminals to gain access to card information from a distance. Even if they could, the stolen card data cannot be used to create a counterfeit card capable of being used for fraud.
-   **Encryption** – Contactless cards do not use the same RFID technology typically used for inventory management that just transmit information, but instead use the much more secure international EMV chip standards and advanced cryptography. During a transaction, the card and the terminal communicate with each other, doing security checks and transmitting a unique encryption code, which expires after the transaction is finished. If someone was able to get close enough to steal data from your card, they would not be able to use the encryption code because it would have expired.
-   **Limited information** – The information transmitted during a contactless transaction is very limited and includes things like language preference, card number and other coding. The customer’s name, bank account number and the three-digit security code on the back of your credit card are not transmitted during a contactless transaction.
-   **Low transaction limits** – Contactless cards generally have low transaction limits – typically around $50 – and any larger purchase will require you to enter your PIN. If you card is lost, this will prevent large purchases from being made.
-   **Zero liability** – Visa, MasterCard and Interac all have zero liability policies for credit and debit card holders. In cases of fraud, you won’t be held responsible and will get your money back.

However, this article says differently:
[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/finance/...ess-card-could-be-hacked-by-mobile-phone.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/finance/personalfinance/borrowing/creditcards/10095303/Your-contactless-card-could-be-hacked-by-mobile-phone.html)

# Thread: A bread that isn't a bread
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-bread-that-isnt-a-bread.10006/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-03-04T15:24:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-bread-that-isnt-a-bread.10006/post-113337>

A recipe sent to me recently that is from a lady called Kelley Herring. It sounds amazing.

**Paleo Herb & Sun-Dried Tomato Focaccia Bread Recipe**
(low-carb, gluten-free, high fiber and healthy fats)

Servings: 12
Active Mixing Time: 10 minutes
Total Time: 50 minutes

Ingredients

• 5 pasture-raised eggs (or organic, omega-3 eggs)
• 1 pack [Let's Do Organic Creamed Coconut](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00113ZZ5U/ref=as_li_ss_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=390957&creativeASIN=B00113ZZ5U&linkCode=as2&tag=truthaboutabs-20) (key ingredient; \*details below about this)
• ¼ cup organic virgin coconut oil, melted (or extra virgin olive oil)
• ½ Tbsp. dried organic basil (an Italian herb blend and/or rosemary would be delicious too)
• 2 tsp. organic garlic powder (to taste)
• ¼ cup organic Parmesan cheese, divided
• 5 organic sun-dried tomatoes packed in olive oil, sliced (try Mediterranean Organic)
• ½ tsp. Sea Salt
• ¾ tsp. baking soda

\* For the "Let's Do Organic Creamed Coconut", this may not be in every grocery store, but I found it in Whole Foods, or you can get it at Amazon. This is VERY different than just coconut cream or milk, as this contains the fiber from coconuts too, which is key to making this recipe come out properly.

Preparation

1\. Soften creamed coconut by placing packet in a bowl of warm water. Work with your hands to create a pourable consistency.
2\. Preheat oven to 300 F and lightly grease a 9" round cake pan (preferably stoneware).
3\. Add eggs, melted coconut oil, creamed coconut, sea salt and baking soda to a blender. Blend until very smooth (no lumps!).
4\. Pour batter into a medium mixing bowl and fold in herbs and parm cheese (if using).
5\. Pour into greased pan. Top with sliced sun-dried tomatoes and a little extra parm cheese, if using.
6\. Transfer to oven and bake 40-45 minutes until golden brown and firm.
7\. Serve with a dipping oil made with high quality organic extra virgin olive oil, minced garlic and herbs, to taste.

Nutrition Information Per Serving:
192 calories, 18 grams healthy fats, 6 grams carbohydrate (mostly fiber), 3 grams protein

This is a perfect bread for diabetics since there's no blood sugar impact hardly at all, and also just good for anyone looking to control blood sugar and cravings for the fat loss and appetite control effects.

### caroline — 2014-03-05T01:24:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-bread-that-isnt-a-bread.10006/post-113463>

This looks very, very nice ..... The trouble for me - I would eat half of it at one go! serves 12??? not in my world!

I have to stay away from stuff like this ......

Are you going to make it Soo?

### sooperb — 2014-03-08T00:39:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-bread-that-isnt-a-bread.10006/post-114058>

I keep meaning too, it's the 5 eggs lol, I like to eat them and five seems a lot for a pseudo bread recipe. Plus the recipe is very specific about the creamed coconut which I'm not sure I can get.

### Lahelada — 2014-03-08T01:54:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-bread-that-isnt-a-bread.10006/post-114067>

Soo,down here at the end of teh world I do not get most things . The coconut milk they are referring too sounds like coconut milk with rehydrated desiccated coconut to me. I make my own coconut milk quite often when fatty desiccated coconut is available and one thing you learn is that the coconut flour everyone buys is just the fibre leftover from making the milk.So if you whizz the fibre in a mill and dry it you have coconut flour. For you recipe you just ue the desiccated cc nut as is in conjunction with commercially available cc milk.

### sooperb — 2014-03-22T21:33:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-bread-that-isnt-a-bread.10006/post-117228>

### cantweight — 2014-03-04T19:25:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sensation-of-hot-water-running-down-leg.10012/post-113390>

What the fresh hell is this...
Feels like hot liquid running from right buttock down thigh to back of knee.

My poor pup that was on the couch behind me... I jumped up thinking she peed!

Googled it and got parasthesia...not much info to gather. It's happened probably 25-30 times since this morning.

What gives???

### kovita — 2014-03-04T19:32:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sensation-of-hot-water-running-down-leg.10012/post-113392>

i have paresthesias regulary. Some weeks few some weeks more often. It is not very good sign, I think, it is connected with a whole bunch of chronic diseases. I have observed, that I get much more of it when I seem to be overhelmed by toxins. The clearing usually presents itself by moving deep electricity like feelings closer to the skin and it eventually clears as itchy feelings on the skin and than it disappears for some time. My "energetical "physio who works with me always connects this with relieving of deep tissue tensions and I can say that over the time I always emmerge out of this periods with a step forward in my subjective measurements of overall health. Very personal and very laic opinion here.

### cantweight — 2014-03-05T02:26:31+0200

You are totally right Kovita.... it is the toxins. The mold to be exact. Damn. I can not catch a break. Water has just started leaking from yet another light fixture in this house that I want to love so badly!! It is an amazing house and was not moldy when we moved here 18 months ago. Poor renovation work done has created a mess of water issues and we are paying the price. I was going to try to muscle through and deal with the problem as much as i could on my own but as my youngest has started stuttering again and developed nose bleeds I have no choice but to address this professionally now. ugh.

Oh well!!! At least it is something that can be addressed.....will count my blessings

### cantweight — 2016-07-20T21:35:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sensation-of-hot-water-running-down-leg.10012/post-198691>

> [Dylan848 said:](/goto/post?id=198653)
>
> Hey was just wondering if your symptoms improved and if so what helped?

I handled the situation very poorly if i am being honest. I thought I could focus on improving my redox, and muscle through the situation in our home...I regret that. Many wise people advised I have the house cleared of any issues and I did not heed their advice.

I pushed hard with a seafood based ketogenic diet, very very little carbohydrate, practiced CT and adhered to strict light blocking and sunning. I got worse....far worse. So did my family.

2 years since I had made this initial post I declined severely in energy, gained a lot of weight and was in much pain...nothing showed on my labs other than odd things like high C4a, slightly elevated hscrp. My skin (face) became covered in lesions that bled and pussed and burned relentlessly...no dermatologists could figure out what it was...many medications were prescribed, the last one being for freaking leprosy....nothing worked.

I broke down this past spring, told my husband I was fearful for what would come next as the rash began taking over my eyes. I told him to find a way to get me out of this house or fix it...fast.

We brought in experts and they found the house to be unsafe and told us any babies or elderly in the house must leave immediately...no babies or elderly just us for 3 fucking yrs in this mess. They found the HVAC in the attic was not hooked up properly and our air was full of fiberglass...one of the gentlemen that came said he'd seen someone with fiberglass in their air have the exact same facial rash i did. There were mold issues, mouse feces...every place we looked a new and even more expensive problem.

Our HVAC systems were repaired and UV light systems and high grade filters were installed on both, much drywall was replaced and repaired, windows that harbored a shocking amount of mold are being replaced...the house that I swore was trying to kill me is starting to feel like a home again. I actually have the desire to decorate and hang pictures where as previously I only had the desire to move again.

My whole family went back to our old doctor that I love...she is helping us improve our redox and as it improves helping us to slowly detoxify. I have been getting uv light IV treatments...they are amazing...after 2 treatments my skin was almost human again. My energy is increasing by the day. I respect that my body has a hierarchy at which it will address its states...I am simply supporting it now, not pushing too hard, and appreciating this rapid turn around.

Find what is in your environment driving your inflammatory symptoms...

>
> Find what is in your environment driving your inflammatory symptoms...

Great to hear you and your family's recovery!

Given the chance and better environment, our body can thrive.

Thanks for sharing.

# Thread: Viruses and bacteria and the human race
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viruses-and-bacteria-and-the-human-race.10013/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nicld — 2014-03-04T21:03:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viruses-and-bacteria-and-the-human-race.10013/post-113409>

I was already thinking about this post when Fitness@home posted yesterday about a new virus that has emerged and what other ones are laying dormant.

I am reading a book The Story of the Human Body (Evolution, Health and Disease) by Daniel E Lieberman on human evolution and started going back and reading the brain-gut series and a thought has popped into the head. The world is so obsessed with conserving life and not improving life are we ultimately hurting the human race by NOT letting bacteria and viruses force the genes to adapt for survival? In the book he talks about evolutionary mismatch and how the human genes are not able to keep up with our environment. In Brain gut 2, Jack talked about how viruses caused the body to make antibodies to survive and pass that trait on to the next generation. Those that do not make it do not pass that weak gene on. Maybe cancers and bacteria and viruses are around to strengthen the human genes and by us messing with it so much we are ultimately creating a weaker human.

I saw a documentary on the History channel back from 2005 call “The Plague” about the bubonic plague. It said that millions of people died from it but there were those who were unaffected even when in contact with the sick. These people then passed that gene on to the next generation. They started to look into why some people can be exposed to HIV and never develop AIDS and they found that their genetic history seems to point to the fact that their ancestors may have been ones that survived the plague and passed some genes down to them.

It is no longer survival of the fittest and natural selection but in some cases who has the most money. Don’t get me wrong, people dying early and of cancers and other diseases is horrible to see and so very painful for those that are left behind but is what we are doing now going to make this even worse for future generations (if we do not kill ourselves first from obesity and other mismatched diseases).

We are already seeing the effect with sicker children due to the over use of antibiotics and trying to keep everything sterile. Kids need to play in the dirt (how cow manure as I was told I did) and get their bodies exposed to all sorts of stuff to build up their immunities. I remember finding sand in our first daughters diaper and she had not been outside that day. She used to shovel spoonful of sand into her mouth.

Are we harming future generations by not allowing sickness and disease to make the human race stronger? Isn’t disease a way of population control? Just a thought.

### Josh — 2014-03-04T21:22:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viruses-and-bacteria-and-the-human-race.10013/post-113412>

Watch this and look at the powerpoint:

[http://www.waterconf.org/participan.../Luc Montagnier Borovets October 23, 2013.ppt](http://www.waterconf.org/participants-materials/presentations/Luc%20Montagnier%20Borovets%20October%2023,%202013.ppt)

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-04T21:56:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viruses-and-bacteria-and-the-human-race.10013/post-113418>

I'm immune to Norovirus. My cousin is working in Africa on AIDS - particularly the individuals who have developed immunity against the virus.... they're trying to figure out how it happened. I sent her the BG series... but when she broached the concept with the CDC folks the told her it doesn't happen that way.... She's still questioning their thought process and paradigms....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-05T09:28:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/viruses-and-bacteria-and-the-human-race.10013/post-113528>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=113409)
>
> We are already seeing the effect with sicker children due to the over use of antibiotics and trying to keep everything sterile. Kids need to play in the dirt (how cow manure as I was told I did) and get their bodies exposed to all sorts of stuff to build up their immunities. I remember finding sand in our first daughters diaper and she had not been outside that day. She used to shovel spoonful of sand into her mouth.
>
> Are we harming future generations by not allowing sickness and disease to make the human race stronger? Isn’t disease a way of population control? Just a thought.

I think there are two different thoughts mixed up there, Nicole.

First, there's the thought that hyper-cleanliness is actually bad for us. (_That's a familiar thought now, and I'm sure it's true. Besides, the suggestion you make there's also the growing recognition that many bacteria are benign. Using some kind of powerful agent that kills everything -- a "scorched earth policy" -- can therefore be counter-productive. Completely harmless bacteria had been occupying space on your hands or in your mouth or gut or wherever. If you wipe them out along with the possibly harmful bugs, you can't know in advance what will expand to fill the space you've just made. It may be something harmful._)

Secondly, you kind of segue over to the find thought: "Isn’t disease a way of population control?"

This is coming out of the same general area -- thoughts about disease and how we cope with it, but it's not the _same_ thought. I think this one can take people into very dangerous areas. (I do think the relief of suffering is a duty we have.) It comes out of taking the notion of "the survival of the fittest" too seriously and too schematically. It's a hypothesis for explaining where we've come from; not a guide to action. It's comes from Darwinian biology (and the roots of it are in Malthus). Darwin himself tended to avoid the notion that natural selection could and ought to be applied socially. I say tended: sometimes people more eager to defend his reputation than well-informed say he always eschewed this; in fact, there are occasions when he didn't. But mostly he did. The person this is approach is strongly connected with is his cousin Francis Galton.

This is nowadays known as "Social Darwinism". Suffice to say, some 19th century gentleman were terribly worried that perhaps the poor, whom they assumed were less "fit", were reproducing too fast and should be discouraged. There were even people who thought that charitable relief should not be provided for the starving, this being supposedly an interference with "nature" (if not "the market").

These ideas get around. IIRC, as late as the 1970s Sweden had a pretty gung-ho policy of sterilising the "unfit" -- and who counted as unfit is something we'd best not go into here.

I can't go into it deeply here, but this whole notion contains a logical fallacy. Briefly, it's this: according to the theory, evolution by natural selection is what happens _always and everywhere_. There's something self-contradictory and ridiculous in saying: this is what always and everywhere holds … oh, and let's give it a helping hand. The Australian philosopher David Stove gives an in-depth discussion of this, and some other problems, that pop up in biological thinking here:

[http://www.amazon.com/Darwinian-Fairytales-Selfish-Heredity-Evolution/dp/1594032009/](http://www.amazon.com/Darwinian-Fairytales-Selfish-Heredity-Evolution/dp/1594032009/)

There has been a recent BBC podcast on the theme of Social Darwinism where Melvyn Bragg talks with three academics. I haven't heard it yet myself, but it's probably worth a listen for anyone who's interested in these themes:

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/b03vgq1q](http://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/b03vgq1q)

It should be in iTunes or in the web in the BBC iPlayer.

# Thread: shivering and mitochondria
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering-and-mitochondria.10017/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Julie5 — 2014-03-05T00:59:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering-and-mitochondria.10017/post-113451>

I just read in Ask Jack that shivering after CT is a sign of poor mito. So do I just stay the course: continue CT iin 55 deg water for 45 min until it gets better? Or cut back? I shiver in the water some, but the shivering afterward is worse and lasts longer than the 45 min I was in the water.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-05T01:05:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering-and-mitochondria.10017/post-113454>

Everyone shivers. I've been having such a hard time with CT this winter - I've found I shiver just in my house (we live in an old 1950s post war construction w/ single pane windows and the warmest part of the house may be in the 60's - the rest of the house is in the 50s!) hang in there... you can try backing the temp off, or go for a shorter period. When I'm in my CT groove I prefer colder temps. Though right now I'm fighting some really temp challenges. We all take a few steps forward, and a few steps back....

### nonchalant — 2014-03-05T01:07:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering-and-mitochondria.10017/post-113456>

Maybe the Mitochondrial Rx will help? I shiver also, for a lot longer than I was in the tub, unless I'm outside basking in the sun afterwards.

### nicld — 2014-03-05T03:47:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering-and-mitochondria.10017/post-113485>

> [Julie5 said:](/goto/post?id=113451)
>
> I just read in Ask Jack that shivering after CT is a sign of poor mito. So do I just stay the course: continue CT iin 55 deg water for 45 min until it gets better? Or cut back? I shiver in the water some, but the shivering afterward is worse and lasts longer than the 45 min I was in the water.

I think I asked that question or at least something like it. I used to get full body shakes when I got out but I no longer shiver like I used to and now can do deep CT. It will get better most of the time.

Gretchen, when did you start on the Lithium Orotate? I have been finding not dealing with the cold well the past couple of days and I just started it.

### Don — 2014-03-05T14:13:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering-and-mitochondria.10017/post-113569>

Stay the course ... When I started CT, 5-10 minutes in 55 degree water would leave me shivering uncontrollably for 2-3 hours. It stayed that way for a long time. About a year or so into CT I felt the heater turn-on. Now the shivering, after a much longer CT at cooler temps, is almost nonexistent. As your body adapts to what Jack is teaching us, I have found playing with water and fat intake helps. It apparently takes a while for our bodies to adapt and be able to uncouple the electron transport chain to get the heater to kick in. There are no small details on this path, and all of them need to be tested on your n=1.

# Thread: Acupressure mat, WOW!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/acupressure-mat-wow.10021/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### margor_j — 2014-03-05T08:42:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/acupressure-mat-wow.10021/post-113525>

hey,
So, after telling caroline i wouldnt use one cos i didnt think they would do anything for me (there was a thread started by caroline earlier, because dave asprey is now manufacturing 'acupressure mats') but i had a look on the inet, and found them available in NZ - the brand is 'shanti'. $40 a piece, what do i have to lose!

Ok, im the first to admit im not using it every nite, but also, its not nearly as painful as it looks - or feels. touching it definitely hurts more than lying on it!

What really blew me away though, was taking it away to my last race and the boys all saw it at some point in the first couple of days (I had 8-9 riders for these races). So they were intrigued when I gave a speel, and all tried it. typical boys - shirts off and straight into it. And they all LOVED it! Bloody hell, every day my mat was disappearing so they could do sessions on it, or id find someone passed out on it on my floor.. lol .. (i do the massages in my room ,so its kinda an open door policy!)
But the token insomniac is the one that blew me away - it was like he had been drugged, within minutes of getting on it. this was early afternoon - he went and slept for 1.5 hours afterwards, he was struggling to hold a conversation. then, that nite, he crashed out from 9pm to 6am - one of his best sleeps ever! WTF! thats awesome aye! they pretty much all want to buy one now cos they found it so nice.

SO, i will perserve and get better at using it! it is definitely relaxing! I would recommend them, if youre thinking about it..

MG

# Thread: (Book) Decoding the Human Body-Field: The New Science of Information as Medicine
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/book-decoding-the-human-body-field-the-new-science-of-information-as-medicine.10023/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-05T10:58:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/book-decoding-the-human-body-field-the-new-science-of-information-as-medicine.10023/post-113536>

Today I found out this book on Google Books while looking for some info on cell metabolism and stuff like that. I've been quite impressed to find out that it talks a lot about some of the hot topics that we cover here, and the author seems to realize the role of phyisics on the human body and mentions a lot quantum physics. It also presents the data in very undestandable way (as far as I have read). Here's an small excerpt:

_"The human body-field is a self-organizing, self-directed, intelli-
gent system that directs the information flow of the body, information
that is crucial to the genetic, chemical, and physiological processes of
the body. To understand the NES model of the body-field you have
to unlearn biology, in a sense. You have to put aside your focus on the
diagnosis of disease based on symptomology. Symptoms are a conse-
quence of the breakdown of biochemistry. However, chemistry is driven
by the interaction of subatomic particles and waves. The root cause of
disease, as most complementary practitioners will tell you, is at the level
of the energy and information of your body."
_​

I see a lot of comments on JK podcasts and blog entries about the work from people like Robert O. Becker or Pollack about water, but can't recall having heard about this book. I wonder how much of his thinking might be influnced by Frasier & Massier and some of these people that call this the nutri-energetics system (NES)? as it is massively tied.

​

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-05T11:01:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/book-decoding-the-human-body-field-the-new-science-of-information-as-medicine.10023/post-113538>

Oh great, here's the full book:
[http://www.hekli.se/wp-content/uploads/2012/11/Decoding-the-HBF.pdf](http://www.hekli.se/wp-content/uploads/2012/11/Decoding-the-HBF.pdf)

### Lahelada — 2014-03-06T03:53:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tanzanian-sprats.10025/post-113701>

I can actually do these if they are spratlings but the ones I find here are huge so I am not that enamoured with them.( I still have not got over my phooia of eating something that stares back at me from the plate , insolent food for short) When I was in Italy they were tiny beasts that tasted real good. I will scout for some. With Ea ster now approaching and this being aCatholic country the fish supply must improve.

# Thread: can detox present itself as paresthesia?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-detox-present-itself-as-paresthesia.10026/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kovita — 2014-03-05T15:09:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-detox-present-itself-as-paresthesia.10026/post-113576>

guys, I am neverending source of questions! I happen to get one of my old lyme bothers more often now. Better said I had a lot of paresthesias in feets and hands and big no blood feelings in my hands during the sleep, the worst during my initial doxycyclin tratment. This eventually got much better over the time. Now after lets say 2 months of consistent CT I seem to be unevitable going to be one of the dominant problems again, it is more is is affecting the whole right leg, that is new. It came back slowly and it is definitely intensifying. Any thoughts? Should I get back to neurologist asking for brain/spine MRI ( a year back it was completely clean)? Should I just stay calm and assume it is part of recovery or cleaning? Anyone with similar experience?

### Lahelada — 2014-03-05T15:31:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-detox-present-itself-as-paresthesia.10026/post-113578>

How about similar but different? I used to wake up with numb hands , found things difficult to grip . I suspected night shades ,arthritis you name it. Nomoney to throw at the problem so I just did what i could namely a very high fat diet. I have s avery faint stiffness in the joints of the hands now but the symptoms disappeared as soon as ketosis took hold. If you are not living ketogenically it might be worth looking into. Fibro at least from what I remember responds well to it.
If you have not read these yet
[http://jackkruse.com/quantum-biology-4-metabolic-syndrome/](http://jackkruse.com/quantum-biology-4-metabolic-syndrome/)
[http://proactivemed.org/881/center-...kruse-md-readers-summary1-why-is-lyme-misund/](http://proactivemed.org/881/center-for-proactive-medicine-facebook-wall/amazing-read-written-by-jack-kruse-md-readers-summary1-why-is-lyme-misund/)

### kovita — 2014-03-05T15:34:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-detox-present-itself-as-paresthesia.10026/post-113580>

I am in and out of ketosis for a few months now. It seems like it is not a big player for me :-( I starteda. diary to make some sense of it and even with some proper analysis, ketosis is not implied in my paresthesias. Thanks for your thoughts!

# Thread: Meat and exercise are "the key to aging well"
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-exercise-are-the-key-to-aging-well.10027/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-05T15:37:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-exercise-are-the-key-to-aging-well.10027/post-113581>

> Protein and muscle-building exercise could be the recipe for staying spritely in old age, according to a new study.
>
> The protein and exercise combination may reduce the risk of falls and improve mental function, say Deakin University researchers.
>
> A first phase of their study shows marked muscle-building benefits for women aged 60 to 90 who ate a diet high in lean red meat and did strength training.
>
> Those on a high-protein meat diet achieved an 18 per cent greater increase in muscle strength than a control group who ate normally. ...

[http://health.msn.co.nz/healthnews/8806547/ageing-meat-eaters-pack-on-muscle-study](http://health.msn.co.nz/healthnews/8806547/ageing-meat-eaters-pack-on-muscle-study)

Rather begs the question of what's meant by "normal". Anyway, good to see some sense in this debate.

### Tanya — 2014-03-05T21:25:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-exercise-are-the-key-to-aging-well.10027/post-113637>

another study: [http://www.washingtonpost.com/natio...f0603e-a3b5-11e3-8466-d34c451760b9\_story.html](http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/too-much-protein-could-lead-to-early-death-study-says/2014/03/04/0af0603e-a3b5-11e3-8466-d34c451760b9_story.html)

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-05T21:54:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-exercise-are-the-key-to-aging-well.10027/post-113642>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=113637)
>
> another study: [http://www.washingtonpost.com/natio...f0603e-a3b5-11e3-8466-d34c451760b9\_story.html](http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/too-much-protein-could-lead-to-early-death-study-says/2014/03/04/0af0603e-a3b5-11e3-8466-d34c451760b9_story.html)

Yeah. That one's doing the rounds now.

Usual caveats apply. It's a questionnaire for a start. Andreas Eenfeldt has some pertinent criticisms. Someone also remarks wittily in his comments that:

> Just the average of _"1,823 calories"_ a day for the USA, ought to start cautionary bells ringing about how reliable this data could possibly be...

[http://www.dietdoctor.com/dangerous-eat-meat-age-65](http://www.dietdoctor.com/dangerous-eat-meat-age-65)

Mind you, I do wonder if the occasional low-protein day (or day of total fasting) could be a healthy practice. As I understand it, it should encourage autophagy. Established practice in Orthodox countries, like Russia, of course, isn't it? In Western Europe we kind of gave up fasting gradually -- back in the Middle Ages they said fish counted as fasting, then that various birds and whatnot counted as fish, then dropped out most of the seasons for fasting, and finally dispensed with it altogether after the Reformation in Protestant countries. Maybe not a good idea ...

### Tanya — 2014-03-05T22:27:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-exercise-are-the-key-to-aging-well.10027/post-113648>

Being strict vegetarian was one of the diets that I tried at one time...I just pretty much ignore all these studies these days. So liberating to follow the diet I am on now and not to have any cravings

### caroline — 2014-03-06T02:30:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-exercise-are-the-key-to-aging-well.10027/post-113688>

Having no cravings and no hunger beats the crap out of anything else ..... in my opinion of course!

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-06T11:39:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-exercise-are-the-key-to-aging-well.10027/post-113744>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=113648)
>
> Being strict vegetarian was one of the diets that I tried at one time...I just pretty much ignore all these studies these days. So liberating to follow the diet I am on now and not to have any cravings

Nor hunger as Audrey says.

As Taubes points out, it's an apparent paradox that you get hungrier eating carbs than eating nothing. I can't remember when I was last hungry. It wouldn't bother me if I did miss a meal -- I don't think I'd even notice -- but I enjoy Epi-paleo food so much I usually don't.

As you imply, one can't run around like a headless chicken every time one of these "studies" comes out. It's just an observational study, anyway. I wish the media would put these things in the bin when they're sent them. It was a questionnaire for goodness sake! As someone points out at Andreas's blog this (surely mythical) average consumption of 1,823 kcals/day worked out on average to 51% of carbohydrate and 16% of protein. So, he says, they blame the protein! They know what they want to to "find" before they start is what it amounts to. They also added in some data on mice. As an Epi-paleoist I'd prefer data on Great White Sharks, but never mind ...

I did the arithmetic.

1,826 x 16% = 292 kcal

There are 4 kcal per gram of protein

292 / 4 = 73 grams

So 73 g of protein a day are going to give me cancer? I'm in trouble then: I should think I regularly eat 100 to 110g a day and sometimes more. Except I'm not.

It's balderdash! We know that medical missionaries in the nineteenth century -- people like Schweitzer -- said that there was virtually no cancer among native peoples eating their traditional foods. It came in with what Weston Price called "the displacing foods of modern commerce".

They're just putting out rubbish science. Apart from the fact that you can drive a bus through the massive holes in their assumptions, what they say can be immediately refuted by looking at other sources of evidence. I think people in the biological sciences are in severe danger of becoming regarded as clowns by real scientists. I haven't forgotten the physicicst who remarked to Robert O. Becker, when a biologist had had an experiment demonstrated to him and wouldn't believe it because it went against his preconceptions, "Biologists are the worst."

# Thread: bldy voltage meter grounding help
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bldy-voltage-meter-grounding-help.10036/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kovita — 2014-03-06T10:27:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bldy-voltage-meter-grounding-help.10036/post-113736>

guys, after the yesterday's webinar I am angry, schocked and ready to take steps. I did some preliminary research this morning and it seems like I have no company around to do the measurements for me. body voltage meter seems to be the best straightforward approach in my situation. I live in Vienna (Austria) in city center in the second floor of a typical townhouse from turn on 19/20.century. i am not skilled to use the fuse and the administrator of the building is not able to confirm the water tubing is made from metal in it's whole lenght. My house is surrounded by pavement and concrete. Some windows face patio, where we have big panting pots filled with clay (photo attached). Could I use it as a 0v refference?

### Martin — 2014-03-06T15:25:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bldy-voltage-meter-grounding-help.10036/post-113766>

kovita, I looked at your picture and I have doubts as to whether you can use the planter to establish an ground/earth connection to be able to accurately check any voltage going through your body. I know there is dirt in the planters, and the planters and patio seem to be made of concrete, but my doubt is whether the voltage could travel through the concrete easily to give you and accurate reading. I am comparing it, of course, with actual earth. I have heard that concrete can be okay for grounding with bare feet, so there may be a chance. You would have to do a side-by-side test to see. I suppose a very long wire with a metal rod attached to one end could be run all the way to actual soil would by okay, but I don't know if it's practical in your area.

# Thread: Fish consumption aids in heart health
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-consumption-aids-in-heart-health.10037/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-06T13:04:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-consumption-aids-in-heart-health.10037/post-113750>

Quite a nice article. This turns out to be a five-year international study and funded by the NIH. The sample isn't large, but it is also clearly stated:

> ... a larger clinical trial would be necessary to confirm those results.

The following seems significant, in one way or the other, and worth highlighting even if it turns out not to be the fish (or not only that):

> It included 175 middle-aged Japanese men and 113 middle-aged Caucasian Americans. Japanese-American men in the study were found to have levels of coronary artery calcification -- the accumulation of calcified plaques in coronary arteries -- proved to be as high or even higher than Caucasian American men, revealing the lower rate of the calcification among Japanese men is not genetically based.

[http://www.post-gazette.com/news/he...-health-Pitt-study-finds/stories/201403050054](http://www.post-gazette.com/news/health/2014/03/05/Fish-consumption-aids-in-heart-health-Pitt-study-finds/stories/201403050054)

# Thread: Otago researchers gain new insights into ancient Pacific settlers’ diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/otago-researchers-gain-new-insights-into-ancient-pacific-settlers%E2%80%99-diet.10046/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-06T19:50:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/otago-researchers-gain-new-insights-into-ancient-pacific-settlers%E2%80%99-diet.10046/post-113805>

It sounds like a pretty good diet …

> Researchers from New Zealand’s University of Otago studying 3000-year-old skeletons from the oldest known cemetery in the Pacific Islands are casting new light on the diet and lives of the enigmatic Lapita people, the likely ancestors of Polynesians.
>
> Their results—obtained from analysing stable isotope ratios of three elements in the bone collagen of 49 adults buried at the Teouma archaeological site on Vanuatu’s Efate Island—suggest that its early Lapita settlers ate reef fish, marine turtles, fruit bats, free-range pigs and chickens, rather than primarily relying on growing crops for human food and animal fodder. ...

I don't know what fruit bat tastes like, but I'd go with a diet like that!

[http://www.otago.ac.nz/news/news/otago066271.html](http://www.otago.ac.nz/news/news/otago066271.html)

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0090376](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0090376)

**Posts:** 16
