### Shijin13 — 2014-03-13T18:42:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-lab-offers-tests-for-10-and-only-1-drop-of-blood-needed.10131/post-115208>

Here's my dream sheet. I try to run these ever quarter (CPT codes are the same for Labcorp and Quest)
Lipid Panel - VAP, NMR or Berkley or HDL Labs
hsCRP Test Number: 120766 CPT Code: 86141
HbA1C Test Number: 001453 CPT Code: 83036
Fasting blood glucose Test # 001818, CPT Code 82947
CBC Test # 005009, CPT Code 85025
Comp Metabolic Panel Test # 322000, CPT code 80053
Thyroid:
TSH, Test # 004259, CPT Code 84443
Free T4, Test # 001974, CPT code 84439
Free T3, Test # 010389, CTP Code 84481
RT3, Test # 070104, CPT 84482
Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) Antibodies Test Number: 006676 CPT Code: 86376
Thyroglobulin, Comprehensive (With Anti-Tg Screen and Reflex to RIA) (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500540 CPT Code: Call client services.
Progesterone, Test # 004312, CPT Code 84144
Estradiol, Sensitive, Test # 140244, CPT Code 82670
Total Estrogens Test# 004549, CPT Code 82672
SHGB Test #082016 CPT Code: 84270
Testosterone Free Direct, & serum with Total Testosterone: Test # 140103, CPT Codes 84402, 84403
Prolactin Test # 004465, CPT code 84146
cortisol Test # 004051, CPT Code 82533
DHEA-S Test # 004020, CPT Code 82627
IGF-1 Test #010636, CPT Code 84305
RBC magnesium Test # 080283, CPT Code 83735
Vitamin D25 Hyrdoxy Test # 081950, CPT code 82306
Serotonin Test# 120204, CPT Code 84260 (requires frozen specimens)
Pregnenolone Test# 140707, CPT Code 84140 (requires Frozen Specimens)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-14T04:15:49+0200

### JanSz — 2014-03-16T15:44:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-lab-offers-tests-for-10-and-only-1-drop-of-blood-needed.10131/post-115776>

How can improved processes actually save lives?

We’ve created a tool for physicians to look at lab-test data over time and see trends. We don’t usually think about lab data this way today. It’s “Are you in range, or are you out of range?” Instead, we like to think, “Where are you going?” If you showed me a single frame from a movie and asked me to tell you the story, I wouldn’t be able to do it. But with many frames, you can start to see the movie unfold.

Would you mind sharing details of how you have accomplished that.

So I came to this board with
Glucose, fasting(80-90)
HgA1c(5.6)
Insulin,fasting=(2-3)

after about 2 years on Epi-Paleo-Rx
Glucose, fasting(95-100)
HgA1c(5.5)
Insulin,fasting=(2-3)

Some things are right, other are not.

Biggest change from my previous diet to Epi-Paleo-Rx was
removal of most of remaining carbs
increase on fat
some animal proteins replaced with seafood

\--------------
I think the problem is with carbohydrates.
Carbohydrates is a name that accompasses to large variety of foods.
I see need to divide carbohydrate into more categories, not only high or low GI or nightshades.
Some carbohydrates are eaten by us (people)
other carbohydrates are eaten by our friendly bacteria (so they can multiply and prosper).
I see very little being said about all this. I guess this is the source of my problem.
\--------------
I eat lots of oysters
oysters
carbs-23%
Fats-33%
Protein-44%

What type carbohydrates are in oysters?
\---------------

Anyhow, please share your details to your success.

### giskard — 2014-03-19T17:45:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-lab-offers-tests-for-10-and-only-1-drop-of-blood-needed.10131/post-116436>

Background: 45 y.o. male in decent health with semi regular weight lifting and no chronic diseases.

I was never much of a soda drinker or sweets/junk eater though I ate a lot of rice and bread. About 4 years ago I discovered the "5 rules of leptin", right about discovering slight gluten sensitivity (migraines after combining wheat and alcohol, or wheat beer) I followed the rules and lost about 20 lbs which mostly stayed off for years, then relaxed said rules:
[http://www.wellnessresources.com/weight/articles/the\_five\_rules\_of\_the\_leptin\_diet/](http://www.wellnessresources.com/weight/articles/the_five_rules_of_the_leptin_diet/)
It also cured my reactive hypoglycemia. I used to have to eat every 3 hours.

About 7 months ago I noticed that my A1c over the years crept up from 5.3 to 5.6 despite the above. (I did not have A1c numbers before I started the "leptin rules") My lab fasting BG was always around 95-99.
I found this article by Chris Kresser
[http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-diabetes-and-heart-disease-for-16](http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-diabetes-and-heart-disease-for-16)
So I bought this blood glucose meter based on Consumer Reports and other reviews
[http://www.amazon.com/Freestyle-Freedom-Glucose-Monitoring-System/dp/B001A5R2FK](http://www.amazon.com/Freestyle-Freedom-Glucose-Monitoring-System/dp/B001A5R2FK)
I was shocked at how sensitive I was to starch. For example, 2 buns with a hamburger would spike me to 150; an 8 oz "sweet potato challenge test" would get me to 160. Upon waking my FBG would be 95-105.
I discovered my ferritin was in the 80s, so I started donating blood every 3 months.

After reading Jack Kruse I did the giant morning high protein breakfast, and started eating more Kerrygold butter, coconut oil, seafood, shellfish, and crustaceans. I drastically reduced my starchy food intake, so that post meal spikes were <120. I also started eating homemade kefir regularly.

After 4 months (and 2 blood donations) I got:
\- weight loss of 10 lbs
\-FBG-90-99
\-A1c 5.3
\-insulin 0.5
\-ferritin high 40s
\- 90 minutes post typical meal BG 100-110
\- most headaches gone
\- flat, even energy
\- better sleep

I was still very sensitive to starchy food. I was hoping the blood donations would improve that.
Then in early December I read Nikoley's many blogs on Resistant Starch and Bob's Red Mill Unmodified Potato Starch.
Here's a good starting summary on the topic:
[http://www.gestaltreality.com/2014/02/27/resistant-starch-a-concise-guide/](http://www.gestaltreality.com/2014/02/27/resistant-starch-a-concise-guide/)

I started immediately and ramped up to 4 tbsp per day pretty quickly (with a week of backing off due to extreme butt-wrenching flatulence). The effects were dramatic:
\- greatly reduced sensitivity to starchy meals:
\- 8 oz sweet potato challenge test peak BG: 145
\- 90 minute post typical meal: 90-95
\- feel full for my typical 5-6 hours even after a meal with some starch e.g. half cup of rice... some starch in a meal used to get me hungry again after just 3-4 hours
\- FBG went down to 70-93
\- after about 2 months, my weight had stayed flat, but my abs started coming out. I now have a 4-pack (plus a bicycle tire!) I hadn't seen them since big hair was in. Alas now I have sparse hair.
\- more lucid dreaming

That is where I'm at.

BTW there is a variable I recently discovered wrt FBG. If I have a bit of alcohol with dinner (e.g. 4 oz wine or a small martini), my next morning FBG will be 70-85. Without, it's 80-93. I wonder if this is why moderate alcohol correlates with reduced mortality. I wonder if I should take a small dose slow release metformin when I don't have alcohol with dinner.

Next steps:

This just arrived, for CT. I'm hoping it will rid my spare bicycle tire  :
[https://www.coolfatburner.com/](https://www.coolfatburner.com/)

I just started AOR probiotics in case some species are extinct in my gut (I have had around 5 antibiotic courses in my life, including Chloramphenicol for Typhoid in my early teens.):
[http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2014/03/health-diagrams-ii-curing-autoimmunity.html](http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2014/03/health-diagrams-ii-curing-autoimmunity.html)
After 1-2 months of that I plan to try Prescript Assist soil based probiotics.

I'm also now looking for homemade style kimchi, perhaps from a small Korean mom and pop store. I just went through a jar of Costco kimchi.

# Thread: Probiotics article in BJN
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-article-in-bjn.10133/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-03-13T00:41:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-article-in-bjn.10133/post-115077>

Study: Probiotics Help Women Slim Down

Specific probiotics could help women lose weight and keep it off, according to a recent study published in the British Journal of Nutrition by a team of researchers at Canada’s Université Laval.

Studies have already demonstrated that the intestinal flora of obese individuals differs from that of thin people. That difference may be due to the fact that a diet high in fat and low in fiber promotes certain bacteria at the expense of others. Professor Angelo Tremblay and his team tried to determine if the consumption of probiotics could help reset the balance of the intestinal microbiota in favor of bacteria that promote a healthy weight.
Sponsor

To test their hypothesis, researchers recruited 125 overweight men and women. The subjects underwent a 12-week weight-loss diet, followed by a 12-week period aimed at maintaining body weight. Throughout the entire study, half the participants swallowed two pills daily containing probiotics from the Lactobacillus rhamnosus family, while the other half received a placebo.

After the 12-week diet period, researchers observed an average weight loss of 9.7 pounds in women in the probiotic group and 5.7 pounds in the placebo group. However, no differences in weight loss were observed among males in the two groups. "We don't know why the probiotics didn't have any effect on men. It may be a question of dosage, or the study period may have been too short," says Tremblay, who is also the Canada Research Chair in Environment and Energy Balance.

After the 12-week maintenance period, the weight of the women in the placebo group had remained stable but the probiotic group had continued to lose weight, for a total of 11.5 pounds per person. In short, women consuming probiotics lost twice as much weight over the 24-week period of the study. Researchers also noted a drop in the appetite-regulating hormone leptin in this group, as well as a lower overall concentration of the intestinal bacteria related to obesity.

According to Tremblay, probiotics may act by altering the permeability of the intestinal wall. By keeping certain proinflammatory molecules from entering the bloodstream, they might help prevent the chain reaction that leads to glucose intolerance, Type 2 diabetes, and obesity.

The Lactobacillus rhamnosus strain used in this study belongs to Nestlé, which uses it in certain yogurts it makes for the European market, but Tremblay believes that the probiotics found in dairy products in North America could have a similar effect to the Nestlé strain. He stresses, however, that the benefits of these bacteria are more likely to be observed in a favorable nutritional context that promotes low fat and adequate fiber intake.

© 2014 NewsmaxHealth. All rights reserved.

Oh dear. At first glance it seemed a favourable article ...............

# Thread: The CFB, BAT, & PET scans, oh my!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-cfb-bat-pet-scans-oh-my.10139/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eric — 2014-03-13T03:12:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-cfb-bat-pet-scans-oh-my.10139/post-115118>

In the latest experiment, I showed that the Cool Fat Burner could activate brown fat, as verified by PET scans.

[YouTube video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wIzNVw2AgOM)

In the experiment, it only took ~ 1 hour (I'm sure I could do it in less) of wearing the CFB. Also note, this was at shivering level of intensity. (This epi-paleo CT community is one of the few that understands the significance of shivering; many of the so-called CT experts and bloggers and researchers on BAT out there, apparently think shivering is detrimental to the formation and activation of BAT; the opposite is true, and personal experience, and now this experiment, helps prove that.)

You'll also notice in the video/blog, that I didn't even take the "After" PET scan for over an hour after removing the CFB and sat in a 70'F room that entire time... yet still showed BAT activity.

[BLOG](https://coolfatburner.com/Blog.htm#activate_Brown_Fat!!!)

Probably a lot of this is review for experienced CTers, but still a nice confirmation of a few things.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-13T16:53:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-cfb-bat-pet-scans-oh-my.10139/post-115192>

Neat video, Eric. I like your clutch analogy for UCP. I didn't know BAT was related to muscle tissue.
Interesting gloves you were wearing...

When I sit still in a cool room I'll notice that I feel a slight tremble in my back periodically, a shiver that is not visible to anyone. I always fancied that the areas shivering seemed to correspond to the BAT areas, but it didn't make sense if it were fat tissue.

### Eric — 2014-03-14T03:25:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-cfb-bat-pet-scans-oh-my.10139/post-115298>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=115192)
>
> Neat video, Eric.

Thanks!

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=115192)
>
> Interesting gloves you were wearing...

Heh heh, open fingers for keyboards and touchpads. The ideal would be to have heated gloves (not the bulky things you see online), but ones made explicitly for "functional CT" sessions. Real thin, and ultra-hot. (preferably w/ a dial adjuster if possible) As of now, no such gloves exist. Yet.

For me, cold fingers/toes are the only limiting factor in intensity or duration of sessions. If I ever take breaks during a session, it's only ever to run hot water over my hands, or put the feet near the woodstove.

Granted, I know lots of folks here are concerned w/ EMF, and electronic gadgets and battery-powered hot gloves would factor in.

# Thread: 'Fat Gene' Discovered? The IRX3
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-13T12:04:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/post-115154>

These news are being spread like crazy since yesterday

[http://www.medicaldaily.com/fat-gen...r-behind-obesity-excessive-weight-gain-271063](http://www.medicaldaily.com/fat-gene-discovered-irx3-may-be-driving-factor-behind-obesity-excessive-weight-gain-271063)

What are your thoughts about it? When people read things like this they get a hype and a lot of expectations about the future of research on obesity as if the understanding on these things will lead to a cure to obesity.

Based on the ideas we learn here, how epigenetics change the way our genes express and all the stuff, my perception when I read this is that you can discover all the genes related to obesity that you want, you'll never end the circle unless you make something about the field you're playing with.

My take is that evolution will find ways to deal with the stress of the environment, obesity is in part a way to effectively tackle this as many have proposed, Jack has said also many times that insulin and leptin resistance are not a disease in itself but a consequence of an altered field.

Not that I don't think that is amazing to find new things about how this all complex thing works, and knowing the role of each gene is more context to play with... great, but in the end you have to deal with the problem from its roots.

Thoughts?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-13T12:10:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/post-115155>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=115154)
>
> Thoughts?

What you said.

I guess this is the point of it:

> ... illuminating a potential drug target ...

Did you find the original paper, and see what affiliations the researchers had? I think most research is aimed at "illuminating ... potential drug targets" and not at dealing with people's problems.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-13T12:18:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/post-115156>

Nope, I didn't have a look at the details yet, I should and I might also write a post about it on my blog... it will take the 'controversy' motto of my blog to new levels

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-13T12:32:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/post-115158>

I was jumping to conclusions there. I think "... illuminating a potential drug target ..." is the words of the source, _Medical Daily_, that you linked.

It seems the study is in March 12th's edition of _Nature_ -- says so here:

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2014/03/13/irx3.likely.fat.gene](http://esciencenews.com/articles/2014/03/13/irx3.likely.fat.gene)

They mention three academics from round the world as having contributed there.

See also:

[http://www.nhs.uk/news/2013/07July/Pages/Does-genetic-fat-mutation-cause-obesity.aspx](http://www.nhs.uk/news/2013/07July/Pages/Does-genetic-fat-mutation-cause-obesity.aspx)

> The study was carried out by researchers from the UK, Germany and Japan. It was funded by the Rosetrees Trust, University College London Hospital (UCLH) Charities, and University College London/UCLH Comprehensive Biomedical Research Centre.

Still, it seems to me it doesn't really matter whether this propensity in some people is there or not, if conditions don't cause a problem, as you said. They only looked at Europeans AFAICT, but people across Europe did not have high levels of obesity before the 1970s and their genetics haven't changed since then. So the real problem isn't in the genes.

I think the fact that _Medical Daily_ did use that expression is telling. This seems to be how people in the medical profession think.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-13T12:43:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/post-115160>

I linked to that Medical Daily as it's the first that came out in a Google search but I could have used any news source... I don't have a doubt that the researches are desperately trying to solve the problem of obesity with the best of their intentions, but as you say, does it really matter or not? Well, I guess that if that serves to look at which things alter the gene expression of these troublesome genes, then it can be useful.. if they are thinking in developing a drug that deals with the problem... wouldn't it then be very much like the cholesterol blocking idea with statins, but at the DNA level? An epic fail?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-13T13:02:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/post-115162>

The researchers could say that it was pure research -- that they just wanted to see what was going on.

But then the funding seems to be philanthropic from charities: "It was funded by the Rosetrees Trust ... etc."

Let's look them up:

[http://www.rosetreestrust.co.uk/](http://www.rosetreestrust.co.uk/)

Here's what it says on their frontpage:

> We fund cutting edge medical research to achieve extraordinary improvement s in human health

IOW, _they_ at least are evidently hoping for some practical outcome.

Well then -- I'd say they're looking in the wrong place. They're not going to get much public health bang for their buck here. What could they possibly be hoping for here? That some kind of treatment would allow people who have this genetics to carry on eating crap (and sitting up late at night under electric light, etc.) without getting fat?

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-13T13:49:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/post-115163>

That's the point, I think that researchers might have their focus on people with morbid obesity that does not seem to be able to be explained just from a calories in calories out common viewpoint. I really hope for the best and that they can find some insight about what can do to ease these people dearrangements. But, the rest of the world? They might be expecting to have a solution for allowing to eat more without the penalties of obestity. But if you cut out obestity when it's needed, what will you have? An offensive homeostatic attack that cannot be counteracted and will speed up other diseases, much in the way like the paradox of high cholesterol is being seen these days.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-13T16:05:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/post-115185>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=115163)
>
> That's the point, I think that researchers might have their focus on people with morbid obesity that does not seem to be able to be explained just from a calories in calories out common viewpoint. I really hope for the best and that they can find some insight about what can do to ease these people dearrangements. But, the rest of the world? They might be expecting to have a solution for allowing to eat more without the penalties of obestity. But if you cut out obestity when it's needed, what will you have? An offensive homeostatic attack that cannot be counteracted and will speed up other diseases, much in the way like the paradox of high cholesterol is being seen these days.

Your last sentence sums up my response as well. They find some magic pill and people no longer are obese, then what can the body do to compensate? Sounds like heart attacks, suicide, or cancer waiting to happen.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-13T16:23:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-gene-discovered-the-irx3.10142/post-115189>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=115185)
>
> Your last sentence sums up my response as well. They find some magic pill and people no longer are obese, then what can the body do to compensate? Sounds like heart attacks, suicide, or cancer waiting to happen.

I forgot to "like" Albert's post -- I do that now.

Albert's alluding to statins again. Dr. Rosedale on that:

> Ron: Yes, it lowers the cholesterol number. It does. Does it lower mortality rate? Will it reduce your risk of dying?
>
> Sam: I don’t know.
>
> Ron: It won’t. In fact, it’ll increase it. ...

And again:

> Ron: ... Firemen are correlated with house fires. Rarely is it the firemen who cause it. Occasionally, maybe, but I would certainly not want to kill the firemen as a means to put out the fire. And that’s what they’re doing with cholesterol. ...
>
> Why is your body going to such pains to first make sure that it manufactures cholesterol in the liver and create proteins to transport it? So that it can give you cardiovascular disease and kill you? Evolution doesn’t work that way! ...

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2013...sedale-keep-the-butter-and-give-up-the-bagel/](http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2013/05/12/sam-should-i-give-up-butter-on-my-bagel-ron-rosedale-keep-the-butter-and-give-up-the-bagel/)

IOW, that kind of approach makes things _worse_.

I don't think anyone here wants that kind of sticking-plaster approach to problems that can be solved with lifetsyle changes you can make. Maybe many of the general public just want to keep on doing whatever they're doing, but they're going to come to grief ...

If Jack's going to say, for example, that maybe the rubber soles on my shoes are a problem, then I want to hear it. Better to hear inconvenient truths.

# Thread: Strange frontpage for a serious newspaper
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-13T19:39:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115219>

Hmmm … I was just buying something for dinner in Waitrose and took a glance at the newspaper headlines.

Right across the frontage of the _Daily Telegraph_ in huge type as the main story was this:

**Statins have no side-effects**

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/h...ve-virtually-no-side-effects-study-finds.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/healthnews/10693895/Statins-have-virtually-no-side-effects-study-finds.html)

On the website it's filed away under "Health" (and they've inserted the word "virtually") but it was the lead story for the day.

I'm sorry but I smell a rat here.

Hugely disappointed. The _Telegraph_ used to be Britain leading quality newspaper and had some journalistic standards at one time.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-13T19:45:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115220>

I wonder if this is part of the PR push to make Statins OTC......

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-13T19:55:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115223>

A Google News search brings up the same story in the _Guardian_ and _Daily Mail._ But it also brings up recent stories expressing worries about statins:

_Daily Express_ of March 2nd:

**Health chief slams statins: Millions face terrible side effects as prescription escalates**

[http://www.express.co.uk/news/healt...rrible-side-effects-as-prescription-escalates](http://www.express.co.uk/news/health/462607/Health-chief-slams-statins-Millions-face-terrible-side-effects-as-prescription-escalates)

BBC of March 6th:

**Statin debate: A bitter pill to swallow?**

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-26432112](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-26432112)

Those two earlier stories are quite interesting. Turns out:

> The drugs are currently offered to patients with a 20 per cent risk of developing heart disease to help keep their cholesterol levels in check.
>
> Around seven million adults take the drugs. Under guidance to be published later this month by Government drug watchdog the National Institute for Care and Health Excellence (NICE), the threshold will be cut to a 10 per cent risk. ...

It seems likely to me that this latest "study" has been timed as part of a publicity campaign to go with the British Government's watchdog's recommendations.

Some watchdog.

> "You consider that to be important?" he asked.
>
> "Exceedingly so."
>
> "Is there any point to which you would wish to draw my attention?"
>
> "To the curious incident of the dog in the night-time."
>
> "The dog did nothing in the night-time."
>
> "That was the curious incident," remarked Sherlock Holmes.

[http://www.gutenberg.org/files/834/834-h/834-h.htm#link2H\_4\_0001](http://www.gutenberg.org/files/834/834-h/834-h.htm#link2H_4_0001)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-13T21:59:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115257>

> Most people in the general population will not feel perfectly well in every way on every day. Why should they suddenly feel well when taking a tablet after being warned of possible adverse effects? We believe that patients should be empowered to make their own decisions, but we must first make sure they have top quality unbiased information.

Reading the Daily Telegraph article, I was strongly reminded of Andy Marino's book.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-14T08:13:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115323>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=115257)
>
> Reading the Daily Telegraph article, I was strongly reminded of Andy Marino's book.

I don't know Andy Marino but what you quote is just waffle, isn't it?

The point is not people not "suddenly feel\[ing\] well" -- what an absurd formulation! The point is that they are experiencing positive and quite serious problems. And in fact these are not so much "side effects" as direct effects of what these drugs do. They inhibit HMG coenzyme A reductase, because that makes cholesterol in the liver (not that cholesterol is a bad thing in itself, anyway, but once people are sold on the idea that it is …). But HMG coenzyme A reductase is also used to make coenzyme Q10.

This is why:

> Dr Kailash Chand, deputy chairman of the British Medical Association ... suffered “awful” muscle pains while taking statins ...

(as we're informed in the Express article). We know the mechanism for that.

But what's disturbing to me is that the _Telegraph_ ran this story as its main frontpage story. To be blunt, someone wants that headline put in people's faces. The _Telegraph_ is apparently happy to oblige.

The "research" is also coming out of Imperial College, London. This is one of the most prestigious science universities in the world. They apparently don't mind taking part in something that appears to have a primarily political/commercial purpose.

Tim Noakes interview:

[http://vinnietortorich.com/2013/10/angriest-trainer-195-dr-timothy-noakes-interview/](http://vinnietortorich.com/2013/10/angriest-trainer-195-dr-timothy-noakes-interview/)

This one is a real eye-opener. Dr. Noakes basically says here that standards of honesty in his profession have been falling throughout his career.

### caroline — 2014-03-14T08:32:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115326>

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-14T09:23:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115333>

No, I didn't hate it. I just didn't like it a lot. And if Erwan and you liked it, it must be good. I don't know that I wouldn't mind trying the ayahuasca, though, if I was in the Amazon -- not that I touch drugs or anything, but it's not really like that, is it? It's not about "getting high" but about connecting, I guess.

I read a funny piece in the _Times_ by Matthew Parris, an ex-aide to Mrs Thatcher once:

[http://www.thetimes.co.uk/tto/public/profile/Matthew-Parris](http://www.thetimes.co.uk/tto/public/profile/Matthew-Parris)

He was given something by a medicine man -- by the sounds as part of Amazon Rainforest package tour! -- while out there. I think it was the one where they burn your arm and put frog venom on the place. Anyway, he was told to sit on a log in case he got nauseous and wait for the experience. Nothing happened. He concluded it was going to do nothing. That night in his hotel room he had the most vivid dream of his life. He dreamt he was crawling backwards on Brighton beach in front of Mrs. Thatcher, smoothing a path in the sand for her to walk on.

Sand? On Brighton beach!

### Grmoline — 2014-03-14T13:07:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115346>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=115323)
>
> I don't know Andy Marino but what you quote is just waffle, isn't it?
>
> The point is not people not "suddenly feel\[ing\] well" -- what an absurd formulation! The point is that they are experiencing positive and quite serious problems. And in fact these are not so much "side effects" as direct effects of what these drugs do. They inhibit HMG coenzyme A reductase, because that makes cholesterol in the liver (not that cholesterol is a bad thing in itself, anyway, but once people are sold on the idea that it is …). But HMG coenzyme A reductase is also used to make coenzyme Q10.

That this conclusion could be drawn ("no side effects") is astounding. The mechanism for statins inhibition of cholesterol formation is not unknown to researchers and the medical world. It blocks the Mevalonic pathway at its source. There's now some acknowledgement that Co-Q10 is also produced along that pathway. But so is Nuclear Factor Kappa-B (think immune response), Dolichol (think neuropeptide synthesis and glycoprotein formation), Seleno-Proteins, and Tau Proteins. There's a book by Dr. Duane Graveline, a NASA doc, called "The Statin Damage Crisis" that illuminates this pathway. One of the memorable quotes: "I had long wondered why muscle and nerve damage seemed permanent and even progressive.... Now I have found that the ultimate effect of statins on Co-Q10 and dolichols is to damage the DNA of the mitochondrial life-force within our cells - mitochondrial mutation masquerading as premature old age." I'm tempted to send this to the Telegraph.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-14T13:13:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115350>

> [Grmoline said:](/goto/post?id=115346)
>
> I'm tempted to send this to the Telegraph.

Actually, that's a good idea. Perhaps you should. I guess they will get some letters to the editor biting back. Of course, one reason for printing somewhat controversial material _is_ to fill your correspondence columns. I'm more suspicious in this case, though.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-14T13:25:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115351>

Hmmm ... another interesting piece from the _Express_ (which I hadn't expected):

> MOST of the experts who are set to recommend the widespread use of statins next month are in the pay of the drug companies that manufacture them. ...
>
> A Sunday Express investigation has discovered eight out of 12 members of the Nice panel which drew up the guidance have financial ties to companies that make statins or next generation cholesterol lowering drugs.
>
> The financial ties are declared in an appendix to the new draft Nice statin guidance and include payments for speeches, lectures or to attend conferences which can reap thousands of pounds per hour.
> ...

People on both sides of the house have qualms:

> Tory MP Dr Sarah Wollaston has vowed to call on the Commons Health Select Committee to investigate the revelation.
>
> GP Dr Wollaston, a former member of the committee, said: “This should lead to an examination by Nice. It is a concern as this panel’s decision has such widespread implications for prescribing and cost to the NHS.”
>
> Shadow Health Minister Andrew Gwynne said: “Patients across the country will be concerned by these findings. Any suggestion that medics or officials stand to personally profit from decisions about our care will rightly worry patients. Ministers must ask the watchdog to investigate without delay.”

[http://www.express.co.uk/news/health/463873/Experts-recommending-statins-are-paid-by-drugs-firms](http://www.express.co.uk/news/health/463873/Experts-recommending-statins-are-paid-by-drugs-firms)

### sooperb — 2014-03-14T14:51:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115353>

Just like the "experts" from NICE that I posted a link to on here. I wonder how they adjust their consciences or is this just like the political arena, both sides have conflicting views which they firmly believe and won't be swayed about?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-14T15:24:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/strange-frontpage-for-a-serious-newspaper.10146/post-115356>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=115353)
>
> Just like the "experts" from NICE that I posted a link to on here. I wonder how they adjust their consciences or is this just like the political arena, both sides have conflicting views which they firmly believe and won't be swayed about?

Tim Noakes (if I follow right) seems to think that people tell themselves that it doesn't really matter that much. In some cases it's not so much a way to line your pocket as simply a means to get funding ... but, of course, we know that he who pays the piper calls the tune ...

Politicians? Well, yeah, I agree they're locked into belief-systems -- and the emotional rewards (as opposed to material rewards) that those offer. They're not all uninterested in the folding stuff, though.

Office seems to have been a springboard to unimaginably huge financial wealth for one previous Prime Minister at least:

> His wealth ... is estimated at £70 million.

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/pol...lairs-fortune-boosted-13m-by-bumper-year.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/politics/tony-blair/10551183/Tony-Blairs-fortune-boosted-13m-by-bumper-year.html)

There seem to be some curious conflicts of interest involved in some of those deals, too.

# Thread: CT and Detox Symptoms?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ed Warnicke — 2014-03-14T14:50:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115352>

What have folks seen in terms of detox symptoms with CT and how long have they lasted?

I've been doing 10 minute cold showers and 1 hour ice baths... and I'm experiencing headaches and some generalized lethargy. I'm trying to figure out if this is normal detox, or something else, and if its normal detox, how long I can expect it to last (and possibly how I can mitigate the symptoms).

### fitness@home — 2014-03-14T15:30:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115361>

This is an old thread on the subject:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/die-off-symptoms.48/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/die-off-symptoms.48/)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-14T16:20:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115369>

Hi Ed, welcome! I'm not sure about the lethargy. But for the headaches, really push the non-fluoridated water. 2 gallons isn't too much...
Better hydration seems to be extra important when we adopt a new health strategy. When I was getting great results from starting grounding, Dr. K recommended I get lots of water, and yes, it was so helpful!

I don't drink as much now, but anytime I have the slightest headache, it has become a habit to automatically drink more water. Works like a charm.

### Ed Warnicke — 2014-03-15T21:40:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115671>

Thank you both for your replies!

I normally drink a lot of spring water, but have not been measuring my intake... I'll try to increase it and see if that helps

### Inger — 2014-03-15T21:56:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115673>

I got sinus infection first time in my life after starting CT 2 years ago... and I got huge appetite too.. and cravings for raw egg yolk foam lol
Dry skin for a few months.. and I recall I got a bit of dark moods too for a while!

All of these disappeared without going to the doctor or taking medicine

### Ed Warnicke — 2014-03-19T03:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-116323>

CTforLife:

I think you may have misunderstood me a bit. I was not complaining about CT... just checking in to see if others had similar experiences

# Thread: Technical Diabetes Forums
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-15T02:08:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115521>

Does anyone know of any good diabetes forums online with a technical user base, or alternately scientific forums where glucose metabolism is frequently discussed?

I am trying to find an audience that I can ask technical questions to pertaining to things like insulin testing.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-15T02:14:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115523>

No but ask away here. you've got several T2D folks here as well as T1D (they don't post as often)

Look up Dali Dula here on the forum he's got some great experience.

### persistence — 2014-03-15T04:05:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115534>

I am looking for a west coast USA lab that does the insulin tolerance test. In a fasting state, they give you a small amount of insulin, and then observe how well your body uses that to take up your fasting glucose level in the first 15 minutes, before the body starts to create glucose again.

It's apparently a very simple test, but very rarely done, and has very strong correlation to the clamp tests for insulin resistance that are done in large research studies (but never done for a prediabetic patient). It's a direct and very useful measurement of insulin resistance that doesn't stress out the system with a full glucose insulin tolerance test.

### Dali Dula — 2014-03-15T15:01:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115580>

I have never had a GTT. this test will tell you if you diabetic or insulin resistant. You can do this yourself with BG meter. You are young and already aware of your pre diabetic state. This is fortunate. More fortunate is you have discovered this site. Work the protocols and test yourself regularly. Test fasting and post prandial to see how activities, environment and food affect BG. I keep good control over BG without exogenous insulin. With some time you may be able to correct your BG metabolism. When it comes to lab tests I ask myself will the info I get change my actionable options. If it won't I don't see value in it.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-15T16:46:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115598>

> [persistence said:](/goto/post?id=115534)
>
> I am looking for a west coast USA lab that does the insulin tolerance test. In a fasting state, they give you a small amount of insulin, and then observe how well your body uses that to take up your fasting glucose level in the first 15 minutes, before the body starts to create glucose again.
>
> It's apparently a very simple test, but very rarely done, and has very strong correlation to the clamp tests for insulin resistance that are done in large research studies (but never done for a prediabetic patient). It's a direct and very useful measurement of insulin resistance that doesn't stress out the system with a full glucose insulin tolerance test.

I'm just gonna say this test sucks. had it done twice while pregnant to rule out gestational diabetes. oh the suck... and that insulin drink is horrid. I'm glad I only had to do the 1 hr test. the 3 hr would have sucked even more...

Dali's right - you can do this yourself at home with a blood glucose monitor, and a TBS or 2 of maple syrup. - prick your finger before taking, then take levels every 1-15min until your BG is at the same level as FBG.

### persistence — 2014-03-15T22:01:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115676>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=115580)
>
> I have never had a GTT. this test will tell you if you diabetic or insulin resistant. You can do this yourself with BG meter. You are young and already aware of your pre diabetic state. This is fortunate. More fortunate is you have discovered this site. Work the protocols and test yourself regularly. Test fasting and post prandial to see how activities, environment and food affect BG. I keep good control over BG without exogenous insulin. With some time you may be able to correct your BG metabolism. When it comes to lab tests I ask myself will the info I get change my actionable options. If it won't I don't see value in it.

Hi Dali and Shinjin. You are both confusing different tests. Please distinguish the "Glucose Tolerance Test" from "Glucose Insulin Tolerance Test" from "Insulin Tolerance Test". These are three different tests. My imprecise definition of each:

A: Glucose Tolerance Test: They give you 70 grams of glucose liquid, then measure your glucose response every N minutes in order to diagnose possible diabetes

B: Glucose Insulin Tolerance Test: They give you 70 grams of glucose liquid, then measure your glucose AND INSULIN every N minutes in order to diagnose possible diabetes. This test shows insulin resistance or failure to produce insulin, which is something test A does not show.

C: Insulin Tolerance Test: NO GLUCOSE IS GIVEN IN THIS TEST. This test - as I described earlier - INJECTS only a SMALL amount of INSULIN and then they watch to see how your glucose responds to that. This test does not diagnose diabetes. This test measures insulin resistance.

Now that I have defined A, B, and C, let me answer your point.

Dali, I totally agree with you test A can be done at home. The glucometer is my friend, and I can certainly try these measurements if I am curious. It doesn't strike me as the best use of time because even 20 grams of carbs can send me to 130 Glucose. 40 grams can occasionally get me well over 150. I already know something isn't working well. But I would disagree that you would not want to run test B. Insulin cannot be measured at home. This disease is all about insulin resistance. We should be very interested in understanding how our bodies produce insulin, and what is the extent of our insulin resistance when ingesting glucose. But I agree with both of you that the test in A and B is awful, time consuming and a real hassle.

I also disagree with you about test C. In fact every prediabetic or type 2 should want test C. Because it is a SIMPLE easy test that gives a lot of information about insulin resistance. This test cannot be duplicated at home, unless you inject insulin. I'm hopefully never going to get to that stage (my father never did and he had the identical symptoms of prediabetes), so I would need to do test C in a lab environment.

That's why I am looking for a lab that does Test C.

### Dali Dula — 2014-03-16T05:32:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115722>

You are correct. I try to use information to formulate actionable strategies to aid in my well being. The (A) GTT will tell you how you handle a glucose load. You already know that from your BG meter.
The (B) GITT would be useful to tell you that you produce little or no insulin and are Type 1 and will require exogenous insulin. This doesn't sound like you. You might want to do this, but what action would you take if your insulin production is low?
The (C) ITT seems to be most useful in gauging cortisol response to hypoglycemia not assessing IR. I would think that where you are at now even a small amount of exogenous insulin would drop your BG way low. How would this info modify your strategy?
These tests may be valuable for getting a baseline, and I have had quite a few tests over the past five years that in retrospect offered me little in actionable info. If you have the resources test away.
You are obviously a smart person willing to do the due diligence to figure yourself out.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-16T17:29:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115794>

### persistence — 2014-03-16T22:47:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115838>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=115722)
>
> You are correct. I try to use information to formulate actionable strategies to aid in my well being. The (A) GTT will tell you how you handle a glucose load. You already know that from your BG meter.
> The (B) GITT would be useful to tell you that you produce little or no insulin and are Type 1 and will require exogenous insulin. This doesn't sound like you. You might want to do this, but what action would you take if your insulin production is low?
> The (C) ITT seems to be most useful in gauging cortisol response to hypoglycemia not assessing IR. I would think that where you are at now even a small amount of exogenous insulin would drop your BG way low. How would this info modify your strategy?
> These tests may be valuable for getting a baseline, and I have had quite a few tests over the past five years that in retrospect offered me little in actionable info. If you have the resources test away.
> You are obviously a smart person willing to do the due diligence to figure yourself out.

I have never had hypoglycemia. It's never been an issue for me. A typical morning pattern for me is:

1) Wake with fasting glucose 90
2) If I do not eat for an hour that goes to 105. If I eat a big protein meal right at waking, that goes to 115.
3) One hour after eating maybe 20 grams of carbs (from tubers like yam) that goes to 128
4) Two hours: 109
5) Three hours: 99

I overrespond to a glucose challenge, and my body shows insulin resistance because it takes a long time to work its way back down to a normal range. I NEVER get rebound hypoglycemia.

It's in no way clear at all that my response to the test in C would be hypoglycemia. I would be thrilled with such a response, because it would show I am not insulin resistant. I would expect that I would show a lower-than-normal lowering of blood sugar, because my body is not processing insulin efficiently. That's the entire point of prediabetes and type 2.

What actionable information do I get from C? First, I do get a baseline and I can use that to compare against the same test in the future, to see if the insulin resistance is getting better or worse after leptin-reset and other therapies. Second, it helps me to compare my own case against others by looking at population studies that have had that same test. Third, it does help establish that my problem is failure to process insulin efficiently rather than failure to create insulin. Fourth - as you can tell - I am a pretty deductive (and persistent  ) person and having a lot of relevant data points lying around is like having pieces to the puzzle, and slowly I start to put them all together to get a useful picture of the problem and the solution.

What's really interesting to me is that my problem appears to just disappear when I am asleep. I have had my glucose on waking up be 78 to 83, usually after I use GABA to help me sleep, but sometimes just after getting a lot of really sound sleep. GABA \*may\* help suppress glucagon, which is the counterpart to insulin that signals the liver to increase glucose production. The fact that my glucose suddenly rises as soon as I wake up - from as low as 80 to 105 - without even taking any food, is probably a clue. I don't know what to make of that.

One theory: The brain is signaling the body to upregulate glucagon to create sugar, and the endogenous levels of insulin just cannot deal with that added sugar efficiently because of insulin resistance. The result is that my background glucose levels rise, even before I eat any kind of food. Is it as simple as just insulin resistance, or is there some other possible explanation for that effect?

### persistence — 2014-03-17T01:50:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115877>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=115840)
>
> See Tim Ferris's experiments in "The 4 Hour Body" with blood sugar for some perspective....
>
> Also Jimmy Moore's with glucose and ketone tesing on [www.livinlavidalowcarb.com](http://www.livinlavidalowcarb.com)...
>
> Hypoglycemia was probably years ago and you wrote it off as something else in your 20's and 30's.....see literature on hypoglycemic episodes while asleep, etc...

I have probably had hypoglycemia less than 10 times in my entire life, always after some strenuous exercise. Even after exercise, it is so rare for me. Symptoms of hypoglycemia include sweating, heart racing, shakiness, hunger and anxiety. I have never woken up at night with those symptoms. I had heart racing after over-training episodes, particularly when very dehydrated, but hypoglycemia I know the feeling and I don't think it would get past me.

I researched online and I guess you are referring to the so-called "Somogyi Effect" where there is a rebound in blood sugar after a nighttime hypoglycemia? I read about that and I am more inclined to believe I would be having the "Dawn Effect" defined [here](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawn_phenomenon) and maybe the "Cortisol Effect" defined [here](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cortisol_awakening_response). I am a fairly high-strung person, probably way too tense, fairly overwhelmed by a large number of tasks. So it's pretty easy for me to believe that a deep sleep is lowering cortisol, and that upon waking that is getting very elevated. Would I catch this just by measuring a fasting cortisol level? Should I measure epinephrine as well? How does one diagnose whether you have "Dawn Effect" or "Cortisol Effect"?

Assuming the problem is cortisol in morning, how does one fight that? I have to retire and stop working? Short of having a permanent vacation, I don't see a way to avoid daily stress.

I hate to be such a science geek, but I would gladly wear a device 24x7 if it could spit out real-time graphs of glucose, insulin, glucagon, leptin, cortisol, etc. You would learn so much about yourself in literally weeks. I kid you not that it took me probably three months of continuous glucose testing just to figure out my glucose patterns. A real-time glucometer worn 24x7 would have given me the same level of understanding in two weeks. Having to make wild guesses about all of these other chemicals, and patterns of these things while you sleep, is just kind of insane. They put patients through years of drugs and non-effective diets and therapies without really every understanding their problems. Wouldn't it make sense for someone to develop the device I describe and start understanding the disease rather than spending $400 MILLION to develop yet-another-health-destroying drug? Smart people would start to quickly understand the relationships between their behaviors, diets, lifestyle, and underlying metabolic chemicals. I'm sure at least 20% of patients could cure themselves without drugs simply by having the right information as feedback to the corrective actions they try.

I'll take a peek at Four Hour Body and get back.

### persistence — 2014-03-17T07:14:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115912>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=115840)
>
> See Tim Ferris's experiments in "The 4 Hour Body" with blood sugar for some perspective....
>
> Also Jimmy Moore's with glucose and ketone tesing on [www.livinlavidalowcarb.com](http://www.livinlavidalowcarb.com)...
>
> Hypoglycemia was probably years ago and you wrote it off as something else in your 20's and 30's.....see literature on hypoglycemic episodes while asleep, etc...

Okay, I read the Glucose chapter in "The 4 Hour Body". I definitely "get" this guy.  I would love to wear a Dexcom and do similar kinds of experiments.

There were only a few ideas there that I had not confirmed or read about before. One of those was that Vinegar does not affect post-meal glucose. I had not realized that lemon does a better job. So maybe if I make some sugar-free lemonade with each meal that would help.

His recommendation on the cinnamon is a bit dangerous. Saigon Cinnamon is extremely dangerous stuff. It has more than 20 times the Coumarin content of Cassia Cinnamon, and Cassia is the one the Europeans have warned against using. Ceylon Cinnamon is the only really safe one in large doses, and wow I am bummed out to hear him say that Ceylon was not very effective. I wish he had published the details for each of those. Saigon Cinnamon is totally delicious (by far the most tasty) but you wouldn't want to touch that stuff more than a few times a month.

His point on saturated fat needed more elaboration. I had a lot of problems with dairy fats (which might be partly because I cross react to dairy protein as part of wheat intolerance) causing my glucose to jump. There are a lot of studies showing some saturated fats (usually they use butter) cause insulin resistance. So some types of fat reward you by slowing down absorption of glucose and then take it all back by making you temporarily less able to metabolize the glucose that does get through digestion. I am using lots of monounsaturated macadamia oil with non starchy meals and MCT Oil with starchy meals.

I don't claim to understand fats very well, but the studies I have read made pretty clear that fats are very powerful mediators of glucose metabolism. If you eat a lot of carbs together with a fat, expect the response of insulin and glucose to potentially be dramatically different, and it won't always be different in a good way. One study I saw showed insulin at 250% higher levels with the same carb meal comparing saturated fat (butter) to monounsaturated fats. Unfortunately, most of us cannot see these effects because the glucometer at home will not show insulin levels, only glucose. A lot depends on the metabolism of the population studied. Healthy people metabolize differently than people with high trigylcerides.

Regarding Jimmy's web site: I have seen some of his n=1 glucose readings testing low carb foods, and those are great. Generally I find his site very poorly organized, and it is difficult to follow any trend of ideas across different articles. I searched n=1 hoping to find all of his glucose tests, but I couldn't find any real trends or articles that jumped out.

# Thread: Connection between Omega 6 and eczema?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/connection-between-omega-6-and-eczema.10175/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sammy — 2014-03-15T11:29:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/connection-between-omega-6-and-eczema.10175/post-115551>

Hi everyone,

I would like to find out the link between omega 6 and eczema.
I read that eczema/dry/flaky/ scaly skin is linked with an Omega 6 deficiency.
When I was younger(3-6 years old), I had terrible eczema, my doctor megadosed me with evening primrose oil. I was taking 6 capsules a day. This alone had definitely help improve my skin.

My eczema 'cured' around 7 yo. But I have had dry flaky scaly skin since then-nothing too serious. (no bleeding/severe itchiness), this can also being a swimmer and soaking in chlorinated water almost everyday(yikes!) Skin has improved since Epi-Paleo(more fats and seafood) + Leptin Rx started towards the end of last year. Going to the beach and soaking the in the seawater helped as well.

Since starting CT 2 weeks ago, my skin has been back to being scaly, dry and itchy-especially after shower(I've been doing cold showers since I started the Rx's)
Dr. Kruse talks about how Omega 6 gets incorporated into the cell membrane to increase fluidity during cold. Would this lead to an Omega 6 deficiency for the skin? How does Omega 3 play a role?
Omega 3 and 6 are the essential fatty acids that our brain requires. Skin and brain derive from ectoderm so skin would also require the same type of nutrients, I presume. Dr. Kruse has hinted that skin problems are tied to bad Vit A cycle and leaky gut. But does not mention about Omega 6/3. I tried to find some info about dry skin and CT on the forum and found that a few others have had the same 'detox' symptoms since starting CT.

I am interested to find out more about this, would love if anyone can give me some input.
Thank you

### Inger — 2014-03-15T12:27:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/connection-between-omega-6-and-eczema.10175/post-115561>

I got dry weird skin too as I started with CT... after a few months it disappeared

I use EVOO on my skin if I feel it needs some fat. I would suggest keep a bottle in your shower and put it on after the shower after you have dried you off with a towel = when your skin is still a bit moist
I can get a bit dry skin if I shower much with our well water, it is very hard water. I never get dry skin from dipping in the river.

I do use EVOO too pretty often with my raw fish chevice and I sometimes eat raw organic nuts too.. (lately a bit too much!)
Maybe you can measure your O6/O3 to be sure how to use O6 fats in your diet?

Fish heads/eyes/liver are rich in vit. A, I think other livers too...

If I eat 85% chocolate or other sugar I get dry skin on my face. Sugar is not good for my skin.

### Sammy — 2014-03-15T11:57:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115553>

Hi all,

I have been attempting for a while now to up the fat and lower the protein for breakfast. Unfortunately, if I even add another tablespoon of CO in there, after 1-2 hrs I start feeling nauseous.....I also get the runs.

I am following the Leaky Gut Rx(ontop of Epi-paleo Rx) which suggests using CO as the main fat in the diet. Why does this happen? Has anyone experienced this and does this get better over time? Ghee does not seem to cause this problem. (Ghee and CO are the main fats in my diet)

Thank you!

### SeaHorse — 2014-03-15T13:11:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115572>

There are people who are sensitive to coconut...my daughter is immediately nauseous when she eats or smells it, but can chow down on other fats very well. Can you eat coconut itself without a problem? How about adding in duck, beef or pork fat?

### sooperb — 2014-03-15T15:13:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115581>

I experienced bowel issues when I started taking CO when I hadn't ever used it before. It settled down and now it's not an issue. How are you using it? I add mine to coffee, my fruit salad (only occasionally!) my porridge (once a week). I don't think I could eat it straight off the spoon but it is quite tasteless in some drinks. I think gradually upping it might work but as seahorse suggests, there are other fats you can use if you can't tolerate it.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-15T17:06:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115602>

While some people do have tolerance issues with CO. However if you have gut issues - candida for example.... coconut oil can cause die-off . I had serious candida issues that didn't respond to diflucan. I cleared the candida by going keto and eating massive amts of coconut oil. I did have intestinal issues at first... but it cleared a short while later...

keep and eye on it. and you can always eat other fats.... and then bring CO back in later when your gut is healed and see if its still an issue

### nicld — 2014-03-15T17:19:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115606>

May have fat malabsorbtion if you have a leaky gut. May need to look into getting a GI stool test done. Do you have a gall bladder?

All of the above possible, consider molybdenum needed to make lipases.....changed my relationship to fat...

### Infinusss — 2014-03-25T04:54:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-coffee-in-the-am.10178/post-117779>

I can tolerate GF butter decently, but I believe that I do better with ghee. In my BPC I use A TBS or two of 100% GF ghee from pureindianfoods.com and a TBS or two of MCT oil. Because ghee doesn't emulsify, I do the following:

Make the coffee, pour it in the blender, add ghee and mct, blend on high for about 15, then add 1 or two eggs yolks and blend on high for another 5 seconds. Now the fat from the ghee and the mct oil won't separate and float to the top of your coffee.

I know about the eyebrow thyroid link but is this fluff a sign of under / Over anything ?
As you may know I am working without labs which is like reading a Chinese take away menu upside down. You see it as number 68 and hope that it really said Chow Mein beside it.

### Inger — 2014-03-15T18:22:55+0200

Maybe change your make up? I use Lavera organic foundation.. I love that. I also do not use it all over my face at all... just a tiny bit here and there

# Thread: Gastro-oesophageal reflux in infants
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gastro-oesophageal-reflux-in-infants.10193/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-16T18:36:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gastro-oesophageal-reflux-in-infants.10193/post-115802>

I wasn't going to log on again today, but this was so intrinsically interesting ...

So I was reading Galen Cranz's _The Chair_.

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/The-Chair-Rethinking-Culture-Design/dp/0393319555/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/The-Chair-Rethinking-Culture-Design/dp/0393319555/)

The first part on the social history of the chair I didn't find of much interest -- much of it seems kind of valid but over-simplified. _Private Eye_ used to have an amusing term psychobabble. This would be the equivalent from sociology -- sociobabble, perhaps. So I skipped to Part Two. (I'll go back later.) Part Two has stuff on the mechanics of sitting and the problems it causes us, and this is much more interesting (at least to me).

Here's one passage that really caught my eye:

> Other indications of the deleterious effects that sitting has on our internal organs comes from research on a common affliction of infants, gastro-esophageal reflux. The involuntary bringing up of food or liquids is sometimes associated with pulmonary aspiration, pneumonia, bronchial spasm, apnea, asthma, and even death. Since gravity helps prevent reflux in adults, infants younger than six months have routinely been placed in an infant seat since the 1950s. But medical research concludes that the seat, rather than being therapeutic, is actually detrimental. What's a better approach? Simply placing the infant prone. Babies younger than six months do not yet have strong torsos, so sitting them upright prematurely allows the junction between esophagus and stomach to become constricted; when prone, their digestive apparatus lengthens properly.

She cites;

[http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJM198309293091304](http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJM198309293091304)

After that she casually mentions that varicose veins are, according to one medical source, absent in non-chair sitting cultures, and explains the likely reason why.

I guess we all know that there are a number of things that contribute to reflux in adults -- being overweight, poor diet, eating too close to bedtime, etc. I wonder if anyone has thought to ask: "How much of the day do you spend seated?"

There's no doubt that the sitting posture does tend to cramp up internal organs -- and, of course, if you're not strong enough to support your own back, as many modern people are not, you easily end up sliding your bottom forward in the seat while your shoulders rest on the back, thereby collapsing the spine into a C-shape, and crushing up the stomach area up more. (Really, one should sit on the front of the chair, using it as if it were a stool; but most people are no longer able to, so don't -- a vicious circle. )

Obvious comment: we should all spend more time standing and lying, as people in hunter-gatherer societies do, and less time sitting. Maybe one of the things someone with reflux needs to be advised is: "Take a long walk every day."

To return to the infants -- not sitting up an infant when he or she is not strong enough to sit properly in that posture seems so obvious once it's been said. The recommendation of the prone position (face-down orientation) position is also interesting. I have heard from other sources than some experts have doubts about the recommendation to always lay infants to sleep in the supine position (face-up orientation). AFAIK, mothers always used to lie children to sleep on their backs (or sides) but doctors began to recommend the supine position (in the 1950s perhaps?) but later retracted the advice.

However, it seems the supine position may have some advantages: infants, apparently, seem to like it -- among other things it allows them to push up to raise themselves as and when they choose and start to use their arm muscles and coordinate head-movements in their own time. Maybe it's not the best position for sleep, but perhaps it's a good position to use at times in the day ... and better, it seems likely, than sitting the infant ...

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-16T19:16:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gastro-oesophageal-reflux-in-infants.10193/post-115808>

IME - Havoc had silent reflux, only occasionally would he scream bloody murder. to which resolved with nursing and holding up right while being carried.

Havoc spent most of his time doing tummy time, or being worn up right in a carrier, or nursing his first 6mths. while he could sit unsupported he didn't prefer that position, so we didn't put him in that position often. Once he started putting himself in that position - totally different story w/regards to sitting - but he still preferred to be held/worn in a carrier vs being by himself.

he was dx with reflux after he had a severe cough that wouldn't clear. Ped - listened to the cough and said reflux - to my relief. I had seen a different ped when he was 3mths and argued for reflux but the man was uninformed that breast fed babies can't have reflux. Havoc had a barium swallow study to confirm the reflux

reflux in babies is basically the result of the musculature of flap btwn the stomach and the esophagus not being fully matured. After what I've learned from Jack, there's a part of me that thinks their may be a wiring problem btwn the infant's brain and the vagus nerve due to epigenetic influences from mom to baby.

I've seen babies who were born via natural birth (no meds and vaginal delivery), and those with medicalized birth (medications & vaginal delivery and medications and c-section delivery) who have reflux. Looking at that - I think there's something epigenetically that's related to this, as well as possible defects in snps.

Just my 2-cents.....

### Josh — 2014-03-16T19:30:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gastro-oesophageal-reflux-in-infants.10193/post-115812>

Worth looking at the structural issues related to the spine and brain as it relates to vagal stimulation and tone.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-16T19:35:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gastro-oesophageal-reflux-in-infants.10193/post-115814>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=115812)
>
> Worth looking at the structural issues related to the spine and brain as it relates to vagal stimulation and tone.

Chiropractic adjustments helped but didn't fully eliminate the issues. Chaos had her first adjustment btwn 2-3wks. Havoc had his at 5days. Both continue to get adjustments....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-17T12:07:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gastro-oesophageal-reflux-in-infants.10193/post-115923>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=115808)
>
> IME - Havoc had silent reflux, only occasionally would he scream bloody murder. to which resolved with nursing and holding up right while being carried.

So that lengthening she talks of would be able to take place. "\[H\]olding up right while being carried" would, I'm thinking, be most often with the child facing the adult and a support across the upper back with one arm and under the bottom with the other. This is going to allow a more open angle at the hips.

I'm further on in the book now, and she's good on horse-riding -- good for posture, because the legs can drop out of the hips (they tend to be jammed into the hips with 90 degree sitting) and because widening can take place across the pelvis and the lower-back. She also likes the notion of "perching" -- sitting on a high stool, so that, again, a more open angle can be achieved, something like a 135 degree angle. All this I'm already familiar with. Incidentally, we have an organisation called Riding for the Disabled over here, because it's generally recognised to be helpful for people with physical problems:

[http://www.rda.org.uk/](http://www.rda.org.uk/)

What I didn't know is that the use of perching-chairs, and the correspondingly high (and sloped) desks that you need to go with them, are quite widely used in the school system in Scandinavia and France despite the cost implicit in junking existing inventory. At least, she says they are (publication date 1998).

I also hadn't noticed how poor car-seat design has become -- probably because I haven't driven for years. Car seats are now over-designed but badly designed. The understanding of the designers is all upside down -- they don't know how the human body works _as a system_ -- and a _dynamic_ system at that. The seat-part is often dished, which causes the pelvis to collapse in and can compress the underside of the leg painfully. Notions of "support" cause them to fit seats that force the body into the undesirable (and in the long-term tiring) C-shape (and sometimes the neck into a disastrous "swan-shape"). Notions of "comfort" means too much padding, which again causes collapse and subsequent strain, because you sink in. Manufacturers apparently assess "comfort" by asking subjects to sit in several different designs for 8.5 minutes and name their favourite. Why 8.5? Because that's the average time a prospective car-buyer spends sitting in the driving seat ... which shows where the manufacturers' priorities lie.

She's quite good on "hacking" existing furniture to adapt it -- e.g., folding or cutting some kind of material to fill in the dish in a driving seat.
