# Thread: Researchers describe microbe that 'eats' electricity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/researchers-describe-microbe-that-eats-electricity.10195/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### CindyB — 2014-03-17T01:22:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/researchers-describe-microbe-that-eats-electricity.10195/post-115871>

[http://www.eurekalert.org/pub\_releases/2014-03/hu-asd031014.php](http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2014-03/hu-asd031014.php)

### Josh — 2014-03-17T01:31:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/researchers-describe-microbe-that-eats-electricity.10195/post-115873>

> **Researchers describe microbe that 'eats' electricity**
> There have been plenty of fad diets that captured the public's imagination over the years, but Harvard scientists have identified what may be the strangest of them all – sunlight and electricity.
>
> Led by Peter Girguis, the John L. Loeb Associate Professor of the Natural Sciences, and Arpita Bose, a post-doctoral fellow in Organismic and Evolutionary Biology, a team of researchers showed that the commonly found bacterium _Rhodopseudomonas palustris_ can use natural conductivity to pull electrons from minerals located deep in soil and sediment while remaining at the surface, where they absorb the sunlight needed to produce energy. The study is described in a February 26 paper in _Nature Communications_.
>
> "When you think about electricity and living organisms, most people default to Mary Shelley's Frankenstein, but we've long understood that all organisms actually use electrons – what constitutes electricity – to do work," Girguis said. "At the heart of this paper is a process called extracellular electron transfer (EET), which involves moving electrons in and out of cells. What we were able to show is that these microbes take up electricity, which goes into their central metabolism, and we were able to describe some of the systems that are involved in that process."
>
> In the wild, the microbes rely on iron to provide the electrons they need to fuel energy generation, but tests in the lab suggest the iron itself isn't critical for this process. By attaching an electrode to colonies of the microbes in the lab, researchers observed that they could take up electrons from a non-ferrous source, suggesting they might also use other electron-rich minerals – such as other metals and sulfur compounds – in the wild.
>
> "That's a game-changer," Girguis said. "We have understood for a long time that the aerobic and anaerobic worlds interact mainly through the diffusion of chemicals into and out of those domains. Accordingly, we also believe this process of diffusion governs the rates of many biogeochemical cycles. But this research indicates…that this ability to do EET is, in a sense, an end-run around diffusion. That could change the way we think about the interactions between the aerobic and anaerobic worlds, and might change the way we calculate the rates of biogeochemical cycling."
>
> Using genetic tools, researchers were also able to identify a gene that is critical to the ability to take up electrons. When the gene was turned off, Girguis said, the microbes' ability to take up electrons dropped by about a third.
>
> "We are very interested in understanding exactly what that role that gene plays in electron uptake," Girguis said. "Related genes are found throughout other microbes in nature, and we aren't exactly sure what they're doing in those microbes. This offers some tantalizing evidence that other microbes carry out this process as well".
>
> The foundation for the new study was laid more than two decades ago, when researchers first characterized a bacterium that "eats" rust by handing off electrons to the oxygen atoms that make up iron oxide molecules.
>
> Researchers would later use the bacteria to construct a microbial "fuel cell" in which bacteria handed off electrons not to rust, but to an electrode that could harvest this current.
>
> If some microbes could generate the energy they needed by moving electrons outside their cells, Girguis and colleagues wondered, could others do the same by taking electrons in?
>
> "That question brought us back to iron," he said. "The microbes that are the focus of this paper are the mirror image of the ones that eat rust. Instead of using iron oxide to breathe, they actually make iron oxides from free iron."
>
> Getting to that free iron, however, is no easy feat.
>
> The microbes rely on sunlight to help generate energy, but the iron they need is found in sediments below the surface. To reach it, and still remain on the surface, Girguis said, the microbes have developed an unusual strategy. The microbes seem to take up electrons through naturally occurring conductive minerals. Also, as the microbes pull electrons away from iron, they create iron oxide crystals which precipitate into the soil around them. Over time, those crystals can become conductive and act as "circuits," allowing the microbes to oxidize minerals they otherwise couldn't reach.
>
> "What that does is solve the paradox for this sunlight-dependent organism," Girguis said. "These single-celled microbes that grow in biofilms have come up with a way to electrically reach out and pull electrons from minerals in the soil so they can stay in the sun."
>
> Though he remains skeptical about the efficacy of using microbes capable of performing EET for energy generation via fuel cells, Girguis said there are other applications – such as the pharmaceutical industry – where the microbes could be put to use.
>
> "I think the biggest applied opportunity here is to use microbes that are capable of taking up electrons to produce something that is of interest," he said, "knowing you can give them electrons to do that through an electrode."

### Josh — 2014-03-17T01:34:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/researchers-describe-microbe-that-eats-electricity.10195/post-115874>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/release...energy+(Matter+&+Energy+News+--+ScienceDaily)](http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2014/03/140310144000.htm?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+sciencedaily%2Fmatter_energy+\(Matter+%26+Energy+News+--+ScienceDaily\))

> And now we wait for the U.S to weaponize it.

[http://www.iflscience.com/chemistry/scientists-reveal-how-microbe-eats-electricity](http://www.iflscience.com/chemistry/scientists-reveal-how-microbe-eats-electricity)

# Thread: Any help with labs appreciated
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-help-with-labs-appreciated.10197/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rick — 2014-03-17T03:10:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-help-with-labs-appreciated.10197/post-115882>

Hey all,

I had posted this in the opt reset journal but perhaps this is the correct thread for labwork:

I ordered some labs on my own via LEF as per Jack's beginning protocols, looking for LR via salivary diurnal cortisol, FT3/rT3 ratio and hs-CRP but results inconclusive. Also checked Pg/E2 ratio to troll for signs of increased intestinal permeability but again inconclusive (all the numbers and ratios look "fine"). Of course HDL and vitamin D levels are not optimal either which also indicates leaky gut but I remember reading about Pg/E2 ratio within the EPCOTx Rx so naturally I wanted to see those numbers for myself...

I remember reading Hormones 101 and JK says inflammation begins from visceral fat when BMI = 20 - 24. This is due specifically to TNF-alpha, IL-6 and NF-kappa-beta but I don't think I understand his perspective on this at all. Is everyone really that inflamed because at 5' 10" I would have to weigh less than 140 lbs to go below a BMI of 20. Typically BMIs from 25 - 29.9 are categorized as "overweight" so BMI numbers from 20 to 24 fall into the "normal" weight range but does this mean there is significant inflammation and LR even within the so called normal range?

The mirror test says greater than 25-30 lbs of visceral fat signifies LR but compared to what? A BMI of 23 for me would occur at 160 but at 140 my BMI would become 20. So at around 187 to 182 when these labs were taken over Sept, Oct and Nov last year I presume I was and still am LR but by the numbers - to prove it - is simply beyond my ken. I don't get Jack's explanation of LR at all, and he has said we will never be able to gain his clinical perspective and yes, I get that: I lack his years of medical education and experience but I really would like to fully understand LR in the context of weight, BMI, or via cytokine or thyroid panels, rT3, cortisol, hs-CRP and any other pertinent labs; if anyone can educate me in this area, I would be most grateful.

Anyway, there are lots of other labs taken by a BHRT doc I saw two times in Manhattan, so if anyone is willing to read the attached PDF and hack my labs, that would be greatly appreciated too...

Thanks very much,
Rick H.

# Thread: Beads of plastic in a toothpaste
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-03-17T18:05:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-115955>

[http://www.dentalbuzz.com/2014/03/04/crest-imbeds-plastic-in-our-gums/](http://www.dentalbuzz.com/2014/03/04/crest-imbeds-plastic-in-our-gums/)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-17T20:18:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-115975>

> Like I said, I’ve been seeing these blue particles flush out of patients’ gums for several months now. So has the co-hygienist in our office. So have many dental hygienists throughout the United States and Canada who have consulted with each other and realized that we have a major concern on our hands.

Nice to see people networking and taking health concerns into their own hands.
These are professionals who don't just read the glossy pamphlets and listen to the reps during their lunches together. They are observing and deducing and being proactive.

### Tanya — 2014-03-17T21:00:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-115996>

> high density polyethylene. That’s what [plastic trash cans](http://www.ehow.com/list_6918522_types-trash-cans-made-from_.html) are made from! If you throw away the box like most people do, the ingredients aren’t actually listed on the tube (sneaky, sneaky, Procter & Gamble!) However, I was able to track down the box [here at this link](http://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/lookup.cfm?setid=fbe1298d-77b1-4866-9883-5d744b184042). We’re not talking about polyethylene glycol, which is soluble in water. This stuff won’t dissolve in water, or even acetone or alcohol for that matter.

We were given travel sizes of that toothpaste during a dental check-up. It ended-up in a trash can because of the multitude of the ingredients in it, but it escaped my attention that one of them was polyethylene.

### sooperb — 2014-03-18T16:10:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-116201>

I haven't used mainstream toothpaste for years and years, I can only say thank goodness! My alternative, health food store toothpaste has come in for a lot of stick over the years, I'm the one smirking now. Thanks for the heads up, I will be spreading this far and wide.

### Tanya — 2014-03-18T16:17:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-116204>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=116201)
>
> I haven't used mainstream toothpaste for years and years, I can only say thank goodness! My alternative, health food store toothpaste has come in for a lot of stick over the years, I'm the one smirking now. Thanks for the heads up, I will be spreading this far and wide.

That is exactly what I have also been doing, Sue

# Thread: Toddler Constipation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Madison — 2014-03-17T20:25:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-115979>

My 2.5 year old goes though cycles of being constipated, and then being ok for a week or so. I have listened to Dr.Kruse in many podcast interviews, and read though some of this blog. My son's diet is clean, no gluten, and we cut out dairy but still include grass fed ghee. I know Dr. Kruse talks a lot about factors outside of diet. Is there anything that anyone can suggest for my child? I am considering doing a $400 comprehensive stool analysis, what other testing should I do? I know that having stool sitting in his bowl for days on end is leading to a pretty damaged gut. Any advice is greatly appreciated.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-17T20:28:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-115980>

Hi Madison. Does your son drink non-fluoridated water? Does he get much sunlight?

### kovita — 2014-03-17T20:40:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-115983>

madison, I happen to be mother to a boy who had intestinal surgery aged 8 mnoths which resulted in resection of whole ileocaecal piece. I learnt a lot about small child digestion ever since. My son tends to have more soft stools since the valve controlling the movement from small intestine is gone. He has accasionally constipation which mostly must be treated through enema. this is always followed by a few days of bloated belly (dysbiosis). In case of my son. i can see clear connection between the load on immune system and his digestion. Every small viral disease is pronounced in his digestion. What works for him is good amounts of healthy fats regulary, lot of fish, resistant starch, lots of veggies (soluble and insoluble fiber in balance), bone broths or at least great lakes gelatin daily. If the belly is bloated after some infection i give him course of sacharomycc.cerev., special e.coli strain used for ulcerative collitis, good probiotics. i have noticed, taht this winter he is doing better. What we did different is fermented cod liver oil daily, liver weekly at least, 3 hours daily outside, cold house, open windows in the night, wearing less cloths than other children and a lot of walking running on the fresh air. I try to control emf, we do switch off the whole circuit for the night. There is no universal recipe, but I can see one can do a lot with small adjustments to the daily routine.

### Madison — 2014-03-17T21:10:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-115999>

wow thank you for the responses.

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=115980)
>
> Hi Madison. Does your son drink non-fluoridated water? Does he get much sunlight?

We are outside every day, and we live in Florida so there is plenty of sunshine. We are just using the purifier that we routinely change from our refrigerator. I have been thinking that this could be the next step in trying to treat this. What kind of filtration system would you suggest?

### Madison — 2014-03-17T21:15:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-116005>

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=115983)
>
> madison, I happen to be mother to a boy who had intestinal surgery aged 8 mnoths which resulted in resection of whole ileocaecal piece. I learnt a lot about small child digestion ever since. My son tends to have more soft stools since the valve controlling the movement from small intestine is gone. He has accasionally constipation which mostly must be treated through enema. this is always followed by a few days of bloated belly (dysbiosis). In case of my son. i can see clear connection between the load on immune system and his digestion. Every small viral disease is pronounced in his digestion. What works for him is good amounts of healthy fats regulary, lot of fish, resistant starch, lots of veggies (soluble and insoluble fiber in balance), bone broths or at least great lakes gelatin daily. If the belly is bloated after some infection i give him course of sacharomycc.cerev., special e.coli strain used for ulcerative collitis, good probiotics. i have noticed, taht this winter he is doing better. What we did different is fermented cod liver oil daily, liver weekly at least, 3 hours daily outside, cold house, open windows in the night, wearing less cloths than other children and a lot of walking running on the fresh air. I try to control emf, we do switch off the whole circuit for the night. There is no universal recipe, but I can see one can do a lot with small adjustments to the daily routine.

Thank you for all the suggestions. I agree that there is no universal recipe, and like you I am willing to try any and all adjustments. We do not turn off our curcuit at night, my husband thinks I'm nuts to unplug the wifi, so I am slowly trying to move in that direction. We are at least going to move to hard wired internet in the next couple of months. I have never heard of that strain, I will look into it. What probiotic brand do you use? I have kept him on a clean diet with a multi, probiotics, and fats. I probably could up his fat intake. I will look into cod liver. Any other supplements? I have been giving him extra magnesium.

### kovita — 2014-03-17T22:16:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-116026>

I use different brands of probiotics and rotate them. My favourite is biocult. The truth is a just use them when I see bloated belly, normally both kids eat homemade sauerkraut or juice from it daily. They love it. My husband thinks I am nuts as well but after a few nights with the curcuit shuted down, he admitted he sleeps better. I do not use any other supplements but try to feed the kids real food instead. We do eat seaweeds regulary. I usually crash them in to small pieces while still dried and add it to the broths along with vegetables. They seem to like it. Magnesium is good idea.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-17T23:39:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-116043>

> [Madison said:](/goto/post?id=115999)
>
> wow thank you for the responses.
>
> We are outside every day, and we live in Florida so there is plenty of sunshine. We are just using the purifier that we routinely change from our refrigerator. I have been thinking that this could be the next step in trying to treat this. What kind of filtration system would you suggest?

That's great that he gets sunshine. When I was occasionally constipated, I used the sunshine as a stimulant. Works quickly.
Jack has several recent blogs on water. He recommends buying reverse osmosis (RO) water for reversing disease. Filters can't remove much fluoride.

# Thread: Anxiety after dietary meltdown?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-after-dietary-meltdown.10213/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Julie5 — 2014-03-18T07:08:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-after-dietary-meltdown.10213/post-116128>

What a bad day I had a couple of days ago! I don't know what was going on. I have been doing so well the last couple of months even with the death of my father in law and guests who stayed with us for 7 weeks. Then they left, and BAM, I got hit with weak will and ate whatever I could find. I repented and the next day I got right back on the wagon. But there has been fallout from it. Primarily, anxiety. I don't have anxiety problems. Any idea what the connection is between a one day deviation and anxiety a few days later?

Also, it has been hard to get back to feeling satisfied with 2 meals a day. The cravings are back. Of course, I am out of ketosis. Ugh! It was NOT worth it!

### caroline — 2014-03-18T07:28:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-after-dietary-meltdown.10213/post-116133>

Hi Julie.....just jump back on the leptin reset .....with the BAB ...enough protein and it will knock out the cravings! don't worry about 2 meals - go for 3 meals if you need them. Lots of folks here have 3 meals a day.

You have been under so much stress ..... and I can't even imagine having guests for 7 weeks ...YIKES! I would have been a basket case - no question!

Up your water intake and get lots and lots of sunshine and grounding. Don't forget the three legged stool!

Cut yourself some slack - go for long walks and climb over some rocks - make yourself a mini movnat!

Make yourself a gratitude journal and hug your DH and family.......yoga? meditation?

Sometimes life happens ..... xo

I probably would have fallen off for way more than a day! you did good!

### Julie5 — 2014-03-18T07:46:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-after-dietary-meltdown.10213/post-116136>

THanks! I needed that!

# Thread: R Alpha Lipoic Acid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/r-alpha-lipoic-acid.10214/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-18T07:08:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/r-alpha-lipoic-acid.10214/post-116129>

To my surprise there is no thread created to discuss R Alpha Lipoic Acid (R-ALA and ALA), so let's create one.

My questions on this supplement are:

1) At what level are we possibly going to see toxicity?

2) If you are taking this to improve insulin resistance, how far ahead of a meal should you take it?

3) How long does the chemical remain active and affect insulin resistance?

4) Is it important to always take it with some other supplement, specifically for the indication of insulin resistance? I have been taking with Creatine HCL (a more absorbable form of Creatine Monophosphate) on the thought that ALA will help creatine get into the cells.

5) By upregulating GLUT1 and GLUT4, is this chemical helping glucose to get stored as glycogen preferably over body fat? Or will storage to body fat be equally favored to glycogen?

My early experience with this supplement suggests it is extremely powerful as a way to regulate glucose levels. But I'm having tremendous problems getting the dosage correct and getting the timing correct.

I always start by reading studies on toxicity. I couldn't find any animal or human studies that showed any measurable toxicity, even at very very high doses. That's good. I seem to have a pretty wide runway here to experiment without putting myself at much risk.

I have been using up to a maximum about 1,800 mg per day, usually one 200 mg pill right before each meal, and up to two 200 mg ALA doses when glucose is over 130 and I am having any kind of starchy carb.

I have been seeing a pattern last few weeks where I lose control of glucose in the morning, and recover it in afternoon and at night. Typically I wake with fasting glucose around 80 to 95, which quickly rises with no food to 105. With "Big Ass Breakfast" just the protein alone can take me to glucose at 138. Then I take maybe 20 grams of carbs with MCT Oil and I top off 140 to 150. It's downhill for the rest of the day, and I am back to the 80s and 90s by evening.

The ALA is slow to have its effect in morning, but once it does take hold I start coming down rapidly. If I combine it with exercise I can start printing glucose numbers in the 60s mg/dL and that's too low. So this is a bit of a shotgun when I need a hunting rifle, and it can result in my going from 130 mg/dL to 70 mg/dL and sticking under 90 mg/dL for an extended time.

Has anyone seen any studies that show a methodology for taking ALA before/with meals, and what is the minimum required dose that achieves most of the benefit? I see a lot of people online recommending a single 200 mg dose per \*day\*, and that certainly has not had any noticeable impact on my glucose. Larger doses with meals alternate between slight and massive effects, and controlling the result is proving hard.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: What to eat the day before a blood draw?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-eat-the-day-before-a-blood-draw.10215/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2014-03-18T09:56:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-eat-the-day-before-a-blood-draw.10215/post-116153>

Do you do anything different before a blood draw? The day before? a few days before? Do you cut back a little on the protein and throw in some more veg?
My Cholesterol is already high - should I cool it with the fat and protein for few days?
Don't want my Doc to have heart failure!

### Inger — 2014-03-18T10:31:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-eat-the-day-before-a-blood-draw.10215/post-116158>

You can help your Doc not worry too much

### Lahelada — 2014-03-18T11:44:09+0200

I would not do anything different either as you want to know what happens when you eat what you eat,if anything .

### nicld — 2014-03-18T16:14:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-eat-the-day-before-a-blood-draw.10215/post-116202>

# Thread: Mystery of a Neolithic Switch from Seafood To Milk
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mystery-of-a-neolithic-switch-from-seafood-to-milk.10221/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-18T20:31:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mystery-of-a-neolithic-switch-from-seafood-to-milk.10221/post-116245>

This is published on a site called Bio-News, Texas, which I hadn't heard of before. It's a story that's been doing the rounds, but is here covered very fully.

A team from Bristol University think they can show that there was a switch from a high use of marine resources in the Mesolithic period to a dpendence on dairy products in the Neolithic in the British Isles, including "mainland Britain, the Scottish isles and the isles of Man, and Ireland":

> The Bristol team used a compound-specific carbon isotope technique they have developed to identify the actual fats preserved in the cooking pots, showing that dairy products dominated the menu right across Britain and Ireland as soon as cattle and sheep arrived.

The story of the markers they were looking for is quite interesting -- this is based on static isotope analysis and on the analysis of fat remains left on cooking pots. There's controversy about the interpretation of results. For example, there's an argument that the skeletons used may not have been representative.

On the cooking-pot residues:

> This is a highly sensitive means of investigating the significance of marine product processing in pottery. Although the characteristic long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA) distributions of fresh marine fats and oils are lost from degraded marine lipids owing to rapid oxidation and polymerization, their recent work has identified more stable marine biomarkers, including alkanoic acids and vicinal dihydroxy acids originating from degradation of poly- and monounsaturated fatty acids, respectively. Although these products may exist only at low concentrations, operating the gas chromatograph/mass spectrometer (GC/MS) in selected ion-monitoring mode greatly enhances sensitivity of the analyses and makes picogram per gram concentrations routinely detectable.

Here's the conclusion:

> Viewed together the study findings show that Early British hunters ate a diet rich in venison and wild boar as well as eating quantities of seafood, including seals and shellfish. With the introduction of domestic animals some 6,000 years ago they quickly gave up wild foods and fishing was largely abandoned with people adopting a new diet based around dairying.
>
> Dr. Cramp notes that: “Amazingly, it was another 4,000 years before seafood remains appeared in pots again, during the Iron Age, and it was only with the arrival of the Vikings that fish became a significant part of our diet.”
>
> Why people in the British Isles changed so abruptly from a seafood to farming diet remains a mystery.

[http://bionews-tx.com/news/2014/02/...ish-peoples-switched-seafood-milk-4000-years/](http://bionews-tx.com/news/2014/02/20/mystery-neolithic-british-peoples-switched-seafood-milk-4000-years/)

And here's the original paper:

[http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/281/1780/20132372.full.pdf+html](http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/281/1780/20132372.full.pdf+html)

### Josh — 2014-03-20T01:05:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mystery-of-a-neolithic-switch-from-seafood-to-milk.10221/post-116574>

Maybe that's why the vikings were able to come and make so much trouble....

### nonchalant — 2014-03-20T02:20:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mystery-of-a-neolithic-switch-from-seafood-to-milk.10221/post-116586>

I think that the local chieftains and shamans got a tribute on each head of livestock so they encouraged the locals to eat dairy.

### cinnamon — 2014-03-20T04:22:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-posts.10224/post-116600>

I think it works by tagging the posts as "read". The "new posts" list is in two parts. First it shows all the posts that you have not "read" that were created since the last time you did something on the forum (could be reading or posting or even just refreshing the new post list). Then it shows posts that are older than that but which have not yet been tagged as "read" for your user id (posts that showed up the last time you clicked "new posts" but that you haven't yet got to looking at would be here) .

Now, what it means by "read"? My guess is that if you visited the page that the post is on, it gets tagged as read. Also, if you actually created the post. And there is also an option to "mark all posts as read". You would use this last one if you were away for a few days with no chance of getting caught up.

And yup, I don't think the thread you posted in will show up in "new posts" until there is a new post _after_ yours.

### persistence — 2014-03-23T22:12:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-t3.10227/post-117452>

Thanks for the pointer to your test list!

I think the overall description of my problems is in my journal here:
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...igh-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/)

However, the quick summary:

\- I was following a low carb diet to bring down prediabetic glucose numbers. This started a weight loss that I had problems controlling, and that weight loss left me extremely weak.

\- I continue to see prediabetic glucose numbers, typically high in morning and tapering off over the course of the day.

\- I have extremely high LDL particle counts (using NMR Lipoprofile test).

I have started to supplement with D-Ribose and this has helped me recover from exercise and regain energy quite a bit.

I am going through the Leptin Rx slowly and we will see how much that changes.

Having done a lot of reading, my best theory on what might be happening with me:

1) Failure of glucose metabolism = prediabetes. \*probably\* I am slightly insulin resistant, but this is yet to be proven.

2) Assuming I am insulin resistant, high insulin = high production of HMG CoA Reductase = high production of small, dense LDL particles

3) I may have some milder version of familial hypercholesterolemia, or alternately it may just be high levels of cholesterol I get from a Paleo diet, but either of those can cause LDL receptors that would normally clear LDL from the blood to downregulate. So the extra LDL I biosynthesize is not getting cleared from the body.

There is a famous researcher named Goldstein who has studied LDL receptors and Figure 3 in this summary from 1984 shows how the LDL receptors can be broken when you eat a high fat diet rich in cholesterol (which I certainly do):

[http://www.jlr.org/content/25/13/1450.full.pdf](http://www.jlr.org/content/25/13/1450.full.pdf)

It's not the cholesterol that causes high LDL!!! Rather, the cholesterol downregulates your LDL receptors. And if you have \*something else\* causing generation of large numbers of LDL particles, the body won't process them correctly and they hang around in your blood.

So - in the big picture - I need to find a way to get my insulin levels low, to stop overproduction of small, dense LDL. And I \*may\* also have some issue with needing to limit maximum cholesterol intake in diet.

My guess is if I could get glucose and insulin levels low, I would take out the prediabetes and probably make a big dent in the LDL cholesterol particle counts. Whatever LDL-P particles remain after "fixing" insulin I would probably need to address through diet.

### JanSz — 2014-03-23T23:46:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-t3.10227/post-117479>

It may be about resistant starch.

Not sure how resistant starch fits EpiPaleo-Rx.
EpiPaleo-Rx is about being without carbs. Starch is a carbohydrate.
By definition we (the people) do not consume (directly) resistant starch, just pass it thru.
But our intestinal bacteria have a field day with it.
With out it, we end up with less of intestinal bacteria.

Try cold boiled potatoes and rice.
Or better yet 4 tablespoons/day of unmodified Potato Starch.

[http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B004VLVCGU/ref=oh\_details\_o00\_s00\_i00?ie=UTF8&psc=1](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B004VLVCGU/ref=oh_details_o00_s00_i00?ie=UTF8&psc=1)

\------------
With this in mind I will have to re-listen to
January 2014 Webinar – Resistant Starch

going by my notes

Nuts---walnuts, almond, pistachio, pine nuts
they contain natural resistant starch
they make hydroxy-buthyrate
no cooking of nuts
increase bifido bacteria
also
include fermented meats, salami, prosciuto, ham
\============================================

After I listened to that webinar I realised that many nuts are heat treated.

..

You may also try
Butyrate (Calcium/Magnesium) 250 capsules (600mg)
[http://www.bodybio.com/storeproduct361.aspx](http://www.bodybio.com/storeproduct361.aspx)

//

People eating 4 tablespoons/day of unmodified Potato Starch,
report plummeting HgA1c.

I will tell you latter if I am among those people.
I have just started on unmodified starch and plan on adding cold potatoes and rice.
Since I started following my version of EpiPaleo-Rx my fasting glucose went up significantly.

//
Right now it would be about reversing trend, unmodified potato starch is a single bullet type of help. We are usually more complicated than that.
Likely variety of unmodified starches is long range way to go.
So likely nuts have their place. I will just have to figure out if natural (not heat treated) nuts are still available.

////

### persistence — 2014-03-24T06:21:55+0200

The people who have done that stool test reported good results. But what does it really prove overall? What if too much potato starch releases massive amounts of serotonin in the gut, and this in turn blocks the proper repair and formation of new bone? Is the price of better sleep and better bacteria counts that we promote osteoporosis?

I'm pretty sure that raw potato starch is a lot more powerful than any dietary fiber I have ever taken before, and that just makes me a little bit cautious about it. Long-term studies would be nice. There must be some out there.

If it had lowered my glucose substantially I would probably be doing the experiment. Since it did not have that effect for me, I am not diving in too quickly.

>

Like Jack said, problem is that to do good and bad we use same key.

You must figure out better way, being afraid without action to change predicament, just increases stress.

If you know for sure that your bones are going to go, that knowledge gives you power.
Do
Dexa scan now (Current bones status)
Collagen Cross-Linked N-Telopeptide (NTx), 24-Hour Urine (36421X)
bone loss (currrent)

Load up on vit D, K2, magnessium and anything else you may think of that may improve your bones.
Then,
for a year go max with resistant starch.
Evaluate if all that helps you or hurts you and where.
\-----------

You said that you follow **Paul Jaminet diet** at least good part of it.
[http://perfecthealthdiet.com/the-diet/](http://perfecthealthdiet.com/the-diet/)

There is a one pound a day of starches there.

Make sure that you stick to their recommendations in regard to safe starches
potatoes (cold)
rice (cold)
then mind dr Jack emphasis on
nuts (not heat treated)
fermented meats, salami, prosciuto, ham

....

### persistence — 2014-03-24T20:45:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-t3.10227/post-117651>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=117636)
>
> Like Jack said, problem is that to do good and bad we use same key.
>
> You must figure out better way, being afraid without action to change predicament, just increases stress.
>
> If you know for sure that your bones are going to go, that knowledge gives you power.
> Do
> Dexa scan now (Current bones status)
> Collagen Cross-Linked N-Telopeptide (NTx), 24-Hour Urine (36421X)
> bone loss (currrent)
>
> Load up on vit D, K2, magnessium and anything else you may think of that may improve your bones.
> Then,
> for a year go max with resistant starch.
> Evaluate if all that helps you or hurts you and where.

I'm popping so many pills, making so many lifestyle changes, etc. No one would accuse me of paralysis.

But at the same time I usually have a method for deciding if something is safe to take. The first thing I do is look for toxicity. What does the literature say about a dose where something is dangerous? Then I establish the dose at which there is a benefit. The things that are a "no brainer" to take are ones where there is a benefit at a low dose, and the toxicity is at very high doses.

The items I tend to avoid completely are the ones where the benefit and the toxicity live within a hair width of each other. I won't name anything specific, but as an example if I read somewhere that the average diet gets 2 mg/day of X and there is benefit for supplementing up to 3 mg/day of X, but other studies say that you go bonking crazy and develop neuromuscular diseases at 5 mg/day, then probably I am not going to do that experiment. The tolerance for error there is too small.

Potato starch is for me a case somewhere in between. I have problems defining the toxicity, or quantifying where it occurs. I would like to go slow and read more. And for now potato starch is not stopping me from going anywhere I need to go, in the short term.

> You said that you follow **Paul Jaminet diet** at least good part of it.
> [http://perfecthealthdiet.com/the-diet/](http://perfecthealthdiet.com/the-diet/)
>
> There is a one pound a day of starches there.
>
> Make sure that you stick to their recommendations in regard to safe starches
> potatoes (cold)
> rice (cold)
> then mind dr Jack emphasis on
> nuts (not heat treated)
> fermented meats, salami, prosciuto, ham
> ....

I have many food intolerances related to a wheat intolerance, so my starchy carbs for now are limited to tubers (yams, taro, etc). Yes, I am definitely eating from the "super safe" starch list.

I have been playing with 40 to 120 grams of starchy carbs per day. From the standpoint of prediabetes, I suspect that going ketogenic would be a very good direction. But so far when I get too low on carbs I drain glycogen and get weak. My endo wants me to be on 120 grams/day of carbs. Finding D-Ribose has allowed me to recover faster from exercise, so this gives me a little support to continue experimenting on the low end of carbs.

# Thread: Probiotics Producing Nutrients for us in Our Guts?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-producing-nutrients-for-us-in-our-guts.10241/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-03-19T18:31:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-producing-nutrients-for-us-in-our-guts.10241/post-116453>

Of course, will the Beta-Carotene work for Humans as a Vitamin A source....?

> Vitamin A deficiency (VAD) is an overwhelming public health problem that affects hundreds of millions of people worldwide. A definitive solution to VAD has yet to be identified. Because it is an essential nutrient, vitamin A or its carotenoid precursor β-carotene can only be obtained from food or supplements. In this study, we wanted to establish whether β-carotene produced in the mouse intestine by bacteria synthesizing the provitamin A carotenoid could be delivered to various tissues within the body. To achieve this, we took advantage of the_Escherichia coli_ MG1655\*, an intestine-adapted spontaneous mutant of _E. coli_MG1655, and the plasmid pAC-BETA, containing the genes coding for the 4 key enzymes of the β-carotene biosynthetic pathway (geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate synthase, phytoene synthase, phytoene desaturase, and lycopene cyclase) from_Erwinia herbicola_. We engineered the _E. coli_ MG1655\* to produce β-carotene during transformation with pAC-BETA (MG1655\*-βC) and gavaged wild-type and knockout mice for the enzyme β-carotene 15,15′-oxygenase with this recombinant strain. Various regimens of bacteria administration were tested (single vs. multiple and low vs. high doses). β-Carotene concentration was measured by HPLC in mouse serum, liver, intestine, and feces. Enumeration of MG1655\*-βC cells in the feces was performed to assess efficiency of intestinal colonization. We demonstrated in vivo that probiotic bacteria could be used to deliver vitamin A to the tissues of a mammalian host. These results have the potential to pave the road for future investigations aimed at identifying alternative, novel approaches to treat VAD.

[http://jn.nutrition.org/content/early/2014/03/05/jn.113.188391.abstract](http://jn.nutrition.org/content/early/2014/03/05/jn.113.188391.abstract)

[http://www.nutraingredients.com/Res...obiotics-be-modified-to-produce-beta-carotene](http://www.nutraingredients.com/Research/Gut-bacteria-to-battle-vitamin-A-deficiency-Could-probiotics-be-modified-to-produce-beta-carotene)

Note map of worldwide Vit A issues....

# Thread: Why CAM and "Alternative Medicine" Literature Drives Me Nuts...

**Subforum:** The Cave

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cam-and-alternative-medicine-literature-drives-me-nuts.10247/post-116490>

Everything is treated as equal. There is no rigorous review and application of the principles in a given system. Nor is there rigorous application of science such as we are working out here. They just take a treatment and try it and see what happens. Then they review the efficacy of the system for a condition which is also not rigorously reviewed at the conceptual level based on one poorly designed trial. Then someone writes a review like this which really only further confuses patients and practitioners....

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3923011/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3923011/)

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9

### caroline — 2014-03-20T09:02:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-rash-or-the-heeby-jeebies.10253/post-116636>

I have a rash all over my arms - it started just from the elbow down and now is my whole arm. It is so itchy.
I haven't done anything different today ... haven't eaten anything different...
I am just wondering if it could have been the ocean - but I would have it all over.

Has anyone got any thoughts please?

go figure - I am having blood work done tomorrow morning....always something weird happens.

### kovita — 2014-03-20T09:52:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-rash-or-the-heeby-jeebies.10253/post-116637>

Caroline, it sucks! I do not hVe much personal experience with rashes and there are just about thousands reasons for a mystery rash to appear. I always look into my experience with kids, it is somehow good to have them around becUse they are young and relatively well working organisms so they can give you good feedback. I observed my kids would have accassionally rash after viral infections. It is sort of goose skin rash, that would clear up by itself,my thoughts are this must be connected with the clearance after the cytokine storm caused by accute disease. The younger one had very often rash often during the months following the intestinal surgery. It was emergency surgery and he had some viral infection that time so postop pneumonia was imminent and he received a ton of different antibiotics to cover everything. I personally reached conclusion the rash was connected again to the body getting rid of rubbish and balancing back to it's former homeostasis. He got this again after the MMR vaccine last year, again was o. and off for aprox 3 months and then over.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-20T17:27:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-rash-or-the-heeby-jeebies.10253/post-116705>

### sooperb — 2014-03-20T17:49:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-rash-or-the-heeby-jeebies.10253/post-116714>

### Inger — 2014-03-20T18:00:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-rash-or-the-heeby-jeebies.10253/post-116717>

I had a weird rash many weeks ago on my neck, on one side. It was like a big coin, and it was there for maybe 3 weeks or so! It was very weird. With dry skin along the outer line of the "coin", a bit reddish. It itched sometimes a tiny bit. I did not do anything special... I just sprayed some rose water on it as I do on my face too.. and one day it just disappeared!

I think our body detoxes and does weird things once in a while....

You could try to put some idodine water on it? Or coconut oil? Or use the urine therapy  or some olive oil.. or spray rose water on it or something else that comes to your mind or just let it be.  I am sure it will heal soon!

### caroline — 2014-03-21T02:01:17+0200

I have been feeling the EMF in my hands when on my laptop for a long while now.....but for some reason yesterday it just really accelerated. I wish I had thought to take a picture.

This was not just a simple rash moving up my arm ... It was scary.

This morning I can feel the vibrations in my whole arm ..... what a wake up call!

I can't even begin to imagine what Dr. k. did to himself with his cell phone ....I do hope his wife gave him a very stern talking to! He is so important to us... I can't imagine how distressed Sandy must be.

It is curious why it would happen now tho .... but in another thread we are talking about being way more sensitive.....

### caroline — 2014-03-21T07:36:31+0200

Anyway - where my bracelet WAS - the inflammation and rash was way, way worse - the whole area surrounding and above and below the bracelet. It was really red and burning and raised bumps.

that made me wonder about the titanium necklace/cross that Dr. K. wears - is it not possible that it is an antenna for EMF??? I worry about him.....

Actually - I have ben wondering about his necklace for ages now .... doesn't it seem possible? We talked about that in another thread and we seem to think it is better to ditch all the jewelry. I know my very cute copper bracelet was a disaster - it definitely was attracting bad juju.

### Inger — 2014-03-21T08:37:57+0200

That is so cool you found out what is the cause Audrey... I very much agree it must be the EMF! You need to limit it a bit me thinks... try to stay on your laptop as little as possible - or make a 3 day 100% break, just let us know and we will not worry you does not show up here. # Thread: Laptop or monitor?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2014-03-22T04:17:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117073>

Does a laptop emit way more Emf? Or is it just being closer?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-03-22T04:52:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117076>

From where I work, my little laptop (Macbook Air 2013 mid model) doesn't emit much EMF in the 1.5-8GHz range. Usually around 0.01-0.03 uW/cm2

The ELF magnetic field (in the 20-2000MHz range) is horrible close up, basically around 4-10mG where my hands are. then around 0.5-1.0mG about 10cm away, and once you're a foot away, the magnetic field from the laptop is basically not as significant as other sources around me (I turned off the laptop to check, and my ambient ELF field hovers around 0.2-0.3mG where I work).

Michael Neuert's standards for reference: [http://emfinfo.org/guidelines-exposure.html](http://emfinfo.org/guidelines-exposure.html) . As usual, we need to test for ourselves.

### caroline — 2014-03-22T07:29:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117095>

So I got this horrible rash up and down my arms - to my shoulders ....rash doesn't even properly describe it. I am positive it was EMF from my laptop.
The night it happened - for some reason - I had my right hand on the keyboard a lot and my left arm leaning on it and I was leaning over it.

If I got a separate mouse ... would that solve the problem Yew? I have a separate keyboard - but find it frustrating so don't use it - but I will have too.

Even thou I am wired in - close up it is emitting huge EMF???

But I guess the biggest problem is the keyboard? I can also feel it in my face.

so - short of throwing it in the garbage - which will be Dr. K.'s suggestion ....a separate mouse -that is not wifi - should help considerably>

but maybe a whole separate monitor ..... so everything is farther away?

### Inger — 2014-03-22T07:50:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117096>

I would say separate wired mouse and keyboard. Also keeps you further away from the computer.

I have 2 computers one laptop and one with monitor, wired mouse and keyboard. The laptop has pretty bad readings close to where you put your hands when using it. I use a wired mouse when I use my laptop, always. If I have to write more I try to not use the laptop but the other computer instead.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-03-22T08:04:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117097>

Basically anything that would get you away from the actual processing unit of the computer (which is likely the thing that emits the most EMF) is a good thing. I'd second Inger's idea of a separate wired mouse and keyboard; I did do EMF measurements of an external mouse and keyboard, and found them to be pretty low (<0.01 uW/cm2).

I'm reminded by the common quote from Dr Kruse that "skin and brain are both formed from neuroectoderm". Skin issues always trigger a circadian system warning in my mind. I assume you're still using the blue blockers when on the computer?

Do you suspect any other thing that could have led to a change in your EMF environment? I know that my neighbours are starting to turn on the heating more often now that the mornings are getting into the low teens.

### Inger — 2014-03-22T08:30:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117103>

One more idea.. I do this too. Just take your hand off the mouse/keyboard/laptop always when not actively typing or browsing. Try to touch it as little as possible. Easily one can forget and let the hand rest on the mouse - big mistake!
Remember what Jack has told us about collagen.... and the skin... touching the mouse will affect your whole body!!!

### Inger — 2014-03-22T08:32:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117104>

I am not using blue blockers when I am on the computer at day time... maybe I should... But I sit by the window and I want the daylight in...
I should try it out and see if there is a difference. But I hate using any glasses at daytime... I never use them, not even when driving in strong sunlight.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-22T08:39:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117105>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=117095)
>
> If I got a separate mouse ... would that solve the problem Yew? I have a separate keyboard - but find it frustrating so don't use it - but I will have too.

Much the best idea to. Make sure it's a USB keyboard and not a bluetooth one, though.

EMF aside a separate keyboard is better for biomechanical reasons -- whenever you're not actually on the move and can.

There's a reason for that — besides saving wear-and-tear on the built-in keyboard. It's this: on a laptop the keyboard and screen are _too close_.

With DSE (Display Screen Equipment) you get the chair adjusted so that you're sitting normally. This means sitting on what the Germans call the _Sitz_ bones (and not on the end of the spine):

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tuberosity\_of\_the\_ischium](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tuberosity_of_the_ischium)

and with your feet flat on the floor.

The keyboard should then be at such a height that your forearms are horizontal. And the top of the screen should be just below eye-level when sitting up comfortably straight (relaxed and not rigid).

This isn't possible with a laptop. You must needs either: (1) have the screen at the right height, at which height your wrists will be cocked up — which is courting RSI (Repetitive Strain Injury) and unnecessary shoulder and neck tension; or (2) set the keyboard at the right height which means you must slump or slouch in some way to see the screen properly — and that's again causing shoulder and neck tension with consequent pain, headaches and so on, and also possible problems with the lumbar spine.

An external USB keyboard solves that problem, because it decouples the keyboard from the screen. You can use a desk or table (or adjust one) to put the keyboard at the right height and put the open laptop up on a box or a pile of large books or something to bring the screen up high enough.

One large company I worked at had dedicated "docking stations" for every laptop user. When at work they'd plug in and make use of an external keyboard _and_ an adjustable desk-mounted external VDU. I don't know how common that is, but companies don't spend that money for nothing. They're legally required to provide a safe environment for DSE users and have to show wiling by doing that sort of thing.

> but maybe a whole separate monitor ..... so everything is farther away?

It's probably better, if possible. But using the laptop's own screen and raising it up on books or something would work instead and be a cheaper option.

On your original question -- "Does a laptop emit way more Emf" -- the video Jack has online seems to imply that laptops are not ususally grounded. Else why have a video called "How to ground a laptop"?

IIRC, if yours isn't, you snip off a USB cable and plug the it into a USB socket on the laptop, attaching the other end to a grounding cord.

[http://jackkruse.com/grounding-your-computer/](http://jackkruse.com/grounding-your-computer/)

### caroline — 2014-03-22T08:45:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117106>

Thanks Inger and Yew........I am just thinking that I have changed locations of where I use my computer. I used to use it at my breakfast bar and sit on a stool. now I sit at a table on my balcony or my dining table. At the table it is in line with my chest. At the breakfast bar it is more in line with my hips. Even as I type now I can feel burning in my face and arms. This is scary!

I don't think anything has changed in my environment.........

But it sees to me that a monitor might be considerably better?

### caroline — 2014-03-22T08:51:03+0200

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-22T08:59:35+0200

Give it a wipe down with something non-toxic to be on the safe side?

### caroline — 2014-03-22T09:08:35+0200

That "hunching over" is what you've got to watch from the point of view of "posture" (really "posture" is too static a term to cover it). > I am probably the only toxic thing around here ....lol

I had a friend who came up in a rash all over his hands after using a keyboard after someone else who wasn't perhaps very hygenic. I never would have believed it. I'm sure you're non-toxic but careful who you allow to use your PC! LOL.

# Thread: Raw liver and the runs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-liver-and-the-runs.10284/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### healthman — 2014-03-22T15:48:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-liver-and-the-runs.10284/post-117141>

Why would raw liver cause the severe runs?

### seanb4 — 2014-03-23T13:26:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-liver-and-the-runs.10284/post-117348>

Parasites or bacteria from being raw? I had the same with raw fish

### Inger — 2014-03-23T14:11:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-liver-and-the-runs.10284/post-117350>

I have eaten raw liver for years and it never caused me the runs. hmmm.. Was it good quality? I eat only grassfed beef liver, game liver, lambs liver or other high quality stuff. That said.... I have at work (waitress) had some regular beef liver raw the chefs offered me and that was not organic for sure - did not make me sick either. I have eaten raw liver that has stayed in my fridge for months on a wooden plate open to air.. and tasted pretty sour! But it did not make me sick either.

You might need to be careful with quality if your gut is not strong. Not plastic wrapped long time stored stuff either or if it tastes odd. Did it have an odd taste? It needs to taste nice - like fresh liver - you really can taste the quality IMO, especially if eaten plain and raw...

Maybe try to fry it gently on the outside and leave it raw inside thus killing some bacteria on the surface your gut is not used to yet? I have also heard if eating too much raw liver one could get the runs because the body just do not wanted that much vit. A (or something else in it who knows!), but I never had that issue. But I know someone else who had. Have you eaten much liver lately?
Liver is something I eat here and there, never regularly or force me to. When I get appetite of it I eat it! But that is not very often. I sometimes eat much and sometimes I go months without.

Hi!

Does anyone have a reliable source about the amount of vit. D in salmon roe? I've been eating a lot of it lately for the DHA, but read on the Weston Price website that 1 Tablespoon of salmon roe has 17,000 units of Vit. D! This sounds too good to be true! I can't seem to find any other information about this on the internet. WP doesn't specify which type of Vit. D it is high in...

I am eating a few tablespoons per day, I am wondering if I should be cutting back on my D3/K2 drops! Not just in relation to fear of overdosing, but also in terms of cutting back on costs of supplements since I've been spending more money on roe!

Thanks!

### Miguel Toribio-Mateas — 2014-03-23T19:39:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-buy-access-to-premium-materials-because-of-faulty-handling-of-non-us-payments-please-fix-it.10294/post-117415>

Hi Jack,

Firstly congratulations on you work. I'm hooked!

Secondly, I couldn't buy access to the 90-Day Reset programme last week. The form kept failing. I had to contact your customer support, who kindly created another link for me to use. This gave me the same problem. Apparently it was something to do with the form not recognising my billing address. I only have one billing address registered for all my cards. I used 4 different cards and they all failed.

Last Thursday I got a call from my bank saying that 4 times a payment had been taken from my account, and then refunded. It was you guys. My bank says that it is due to a faulty handling of the payment, which was coming from an UK bank, possibly by using the only option available for non-US, non-Australian customers: "My state is not listed".

To cut a long story short, I missed the early bird and now I can't even buy access at full price. Your customer support has stopped answering my emails. So, basically, I'm stuck. There must be a way to solve this? Have you had any other UK customers with the same issue?

Look forward to being able to buy access to your premium materials!

Thank you!

Miguel

# Thread: Mummy scans
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mummy-scans.10298/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T13:05:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mummy-scans.10298/post-117564>

> Thanks to a new exhibition at the British Museum, the public can finally gain a glimpse into what lies beneath after eight mummies underwent CAT scans at London hospitals, proving the most detailed picture yet of our Egyptian forbears.
>
> Ancient Lives: New Discoveries, which opens on May 22, reveals that despite the separation of centuries, the Egyptians suffered from the same health issues that plague us today, including high cholesterol, fatty diets and bad toothache. ...

How one can deduce "fatty diets" from a CAT scan is a mystery to me, but never mind. This is the mainstream all over, isn't it?

Later on they tell us:

> Two of the eight mummies tested were found to have a build-up in their legs of plaque - essentially cholesterol, calcium and tissue - suggesting they suffered from heart problems.
>
> This can be caused by a rich diet high in fat, or it can be genetic.

So this is how they say they deduced "fatty diets" from the scans. So fat in the diet causes calcium to be deposited in arteries? How? ...

> Many experienced poor dental health and had multiple abscesses, which if left untreated, may have led to inflammation of the throat, asphyxiation and ultimately death.

I suppose that was caused by "fatty diets", too.

> Analysis of digestive remains suggested the Egyptians enjoyed a wide diet that included fish, a little meat, beer, bread and sugar-rich fruits, such as dates.

So here we actually get some real experimental findings -- as opposed to interpretation posing as findings. When actually looking at the digestive remains, they found among other things: "beer, bread and sugar-rich fruits, such as dates".

No chance the beer and bread and "sugar-rich fruits" could have caused the dental problems -- and the atherosclerosis?

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencet...ians-used-hospital-CAT-scans-closer-look.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-2587224/Secrets-ancient-mummies-tattoos-revealed-historians-used-hospital-CAT-scans-closer-look.html)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-24T18:27:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mummy-scans.10298/post-117610>

Fatty diets... a little meat.... Mike, didn't you know that they ate a lot of "vegetable oil"? ;-)

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T19:36:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mummy-scans.10298/post-117633>

It turns out there's a slightly better report at the Telegraph -- although much of the wording is very similar.

> Daniel Antoine, the museum’s curator of physical anthropology and curator of the coming exhibition, said: “The scans showed two of them with plaque in the leg.
>
> “That is an indication of cardiovascular disease that can lead to a heart attack or stroke. This might have been caused by lifestyle which means a diet rich in fat or it might be genetic

So the comment is traceable to the museum's curator of physical anthropology.

As I say … I wonder about that calcium. Dr. Rosedale points out:

> If you put endothelial cells, the inner lining of your arteries, in a Petri dish and expose them to leptin, they will build bone.

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2013...sedale-keep-the-butter-and-give-up-the-bagel/](http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2013/05/12/sam-should-i-give-up-butter-on-my-bagel-ron-rosedale-keep-the-butter-and-give-up-the-bagel/)

Rosedale's analysis seems plausible to me. It certainly seems possible to me that these Ancient Egyptians may have been leptin resistant. But then I'm not a physical anthropologist. I don't like to criticise people in their own fields, but I just don't see what "fatty diets" would have to do with the deposition of calcium in arteries, while Rosedale's explanation sounds plausible and is based on work published in _Science_ in 2002. Also the physical anthropologist seems to be pulling the "fatty diets" out the air: it's not that the've found evidential trace of the diets being "fatty" in the form of stomach remains, or cooking-pot residues or … well, anything, really.

Another snippet from the _Telegraph_:

> The scientists at the British Museum even studied bread found with the remains of the mummies to see if it had a high content of abrasive sand that might have contributed to tooth decay – it didn’t.

I'm not surprised, and I bet you're not.

You might wear your teeth down a bit with grit, but why should it make them rot? Sugar and starch again.

The Eskimo man in the bottom left has teeth that are worn down almost to the gums by grit that's settled on the fish dried in windrows that his people ate. But he has no tooth decay:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price5.html](http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price5.html)

It depresses a little me that this stuff keeps coming round.

I found out what "Bro Science" means today. Vinnie Tortorich explained it on his podcast. Apparently, it's the sort of "facts" about human physiology that people down the gym believe, and that don't really have anything behind them but have stuck through constant repetition. I guess the belief that arterial plaque is caused by "a diet rich in fat" might be Bro Science.

# Thread: New Labs - Please Hack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-please-hack.10301/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Paul S — 2014-03-24T17:19:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-please-hack.10301/post-117601>

Ran new labs for first time since last fall. Been on the Kruse wagon for 2 1/2 yrs. 39 yo male. strict Keto-epi-paleo for 5 months, epi-paleo/paleo for past 2+ years. Sleep very good (using Magnetico for past 10 mo.). I fall asleep easily (9-10pm) and up easy (5:30 - 6:30am, typically). Salivary cortisol assay shows correct pattern through the day, however, within that pattern, I am low/normal across the 4 measurement points. Rev Osmosis water for past 10 mo (1 - 1.5 gal/day). CT 4-5x per week (off and on for past 18 months), most recent 6 weeks has been consistently about 15 min per day at 40 degrees. Body comp is good (6'1"; 175#, 10% body fat) and has been consistently at that level since starting paleo 2 1/2 yrs ago. Workouts: 2-3x per week heavy lifting, 1-2x per week HIIT past few weeks, but little HIIT prior to that. Grounding as much as I can. Home is EMF mitigated. Still, looking for more improvement in several areas, however.

Vit D: 84 (supplementing 5-7k IU daily and using tanning 2-3x per week - helps long time issues w/skin)
BUN/Creatinine: 10x (best it's ever been). Have been 17-20 over past 2 yrs.
DHEA: 200 (see below for more history here)
Estradiol: <20 (has been this level for 15 months, consistently, after being elevated in years past).
Progesterone: 0.2 (range 0.1 - 2.1) First time testing this. Considering cream as support for Free T?
Platelets: < 100 (have been below stated lab range of 150 - 450 for 4 years, pre-paleo and post-paleo). Thoughts? Natural ways to help this?
SHBG: 90 (high). Over past 18 months, this has been between 60-75). Not sure why it jumped recently.
Free T3: 2.7 (pretty consistent result for FT3. I have used BHRT T3 in the past, up to 25mg/day and got same results). Not taking anything for this now. Working keto/CT angle after no results with BHRT.
Total T3: 89 (Range 87-178)
Free T4: 0.98 (Range 0.46 - 1.31)
Rev T3: 24 (range 9-27)
Total Testosterone: 275 (range 175 - 781). Historically, has been between 400-500)
Free Test.: Waiting lab results for this still, but it's always low normal (in past it's been between 10 - 16)
My Liver Enzymes have improved (ALT/ASG) and are well in range. In past they have been elevated slightly.
IGF-1: 96 (range 132 - 333, historically, I have been between 110-150).

I am not currently supplementing with DHEA, having stopped a few weeks ago after getting 23andMe results and linking in the Yasko book (I think) in the section on Decreased G6PDH Activity and the issues surrounding sulfur problems with sulfer donors (DHEA-S!), etc. Each time I have taken DHEA (sublingually or capsules) I have skin issues arise for various amounts of time. It does directly increase my serum labs results of DHEA-S, when tested (I have had DHEA results between 400 - 750 at times in the past 2 years when supplementing 50mg/day) and feel better when on it, however, the downsides are unnerving. I have been limiting/eliminating sulfur foods and supplements for 3-4 weeks now (after 23andMe came back) and may look to add back DHEA in another month or so. Urine Sulfate testing (at home) show levels around 400 - 800 currently. Also, to help skin and gut issues, I avoid all nightshades, dairy, seeds, nuts, etc. Have been strict about this for past 4 months and it has helped. Tried adding 100% cacao late January (after Jan webinar about resistant starch) but that made me regress. Possibly due to seed aspect, but now that I have 23andMe results, may also have been due to sulfur detox pathway issues. Have not had coffee for 5 months either. May retry those down the road after addressing Methylation issues for a few months, which had not been assessed prior to March of this year.

Recent 23andMe results showed: MTHFR C677T +/-; CBS A360 +/- (sulfer/ammonia detox); ACAT1-02 +/-; VDR +/-; COMT -/- (rapid breakdown of dopamine); MTR/MTRR +/-.

Recent 3-4 weeks (before these labs), I started hydroxl- and methly-B12, 5-MTHF, molybdenum, lithium orotate and L-Tyrosine (500mg/day) based on 23andMe. I feel much better, more stable mood, better energy, but continue to need Free T and thyroid support. Other supps I am taking: CoQ10; D-Ribose; Iodoral; Vit K; Vit D; Arginine/Ornithine; Saccharomyces Boulardi and daily, homemade bone broth.

I have next appt with functional medicine doc in April. Am considering progesterone cream, but should I consider testosterone directly instead? if so, in what form/delivery method? More CT and more HIIT? Stinging Nettle Root to address SHBG? Other suggestions alongside these items? Like others, I am working on deciphering recent blogs, but non-science background is making it a slow-go on the redox front and what else to takeaway from an action-items standpoint. On one had, I am pleased that my hydration status has greatly improved, while other things are stagnant or too variable. Thank you for any suggestions.

### Paul S — 2014-03-24T20:37:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-please-hack.10301/post-117648>

One other question - after skimming the links (not time for a full read yet) to CPC#8, I wondered what impact Keto diets and CRON have on SHBG or other hormone markers? My diet is high fat, moderate protein breakfast, typically no lunch, and a dinner of high fat, moderate protein. Low carbs mixed in to both.
