### Shijin13 — 2014-04-18T15:43:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122363>

IIRC Jack has said that CT during pregnancy can be beneficial however, if you're over weight you need to be careful b/c it can cause you to release toxins in your fat stores. So if you're over weight or obese while pregnant - you want to wait until after you deliver the baby and finish your nursing relationship.

That said - I did CT while I was nursing Havoc w/o any ill effect upon him. If you're concerned talk with your MW or OB about getting into Cold water regularly while pregnant. I will say that when I was pregnant with both Chaos and Havoc - I did take more cold showers than hot - due to be constantly hot!

### Marie — 2014-04-18T19:22:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122397>

Ummm... I'm not selling anything. I posted those links for people who read this thread, not just the OP.
And the Business of Being Born video is free on that link.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-18T19:30:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122402>

Business of Being Born is an amazing video! really awesome stuff!

### Marie Negrila — 2014-04-20T14:35:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-122666>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=122363)
>
> IIRC Jack has said that CT during pregnancy can be beneficial however, if you're over weight you need to be careful b/c it can cause you to release toxins in your fat stores. So if you're over weight or obese while pregnant - you want to wait until after you deliver the baby and finish your nursing relationship.
>
> That said - I did CT while I was nursing Havoc w/o any ill effect upon him. If you're concerned talk with your MW or OB about getting into Cold water regularly while pregnant. I will say that when I was pregnant with both Chaos and Havoc - I did take more cold showers than hot - due to be constantly hot!

Thank you so much for your straight forward answer. I truly appreciate it. I will be posting my results over time.

### sooperb — 2014-04-27T10:58:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-123558>

Gretchen I would be cautious about asking your midwife or obstetrician such questions! I can't imagine there are any who would give you informed advice, they would probably throw their hands up in horror and think you are nuts. It's almost certainly outside of their experience/knowledge base and certainly not taught.

When my daughter was expecting her first, she decided quite late on that she wanted a home birth in a birthing pool. She told her midwife who was aghast, told her she couldn't possibly, it wasn't safe. My daughter insisted, she was then visited every few days by midwives who tried to dissuade her, culminating in the head of nursing who also told her she couldn't. Behind the scenes they were checking on her mental health! When they saw she wasn't for budging, they told her that the midwives would only be there if she gave birth 2 weeks either side of her due date. When she said well, I will just have to manage on my own they told her that it was an offence to give birth unaided and that she would be charged and probably jailed if she did.

In the event she gave birth within the "window" and they turned out and were fine but they had given her many weeks of unnecessary anxiety at what was already a very difficult time as her boyfriend had walked away.

### yarralea — 2014-04-29T12:54:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-123849>

So glad this thread was here. I have been trying to get pregnant for a few years, and have given up. The issue is with my partner -who had previously had a vasectomy and reversal. We have one daughter 8 post reversal, and tests indicate pregnancy is unlikely.
But i was wondering whether if a miracle happened whether CT would be damaging or cause any issues. I'm looking pretty fit and have had a pretty clean diet, as as much of a clean environment as I can, but I would not be concerned of a toxic overload in fat stores as an issue.

If there is a star in the east I will let you all know. lol

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-29T18:01:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-123895>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=123558)
>
> Gretchen I would be cautious about asking your midwife or obstetrician such questions! I can't imagine there are any who would give you informed advice, they would probably throw their hands up in horror and think you are nuts. It's almost certainly outside of their experience/knowledge base and certainly not taught.
>
> When my daughter was expecting her first, she decided quite late on that she wanted a home birth in a birthing pool. She told her midwife who was aghast, told her she couldn't possibly, it wasn't safe. My daughter insisted, she was then visited every few days by midwives who tried to dissuade her, culminating in the head of nursing who also told her she couldn't. Behind the scenes they were checking on her mental health! When they saw she wasn't for budging, they told her that the midwives would only be there if she gave birth 2 weeks either side of her due date. When she said well, I will just have to manage on my own they told her that it was an offence to give birth unaided and that she would be charged and probably jailed if she did.
>
> In the event she gave birth within the "window" and they turned out and were fine but they had given her many weeks of unnecessary anxiety at what was already a very difficult time as her boyfriend had walked away.

Sue

A lot depends regarding OBs/MW if they're in a stand alone practice or part of a bigger consortium/ACO/ or HMO here in the US. In Virginia - you can work with MWs who are NPs or CNPs who do not have hospital privileges and only provide birthing support at home! Mine encouraged me to do things that the TRADITIONAL BIRTH guidelines set by ACOG believes is RISKY! Many of these MWs deliver breech babies vaginally - when ACOG REQUIRES OBs to deliver by C-Section. Then when you look a the track record of places like the FARM & What Ina May Gaskin has done embracing natural birth... you can see this is a better way to deliver and is safer for both mama and baby for the majority of the population. Granted there are small percentages that this type of birth and support won't work.

But in General most MW in the US are very cognizant of Eating Real Foods, limiting carbs during pregnancy etc. Some even advocate a glass of wine/week (Gasp!), and eating raw fish.

My International Board Certified Lactation Consultant, is currently finishing up her MW training. She advocated Paleo/epi-Paleo template for her clients, as well as following circadian cycles.... what's interesting is the people in medicine pushing change are the outcasts that the AMA and other Organizations can't control - I think we know someone like that....

### sooperb — 2014-04-29T18:13:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-during-pregnancy.10313/post-123900>

It's gratifying that there are such enlightened professionals around, my daughter could sure have used someone on her side. What you do get here are some who will agree in principle that natural is best but if they work for the NHS (and that's the vast majority) they won't risk assisting in anything that isn't an agreed procedure. That's pretty much across the board for health professionals in most disciplines here. I can see their point but being submissive never enabled any changes and I'm afraid nurses are amongst the worst for sticking to the rules, in the UK anyway.

# Thread: Measuring Serum Leptin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-25T07:24:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117788>

I noticed that Jack's optimizing labs do not list any test to measure serum leptin. Is that because this value cycles in some highly variable fashion and capturing a single fasting value would not give much information? Or maybe it is hidden there included as part of some panel?

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-25T09:22:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117803>

I think it's because it's not serum leptin that is important, but the ability of the leptin receptors to sense it (leptin sensitivity). You can think of it in a similar fashion as insulin, you can have higher or lower insulin levels but what determines that your metabolism is working well is your insulin sensitivity. The more resistant you are, the more of these two substances you have constantly in your blood. Higher leptin might seem useful in order to communicate to the hipothalamus that your energy stores are full and cut back your hunger, yet his has only showed to work in some research in mice and humans that had a genetic defect with the leptin receptors. If you give it to someone that is leptin resistant due to a messed configuration of his epigenetic switches it won't do a thing. That alone is difficult to use in isolation in order to assess how affects you. There are other biomarkers that can be used as a proxy to sense how leptin sensitive you are, like the DHEA levels, reverse T3, the 4x cortisol and melatonin panel, Vitamin D and HDL levels.

### persistence — 2014-03-25T11:07:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117819>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=117803)
>
> I think it's because it's not serum leptin that is important, but the ability of the leptin receptors to sense it (leptin sensitivity). You can think of it in a similar fashion as insulin, you can have higher or lower insulin levels but what determines that your metabolism is working well is your insulin sensitivity. The more resistant you are, the more of these two substances you have constantly in your blood. Higher leptin might seem useful in order to communicate to the hipothalamus that your energy stores are full and cut back your hunger, yet his has only showed to work in some research in mice and humans that had a genetic defect with the leptin receptors. If you give it to someone that is leptin resistant due to a messed configuration of his epigenetic switches it won't do a thing. That alone is difficult to use in isolation in order to assess how affects you. There are other biomarkers that can be used as a proxy to sense how leptin sensitive you are, like the DHEA levels, reverse T3, the 4x cortisol and melatonin panel, Vitamin D and HDL levels.

So I was not suggesting any therapy with Leptin. I was just wondering if the person with Leptin resistance might show that as higher than normal fasting levels of Leptin in serum / blood.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-25T11:38:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117829>

Yeah I understood it that way also, I just brought up the example of the trials as a way to point out that the serum leptin levels that you might find _may_ not be an indicative of anything or at least not one easy to interpret. I'm not an expert at all but my understanding is that, to say, a leptin sensitive person that is well fed might have similar leptin serum levels at one time compared to a leptin resistant individual that is fasted yet their levels are high due to the increased fat mass. Two identical static frames of each context that are part of a totally different film. Maybe Jack might chime in to clarify it.

### nicld — 2014-03-25T15:51:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117857>

I had it done once and he said that it was pretty much a waste of money. My test was in the morning and the leptin levels showed up as low. Don't think that it would hurt to have it done and see what they read but it might not really tell you anything.

### persistence — 2014-03-25T20:53:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117892>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=117829)
>
> Yeah I understood it that way also, I just brought up the example of the trials as a way to point out that the serum leptin levels that you might find _may_ not be an indicative of anything or at least not one easy to interpret. I'm not an expert at all but my understanding is that, to say, a leptin sensitive person that is well fed might have similar leptin serum levels at one time compared to a leptin resistant individual that is fasted yet their levels are high due to the increased fat mass. Two identical static frames of each context that are part of a totally different film. Maybe Jack might chime in to clarify it.

There is a similar issue with fasting insulin. It turns out that the endogenous pattern of insulin production looks a little like a sine wave. It constantly flows up and down and if you measure a single static reading, you have no idea where on that curve you actually sit.

To me it is mind boggling that they don't - as a standard practice for all prediabetics - sit you down for two hours and do a time series on insulin, just to establish what your endogenous pattern is. \*IF\* they could do that same time series for glucagon, leptin, and other key metabolic chemicals, then you would have a complete picture of the patient's actual condition.

As I said in another thread here, don't they want to distinguish the patient who overproduces glucagon and has normal insulin levels from the one who has normal glucagon and has massively high insulin (and probably insulin resistance)? Apparently, the medical profession really doesn't care what disease you actually have.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-25T21:59:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117897>

True, why would they put so much effort and time (aka money) in creating such a detailed profile when they can just tell you to exercise more, eat less and take some pills? duh

### persistence — 2014-03-26T03:45:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-117988>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=117897)
>
> True, why would they put so much effort and time (aka money) in creating such a detailed profile when they can just tell you to exercise more, eat less and take some pills? duh

No amount of exercise is going to reset what is broken there if the insulin resistance is not related to being overweight.

Fixing insulin resistance for a thin person might be addressed by things that make you more insulin sensitive.

Fixing glucagon overproduction might require you to use drugs that suppress glucose production in liver.

I don't think one cure fits all diseases here. I think they should treat the actual disease, which requires for a start defining what the actual disease is.

### Inger — 2014-03-26T08:15:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-118021>

Pone, maybe your actual disease is.. wreaking havoc on your natural circadian rhythm for years (?)......... and other kind of terror for the body... (like way too much EMF...)
What is disease? Isn't it when we cut ourselves off from mother nature? That might be when the suffering begins.

### persistence — 2014-03-26T21:36:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/measuring-serum-leptin.10315/post-118124>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=118021)
>
> Pone, maybe your actual disease is.. wreaking havoc on your natural circadian rhythm for years (?)......... and other kind of terror for the body... (like way too much EMF...)
> What is disease? Isn't it when we cut ourselves off from mother nature? That might be when the suffering begins.

I agree that something has been building up here for years and reached a critical threshold.

I treat what I can measure, and I just keep iterating through the possible causes and hoping one of those will bring more success.

I've tried grounding myself outside more often each day. I do find it brings some peace of mind.

I continue to adjust my circadian rhythm.

# Thread: Vascular Surgeon: Why I've ditched statins for good
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vascular-surgeon-why-ive-ditched-statins-for-good.10317/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T11:14:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vascular-surgeon-why-ive-ditched-statins-for-good.10317/post-117821>

> When I had a routine health check-up eight years ago, my cholesterol was so high that the laboratory thought there had been a mistake. I had 9.3 millimoles of cholesterol in every litre of blood — almost twice the recommended maximum.
>
> It was quite a shock. The GP instantly prescribed statins, the cholesterol-lowering drugs that are supposed to prevent heart disease and strokes. For eight years, I faithfully popped my 20mg atorvastatin pills, without side effects. Then, one day last May, I stopped. It wasn’t a snap decision; after looking more closely at the research, I’d concluded that statins were not going to save me from a heart attack and that my cholesterol levels were all but irrelevant. ...

< .... >

> ... The GP simply suggested I try another brand of statin. The sooner the better, he said, given that I’d already been off my prescription for three months. “Hang on,” I said. “Could you give me a blood test first?” When the results came back, he was amazed that my total blood cholesterol was lower than when I’d been on statins. After three months without the pills, it was 5.4mmol/l (5.4 millimoles per litre of blood) compared with 5.7 mmol/l a year earlier.
>
> The only major changes I’d made to my lifestyle since coming off statins were eliminating sugar (including alcohol and starchy foods such as bread) and eating more animal fat. Many experts now believe that sugar is emerging as a true villain in the heart-disease story; while after decades of demonisation, saturated fat has been acquitted of causing heart disease by a recent “meta” analysis of 70 studies by Cambridge University.
>
> Typically, I was eating red meat three or four times a week and enjoying butter, full-fat milk and plenty of eggs. ...

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/10717431/Why-Ive-ditched-statins-for-good.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/10717431/Why-Ive-ditched-statins-for-good.html)

The background: there's a big push on now in the U.K. to help the the pharmaceutical companies get their hands on NHS (National Health Service) money via changed criteria on the prescription of statins:

> Until 2005, statins were prescribed only to those with at least a 30 per cent or greater risk of having a heart attack within 10 years. This was then reduced to a 20 per cent risk. Now, draft NHS guidelines would have them dished out to those with just a 10 per cent risk — in other words, most men over the age of 50 and most women over the age of 60.

### Lahelada — 2014-03-25T11:24:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vascular-surgeon-why-ive-ditched-statins-for-good.10317/post-117826>

Terrible. I used to do a wellknown diet but when the "inventor" a doc started pushing statins I stopped listening.

# Thread: Interview with Dr. Thomas Cowan
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T15:21:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-117852>

There are so many good podcast interviews out there. I was quite pleased to find this one, because I always liked Dr. Cowan's stuff. This is quite a wide-ranging talk. Much of it is about a book he wrote with Sally Fallon on childcare. So, for example, he says:

> ... it’s just wonderful to be with a child and let a child find their own way – it’s the opposite of the ADD culture which is put a child in a walker before they can walk and teach them languages before they know anything and teach them to read when they’re 3 and this whole pre-school. You know the more we teach people, the stupider and less educated they get.

He's also good on the issue of desertifaction, saying much the same as Joel Salatin:

> ... we have a problem of desertifying the earth ... This has happened over the history of agriculture to the point where there’s already – like Iraq, which used to be the fertile crescent. And now it’s happening in Kansas and all kinds of places. And the only thing that reverses that is large amount of herbivorous animals grazing on the land. So the other reason why you don’t find this in traditional cultures is that in order to have a truly sustainable land base, you have to have herbivorous animals grazing the land. There is simply no other choice. And so just for ecological purposes, the irony of this of course is all these people saying that because of global warming and because of environmental destruction and we should all be vegetarian. It’s of course exactly the opposite, and for people who don’t know about that, **just think for example of the great plains in the United States which were basically from Minnesota to Gulf of Mexico from the Mississippi River to the Rocky Mountains – this expanse of grassland – when the early settlers got there, they all said the top soil was 10 feet deep and there was literally millions and millions of buffalo and other grazing animals.** So then hundred years of converting this grassland into corn and soy beans and wheat which is exactly what the vegetarians tell us to eat; grains and beans, then it became a dust \[bowl\].

How about a nice picture of a buffalo hunt?

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Actually, not much soil in that picture, but it's still an attractive reminder of a lost ecosystem.

> ... here’s the sort of big lesson – humans are part of that system, the native people knew they were made of buffalo, the buffalo were made of grass, and this whole cycle continues. We are made of herbivorous animals – their fat and their proteins, that’s what builds our bodies.

He's also very interesting on vaccines. I always used to assume that the "conventional" view on vaccination was right. I think that was partly because you only get given about four over here -- and three of those come together. And you kind of assume they work. So why not? However, the explosion in the number of vaccinations being offered in the U.S. seems quite alarming. Apparently, there's a legal reason for this:

> By the way, it’s also interesting that while vaccines have driven such a growth in the number of diseases which we can now vaccinate, particularly in the last 10 years. **It’s been an explosion and there’s proposed to be at least 10 or 30 more diseases which we can vaccinate against**. And you know part of the reason for that is the part of the US Patriot Act ... part of \[the\] Patriot Act was exempting vaccine manufacturers from any damages in perpetuity, meaning forever from injury caused by vaccine. ...

Therefore, it makes economic sense for companies to develop vaccines -- because it's all profit and no risk of prosecution even if your vaccines kills or cripples people. What a crazy situation!

Lots more interesting stuff here:

[http://www.liveto110.com/nourishing-traditions-of-baby-and-child-care-with-dr-thomas-cowan/](http://www.liveto110.com/nourishing-traditions-of-baby-and-child-care-with-dr-thomas-cowan/)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-25T16:52:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-117868>

I hadn't heard that about the Patriot Act and vaccines. Thanks, Mike.

### sooperb — 2014-03-25T22:54:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-117905>

I think I have his book, is he Thomas Cowan MD?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T09:06:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118028>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=117905)
>
> I think I have his book, is he Thomas Cowan MD?

That could well be him. He is a medical doctor.

Is the book _The Fourfold Path to Healing_? That's the one he co-wrote with Sally Fallon of the WAPF and a movement therapist called Jaimen McMillan. In fact, his website is called that:

[http://fourfoldhealing.com](http://fourfoldhealing.com)

### sooperb — 2014-03-26T10:02:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118031>

Yep, that's the one. I read some of his book, well quite a lot, as I thought he was on the same wavelength. He is to a point but if I remember rightly, our paths diverged somewhere. I might get the book out and have another read. I used to email updates once in a while but it's been a long time since the last one.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T10:29:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118033>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=118031)
>
> Yep, that's the one. I read some of his book, well quite a lot, as I thought he was on the same wavelength. He is to a point but if I remember rightly, our paths diverged somewhere. I might get the book out and have another read. I used to email updates once in a while but it's been a long time since the last one.

Yes, I get his email updates, but they seem to be pretty rare.

I bought that book after reading _Nourishing Traditions_. I like the broad approach the book has -- in theory. So IIRC he tries to come at health from the point of view of nutrition, medical healing, movement, and exercises in reflecting in certain ways intended to change one's thinking. All very interesting, but I don't actually know I've ever used anything in it.

But I had just the same thought as you: I was just thinking I might re-read it.

His thinking seems to be strongly influenced by Rudolf Steiner's. Maybe Sally Fallon's is, too.

### Gracelind — 2014-03-26T11:28:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118036>

My son and I attended the Fourfold Path to Healing Conference several years ago. It's more intimate than the Wise Traditions WAPF with closer access to Sally Fallon, Dr Tom Cowan and Jaimen McMillan.

About the movement sessions--I had really glossed over that part of the _Fourfold_ book but in person Jaiman is engaging, welcoming, and very healing in his manner. I talked to him about my balance issues (due to quads atrophied by autoimmune myositis) and he gave me some insightful suggestions. His knowledge of the mechanics of how the body moves is masterful and his presentations draw his audience in. For mid-60s he moves with smooth, flexible, lithe and athletic grace--for many years he was a master fencer.

Most of the movements are in the book but there's nothing seeing them presented well in person.

None of these three, of course, work at the electron/proton/photon level. When Jaimen speaks of energy he's clearly talking about a different level--even a difference definition of energy--more of the spirit.

Dr Cowan puts many of his patients on the GAPS diet and also uses low dose LDN. My son's former roommates fed their baby the Cowan/GAPS/Nourishing Traditions menu and he's quite a healthy little guy with beautiful teeth.

My son and I also attended the Wise Traditions conference in Dallas several years ago. The babies there were all beautifully healthy with happy dispositions. I only heard one baby cry in three days and that when Daddy leaned over too far and the baby rolled out of the Snugli. Tom and Sally must be doing something right for such a bright, happy, curious crop of kids to grow up fed and raised by their protocols.

### sooperb — 2014-03-26T12:47:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118038>

Sounds like an all round good experience. The States has so many enlightened practitioners, the UK doesn't seem to have any, not that I've seen. All the books I've read on health, bar one, have been written by Americans. I have a few Patrick Holford books, one is interesting "Food is better medicine than Drugs", it is quite hard to get past his reputation as a charlatan though. Personally I've found his stuff quite interesting, especially his work on homocysteine.

I will be getting the book out again, you can't beat personal recommendation

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T21:02:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interview-with-dr-thomas-cowan.10318/post-118107>

> [Gracelind said:](/goto/post?id=118036)
>
> My son and I attended the Fourfold Path to Healing Conference several years ago. It's more intimate than the Wise Traditions WAPF with closer access to Sally Fallon, Dr Tom Cowan and Jaimen McMillan.

I've just started re-reading the book, Gracelind. I just put it down and logged on, because I just came across something interesting almost immediately ...

> None of these three, of course, work at the electron/proton/photon level. When Jaimen speaks of energy he's clearly talking about a different level--even a difference definition of energy--more of the spirit.

Or another way of putting it would be that they don't talk in those terms, although doubtless many of the things they do would get a nod of approval from Jack, who does.

But anyway -- here's the passage that struck my eye. It's on page 3 of the book _Fourfold Healing_:

> … there actually is some scientific evidence for the existence of non-physical-life-force bodies in all living organisms … Harold Saxton-Burr, a professor of anatomy at Yale University, spent many years researching what he called "fields of life" … He proposed that all living things were molded and controlled by "electrodynamic fields" … analogous to magnetic fields ...

Now I've no idea how closely what someone like Becker found relates to what this guy Harold Saxton-Burr was saying. And further than that just how closely that would map onto Rudolf Steiner's notion of the "Life-Force Body" is a big question.

It's really easy for people to take theories that have some vague similarity hold them up together and say "There! You see the connection?" (as Umberto Eco puts it) when there really is none. People do it all the time. But it's not a substitute for an argument showing the connection, as Eco says.

That said, Dr. Cowan does seem to be feeling his way towards the electromagnetic effect.

# Thread: Iron levels

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ken23 — 2014-03-26T19:34:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-levels.10330/post-118090>

In EE4 Dr. K writes about iron being critical for thyroid and temp. My question is what is an optimal ferritin level on a blood test?

I took a look at my blood work and showed ferritin at 84 (30-400 ng/ml). Not sure if this may have anything to do with my cold hands/ feet, hairloss, dry skin.

### JanSz — 2014-03-26T20:32:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-levels.10330/post-118101>

> [Ken23 said:](/goto/post?id=118090)
>
> In EE4 Dr. K writes about iron being critical for thyroid and temp. My question is what is an optimal ferritin level on a blood test?
>
> I took a look at my blood work and showed ferritin at 84 (30-400 ng/ml). Not sure if this may have anything to do with my cold hands/ feet, hairloss, dry skin.

[http://www.ironoverload.org/information/diagnosis-pt1.html](http://www.ironoverload.org/information/diagnosis-pt1.html)

Iron workup:
• Iron and Iron Binding Capacity
• Iron, Total
• Ferritin
• Transferrin
• Folate, RBC & Hematocrit
• Hemoglobin A1c (496X)
• Hemoglobin, Plasma (514X)

Thyroid workup
T3, Total
T4, Total (Thyroxine)
T3, Free
T4,Free
T3, Reverse
Ultrasensitive TSH
Thyroid Peroxidase and Thyroglobulin Antibodies

\--------------------------------------------
cold hands/ feet try

Lugol's solution
Selenium

....

.

### Ken23 — 2014-03-26T23:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-levels.10330/post-118140>

Thanks Jansz. Tried iodine.... Hands and feet got colder

### 400lbbenchpress — 2014-03-28T06:08:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-118423>

If all you do is look at the body mass index and don't incorporate body fat% you are an idiot and that is what most docs and insurance companies do. I am extremely obese by the BMI yet I have sub 10% body fat and am like 240 lbs of muscle.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-28T08:07:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-118427>

This seems pertinent, too:

> Unfortunately the entire edifice of their argument is based on the long-discredited Body Mass Index (BMI), a frankly bizarre method of assessing how fat people are which was [developed by an obscure Belgian social scientist without any medical qualifications](http://ndt.oxfordjournals.org/content/23/1/47) in the early 19th century. The BMI assumes that healthy human mass goes up in proportion to the square of height, a patently absurd suggestion given that human bodies are three-dimensional rather than flat 2D shapes. All other things being equal a human's weight should go up related to the cube of height - and indeed they aren't equal. Any engineer will point out that cross-sectional area in support structures (feet, leg bones etc) needs to go up in direct proportion to weight carried, adding still more heft than a cube law would as height goes up. This is why elephants are not simply scaled-up dogs, and dogs are not simply scaled-up insects - they have proportionally thicker legs and other supporting structures and come out much heavier

The article that's taken from is very funny:

> A famous mad professor who has previously called for Britons to starve their children into dwarfism so as to ease strains on the planetary ecosystem has reiterated his arguments, this time insisting that the amount of surplus flab carried by the human race will soon be equivalent to having another half-a-billion people on Earth.
>
> Regular readers will be familiar with Professor Ian Roberts of the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine already: he and his colleague Dr Phil Edwards [wrote a paper](http://www.theregister.co.uk/2009/04/20/more_bmi_foolery/) in 2009 in which they suggested that it would be a good idea for Britons and Americans to model their diet and physique on that of the "lean" Vietnamese, as this would assist in such things as meeting British government carbon pledges. Lightweight Vietnamese people, according to the two scientists, not only need less food but use less energy to move themselves around.
>
> Unfortunately, as we pointed out at the time, this would not merely have been a matter of Britons shedding some flab. In order to match the Vietnamese on weight, Brits would also have to lose four inches or so of height. Extrapolating from Roberts' and Edwards' figures, in fact, the people of the UK would need to shrink to a Hobbit-like stature barely over three feet to meet the more ambitious governmental carbon goals.
>
> Even relatively hefty Vietnamese heights and weights are achieved only by means of serious child malnutrition, so it was clear that Roberts' and Edwards' ideas were not going to be taken seriously (though it was perhaps a little alarming to hear such things from official government-funded experts on public health). ...

[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2012/06/19/fat\_people\_equivalent\_to\_population\_of\_billions/](http://www.theregister.co.uk/2012/06/19/fat_people_equivalent_to_population_of_billions/)

This is the tospy-turvy world we live in: the lunatics are the "official government-funded experts on public health".

### sooperb — 2014-03-28T13:18:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-118457>

Add Professor Ian MacDonald to that list, he hears that sugar is bad but he's not about to start advising anyone to reduce it, despite being a Whitehall adviser on nutrition.

### nicld — 2014-03-28T16:49:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-118471>

BMI is bull.....beeeeeeep!!!!

If I would start weight training again my thighs would be that muscular too. That is just the stock that I come from and I love it. I always said that I would have made a great farmers wife back in the day. Nice broad shoulders to carry stuff with, great WIDE hips to birth lots of babies from and strong thighs to let me do lots or work with, oh and I am 99.9% northern European so I do come from hearty stock.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-28T16:54:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-118475>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=118471)

She's no need to lose fat. There isn't really much there. The shape of her thighs indicate musclulature not adipose.

### Stella — 2014-03-29T23:50:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-118715>

This is crazy. The guidelines on Obesity from the AACE -American Assoc Of Clinical Endocrinology have just been issued. The big pharma companies will be jumping for joy. Intervention with diet and exercise and then for those with comorbidities, drugs or bariatric surgery.

Here is what they say:
There is now solid evidence that we can intervene to help patient lose weight and decrease the complications that result from obesity. To this end, the American Association of Clinical Endocrinology (AACE) has issued [recommendations](https://www.aace.com/files/algorithm-07-11-2013.pdf) giving guidance for clinicians about how to approach this issue. Intensive approaches to lifestyle modification with diet and exercise can help patients lose 7% or more of their body weight and have been show to decrease progression from prediabetes to diabetes. Two new medications, lorcaserin and phentermine/topiramate ER, have received Food and Drug Administration approval over the past 2 years as an adjunct to diet for weight loss. Bariatric surgery has emerged as a safe and effective method of weight loss as well.

Just 7% or more weight loss. With most people I see walking around, that's not enough.

Look at what they are recommending:
The diet recommended reduced saturated fat to less than 7% of calories, reduced cholesterol intake to 200 mg/day, increased fiber to 10-25 g/day, increased plant sterols/stanol esters to 2 g/day, caloric restriction, reduced simple carbohydrates and sugars, increased intake of unsaturated fats, elimination of _trans_ fats, increased marine-based omega-3 ethyl esters, and restriction of alcohol to 20-30 g/day.

Pathetic!!!! This is an example of the blind leading the blind.

[http://www.clinicalendocrinologynew...aebbc85431b949.html?tx\_ttnews\[sViewPointer\]=1](http://www.clinicalendocrinologynews.com/views/commentaries/single-article/obesity-american-association-of-clinical-endocrinology-aace/7520072f52fc59c21caebbc85431b949.html?tx_ttnews%5BsViewPointer%5D=1)

### Gracelind — 2014-03-30T00:33:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-118718>

A couple of years ago my husband was deemed borderline obese by Kaiser. Never mind his percent body fat was 6%. He raised holy hell and the next time they came around the company for the annual physicals/health education they had changed the criteria. It pays to raise a ruckus sometimes.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-30T11:06:30+0300

### sooperb — 2014-03-30T19:06:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-118833>

The last time my daughter was in hospital and rang her bell for assistance, it took the nurse who came several minutes to get from the nursing station to her room. When she arrived her forehead was covered in beads of sweat and she was panting, couldn't speak and had to sit down, which she did on the side of the bed! She must have easily been 20 st or more and her uniform was bursting at the seams. It's a disgrace that the hospital, as employers, care so little for the health and safety of their patients by permitting someone so inherently unsuitable and incapable to be looking after sick people.

The same goes for the Police force, so many fat and unfit policeman who can't possibly fulfill their role because they can't run, can't bend and can't stand for long.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-31T10:42:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-119003>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=118837)
>
> The rabbit livers are readily available here but it's hard if not impossible to find organic.

Surely, most rabbit in this country is wild. They were originally introduced as a farmed animal. Hence the surname Warrener -- a man who looks after a warren, warrens being enclosures constructed for rabbits. (The excavations wild rabbits make themselves are known as "buries".) IIRC, rabbits were a luxury, particularly prized by ladies. The rabbits, of course, escaped over time, adapted to the climate, and throve.

They were a good source of meat for country people. They were snared, shot -- or ferreted:

> In the winter half a dozen young gentlemen have a turn at the ferreting; a great burrow is chosen, three or four ferrets put in at once without any nets, so that the rabbits may bolt freely, and then the shooting is like volleys of musketry fire ...

[http://www.gutenberg.org/files/36948/36948-h/36948-h.htm](http://www.gutenberg.org/files/36948/36948-h/36948-h.htm)

That was gentlemen not labourers, who I think would have used nets. At one time if you had a "long net" in your pocket you could have found yourself transported -- because that was not for rabbit but for hare. Hares were wanted for hare-coursing and would have been living on land belonging to a local landowner, so that a "long net" would have shown an intention to poach. Rabbits were everywhere and regarded as free-for-all.

I don't think anyone maintains warrens any more. I fancy if they've got rabbits at your butcher, they're the results of "rough shooting" -- walking the fields and knocking them down with a shotgun:

> From his father he has imbibed the spirit of the woods and all the minutiae of his art. First he learned to shoot; his highest ambition being satisfied in the beginning when permitted to carry the double-barrel home across the meadow. Then he was allowed occasionally to fire off the charges left in after the day’s work, before the gun was hung against the beam. Next, from behind the fallen trunk of an oak he took aim at a sitting rabbit which had raised himself on his hind-quarters to listen suspiciously—resting the heavy barrels on the tree, and made nervous by the whispered instructions from the keeper kneeling on the grass out of sight behind, “Aim at his shoulder, lad, if he be sitting sidelong; if a’ be got his back to ’ee aim at his poll.” From this it was but a short step to be trusted with the single-barrel, and finally with the double; ultimately having one of his own and walking his own distinct rounds.

[http://www.gutenberg.org/files/36948/36948-h/36948-h.htm](http://www.gutenberg.org/files/36948/36948-h/36948-h.htm)

I reckon any rabbit you'll be getting in East Anglia will be wild.

Of course, wild animals might have eaten just about anything, and I guess they don't know to stay off land where farmers, or the local council, etc. have been incontinently spraying. I should think it's as least as good a bet as farmed meat, though.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-31T11:52:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-119016>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=119015)
>
> What a lovely thought to add the history behind this, very interesting, something I didn't know for sure.
> Is it something you eat?

Not often. I don't usually buy rabbit. My mum cooks them sometimes, so I might have it if I'm visiting. I don't turn down the liver or kidneys.

> They are not as tender as say veal liver.... (this sends me thoughts of veal crates and I'm not sure if veal welfare here is a priority other reason why I rarely buy veal livers)

Not a worry here. AFAIK, they like veal very pale on the Continent, so they're exclusively milk-fed -- and pretty much denied light and movement, as you say. Over here they're simply the male animals, which are slaughtered young anyway, since they'll never be any use as milch-cattle or to bear calves. They do get out; they do eat grass.

Unfortunately, people are now put off by the term "veal", so it tends not to sell. Of course, if farmers can sell the meat from the young male animals they'll get a somewhat longer life and likely be better cared for. That's what I understand, anyway. So there are arguments in terms of ecology and humane farming for eating veal.

> Splash of dry Marsala and a couple of fresh laurel leaves later and you got yourself a lovely plate of food, no fuss.

Sounds great. We'll alll be wishing you were cooking dinner for us -- after we'd managed to get breakfast with middle bacon off Sue in Lancashire. A bit of travelling in between!

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-01T10:36:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nhs-tells-bodybuilder-she-must-lose-weight-and-exercise-more.10349/post-119182>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=119040)

> We should all meet and cook! Share recipes, have a good time, could be done? I'm back on April 27  or take it in turns to visit each other's I'm in East England Sue in Lancashire your in the big smoke? Who else is around within ear shot?

I'm actually on the South Coast. There are some Londoners here, though, and one of them was floating the idea of meeting in London:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/calling-all-londoners.10183/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/calling-all-londoners.10183/)

# Thread: Bean sprouts
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bean-sprouts.10350/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Infinusss — 2014-03-27T20:52:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bean-sprouts.10350/post-118345>

I searched for bean sprouts on this forum and nothing came up. Does anyone know if bean sprouts (mung beans) are Kruse-approved?

I've read some articles stating that sprouting beans lowers lectins and makes them more suitable for consumption.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-27T21:26:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bean-sprouts.10350/post-118348>

> [Infinusss said:](/goto/post?id=118345)
>
> I searched for bean sprouts on this forum and nothing came up. Does anyone know if bean sprouts (mung beans) are Kruse-approved?
>
> I've read some articles stating that sprouting beans lowers lectins and makes them more suitable for consumption.

Sure. Don't think they're in, nonetheless:

See "Brain Gut 6: Epi-Paleo Rx"

[http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/)

See the section entitled "Foods that I would totally eliminate from the Epi-Paleo Rx" …

> 6\. All Legumes without exception even for cultural reasons.

AFAIK, Terry Wahls, who Jack rather approves of, _does_ include some sprouted foods, but this is mainly because she tries to serve vegetarians and vegans, too -- which is rather trying to square the circle when you're trying to do what she's doing.

I guess Jack's "totally eliminate" and "all without exception" means what it says. That said, I dunno he'd say anyone who "had a good redox potential" should actually turn down a bowl of some Chinese food that was all right in every other way just because it had a few beansprouts in it.

### Infinusss — 2014-03-28T05:33:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bean-sprouts.10350/post-118418>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=118348)
>
> Sure. Don't think they're in, nonetheless:
>
> See "Brain Gut 6: Epi-Paleo Rx"
>
> [http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/)
>
> See the section entitled "Foods that I would totally eliminate from the Epi-Paleo Rx" …
>
>
> AFAIK, Terry Wahls, who Jack rather approves of, _does_ include some sprouted foods, but this is mainly because she tries to serve vegetarians and vegans, too -- which is rather trying to square the circle when you're trying to do what she's doing.
>
> I guess Jack's "totally eliminate" and "all without exception" means what it says. That said, I dunno he'd say anyone who "had a good redox potential" should actually turn down a bowl of some Chinese food that was all right in every other way just because it had a few beansprouts in it.

Yeah, I've pretty pretty much cut my diet down to bare bones at this point, for the reset.

Meat: Salmon, Chilean sea bass, shrimp, oysters, mussels, clams, scallops, GF beef, pork and bacon and sometimes chicken (trying to get pork and chicken pastured from now on)

Veggies: Asparagus, broccoli, avocado, Brussels sprouts, spinach, tomatoes, peppers, onions, garlic, carrots, celery, mushrooms (nightshades don't really bother me)

Fats/oils: GF butter, GF ghee, olive oil, coconut oil

No dairy (spare butter/ghee), no grains, no starchy carbs, no corn, no soy, no veggie oils.

# Thread: Let's Learn to Make A Magnet Bed Together!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-learn-to-make-a-magnet-bed-together.10363/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Casey Inman — 2014-03-28T14:34:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-learn-to-make-a-magnet-bed-together.10363/post-118459>

Has anyone attempted or done this?

Can anyone recommend any books that would be beneficial in this area?

It would be awesome if we could compile all of our ideas and learn to make magnet beds together.

### PaulG — 2014-04-04T10:38:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-learn-to-make-a-magnet-bed-together.10363/post-119750>

I was considering doing this as the magnetico is very expensive to ship to Australia.

Bonlie the inventor of magnetico has a patent for his unipolar design but given it uses standard magnets that are placed next to each other all either north or south facing depending on what hemisphere you live in I dont know what could be so special in the patent. (Can anyone find the patent). You need to use alot (1000-2000) of ceramic magnets 24x20x10cm depending on single,queen or king spaced quiet close together. Cost of magnets alone is quite high. Then you need to test all magnets and mark north polarity, then you need to fix them to a peg board. Must make sure that both you and your partner are lying on same number of magnets.

However its not something you want to get wrong so if you can afford it go with the magnetico.

I was thinking a smaller magnetic field using a few large magnets could be moved around each night but perhaps not a good idea to subject most of your body to return positive fields for eight hours each night.

Any other options? Earth Pulse. I dont think Bonlie or Jack are fans of pulsed emfs devices certainly not ones on all night long.

# Thread: Vitamin A and the RDA
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-a-and-the-rda.10377/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-29T11:13:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-a-and-the-rda.10377/post-118644>

I recently started re-reading Thomas S. Cowan's _Fourfold Path to Healing_. Then I thought, "What the heck, I'll buy the lastest book from him and Sally":

_The Nourishing Traditions Book of Baby & Child Care_

[http://www.amazon.com/Nourishing-Traditions-Book-Baby-Child/dp/0982338317/](http://www.amazon.com/Nourishing-Traditions-Book-Baby-Child/dp/0982338317/)

(I just discovered Sally Fallon and Kaayla Daniel have one forthcoming so I've pre-ordered that, too:

[http://www.amazon.com/Nourishing-Broth-Old-Fashioned-Remedy-Modern/dp/1455529222/](http://www.amazon.com/Nourishing-Broth-Old-Fashioned-Remedy-Modern/dp/1455529222/)

I buy more books than I can well afford.  )

Anyway, I thought the _The Nourishing Traditions Book of Baby & Child Care_ would be interesting, if not relevant to me as I haven't got children. Among other things, I thought it would show their latest thinking.

_Fourfold Path to Healing_ has now been out for ten years. Sally's chapter on nutrition in that is pretty good: she even points to studies warning against fructose, and this is 2004 before _that_ video was yet a twinkle in Robert Lustig's eye. She really is very well-informed and very sharp, and Professor Cordain did himself no favours talking her down in such a rude manner on Jimmy Moore's show. IMO, what Cordain should have said to Jimmy is, "I will come on your show, but to talk about our latest work, not to comment on anyone else's opinions," and handled the interview in those terms. (I think Sally's criticisms of Paleo were at least in part valid, if overstated, and Jimmy also made her sound unnecessarily strident by asking her to repeat them numerous times.) Not Jimmy's finest hour.

Having put a digression inside a digression, I'll return to the point. I thought it would show their latest thinking. Indeed, it does. Among other things, there's a section early on in the book on EMF pollution.

But one of the things that caught my eye was this on vitamin A:

> The RDA of vitamin A for pregnant women is only 2,600 IU -- just 300 IU more than the RDA for women who are not ... To obtain this figure, the scientists at the Institute of Medicine made the following calculation: first they ascertained from previous reports the amount of vitamin A stored in the livers of fetuses that were spontaneously or voluntarily aborted between thirty-seven and forty weeks; second, they doubled this figure, assuming that half of the fetal vitamin A store exists in the liver; and third, they divided this amount over the number of days in the last trimester, during which they presumed this vitamin a would accumulate.

Asumption upon assumption upon assumption.

The authors comment:

> There are several problems with this calculation. Since the fetuses were aborted, we have no idea what their future health would have been like -- their visual acuity, their hearing, their intelligence, their facial and dental features, their reproductive health, or their length of life.
>
> And the function of vitamin A is not to be stored but used. The fetus does not simply hold on to vitamin A to use it after birth, but rapidly uses it and metabolizes it to regulate the entirety of its growth and development. ...

I'm sure there's much else one could say about vitamin A. I know Jack has talked about the vitamin A cycle and light, for example:

[http://jackkruse.com/quantum-bio-10-hormone-102-drawn-to-light/](http://jackkruse.com/quantum-bio-10-hormone-102-drawn-to-light/)

But what really struck me here was the sheer fatuity of how the RDA was got to.

We put the label "science" or "medicine" or "government advice" on something and the public thinks that _means_ something. Look closer and it doesn't necessarily.

### nonchalant — 2014-04-01T04:26:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-a-and-the-rda.10377/post-119159>

Don't civil engineers add up all the stresses and then just multiply everything by 10?

# Thread: Mutation in Coenzyme Q gene defines most major mtDNA haplogroups
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mutation-in-coenzyme-q-gene-defines-most-major-mtdna-haplogroups.10382/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gracelind — 2014-03-30T10:25:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mutation-in-coenzyme-q-gene-defines-most-major-mtdna-haplogroups.10382/post-118784>

This from a discussion in a forum on anthrological genetics, genealogy, MtDNA. Interesting that at least one of the mutations that defines different haplogroups may be in Coenzyme Q. So this area was one in which Mother Nature tinkered, with some groups succeeding and others fading away?

[http://www.eupedia.com/forum/thread...e-Q-gene-defines-most-major-mtDNA-haplogroups](http://www.eupedia.com/forum/threads/29676-Mutation-in-Coenzyme-Q-gene-defines-most-major-mtDNA-haplogroups)
_
While analysing the mtDNA phylogeny, I noticed that most of the common, successful mitochondrial haplogroups were defined by a new mutation in the [Coenzyme Q - cytochrome c reductase](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coenzyme_Q_-_cytochrome_c_reductase) gene (**MT-CYB**) encoding the [Cytochrome b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytochrome_b) protein, located between positions 14,747 and 15,887 in the mtDNA sequence.

Many macro-haplogroups are defined by such polymorphisms, including L0a'b'f, L1b, L1c, L2b’c, L3b, L3d, L3e1, L3e2, L3f, L3k, L5a, L6, M, N, HV, JT. This covers over 95% of the population of Africa and close to 100% of the people outside Africa. Many top level or major European or Middle Eastern haplogroups are also defined by additional mutations in MT-CYB, e.g. H5a1, H13, J1c, J2, J2b, T, U1, U2b’c’d’e, U3, U4, U5a, K, W, W5, X1 and X3.

The MT-CYB gene plays a critical role in biochemical generation of ATP ([oxidative phosphorylation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidative_phosphorylation)). Mutations occurring in the wrong place of the MT-CYB gene can result in exercise intolerance and multi-system pathologies.

**Deleterious mutations would be so debilitating that they would be eliminated from the gene pool within a few generations. Other mutations could have no real impact (synonymous mutation), be slightly more efficient or even greatly advantageous from an evolutionary point of view. It is likely that those found in major haplogroups considerably ameliorated the functioning of the gene in certain environments. They would as a result have been positively selected, increasing the frequency of that haplogroup in the population.**

Mutations that appear to have been particularly beneficial among European lineages include T14766C (haplogroup HV), A14793G (U5a), T14798C (J1c, K and T2g), C14872T (H13), G14905A (T), A15218G (U5a1), C15452a (JT), A15607G (T), G15812A (J2b), and C15833T (H5a1).

The most fascinating is T14798C, which defines three important haplogroups that were all major lineages among Neolithic farmers. It could suggest that this mutation could have become beneficial in the energy production of people with a cereal-based diet._

# Thread: Heartfelt letter to Andreas Eenfeldt
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heartfelt-letter-to-andreas-eenfeldt.10384/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-30T17:05:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heartfelt-letter-to-andreas-eenfeldt.10384/post-118821>

I thought this worth linking. Andreas has entitled it:

**“Ignoring the Advice of Doctors and Dietitians Is the Best Thing I’ve Ever Done”**

Here's what a reader writes to him:

> … For several years, I was treated for my eating disorders without really getting “well”. At the treatment center/hospital we were feed processed products, full of sugar, had very little protein and were showered with bad vegetable oils. Ice cream, candy and sweets were included every day during the treatment. My digestive system literally screamed after every meal, and nausea, stomach pain and diarrhea became my everyday life. But did they listen to me? No, of course not. I had an “eating disorder” and therefore, according to them, I was lying. Nutritional drinks full of added sugar, sweetened yogurt, pastry, pasta, rice, white bread, margarine, fruit juices, fruit in heavy syrup, ice cream and cookies are not what a sick person should eat. ...
>
> According to my caregivers, carbohydrates were the most important things to consume ...

Sounds about right. Most people in healthcare systems seem to be utterly clueless when it comes to nutrition. They apparently don't even suspect that processed food are a bad idea. I heard Professor Cordain say recently that most processed food is made from only four basic ingredients, none of which you want to be ingesting. The same four are listed with a similar comment in Sally Fallon Morell and Thomas Cowan's latest book: refined flour, refined sugar, vegetable oil, and processed dairy. That's the pizza; that's the pastries; that's the cookies.

They feed that to someone who then complains of nausea and diarrhoea and they tell her she's lying  …

[http://www.dietdoctor.com/ignoring-advice-doctors-dietitians-best-thing-ive-ever-done](http://www.dietdoctor.com/ignoring-advice-doctors-dietitians-best-thing-ive-ever-done)

### sooperb — 2014-03-30T18:46:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heartfelt-letter-to-andreas-eenfeldt.10384/post-118831>

Doesn't it make you want to scream? When I try telling people this, their eyes glaze over and they clearly don't believe me yet if a doctor told them the exact same thing, they'd believe them. It's a sorry state we've gotten into and I don't believe we will ever be able to lift society out of it until/unless the medical profession change their stance and get behind a drive to improve the nation's diet. That won't happen until someone in government makes it happen; as we are paying fools to advise us who have been bought by big food companies and pharma there's not much hope that it will change.

### nonchalant — 2014-04-01T05:29:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heartfelt-letter-to-andreas-eenfeldt.10384/post-119162>

But the government person has also been bought by big food and pharma.

# Thread: Black Death bodies from London
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/black-death-bodies-from-london.10385/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-30T18:45:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/black-death-bodies-from-london.10385/post-118830>

> An investigation into skeletons found in a plague burial ground concluded that four in 10 Londoners killed during the epidemic grew up in other parts of Britain.

Hardly staggering news. Pre-modern cities were killers of people; they sucked in people constantly, who perhaps did not live long, but were soon replaced by others. I fancy there's a lot about that here -- but I can't recall:

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/b00rfhx2](http://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/b00rfhx2)

Continuing:

> Carbon dating techniques conducted by experts at Queen's University Belfast indicated three separate "phases" of burials - coinciding with outbreaks of the plague in the capital during the 14th and 15th centuries. …
>
> DNA analysis revealed that four of the people had remnants of the Yersinia pestis bacteria on their teeth - which causes the bubonic plague.

All this is interesting, but it perhaps gets more so:

> They also showed signs of having jobs that involved heavy manual labour as experts noted a high rate of back damage and strain.
>
> The remains also revealed that one of the bodies could have been that of a monk - after showing signs of vegetarianism in later life, which is something a Carthusian monk would have done during the 14th century.
>
> Others showed signs of malnutrition and childhood diseases such as rickets.
>
> One of the skeletons showed evidence of malnutrition and a large variation of diet 30 years prior to death, coinciding with the Great Famine of 1315 to 1317, the experts said. ...

The back damage is interesting. A comparison with bodies of similar ages for farm workers of the period would be even more interesting. Agricultural work before mechanisation could be very heavy. But could labouring work in town involve more unnatural movements? Would the physical demands of the work take place against a diet of food that was less fresh and less varied?

One wonders what the "signs of vegetarianism in later life" were. It doesn't sound like anything good. I suspect vegetarianism is something that dieticians cry up or at least accept, either from ignorance or because they don't wish to offend vegetarians, but that physical anthropologists, osteologists, etc. know to take a different view on, because of what they've seen.

The "malnutrition and childhood diseases such as rickets" is depressing. AFAICT, town-dwellers in pre-modern times (and, indeed, far later), if poor, might do pretty badly. And in town or country people could get hit by famine, as one of the skeletons shows.

Rickets have been said to occur in cities of the period, because the streets were narrow and the houses relatively high and even built out on the upper stories, blocking the light. I don't know … perhaps some of these people spent quite a lot of time indoors, too. And dietary sources of vitamin D, not abundant in food anyway, were probably lacking.

> Osteologist Don Walker, from the Museum of London Archaeology … \[said\] "There was one individual we looked at and we know that they came to London from elsewhere, we know they were breastfed as a child, we know what sort of diseases they had, we know they had bad tooth decay throughout their childhood, we know they were involved with quite hard work from lesions of the spine, they had a quite active laborious life. The testing also shows that they then died in early adulthood and had been exposed to Yersinia pestis so they might have died from the plague during the first outbreak of the Black Death."

Good article.

[http://www.wiltshirebusinessonline..../11112747.Black\_Death\_victims\_\_cosmopolitan\_/](http://www.wiltshirebusinessonline.co.uk/uk_national_news/11112747.Black_Death_victims__cosmopolitan_/)

# Thread: My labs from last year

### Tanya — 2014-03-30T18:54:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-from-last-year.10386/post-118832>

After seeing Seanb4’s post about his labs, I decided to do the same with mine even though they are also not recent ( they were all done last year but prior to the time of me starting Epi-Paleo Rx.).

I had 2 tests done during that time but for this discussion I will post only some of the results.

Test 1. Bun - 14 (7-17); Creatinine 0.5 (0.80 - 1.50); Potassium 4.4 (3.5 - 5.5)
Fasting Glucose 88 (70-99): Insulin 2.46 (2.6-25); Uric acid 2.1 (2.5 - 8)
c-reactive protein 0.1 (0.0 - 0.8); CK 21 (30-135); CCP 186 (<20)

Test 2. Bun 15 (7-17), creatinine 0.5 (.80-1.5); Potassium 4.0 (3.5-5.5)
Fasting Glucose 90 (70-99); Insulin 2.44 (2.6-25)

Thyroid panel:
Free thyroxine 0.9 (0.9-1.8)
T3% Binding 38.53 (22.00-37.00)
TSH 4.58 (0.35-5.50)
Antithyroid AB panel -negative

ACI from Diagnos-Techs:
Free cortisol rhythm: 5-8 AM 10 (13-24)
11-1PM 4 (5-10)
4-5 PM 5 (3-8)
10PM-12AM 3 (1-4)
DHEA 4 (3-10)
Fasting Insulin  (3-12); Non-fasting 11 (5-20)

I am seeing my bun/creatinine ratios as 28 and 30. No doubt there, I have a very poor redox potential..... Please don't hesitate to comment

What are the recommended daily doses of:

\* R Lipoic Acid
\* CoQ10
\* Acetyl L-Carnitine

I gather these work together? I take a lot of Lipoic acid throughout the day trying to control my glucose, and I'm unclear on upper limits (dose and frequency) for CoQ10 and ALC.

My current dose on Q10 is 100 mg once a day. My current dose on ALC is 500 mg.

I don't see Lipoic Acid or ALC in the recommended supplements list at all.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommended-daily-doses-r-lipoic-acid-coq10-and-acetyl-l-carnitine.10389/post-118860>

ITs going to depend on your context.

I take anywhere btwn 1500-2400mg of CoQ10 in a day, and more if I have a migraine.
Acetyl L-Carnitine, is part of the mitochondrial Rx. - I take anywhere btwn 1400mg - 2250mg depending on where I'm at in my cycle.

What I take may not work for you. I also adjust my doses based upon my labs.

I would rather eat lots of raw heart instead of supplements. Raw heart has lots of CoQ10 and Acetyl L-Carnitine, maybe R Lipoic Acid too IDK...

I can tell from my own N=1, when I do the food as supp, preferably raw, it works way better than the supplement. If I had to take supplements I would do the food source + supp, if anything. I would never leave the food source out.

I am not sure about supplements at all. I think sometimes they might just add to the unbalance... as Josh says, there might be many other things needed too, it is hard work. What if you just screw it up instead  especially if you have no money to test? OMG it will be super expensive - testing is expensive, supps are expensive.. holey moley
No.. way too hard for my tiny brain. I much prefer the safe and cheap way
I get one whole raw grassfed Angus beef heart for 10 €  it is pretty huge!

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-31T15:25:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommended-daily-doses-r-lipoic-acid-coq10-and-acetyl-l-carnitine.10389/post-119042>

> [persistence said:](/goto/post?id=118876)
>
> Great to know about NutraEval thanks. Here is their sample output:
>
> [http://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/NutrEval\_FMV-Sample-Report.pdf](http://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/NutrEval_FMV-Sample-Report.pdf)
>
> This report shows a high dosage - and end of their treatment scale - for CoQ10 at around 90 mg. Where is the recommendation - for any condition - to dose at 1500 mg+ per day?

my dosing came from working with 3 docs on my migraines. CONTEXT What works for my n=1 may not work for you....

### persistence — 2014-03-31T23:06:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommended-daily-doses-r-lipoic-acid-coq10-and-acetyl-l-carnitine.10389/post-119118>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=118965)
>
> I would rather eat lots of raw heart instead of supplements. Raw heart has lots of CoQ10 and Acetyl L-Carnitine, maybe R Lipoic Acid too IDK...
>
> I can tell from my own N=1, when I do the food as supp, preferably raw, it works way better than the supplement. If I had to take supplements I would do the food source + supp, if anything. I would never leave the food source out.
>
> I am not sure about supplements at all. I think sometimes they might just add to the unbalance... as Josh says, there might be many other things needed too, it is hard work. What if you just screw it up instead  especially if you have no money to test? OMG it will be super expensive - testing is expensive, supps are expensive.. holey moley
> No.. way too hard for my tiny brain. I much prefer the safe and cheap way
> I get one whole raw grassfed Angus beef heart for 10 €  it is pretty huge!

I am really glad you said that Inger. I share the same concern that supplementing can actually be extremely complex and who has all of the facts and interrelationships between things understood so clearly? Even if you test often I think it can be complex.

This is why I always start by looking what is the established range where toxicity begins, and what are typical therapeutic doses for different conditions. I just want to get a general feeling for the supplement by understanding what a high or low dose looks like.

I think even if you do everything perfectly, I share your concern that we might add to the imbalance.
