# Thread: Trees
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trees.10401/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-31T08:06:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trees.10401/post-118955>

Mentioned in a recent thread, weren't they?

I think it interesting that what I suppose would be the two most widely selling post-war children's authors in the English-speaking world (and beyond) seem to have tried to restore a sense of what you might call the numinousness of trees. (Safari doesn't like that word: changed it to "numerousness" LOL  )

> "Woe, woe, woe!" called the voice. "Woe for my brothers and sisters! Woe for the holy trees! The woods are laid waste. The axe is loosed against us. We are being felled. Great trees are falling, falling, falling."
>
> With the last "falling" the speaker came in sight. She was like a woman but so tall that her head was on a level with the Centaur's yet she was like a tree too. It is hard to explain if you have never seen a Dryad but quite unmistakable once you have - something different in the colour, the voice, and the hair. King Tirian and the two Beasts knew at once that she was the nymph of a beech tree ...
>
> Right through the middle of that ancient forest - that forest where the trees of gold and of silver had once grown and where a child from our world had once planted the Tree of Protection - a broad lane had already been opened. It was a hideous lane like a raw gash in the land, full of muddy ruts where felled trees had been dragged down to the river. There was a great crowd of people at work, and a cracking of whips, and horses tugging and straining as they dragged at the logs.

> They found that they were looking at a most extraordinary face. It belonged to a large Man-like, almost Troll-like, figure, at least fourteen foot high, very sturdy, with a tall head, and hardly any neck. Whether it was clad in stuff like green and grey bark, or whether that was its hide, was difficult to say. At any rate the arms, at a short distance from the trunk, were not wrinkled, but covered with a brown smooth skin. The large feet had seven toes each. The lower part of the long face was covered with a sweeping grey beard, bushy, almost twiggy at the roots, thin and mossy at the ends. But at the moment the hobbits noted little but the eyes. These deep eyes were now surveying them, slow and solemn, but very penetrating. They were brown, shot with a green light. Often afterwards Pippin tried to describe his first impression of them.
>
> 'One felt as if there was an enormous well behind them, filled up with ages of memory and long, slow, steady thinking; but their surface was sparkling with the present: like sun shimmering on the outer leaves of a vast tree, or on the ripples of a very deep lake. I don't know but it felt as if something that grew in the ground-asleep, you might say, or just feeling itself as something between roof-tip and leaf-tip, between deep earth and sky had suddenly waked up, and was considering you with the same slow care that it had given to its own inside affairs for endless years.'
>
> 'Hrum, Hoom,' murmured the voice, a deep voice like a very deep woodwind instrument. 'Very odd indeed! Do not be hasty, that is my motto.

I doubt I need to name the authors, who were, of course, friends. I think the second passage is more successful, at least in isolation, but then it comes from a book not primarily aimed at children, so can be more leisurely and complex.

I never got round to reading Tolkien's letters. There's apparently one in which he gets angry about a tree that was outside his window getting chopped down. It's like an old friend, not just a lump of wood.

Nice quotations from _The Nourishing Traditions Book of Baby and Child Care_:

> Traditional man would consider it absurd to merely objectively observe a tree, and to deny that the tree has intention and qualities
>
> This view changed slowly … until its culmination in the Renaissance period … when for the first time … humans began to see the stuff of the world as "just" a collection of molecules, essentially devoid of attributes. Modern man refers to the old outlook as "animism" … and best relegated to the dustbin of history ...

I think that's probably an exaggeration. It seems to me that some intellectuals thought like that, mostly rather later, and I think it really took till the 19th century before that view was dominant. But not everyone did even then: see how country people thought in, say, the novels of Thomas Hardy. There is, however, an important shift in how science as an activity is viewed in the writings of Francis Bacon (_Novum Organum Scientiarum_, 1620).

Slightly further on they continue

> The pre-eminent centrist and thinker of the early 1800s, Johnan Wolfgang von Goethe, did not embrace the mechanistic views that contemporaneous science adopted during his time, and he denied rationality's superiority as the sole interpreter of reality.

I shan't comment on Goethe: I don't read German. But I tend to think that Saruman's assumption of a many-coloured garment in the _Lord of the Rings_ is a reference to Goethe's writings on the rainbow and colour, which I'm sure Tolkien must have read and talked about. Saruman, who has discarded his white cloak, can't see that sometimes a whole is greater than the sum of its parts. There's surely a link between this and his destructiveness.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: LiFi
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lifi.10404/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-31T12:35:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lifi.10404/post-119029>

I hadn't heard of this before ...

> CEA-Leti will demonstrate its new prototype for wireless high data rate Li-Fi (light fidelity) transmission at Light + Building 2014 in Frankfurt, Germany, March 30-April 4. The technology employs the high-frequency modulation capabilities of light-emitting diode (LED) engines used in commercial lighting. It achieves throughputs of up to 10Mb/s at a range of three meters, suitable for HD video streaming or Internet browsing, using light power of less than 1,000 lumens and with direct or even indirect lighting.

This is the bit that caught my eye:

> Other technical and market factors also are increasing interest in data transmission through lighting. These include crowding of the conventional radiofrequency (RF) spectrum, the mobile data-traffic explosion in cellular networks, **and the need for wireless data transmission without electromagnetic field (EMF) interference.**

There's a tacit admission that EMF is problematic ... even if only in the context of too much in the spectrum already.

[http://www.nanotech-now.com/news.cgi?story\_id=49241](http://www.nanotech-now.com/news.cgi?story_id=49241)

### sooperb — 2014-03-31T12:41:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lifi.10404/post-119031>

Thanks for that snippet, I will be floating that past doubting Thomas!

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-31T12:57:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lifi.10404/post-119032>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=119031)
>
> Thanks for that snippet, I will be floating that past doubting Thomas!

_Caveat --_ I take it by "interference" they mean with other EMF signals and devices not with biological systems. Of course, we know both are possible ...

It seems to me all you really have to do to raise the possibility is to show that at the cellular level communication is electronic. After that, once you start to look at the evidence ...

The piece that someone recently tweeted to Jack is chilling -- and that is based on a study published in the British Medical Journal not just ... well you know what people say.

[https://twitter.com/fredgabberline/status/449337496422600705](https://twitter.com/fredgabberline/status/449337496422600705)

Short piece in the Independent on LiFi. It's British technology.  I didn't know anything was these days!

[http://www.independent.co.uk/news/s...-250-times-faster-than-broadband-8909320.html](http://www.independent.co.uk/news/science/lifi-revolution-internet-connections-using-light-bulbs-are-250-times-faster-than-broadband-8909320.html)

Note:

> the technology would also be of use in situations where radio frequencies cannot be used for fear of interfering with electronic circuitry.

_That's_ what they're worried about. If RF transmissions bugger up someone's brain so what?

It's not going to replace existing technology:

> One draw-back is that the data receiver would have to be in sight of the transmitter-bulb as visible light does not penetrate solid materials.

Kind of interesting, anyway. More here:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Li-Fi](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Li-Fi)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: More schools act against mobile phones

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-31T13:47:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-schools-act-against-mobile-phones.10405/post-119036>

It's not the EMF; its the distraction they represent:

> Schools in the Truro area are taking measures to control the increasing numbers of children using mobile phones.
>
> Latest survey results show nearly nine in ten (88 per cent) of 12-14 year olds now own a mobile phone.
> Schools insist on pupils keeping their phones out of sight but face a difficult battle with children intent on using the mobile devices.
>
> The survey by Halifax Insurance said the study found today’s children each own an average of £924 worth of electronic devices, and a third (33 per cent) check their phone for messages several times an hour ...
>
> \[At some schools in Truro\] no mobile phones are allowed but if they are brought to school they must be switched off.
>
> A spokesman for the school said: “If a pupil breaches the school policy then the mobile phone will be confiscated and handed to the head teacher." ...

[http://www.westbriton.co.uk/Schools...le-phone-use/story-20878542-detail/story.html](http://www.westbriton.co.uk/Schools-Truro-area-act-control-mobile-phone-use/story-20878542-detail/story.html)

# Thread: Aluminum Cans and Seafood
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aluminum-cans-and-seafood.10415/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kyrakitty — 2014-04-01T01:19:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aluminum-cans-and-seafood.10415/post-119127>

I do eat fresh raw oysters as often as I can but it isn't always possible. Smoked oysters in a can are usually packed in aluminum with olive oil not spring water. I have been having a can 5-6 days a week. I really like eating smoked oysters in the can they taste soooo yummy to me. I know a lot of people can't stand them BUT...

QUESTION: Is this doing more harm than good due to the aluminum cans and/or the oysters not being fresh?

### Tanya — 2014-04-01T03:01:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aluminum-cans-and-seafood.10415/post-119150>

> [kyrakitty said:](/goto/post?id=119127)
>
> I do eat fresh raw oysters as often as I can but it isn't always possible. Smoked oysters in a can are usually packed in aluminum with olive oil not spring water. I have been having a can 5-6 days a week. I really like eating smoked oysters in the can they taste soooo yummy to me. I know a lot of people can't stand them BUT...
>
> QUESTION: Is this doing more harm than good due to the aluminum cans and/or the oysters not being fresh?

I don't know what brand you buy, but if it is Crown Prince oysters, for example, they are not on their list of products in BPA free cans. Another thing to consider [http://www.crownprince.com/bpa-free-cans.htm](http://www.crownprince.com/bpa-free-cans.htm)

### kyrakitty — 2014-04-01T03:30:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aluminum-cans-and-seafood.10415/post-119152>

Aie ya yai! Yes that stinking BPA. You are so right great reminder thank you for looking out Tanya. That is yet another thing on all our lists to have to find products without them.

Well the good news, if there is such a thing with BPA, is that we are clear BPA is bad for you so it is easy to do research to find canned items without it.

However, what I am extremely unclear on AND I have scoured the world wide web AND this forum is the aluminum issue. If I eat a lot of canned fish products like smoked oysters, mackerel, sardiens and if it is BPA free but is all packed in aluminum Is this doing more harm than good due to the aluminum cans? I don't want to have aluminum getting stored up in my brain or anywhere else in my body. Coca cola can eat through anything so I can see why aluminum and soda is a bad bad deal but does the same hold true for fish packed in olive oil or spring water?

### Tanya — 2014-04-01T03:48:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aluminum-cans-and-seafood.10415/post-119154>

> [kyrakitty said:](/goto/post?id=119152)
>
> Aie ya yai! Yes that stinking BPA. You are so right great reminder thank you for looking out Tanya. That is yet another thing on all our lists to have to find products without them.
>
> Well the good news, if there is such a thing with BPA, is that we are clear BPA is bad for you so it is easy to do research to find canned items without it.
>
> However, what I am extremely unclear on AND I have scoured the world wide web AND this forum is the aluminum issue. If I eat a lot of canned fish products like smoked oysters, mackerel, sardiens and if it is BPA free but is all packed in aluminum Is this doing more harm than good due to the aluminum cans? I don't want to have aluminum getting stored up in my brain or anywhere else in my body. Coca cola can eat through anything so I can see why aluminum and soda is a bad bad deal but does the same hold true for fish packed in olive oil or spring water?

I see what you mean..... I could not find anything specifically about the aluminum either. However, canned fish is an oily product (greater risk there) and heated in the can for sterilization purposes (another risk)... I might need to stop using canned fish as much as I do now

### Tanya — 2014-04-01T16:03:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aluminum-cans-and-seafood.10415/post-119214>

Still thinking about it, if the aluminum cans have the BPA free lining, it should hopefully reduce the risk of aluminum getting transferred into the food.....

### kyrakitty — 2014-04-01T18:49:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aluminum-cans-and-seafood.10415/post-119258>

Tanya!!! Regarding quote below. I know how yo feel that's why I finally posted the link asking the question.

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=119154)
>
> I see what you mean..... I could not find anything specifically about the aluminum either. However, canned fish is an oily product (greater risk there) and heated in the can for sterilization purposes (another risk)... I might need to stop using canned fish as much as I do now

Regarding quote below. It starts to really get your curiosity going when you your hungry and wonder what to eat right that moment doesn't it?

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=119214)
>
> Still thinking about it, if the aluminum cans have the BPA free lining, it should hopefully reduce the risk of aluminum getting transferred into the food.....

### Tanya — 2014-04-01T18:56:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aluminum-cans-and-seafood.10415/post-119263>

Yes, canned fish does serve its purpose and can be very handy at times. We all need to strive not to rely that much on it though

### kyrakitty — 2014-04-01T19:11:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aluminum-cans-and-seafood.10415/post-119267>

What's even more interesting, that I've noticed, is that producers go to great lengths to seemingly hide the materials used in the can.

If you go to their websites most will not list the materials of the cans. They list BPA free or not and if it is cooked in the can or not but in my paranoia most seemingly stay far away from listing their cans as tin or aluminum. You have to call to find out if it's tin or aluminum. I am sure I just made up this conspiracy theory but it sure feels true when I am researching and hitting dead ends. LOL

# Thread: Technology Killer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technology-killer.10420/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2014-04-01T16:17:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technology-killer.10420/post-119218>

[http://www.airtrafficmanagement.net/2014/04/lithium-cargo-facing-outright-ban/](http://www.airtrafficmanagement.net/2014/04/lithium-cargo-facing-outright-ban/)

LI batteries power everything tech from your laptop, to cellphones, tablets, toys etc. back in 2010 - there was a crash of a UPS plane that was carrying a full load of these batteries. If you look at apple products 2010 was the last year the had a BIG product release.

Apple knew the score and has spent the past 4 years working on a new manufacturing and supply chain process, changing its entire mfr and supply chain models. Right now I'd be looking at 3 things - what type of battery have they developed that gives the same power as LI, whats the first product its going to be in, and how much $$$ is apple going to make off making batteries.

Its going to be interesting to see how the tech industry is going to get around this. I don't think they can. especially if they every find MH370 and the 500KG of batteries the a/c was carrying was the source of the accident.

computers, smartphones, and ipads are going to be come status symbols people won't be able to afford them any more.

this might be a way out.

### nonchalant — 2014-04-02T15:04:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technology-killer.10420/post-119392>

They'll just start making potassium batteries... but those would probably be dangerous as well.

# Thread: Medieval Danish latrines unearthed
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/medieval-danish-latrines-unearthed.10423/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-01T21:06:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/medieval-danish-latrines-unearthed.10423/post-119284>

> A number of Medieval wooden barrels have been uncovered in Denmark, revealing their less- than-glamorous contents.
>
> Originally built to transport goods and store fish, the barrels were converted into latrines — still filled with their original contents. …
>
> Described as being in "excellent condition," the human excrement can give scientists unique insight into what people ate in Denmark in the Middle Ages.
>
> "Preliminary results of analysis show that raspberries were popular in Odense in the 1300s." … \[Maria Elisabeth\] Lauridsen, \[the archaeologist in charge of the excavation\] said. ...

[http://news.discovery.com/history/archaeology/medieval-poop-still-smelling-bad-found-140401.htm](http://news.discovery.com/history/archaeology/medieval-poop-still-smelling-bad-found-140401.htm)

Also:

[http://cphpost.dk/news/archaeologists-make-smelly-discovery-in-odense.8916.html](http://cphpost.dk/news/archaeologists-make-smelly-discovery-in-odense.8916.html)

# Thread: Do you think this RO is as good as it gets?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-you-think-this-ro-is-as-good-as-it-gets.10424/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kyrakitty — 2014-04-01T21:32:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-you-think-this-ro-is-as-good-as-it-gets.10424/post-119291>

So Crystal Springs home water delivery was a dud for me here in California because they add salts to improve flavor and every other home bottler does the same but then I stumbled upon Culligan. This is a national company and the local sales reps have local bottlers that produce RO water without added salts/minerals.

Attached is the water analysis report. Everything on it they tested for says ND no detectable traces except the minute amounts listed below. I know either way we need to periodically test the water ourselves to make sure they are living up to the advertised standards but based on the attached report and the few detectable traces I think this is the best bet for RO water. Notice the PH is 4.8 not 7 very interesting….

So, if you are in the US and having problems finding RO water without added minerals I bet Culligan can help but just to be sure I suggest you ask each supplier “do you offer RO water without adding minerals” if they say yes then ask your local Culligan rep to provide a water analysis. 877-386-0823

FYI the website lists almost zero details about their home water delivery service instead the site rambles on about water softners and installing water filtration systems. So if you want info on home delivery water service call cuz you won’t find anything online. 877-386-0823

I think this is as good as it gets for bottled home delivery and if I test it and it matches then I hit a home run. Any thoughts and comments would be appreciated if anything is missing that I should ask for from this bottler.

A few notable details:
\-Municipal water turned into RO water that does not have added minerals by the bottler
\-Sample date 3/25/2013
\-The following was detectable on the attached PDF:

PAGE 2
Chloride = .6
Copper = ND but then one line below it continues with copper stats & says.
Est TDS by Cond = 1.0

PAGE 9
Conductivity = 2
PH = 4.8 (not exactly neutral or 7)

PAGE 11
Sodium (Na) 0.1

PAGE 12
Potassium (K) 0.2

### kyrakitty — 2014-04-01T21:32:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-you-think-this-ro-is-as-good-as-it-gets.10424/post-119292>

Was previously using local Mt. Shasta spring water (claimed TDS ~70).

Of course I need to do some testing next and see if they are living up to what they claim.

RobH

# Thread: Instead of Big Ass Breakfast How About Big Fat Breakfast?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instead-of-big-ass-breakfast-how-about-big-fat-breakfast.10427/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-04-02T08:21:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instead-of-big-ass-breakfast-how-about-big-fat-breakfast.10427/post-119368>

What would be wrong with making the first meal at breakfast a large fat meal, trying to prime the body for ketosis, then follow that up with protein later? Someone argue against that idea, please.

One reason for protein first might be appetite suppression, but if you are skinny person who has problems gaining weight, suppressing appetite might not be a requirement for you?

If you are prediabetic or diabetic, getting your body into ketosis early on in day might be a good way to lower the body's endogenous levels of glucose production and fight the "dawn effect" with glucose rising when you awake and keep endogenous glucose production low.

### nicld — 2014-04-02T16:22:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instead-of-big-ass-breakfast-how-about-big-fat-breakfast.10427/post-119395>

I think Jack has said that if you are not leptin resistant a big fat breakfast is great. I would add that if you have issues with fat malabsorption protein is the way to go.

### persistence — 2014-04-02T20:55:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instead-of-big-ass-breakfast-how-about-big-fat-breakfast.10427/post-119453>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=119395)
>
> I think Jack has said that if you are not leptin resistant a big fat breakfast is great. I would add that if you have issues with fat malabsorption protein is the way to go.

I may have issues with fat absorption (doctor is testing for this), but I know if I have a few hours of teas with fats added I produce ketones. Therefore I must be absorbing \*enough\* fat to get the desired effect.

I think proteins are NOT a substitute for fats. My insight is that by forcing the body to produce ketones right as you rise, that this might reset the liver's endogenous level of glucose production. Protein won't give you ketone production and - for some people - the protein apparently creates more glucose through gluconeogenesis, so it would have the opposite of the desired effect of lowering glucose levels.

Also a lot of Jack's advocacy is based on building fat-burning pathways and these require you to be in ketosis.

### nicld — 2014-04-02T22:17:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instead-of-big-ass-breakfast-how-about-big-fat-breakfast.10427/post-119475>

I have a fat malabsorption issue and was told that I need to stay away from alot of fats and concentrate on proteins until I get the issue corrected. Specifically no ketogenic diet. This is easier said than done and may be the reason why I can not get my ketosis strips to read anything but that's just my N=1.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-02T23:53:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instead-of-big-ass-breakfast-how-about-big-fat-breakfast.10427/post-119500>

Protein is the gateway to the Pentosephosphate pathway for most people who are metabolic broken. Very few of us aren't metabolically broken. I will say that over time I've gradually transitioned to FAT being my primary fuel for breakfast. Last year I got to the point where I could barely choke down 1 egg for breakfast. but fat. SIGN ME UP. But I'd been doing the BAB for about 2yrs at that point (I'm metabolically deranged - it took me that long to get to the point where protein wasn't the solution)

That said... if you don't have the issues Kate or Nicole mention - no reason not to try it. If you find that things are going well... go back to the BAB - it helps ease the transition so you can get into the PPP. it normally takes 18-24mths to transition into the PPP

### persistence — 2014-04-03T05:25:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instead-of-big-ass-breakfast-how-about-big-fat-breakfast.10427/post-119559>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=119475)
>
> I have a fat malabsorption issue and was told that I need to stay away from alot of fats and concentrate on proteins until I get the issue corrected. Specifically no ketogenic diet. This is easier said than done and may be the reason why I can not get my ketosis strips to read anything but that's just my N=1.

What was your doctor's strategy for "fixing" the fat absorption problem? I think a lot of doctors seem to have the attitude "eat carbs not fat, problem fixed". But of course for me that creates a different problem (high glucose).

### persistence — 2014-04-03T05:26:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instead-of-big-ass-breakfast-how-about-big-fat-breakfast.10427/post-119560>

I will say that over time I've gradually transitioned to FAT being my primary fuel for breakfast. Last year I got to the point where I could barely choke down 1 egg for breakfast. but fat. SIGN ME UP. But I'd been doing the BAB for about 2yrs at that point (I'm metabolically deranged - it took me that long to get to the point where protein wasn't the solution)
>

Did you feel anything different when you transitioned to the PPP? What are the markers that this change is happening?

### nicld — 2014-04-03T16:01:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instead-of-big-ass-breakfast-how-about-big-fat-breakfast.10427/post-119600>

for me it was the in ability to eat anything other than fat upon waking. It was also an intense desire to get in the CT tank. Its going to be different for everyone.... but remember non-native emf can knock you out...

### persistence — 2014-04-03T20:24:34+0300

### persistence — 2014-04-03T22:08:19+0300

You're still in charge.....and you can make the fake stuff do whatever you like. Don't like something someone says? just move to the next virtual reality. Or website. Or or or. I've heard it's also why online p.orno.graphy is so powerful because the people using it are always in charge...they don't have to relate, to give, and receive or be told what they do is not welcome. Just pushing buttons or typing 'commands' (pun intended) is much easier....and way less vulnerable. It takes not much relational skills.

I remember reading some sci-fi novels in school....

### sjoshua — 2014-04-03T20:03:13+0300

Hmmm, I understand reducing uneccessary scans, but bloodwork ? can anyone explain which basic standard bloodwork shows "false positives"? All just a $$$ issue and let'em get really sick before we have to treat.

A few years ago, the vitamin d test was descheduled from our universal health care (ontario) due to a 700% or more increase in testing. The cost was I think $55 to the system. Since then, a patient still needs a requ from the dr. but the cost to the patient is $30??? Who was making the difference? Worse is that eventhough you pay out of pocket some drs will not give the req. I wouldn't care if we could order our own tests but we cannot get blood draws from a lab without a dr's signature...unless we cross the border....seriously!

### PaulG — 2014-04-03T03:04:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-electrical-properties-of-cancer-cells.10437/post-119532>

For example..

-   Living systems are characterized by a complex spectrum of coordinated action and rapid intercommunication between all parts (Ho, 1996). The ideal complex activity spectrum of a healthy state is polychromatic where all frequencies of stored energy in the spectral range are equally represented and utilized (Ho, 1996). In an unhealthy state some frequencies may be present in excess and other frequencies may be missing. For example it has been reported that a healthy forest emits a polychromatic spectrum of acoustical frequencies and an unhealthy forest will have holes in its frequency spectrum. Yet when the forest regains its health it again emits a polychromatic spectrum of frequencies. The frequency holes got filed in!

# Thread: Is this too drastic for a thyroid solution?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-this-too-drastic-for-a-thyroid-solution.10440/>
**Subforum:** Heal Your Hormones
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-04-03T18:41:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-this-too-drastic-for-a-thyroid-solution.10440/post-119627>

I know that there is a ton of information both on the web and on this site regarding thyroid health. I have a few books about it as well.

I’ve been dealing with this issue for a very long time now since I was diagnosed as being hypothyroid some years ago and was put on 60 mg. of Armour thyroid. That dose had stayed the same even though my labs results varied.

Here is the last one that I had:
Thyroid panel:
Free thyroxine 0.9 (0.9-1.8)
T3% Binding 38.53 (22.00-37.00)
TSH 4.58 (0.35-5.50)
Antithyroid AB panel -negative

ACI from Diagnos-Techs:
Free cortisol rhythm: 5-8 AM 10 (13-24)
11-1PM 4 (5-10)
4-5 PM 5 (3-8)
10PM-12AM 3 (1-4)
DHEA 4 (3-10)

My basal temp was low - it never reached above 95.8, but I had seen it to go down to 93.5.

Here is my dilemma. Starting a month ago, I decided myself to cut my Armour Thyroid to 30 mg because it was my last refill and I did not feel like making an appointment to see my doctor for a new one. Yes, I always need to see the doctor for it and it and I feel drained afterwards.

Since I don’t take any additional iodine, at the same time I increased my consumption of seafood/seaweed.

This is what I see now. In the past, I could check mark almost all the symptoms (except for the weight gain) on the hypothyroid check list. Now only a few of them remain and even they could be interpreted as
signs of my other health problems. My basal temp went to 96.4 five days ago and it is the same since.

In a few days I will be completely out of the Armour thyroid and I still want to continue on this route to see if I can make it without it altogether and control my thyroid with everything else that I’ve been doing lately.

I hope I am not playing Russian Roulette here and I need to know how long can someone go on without their thyroid meds before their condition gets worse.

### nonchalant — 2014-04-03T19:00:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-this-too-drastic-for-a-thyroid-solution.10440/post-119629>

Tanya, you always had to see the doc for every Armour refill, but that doc never changed the strength?
I don't know the answer to your question, but I applaud your taking things into your own hands. Keep watching your temp and other indicators (as if you need to be told, lol!)
It would be nice to go to the doc after a week or so, get the labs again, and also a refill to keep on hand.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-03T19:06:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-this-too-drastic-for-a-thyroid-solution.10440/post-119632>

It looks like your body's trying to make T4 - I'd be interested in seeing what your Rt3 looks like as well as Free T3. looking at your TSH you've got an issue with conversion. how's the rest of your hormone and your lipid panel look? everything with that will tell the story....

### kovita — 2014-11-12T15:38:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-this-too-drastic-for-a-thyroid-solution.10440/post-147126>

> [Pebbles said:](/goto/post?id=147121)
>
> Hi All, I am new here ( even my profile is not fully fixed, will sort it out) and new at all on FB -quantum/epipaleo as well (around 3 weeks), so just swallowing the news, knowledge and everything..... However I need to ask you about hormone lab results, since Friday I am going back to my endokr doc for lab test interpretation: I am on keto-paleo, sometimes ketone bodies appearing in lab test as well- around 1,5-2,5 mmol/l.
> Found out up to now, that being on paleo/keto thyroid hormones are on a lower level, b/c less is needed to run the 'operation'. The gluc-inzulin test 0-60-120min also gives a false result = periferial IR, so IR can not be proved. Does affect the oestrogen, progesterone, testosterone, aldosterone hormones as well? Are these hormones also lower a bit in case of paleo/keto ( on purpose not written epi-paleo, since I am not doing CT yet).
> [http://www.jackkruse.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/12/Optimizing-Labs-PDF.pdf](http://www.jackkruse.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/12/Optimizing-Labs-PDF.pdf) - these data given by JK are related to anyone, or just for the ones being on epi-paleo?
> Thanks for clearance,

Hi Eniko,

how long have yo been on keto diet? How was your ketogenic diet? Fish and seafood based, seasonal...? If i understand well you are concerned about insulin resistance. It is normal physiological reaction to the lack of carbs in the diet, that carb metabolism is depressed (including insulin production). Physiological insulin resistence on low carb diet is normal and expected. it may be difficulti to explain thi to your doctor, but unless he is complete moron he should accept it. You would not normally pass the oral glucose load test unless you get prepared with few days of cRb loading (some people might need weeks to regain the insulin resistance expected to see in carb burners). Thyroid hormones might get on lower levels on ketodiet as well, but I would suggest epipaleo with loads of fish and seafood would not cause this long term. The data given by JK are valid for everyone, those the values to target.

### nonchalant — 2014-11-13T03:32:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-this-too-drastic-for-a-thyroid-solution.10440/post-147222>

I stopped taking armour thyroid a month ago. A few days ago, however, I noticed a strange sensation while in my throat while drinking cold water. It felt like I had an enlargement in there. I realized that for the previous week I hadn't had much raw seafood, relying on sardines and tuna, and skipping some days. (I was out of town and not near my seafood source.) So I got some pronto, since my body was obviously calling for more iodine. Two days (and 3 dozen oysters later) the sensation went away. It will be a long time before I forget to buy my oysters before leaving town again! No, I didn't even consider taking Lugols.

Daily sardines/tuna just doesn't have the charm it used to. Daily oysters is easy, though. I think one reason Jack recommends them is because in some areas, like where I am, oysters are the only seafood you can get raw and never frozen.

Oh, and about a week after taking my last thyroid pill, I went to the doc for more labs. I expected my TSH to shoot up, but it was ok. And the others, T3/free T3, T4/free T4, and reverse T3 were ok. Interesting. I kept the rest of my pills, but if I have any issues, I'll just up the seafood again. It appears to be the only medicine I need.

### melrito — 2014-11-14T16:23:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-this-too-drastic-for-a-thyroid-solution.10440/post-147377>

Nonchalant, it generally takes several weeks (or longer) before the thyroid hormone levels change after stopping thyroid replacement such as Armour (if they are going to change). When I stopped, it took 6 weeks before my levels changed.

### AJT — 2014-11-16T17:37:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-this-too-drastic-for-a-thyroid-solution.10440/post-147521>

the thyroid medicine is your iodine replacement, once its used up you begin to have iodine deficiency issues.

### kovita — 2014-11-16T17:55:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-this-too-drastic-for-a-thyroid-solution.10440/post-147523>

> [Pebbles said:](/goto/post?id=147135)
>
> Hi Kovita, ( we are neighbours:, am in BP))
> Thanks for your answer!
> I am concerned about the doc misinterpreting my results ) When told, that test will look like IR, even if I am not, or I am, they said is not true, b/c even on leto, glukogan is working in my pancreas, so I get glucose regularly in my blood, as a result insulin does not stop either....
> As mentioned, I know that gluc-ins tests are giving false results, but wanted to know about all the other hormones as well, what refs to consider for myself. I am on paleo since 5 years, on keto since 2-3 month ( decreased veggies, increased fat). I am on a mixed sea-offals-meat menu.
> Unexplained weight gain not detected on the time being ( LR/IR/PCOS or whatever), than with paleo lost 20kg in first 6 month, than in next 2 years regained 10kg.
> So now doing a full check what could be/should be.
> It is harder to compare, b/c in Jacks optimum labs the unit of measure is not mentioned, and we have sometimes different unit.... I will have to do the cross calculations.

your doc is right about glukagon and insulin being secreted and present, what is he missing is the part about timing. If your body expected carbs, the insulin will be released while chewing already and if your body expects fat it will get released in minimal amount much later in the digestion process and mainly in the night, when we are designed to be ketogenic by default. If you have regulary longterm 15g of carbs a day and you receive 75g of pure glucose first thing in the morning, you have no chance to react "on time as per the tables". Because the normality is being defined by peolle who ingest 300g plus carbs a day. You need first grade for this mathematics ;-)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: MCT Oil and How Fats Metabolize
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-how-fats-metabolize.10441/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-04-03T22:47:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-how-fats-metabolize.10441/post-119662>

As I pointed out in another thread, I may have a defect in how I digest fats. Apparently, however, not all fats process in the gut the same way? MCT Oil is apparently bypassing some steps and being shunted right to the liver?

My first question is does anyone have a good reference that would quickly explain in summary level detail how different types of fats metabolize in the gut and body? I'm thinking monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and the various kinds of saturated, as well as the "specialty" fats like MCT Oil. My goal here is practical: I want to identify which fats I have the best chance to digest easily, and then I want to experiment with those.

I also want to identify which fats raise ketones and which are more benign on ketones.

My second question is on MCT specifically: is there a maximum amount of this that is safe to ingest in a single day? I read somewhere it is fed to critically ill patients, and you would guess it is making up very substantial percentages of calories for such people. Yet others make claims that more than three tablespoons a day makes them feel bad. That's a pretty big ocean living between those two end points....

### Dali Dula — 2014-04-04T05:27:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-how-fats-metabolize.10441/post-119709>

You will not retain MCT that your body doesn't use. What isn't needed is excreted. Increase amount until stools are lose then back off. My max is two to three tbs. This could change over time and with variations in activity.

### persistence — 2014-04-04T05:34:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-how-fats-metabolize.10441/post-119711>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=119709)
>
> You will not retain MCT that your body doesn't use. What isn't needed is excreted. Increase amount until stools are lose then back off. My max is two to three tbs. This could change over time and with variations in activity.

I read one place that for some applications, like seizures in children, that MCT is used for up to 60% of the calories ingested:

[http://www.webmd.com/vitamins-supplements/ingredientmono-915-MEDIUM CHAIN TRIGLYCERIDES MCTs.aspx?activeIngredientId=915&activeIngredientName=MEDIUM CHAIN TRIGLYCERIDES (MCTs)&source=3](http://www.webmd.com/vitamins-supplements/ingredientmono-915-MEDIUM+CHAIN+TRIGLYCERIDES+MCTs.aspx?activeIngredientId=915&activeIngredientName=MEDIUM+CHAIN+TRIGLYCERIDES+\(MCTs\)&source=3)

So if I could only eat three tablespoons, it clearly wouldn't be useful as a part of caloric planning for a diet.

I need to find some fat that:

1) I can eat in large quantity
2) Effectively generates ketones
3) Is easy to digest. MCT apparently bypasses some digestive steps and shunt straight to the liver, which for a person who has problems digesting fats might be a plus?
4) And - ideally - tends to not raise LDL. I guess coconut oil tends to raise LDL for most people significantly. Maybe MCT does the same?

### Dali Dula — 2014-04-04T05:51:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-how-fats-metabolize.10441/post-119713>

Perhaps if MCT was your main fuel your body would utilize more of it. I have never tried MCT to 60% of calories. I would stay close to home if I did until I adapted. Dave Asprey refers to this as "disaster pants"

### Penny — 2014-04-04T05:55:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-how-fats-metabolize.10441/post-119715>

I thought Jack Kruse said somewhere that it takes a couple of weeks for your gut flora to adapt to digesting the fat? You might try bile salts for the heck of it...

# Thread: Does Ribose Fuel Pancreatic Cancers?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ribose-fuel-pancreatic-cancers.10453/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-04-05T23:48:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ribose-fuel-pancreatic-cancers.10453/post-119961>

See this article here:

[http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v5/n2/full/cddis201438a.html](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v5/n2/full/cddis201438a.html)

and look for the section on the Pentium Phosphate Pathway.

It contains the last paragraph "The pentose sugars generated in the nonoxidative PPP phase serve as primary intermediates in the synthesis of nucleotides and nucleic acids. At times of increased cellular demand for the pentose monosaccharide, such as during cell growth and repair, these metabolic reactions enable excess pentoses to be recycled as intermediates of the glycolytic pathway and essential precursors for nucleic acid biosynthesis.[59](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v5/n2/full/cddis201438a.html#bib59) Recent work by Ying _et al._60 highlighted the importance of the reversible nonoxidative PPP phase in pancreatic tumor cells. Maintenance of pancreatic tumors was found to be strongly driven by mutated K-Ras, which stimulates glucose uptake and conveyance of its biosynthetic intermediates into the nonoxidative branch of the PPP, generating ribose-5-phosphate, which is then utilized for the synthesis of nucleic acids. Nevertheless, that study revealed, remarkably, that in contrast to the canonical paradigm in which generation of nonoxidative mediators is coupled with the synthesis of oxidative ones, the K-Ras-driven accelerated glycolytic flux in pancreatic tumor cells is reprogrammed to bypass the oxidative NADPH biosynthesis phase of the PPP and instead robustly enhances the nonoxidative branch[60](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v5/n2/full/cddis201438a.html#bib60) ([Figure 1](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v5/n2/full/cddis201438a.html#fig1)). Thus, this study disclosed a novel connection between oncogenic K-Ras and the nonoxidative PPP phase, linking K-Ras directly to DNA biosynthesis. The finding that the generation of pentoses was decoupled from NADPH generation led to the suggestion that a substitute metabolic pathway may be responsible for maintaining cellular redox homeostasis PDAC. An alternative glutamine-consuming pathway generating NADPH was indeed discovered recently, as discussed below in the chapter on glutamine addiction."

I don't understand enough of the passage to connect the dots. Would free ribose in the blood fuel this PPP pathway in the tumor? Is there anything that might provide similar energization in normal cells but not in pancreatic tumors that use the PPP?

# Thread: Elderly female relative given CHALK by the NHS!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-female-relative-given-chalk-by-the-nhs.10459/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-06T18:10:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-female-relative-given-chalk-by-the-nhs.10459/post-120066>

This angered me at first, but I soon cooled down. Resenting such things doesn't really help. I think it important to note them, though.

So this lady had been sent for bone scans, there being a concern after X-rays for a broken arm showed less bone density than expected. In fact, it turns out she's not too bad on that score.

I had persuaded her to take some vitamin D3 and some K2 complex capsules. She also takes a little magnesium in the evenings -- when she remembers. She doesn't like swallowing magnesium malate tablets, in common with many people -- admittedly, they are large -- so I bought a drum of Naturalcalm and gave her that. It may not be the best form, but if we can compromise, maybe we can get people to do _something_.

Time passes. She now tells me that the doctor has given her vitamin D plus calcium tablets after the results came back from the hospital -- and told her to take nothing else, in case she "gets too much". I looked at the tablets. They have got D3 in: I suspected they might be D2, but at least that was not so. However, they're quite clearly stated on the packet to be calcium carbonate -- in other words, _chalk_. This is what made me angry. I was disgusted at this.

It's such primitive thinking it's like something out of _The Golden Bough_. (Fraser may not capture the full complexity of the thinking of some of his subjects, but he does show people making some pretty silly connections.) Not only is this is the simplest kind of jumping-to-conclusions thinking -- "bones have calcium in; therefore, give calcium" -- it's the _form_ of calcium that concerns me.

I'm not convinced that people need as much calcium as in the U.S. RDA. Cordain has argued not. However, if you look back at Weston Price's figures, he apparently _did_ find isolated populations getting rather a lot in their food. I think it would be reasonable to see this as an open question. But surely what should be clear enough is that you don't give people chalk. If she had been given microcrystalline calcium hydroxyapaptite (the form in which the mineral is found in bone) that would be one thing. That's an absorbable form. Chalk is not -- although it is cheap, of course.

There's been evidence that this is not a good idea out for quite some time now. Here's a recent article from the BMJ:

> Calcium supplements might increase the risk of having a heart attack, and should be “taken with caution,” concludes research published in the online issue of the journal **Heart.**
>
> Furthermore, boosting overall calcium intake from dietary sources confers no significant advantage in terms of staving off heart disease and stroke, the findings indicate. …
>
> Supplements, on the other hand, cause calcium levels in the blood to soar above the normal range, and it is this flooding effect which might ultimately be harmful, they suggest.
>
> “Calcium supplements have been widely embraced by doctors and the public, on the grounds that they are a natural and therefore safe way of preventing osteoporotic fractures,” they write.
>
> “It is now becoming clear that taking this micronutrient in one or two daily \[doses\] is not natural, in that it does not reproduce the same metabolic effects as calcium in food,” they say.

[http://group.bmj.com/group/media/la...to-significantly-increased-heart-attack-risk/](http://group.bmj.com/group/media/latest-news/calcium-supplements-linked-to-significantly-increased-heart-attack-risk/)

I'm also a little disgusted that the NHS didn't test for this lady's level of 25-hydroxy Vitamin D2 and D3. How can you know how much to give, if you don't test?

Give someone a product and push them out the door is effectively what we have here. Is this a "standard of _care_"?

It's unconscionable.

Fortunately, the lady's husband who used to be an industrial chemist was greatly amused when he found out she'd been given chalk. "We're on the chalk here," he pointed out, "and it's in the drinking water anyway," so if that's what's needed, you get it anyway.

Anyway, the lady has agreed that regular consumption of leafy green vegetables along with raw cheese and bone-in canned fish is likely to give her much calcium as she needs. She's not going to take the CaCO₃ tablets. I'm going to get the Path Lab at the City Hospital in Birmingham, who do a vitamin D bloodspot test direct to the public as a sideline, to send her a kit.

Thank God. The NHS would have been setting her up for a heart attack:

[http://abcnews.go.com/Health/HeartH...ased-heart-attack-risk-post/story?id=13411283](http://abcnews.go.com/Health/HeartHealth/calcium-supplements-linked-increased-heart-attack-risk-post/story?id=13411283)

### [email redacted] — 2014-04-06T19:11:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-female-relative-given-chalk-by-the-nhs.10459/post-120070>

Mike - you know SO much! I really am impressed. What do you 'do for a living' if you care to answer. Fine if you do not.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-06T20:12:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-female-relative-given-chalk-by-the-nhs.10459/post-120075>

Nothing very exciting, Patrick. I was ill for quite a long time. Depression, which turned out to have hypothyroidism as an underlying cause. I can remember having to lie down on the edge of a fountain I was so tired once -- I could hardly move: it was like I'd been poleaxed. I guess after you've had a lot of time not working you kind of lose your employability, unless you have some trade or professional qualification. (Studied a purely academic subject at university and never got any professional qualifications.) I'm currently back to doing fairly basic office work.

I guess I have a bone to pick with the doctors over here myself, too. I went in with some slight vision problems a few months ago. The doctor was very good. He was actually an eye specialist doing GP locum work. He took a thorough look, but told me to get an optician's appointment, too, because they have more equipment than GPs have in a surgery. But what he said to me, which was interesting, was that as I came through the door he noticed my eyes were a little red. He'd obviously looked at my notes and knew I was hypothyroid, and told me that sometimes "the antibodies can attack the fat at the back of the eye". Now that's the _first_ time _any_ doctor has ever even hinted that my condition might be autoimmune in origin. They don't test for it over here. Their solution is to give you levothyroxine and push you out of there.

To be honest I don't think that's anywhere good enough. They're not even interested in getting at the cause. Politicians on the make over here used to say that our National Health Service was "the envy of the world" -- with the implication that they personally were responsible for its being so. Well, if that's so I hate to think of what others are putting up with. But I very much doubt it is. I keep hearing of services and lab tests in the U.S. that simply don't exist over here.

I think if I'd known what I know now a few years ago, I'd have tried whatever alternative methods I could to try to get the gland working properly again for itself. Even supposing a Paleo Diet or whatever could remove the problem, it's not going to start working again fully, if it's getting hormone supplied exogenously, is it?

### [email redacted] — 2014-04-07T04:15:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-female-relative-given-chalk-by-the-nhs.10459/post-120146>

Thanks Mike - Interesting what you say. About having 'the best health care system in the world' you are not alone in that LOL! Politicians especially Republicans here are constantly saying that though I think they have a hard time being heard about the kind of open laughing that causes amongst a lot of people. The system here is riddled with profiteering on every level it's hard to know where to start. I don't even have 'health insurance' now I prefer to BE healthy if I can rather than rely on 'insurance'

I read a book recently "Stop the Thyroid Madness" by Janie Bowthorpe which I think is quite good in de-constructing the 'standard care' in thyroid health. You may have heard of it or even read it but if you are interested I would say it's pretty good. Yeah I have wondered about taking ANY kind of hormone help, as you say aren't you then kind of setting up a situation where getting off it is difficult? I dont know if you followed the hormone series with Dr Tim Jackson but what I got from that above all was to mind of modulate the stress response. It seems so much can go bad or good depening on that starting point. I have done everything I can think of to de-stress ride a bike instead of a car etc etc

I will probably be in England June/July, London/Colchester area if it is at all interesing and not inconvenient we could possibly meet up? Just a question I am going to Ireland too so it should be a nice Summer. All the best.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-07T08:38:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-female-relative-given-chalk-by-the-nhs.10459/post-120188>

Yes, everyone always says they have the best regarding whatever it is, don't they? Maybe they actually believe it.

At least I don't think the Scots have claimed to have the best rugby team. But then no one would believe them if they did.

I just remembered an uncle was visiting at the weekend and related the agony he'd gone through after being prescribed statins. He refused to take them in the end, because he still likes to play tennis and couldn't with his muscles in pain. And shouldn't it be obvious to everyone by now, or at any rate doctors (whose business it is to know) that they don't even help what they're supposed to help -- i.e., the heart? Since that's a muscle, too, they're more likely to damage it. I also know people with diabetes who've been given silly -- or to be frank _**lethal**_ -- advice. One of them had another toe off recently.

My doctors' practice waiting-room has a poster up warning about the "dangers" of saturated fat. But a meta-analysis of nearly 350,000 subjects showed:

> there is no significant evidence for concluding that dietary saturated fat is associated with an increased risk of CHD or CVD. More data are needed to elucidate whether CVD risks are likely to be influenced by the specific nutrients used to replace saturated fat.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20071648](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20071648)

Just how large a sample does it take to convince people they're on the wrong track?

I feel rather disillusioned with the medical profession. They have some hard-won skills, and I think most of them mean well, but boy do they need to raise their game.

Looking at some of the things they're doing I wonder should we laugh or cry?

Thanks for mentioning the book. I haven't seen that one.

I had a thyroid book by Datis Kharrazian, but it was so difficult to understand and so far from anything I could do for myself it was no use to me.

It'd be nice to see you. Thanks. I could probably get to London, if I can get time off then.

All the best.

### [email redacted] — 2014-04-07T09:35:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-female-relative-given-chalk-by-the-nhs.10459/post-120194>

I was diagnosed as diabetic in 2003 and I don't taky any medication, never have. Though to be honest I don't know how 'well' I am doing really.

But it reminded me.................I was taking to my doctor oh about a year after that and he knew I had decided to go the 'diet and exercise' route so he knew I was 'against' medication in general. So at the end of our (5 minute) meeting he says 'I want you to take half an aspirin' and it feels weird going against a doctor. It's like being in confession (Catholic) and saying to the priest as he tells you your 'penance' "no I won't be doing that"

I mean you DON'T do that you CANNOT! Anyway I have been reading blah blah blah so I hold my ground but really don't really know why

Then he says "well ok then BUT I have just come back from a medical confererence and they clearly showed lipitor (a statin) is 'protective' in the case of diabetes even when there is no indication of a problem with cholesterol. This was new to me, I knew a little about statins eneough to know I did not care for them. Plus this was 'off label' use to be quite honest about it. So I objected...........he said no at the conference they clearly showed no question it was 'protective'.

I kind of 'smell a rat' I think 'medical conference' a bunch of salespeople running around giving 'inducements' so I decide to tell the doctor(priest) AGAIN "no I won't be doing that penance EITHER" I feel quite weird about it but decide 'keep your head here"

He looked dissapointed and said "ok I will set you up for your flu shot then" and I go "aargh......ummm...............no you won't actually" and well that was the end of our meeting. Now I like my doctor, though I no longer have health insurance he was my doctor for another 8 years and was always civil to him and I felt he meant well. He did a huge amount of swimming to 'stay fit' but then at the end of my time with him he had to stop that because of shoulder problems, he also 'proudly' showed me how they took out his prostate. Real easy, done by a robot actually or a robot controlled by a surgeon with a joy stick. Took about 10 minutes he said and no real side effects!!!

I decided to hold onto my own prostate unless things got very bad and he told me they no longer did the finger up you know where to 'test' so I said I will presume it's all good then

Yeah I got that book on thyroid you mentioned also but the other one was much more 'user friendly' and a lot more practical. It's main point is the 'older' thyroid medication taken directly from animals or what not is much better than the modern 'chemically pure' one. She gives lots of reasons and anecdotes as to why this is the case.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-08T21:23:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/elderly-female-relative-given-chalk-by-the-nhs.10459/post-120428>

> [[email redacted] said:](/goto/post?id=120194)
>
> He looked dissapointed and said "ok I will set you up for your flu shot then" and I go "aargh......ummm...............no you won't actually" and well that was the end of our meeting.

# Thread: Alzheimers, a positive step forward?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-a-positive-step-forward.10461/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-04-06T20:21:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-a-positive-step-forward.10461/post-120078>

I have copied this from a newsmail sent out by Mike Geary, an American fitness expert, he is always coming up with interesting stuff:

_"According to the Alzheimer’s Association, the number of people diagnosed with this debilitating disease is on the rise. It is expected that by the year 2050 almost 14 million people will be living with the condition known to rob people of their memory and impose anxiety and confusion.

Presently, over 5 million people suffer the effects of this disease that is now known as the 7th leading cause of death in our country. It is estimated that over $148 billion is spent each year treating this disease. There are no pharmaceutical options available to halt or reverse the symptoms of Alzheimer’s disease. Drugs being created now are made to reduce the amyloid plaques, made from a protein called Amyloid B (AB) that build up on the brain. These are a trademark of the disease.

However, recent studies reveal that small clumps of AB call oligomers appear years before the plaques even start to develop. The molecular structure of these oligomers is very different from AB. Further, they found that drugs designed to destroy amyloid plaques have no effect on oligomers. This could explain why experimental Alzheimer’s drugs have failed; they are focused on plaques, not their precursor, oligomers.

Case Study: A Natural Approach

Mary Newport, MD, has had some up close and highly personal experience with dementia and Alzheimer’s. When her 53 year old husband started showing signs of progressive dementia, which was later diagnosed as Alzheimer’s, she took action.

He began taking Alzheimer’s drugs such as Namenda, Exelon and Aricept – however, his disease continued to worsen. It was not until Dr. Newport tried to get her husband into a drug trial for a new Alzheimer’s drug that she started to research Alzheimer’s triggers.

Her research led her to the discovery that some brain cells may have a difficult time using glucose – the brain’s main source of energy. Without this fuel, neurons begin to die. An alternative energy source for brain cells is fats, known as ketones. When the body is deprived of carbohydrates, it naturally produces ketones.

The hard part is that most people can’t cut carbohydrates out of the body altogether, and in many respects this isn't always healthy. So another way to produce ketones is by consuming oils made from medium-chain triglycerides. MCT is an oil that is produced from coconut and palm oil.

The drug which was being used in the drug trial was just MCT oil at a dose of 20 grams. When MCT oil is metabolized, ketones, which are created by the body, not only protect against Alzheimer’s but may also reverse the symptoms. This is also a potential treatment for Parkinson’s, Huntington’s, type 2 diabetes and multiple and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.

Mr. Newport began to take coconut oil twice a day at a point where he could not even remember how to draw a clock. Two weeks after adding coconut oil to his diet his drawing ability improved. After a little over a month, the drawing had more clarity. It appeared as though the oil was lifting the fog.

After sixty days, he was alert, talkative and happy. He had more focus and concentration and was able to stay on task. He kept taking the same amount of coconut oil each day and the dementia continued to reverse. He was able to run again, and his reading comprehension improved dramatically. Over time, his short-term memory returned, and he was able to talk about past events with clarity. When he had a brain scan, the atrophy that had once been present was halted.

What We Know

We know that Alzheimer’s drugs have failed. We know that a drug company put a non-patentable natural substance (MCT) through an FDA trial, and it worked. But, it has now been discovered that a natural substance can be substituted for the expensive drug.

The amazing thing is that the natural substitute works better than the drug version. The ketones actually last for eight hours in the body when coconut oil is used versus three when the synthetic version is used. This is YET another example of a natural substance that is safer and more effective than dangerous drugs, which always have side effects since they are isolated compounds without the normal natural synergistic micronutrients found in natural remedies.

The benefits of organic coconut oil are remarkable and well documented. Always use high quality Extra Virgin Unrefined and Organic Coconut Oil."_

RobH

# Thread: Pacific herring once far more abundant
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pacific-herring-once-far-more-abundant.10462/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-06T20:53:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pacific-herring-once-far-more-abundant.10462/post-120081>

> Over the last century, Pacific herring, a forage fish of tremendous cultural, economic, and ecological importance, has declined in abundance over much of its range. We synthesize archaeological fisheries data spanning the past 10,000 y from Puget Sound in Washington to southeast Alaska to extend the ecological baseline for herring and contextualize the dynamics of modern industrial fisheries. While modern herring populations can be erratic and exhibit catastrophic declines, the archaeological record indicates a pattern of consistent abundance, providing an example of long-term sustainability and resilience in a fishery known for its modern variability. The most parsimonious explanation for the discrepancy between herring abundance in the ancient and more recent past is industrial harvesting over the last century

[http://www.pnas.org/content/early/2014/02/13/1316072111](http://www.pnas.org/content/early/2014/02/13/1316072111)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: In Case Anyone Feels Death Statistics Are Accurate
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-case-anyone-feels-death-statistics-are-accurate.10464/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-04-07T01:24:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-case-anyone-feels-death-statistics-are-accurate.10464/post-120118>

> It starts with a dead body, as so many mysteries do. A middle-aged man is found unconscious and rushed to a hospital. For four days, he lingers in a coma; on the fifth, he dies. The clues are few and dark and point in different directions. The man was a drug addict. He was diabetic. Some of his family members say that he acted strangely the last time they saw him conscious. Others disagree. The lab tests are inconclusive. Everything is inconclusive. If this were a mystery of the Conan Doyle kind, there would be a detective, and there would be a solution. In the event, there is neither. Instead, there is a young doctor, in her first year on the job, and there is a single piece of paper: a death certificate, on which she is meant to record, precisely and for posterity, why this person died.

[http://www.newyorker.com/reporting/2014/04/07/140407fa\_fact\_schulz](http://www.newyorker.com/reporting/2014/04/07/140407fa_fact_schulz)

Unfortunately a great deal of healthcare spending and assumptions about health are based on this....

Sharing this with my DH. He is an IT guy, and finds my collection of spiral notebooks and need to physically write things down a bit ridiculous and completely inefficient. Requests things emailed and in spreadsheets....I am sure one day he'll realize it is never going to happen. I think he thinks he is helping me to function in a world where this is the way its done. I have very little computer knowledge and no desire to learn, I know I frustrate people but I dont care. I have never skyped or any of those things. I have had a smart phone for a year and half....my contract is almost up and that sucker is going. I only got it because I gave into peer pressure. People were frustrated that they couldnt text me. That effin phone has become my third arm...I am never without it. I broke the screen months ago and thought if I dont fix it I'll hardly touch it. Wrong....been frying myself 10 times as much with my shattered screen I am sure. Ive gained 40lbs since I got that phone....coincidence? Maybe. The number of times I have forgone engaging with someone to stare blankly at some stupidness on my phone is just appauling. Looking forward to downgrading in June. Another step backwards I am going to savor

So looking at these E2 went CRAZY! as did SHGB (there's a surprise) Pregnenolone tanked, as did Progesterone

Taking 100mg Pregnenolone and 2ml of 20% compounded topical Pg & .4ml of 5% sublingual Pg.
50mg DHEA & 25mg 7KetoDHEA

200mg Selenium
500mg calcium d-glucarate and myomin
750mg Acetyl-lCaratine
1500mg CoQ10
40g D-ribose
4000mg Magnesium malate
100mg zinc
30mg Armour
1500mg Metformin
4 x .8mg cycloset

waiting to see what Dzugan's going to do. I haven't had a hormonal migraine since October.

Blood drawn for labs on Menstrual Cycle Day 21

Estradiol 590pg/mL, high should be ~150, consider aromatase inhibitors (Arimidex as first, start with 1mg/E3D then titrate biweekly), keep calcium D glucarate, lots of cruciferous

Progesterone 6.0 ng/mL curent progesterone supplementation is inefficient (and possibly not need), work on pregnenolone/cortisol first using preg-MLM
Testosterone Total=324(2-45) ng/dL (PCOS)
Prolactin=3.9ng/mL(3-30) (Cycloset)
DHEAs=592 Desirable for Women 275-400 μg/dL Reduce supplementation

cortisol total=14.5(4.6-20.6)

sodium=135 (135-146) work on this

glucose,fasting=99.7 think resistant starches, we need simple but effectife directions from dr K, I have same situation (HgA1c good but glucose goes up)

insulin,fasting=8 that is high, again may be resistant starches or anything else that works

TSH=0.669

you have FT3/RT3=17.6 close but still low

Your got relief from migraines because you got progesterone=6.0 ng/mL
There is a good chance that supplementing with pregnenolone will give you that or more.
Anyhow at CD21 you are still low on progesterone, it should be around 15.
I suggest to give pregnenolone priority, but if you still low then add progesterone as need.
I am guessing that if successful, you would use pregnenolone-MLM same daily dose every day, and additionally progesterone, likely on CD(16-24).

Pregnenolone, progesterone, DHEAs (couple others) are neuro-steroids, likely high correlation with migraines.

# Thread: What Is the Right Way to Maintain Electrolytes?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-04-08T14:31:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-120367>

It looks like I have really been hurting myself. I have low sodium. When dehydrated, I drink water, which when taken alone lowers sodium. I have episodes where heavy exercise leads to dehydration all night long. I end up taking a lot of water, which my body urinates quickly (maybe in part because I have low sodium and it doesn't want to sodium concentration to go lower?), which makes me drink more, and I end up in a vicious cycle. This got me to thinking how much electrolytes should we put into normal drinking water in order to maintain healthy levels of electrolytes like sodium?

There are sports drinks, but these have \*excess\* electrolytes. They are intended to be used when you are sweating heavily. I will certainly start taking electrolytes after exercise, but I don't think those are intended to be used as a replacement for normal drinking water. I want to make sure I have enough electrolytes in my normal drinking water so I don't contribute further to any low sodium condition.

Is there a formula out there for how we could make our own drinking water by adding in just the right amount of electrolyte to keep the body well balanced in electrolytes? The idea is to let the body regulate the amount of water it wants to keeps naturally, and not accidentally get into a cycle like the one I was where you have simultaneous low sodium and low hydration that no amount of pure water by itself can fix.

### Dali Dula — 2014-04-08T14:48:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-120371>

Spring water or well water?

### Inger — 2014-04-08T14:59:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-120373>

grounding + spring water? = be barefoot in the woods and drink from a spring there  lay on a hot rock by a lake and tan.. and drink lots of water.. I use to swim and drink all in one at my favorite lake.. the water is so clean I can do that. Feels pretty awesome

### persistence — 2014-04-08T20:34:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-120416>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=120371)

> Spring water or well water?

### persistence — 2014-04-09T02:49:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-120484>

Wondering if anyone has a formula for making your own electrolyte replacement drink. One electrolyte tablet I saw on Amazon has:

215 mg sodium, 63 mg potassium, 11 mg magnesium, 22 mg calcium, 100IU Vitamin D;

I don't see any point in raising calcium for Vitamin D after exercise? Excluding those two, how could I build the above into an electrolyte replacement drink? How much salt to add? How much potassium chloride or other source of potassium? How to add the magnesium?

### persistence — 2014-04-09T04:51:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-120516>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=120493)
>
> your redox

What besides grounding would help treat that?

### Inger — 2014-04-09T09:41:24+0300

> 2.  How do I improve my redox potential?
> 3.  How is the redox potential described in summary?
>

[http://jackkruse.com/redox-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/redox-rx/)

### persistence — 2014-04-16T01:22:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-121743>

I read in the book "The Art and Science of Low Carbohydrate Living" that low carb diets require increased sodium intake. I have only gotten a few chapters into the book, but it looks like they do not give specific recommendations on amount of sodium to include in the diet.

A single teaspoon of salt should contain about 2 grams of sodium and should weigh about 5 grams. For a person on a high or normal level of carbs, this would be considered a typical sodium intake. Has anyone seen specific guidelines on amounts of sodium to take on a low carb diet?

I am taking about 1/4 teaspoon mixed in 10 ounces of water, about three times a day. That has made a remarkable difference in some of my fatigue symptoms. My guess would be that overall I am getting about 1.5 teaspoons per day. I don't have a good basis for adjusting dose.

### persistence — 2014-04-23T21:14:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-right-way-to-maintain-electrolytes.10478/post-123226>

> [persistence said:](/goto/post?id=121743)
>
> I read in the book "The Art and Science of Low Carbohydrate Living" that low carb diets require increased sodium intake. I have only gotten a few chapters into the book, but it looks like they do not give specific recommendations on amount of sodium to include in the diet.
>
> A single teaspoon of salt should contain about 2 grams of sodium and should weigh about 5 grams. For a person on a high or normal level of carbs, this would be considered a typical sodium intake. Has anyone seen specific guidelines on amounts of sodium to take on a low carb diet?
>
> I am taking about 1/4 teaspoon mixed in 10 ounces of water, about three times a day. That has made a remarkable difference in some of my fatigue symptoms. My guess would be that overall I am getting about 1.5 teaspoons per day. I don't have a good basis for adjusting dose.

So reading more of "The Art and Science of Low Carbohydrate Living" they are recommending that people on a low carb diet supplement about five grams of sodium per day. That works out to around two and one half teaspoons of salt per day, a lot of salt!!

I am doing this for a few days and it makes a remarkable difference in how I feel. It's too early to see if this lets me start to recover my exercise levels and strength.

In the category of "Twilight Zone" I have noticed that for about three hours after I take each sodium dose (about 1/2 teaspoon with 12 oz of water), my blood glucose totally corrects to normal. I can eat 50 grams of starchy carbs and peak at 115 mg/dL, whereas without the sodium I was peaking over 150 mg/dL. Three hours after the starchy meal while continuing to take sodium I can see readings under 80 mg/dL, and my steady state numbers stay under 90 mg/dL. Without the sodium my steady-state glucose numbers during the day are 105 to 115 mg/dL. At this point I am not understanding is this all a chemistry trick, where the higher sodium levels somehow fool the glucometer into giving false readings.
