# Thread: PCOS-An Ancient Disorder?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-an-ancient-disorder.10479/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AnnieW — 2014-04-08T16:47:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-an-ancient-disorder.10479/post-120384>

I found this article today, thought some of you might be interested.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/)

### cantweight — 2014-04-08T16:58:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-an-ancient-disorder.10479/post-120386>

**"It is likely that, as for other susceptibility alleles for modern metabolic diseases ([14](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R14), [15](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R15)), the origins of PCOS began in Paleolithic hunter-gatherer communities, in which environmental stressors favored the survival of those males, females and offspring with the greatest capacity for energy storage necessary to endure prolonged episodes of privation, the so-called “thrifty genotype” ([16](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R16)\-[18](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R18)). If so, such a thrifty genotype may have enhanced survival during times of food deprivation, with reduced postprandial thermogenesis from insulin resistance diminishing energy expenditure as an additional evolutionary advantage ([19](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R19))."**

Awesome article...Thanks Annie!

### AnnieW — 2014-04-08T17:05:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-an-ancient-disorder.10479/post-120387>

No problem. I felt it was really appropriate for us on here. Hope Dr. K will chime in with his thoughts.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-08T17:28:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-an-ancient-disorder.10479/post-120390>

If so, such a thrifty genotype may have enhanced survival during times of food deprivation, with reduced postprandial thermogenesis from insulin resistance diminishing energy expenditure as an additional evolutionary advantage ([19](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3164771/#R19))."**

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-04-09T06:30:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-an-ancient-disorder.10479/post-120522>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=120473)
>
> I've downloaded this and I'll read it and see if I can decipher it in the context of the article

they are saying that the insuliin resistant state (to conserve energy) results in a decrease in the loss of heat after eating. so they are diminishing energy expenditure as free heat, to retain energy in the system as an evolutionary advantage.
the "thrifty genes" were amazing for that particular era, however in modern environments and in the face of modern substrates they are disasterous. APOE4 is a similar thrifty gene, or ancestral allele, when carriers live an ancestral lifestyle they are unstoppable.
i like to see these alleles as a free pass to rockstardom, if you align your environment and diets well!

### fitness@home — 2014-04-09T12:47:03+0300

> _i like to see these alleles as a free pass to rockstardom, if you align your environment and diets well!_

Thanks for the interpretation of that phrase. I like the way you think about those alleles!

I'm another that also has had high testosterone & DHEA levels. Was looking at some old medical records from 12+ years ago recently and my doc at the time wrote "PCOS?" in the notes.

Going to read the article today...

### Greenisland — 2014-07-20T20:19:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pcos-an-ancient-disorder.10479/post-134992>

PCOS isn't itself isn't advantageous in any way. Thrifty genes are. Whiskers, a fat belly, acne, hair loss, lethargy? Those are all repulsive on a reproductive level, which is what drives natural selection. If no one want to "mate" with you, you lose and your genes are not transmitted. I know personally when my PCOS was raging, there were very few suitors knocking at my door. PCOS is a neolithic disease state.

# Thread: From athletes to couch potatoes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-08T20:44:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/post-120419>

> Human bones are remarkably plastic and respond surprisingly quickly to change. Put under stress through physical exertion – such as long-distance walking or running – they gain in strength as the fibres are added or redistributed according to where strains are highest. The ability of bone to adapt to loading is shown by analysis of the skeletons of modern athletes, whose bones show remarkably rapid adaptation to both the intensity and direction of strains.
>
> Because the structure of human bones can inform us about the lifestyles of the individuals they belong to, they can provide valuable clues for biological anthropologists looking at past cultures. Research by Alison Macintosh, a PhD candidate in Cambridge University's Department of Archaeology and Anthropology, shows that after the emergence of agriculture in Central Europe from around 5300 BC, the bones of those living in the fertile soils of the Danube river valley became progressively less strong, pointing to a decline in mobility and loading. ...

[http://www.eurekalert.org/pub\_releases/2014-04/uoc-fat040314.php](http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2014-04/uoc-fat040314.php)

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-09T08:24:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/post-120527>

Yes, it's quite fun, isn't it, Audrey?

It seems to turn Marshall Sahlins on his head.

But I guess the title the editors have given the piece, as so often, is more eye-catching than accurate. I don't think anyone who had any idea of the amount of physical labour put in by people in agricultural societies before mechanisation would blithely put up "couch potatoes".

In fact, what we find is:

> Macintosh laser-scanned skeletons found in cemeteries across Central Europe, concentrating in particular on an analysis of engineering-based cross-sectional geometric properties as measures of the loading imposed on the lower limb bones during life.

IOW, there's a fairly narrow focus. This is a measure of "long-distance walking or running". I suppose that even where game is abundant, the hunters must sometimes have spent much of the day walking, and running, in search of the herds or after game.

### nonchalant — 2014-04-09T16:38:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/post-120596>

I'm not sure the decline in strength was entirely from a decrease of mobility. I've seen DH go on a wheat spree, and his joints start popping and cracking in just a few days. What affects the joints must affect bone as well.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-09T20:20:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/from-athletes-to-couch-potatoes.10480/post-120649>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=120596)
>
> I'm not sure the decline in strength was entirely from a decrease of mobility. I've seen DH go on a wheat spree, and his joints start popping and cracking in just a few days. What affects the joints must affect bone as well.

Well, here you have got a relatively smaller change in bone thickness and strength in the lower leg bones of _women_ over time. Now _their_ diets will have changed, too, so why relatively less change in these women's bones after time? The answer would seem to be, because they weren't walking and running as far as the men would have been before the advent of farming, so that there was relatively less change in their patterns of use. Plants, after all, don't run away. (I should think that running, sometimes for long distances, probably played a bigger part in the lives of hunters than some paleo celebrities would like to believe. I'm not pushing the "Born to Run" myth -- just saying what I think is likely to be true.)

So I think it, indeed, is use that's the relevant factor here. The main finding seems to be: people whose lives involved much running and walking had bone changes in the bones of the lower leg. Lots of fanfare. But I wonder should we be surprised at that?

But, sure, I take your point that in general loading isn't the only factor. AFAICT, physical anthropologists (and others) seem almost always to reach for notions of use and impact to explain bone thickness.

Something similar holds for teeth -- Vilhjalmur Stefansson made fun of people for saying that strong teeth were down to plenty of chewing:

> There is for separated districts a wide difference in the amount of Eskimo chewing, but no one has reported that health of the teeth is better among heavy chewers. How could it be when as yet no caries has been found either among the lightest or heaviest masticators?
>
> It is used as a second line of defense by the mastication advocates that even if Eskimos perhaps don’t chew their food so very much they do chew skins a great deal. Their chewing of leather is far less than you might believe from what has been said by a particular kind of writer and pictured in certain movies. In any case, skin chewing is mainly by the women, and **it is not easy to bring under the conditions of modern scientific thought the idea that the wife’s chewing preserves her husband’s teeth.**

Here's something else that has struck me. Weston Price has a photograph of skull fragments from the U.S.:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

He captions that:

> FIG. 27. Left: Example of greater thickness of pre-Columbian Indian skulls in Florida than modern skulls. ...

[http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price6.html](http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price6.html)

Now I know that loading can cause bone to thicken. That kind of thing happens more on one side than the other in archers, for example:

> Bone densities too differ across the shoulder blades and back, as well as from the bow arm to the drawing arm.The same was found among those lost at sea in the Mary Rose.

[http://www.longbow-archers.com/heavybows.html](http://www.longbow-archers.com/heavybows.html)

But the bones of the skull ???

It's not as if people were banging their heads on the ground in pre-Colombian America.

I think in general people underestimate the physical effects of diet. Doctors certainly do. There was a joke going round paleo circles on the internet the other day. It ran thus:

> "I took my dog to the vet and the first thing he said was, 'What are you feeding it?' I went to the doctor and the first thing he said was, 'What meds are you taking?' Next time I'm going to the vet's myself."

# Thread: Inspiration for "bring along dinner" needed
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Andrea — 2014-04-09T10:46:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120549>

I hope there are some food nerds here...

In a couple weeks, we will have an "after work" event at work. Dinner is included, and from what I've heard, we will get a pasta salad... Rather than trying to explain what I eat and what I don't eat, I will bring my own food.

First, I thought I'd put a heap of shrimps on a plate, some smoked oysters, a chopped avocado and some button mushrooms, and some homemade mayo on top. But... That's sort of boring. Anyone could do that!

Many of my coworkers know that I'm on a weird diet, but since I don't eat lunch they have never seen me eat. They THINK I eat huge amounts of meat and fat. This is my chance to show that I eat good and elegant things. I kinda would like people at my table to stare at my plate and drool.

However, there are some limitations:
\- No fresh sea food (there is no fresh sea food available here that specific time of the week). So if I'm to bring seafood, it will have to be from the freezer.
\- I won't be able to do very much more than place my food on a plate at the event site. Practically all preparations need to be made beforehand, at home. This means my food will not be heated. But I will have access to a fridge all day.
\- No dairy whatsoever.
\- The food should, of course, fit within the epi-paleo template.

I don't mind complicated and demanding preparations, as long as I can do it at home and it takes less than 2 weeks. And at home, I have various sorts of fun stuff like a vacuum sealer, a sous vide machine, a smoke gun, meat glue, xanthan gum, etc.

So, please, give me some inspiration!

### Inger — 2014-04-09T11:02:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120551>

A nice cold plate of graved lax, cold smoked salmon (you have to buy a nice brand, preferably one you slice yourself, the already sliced, prepacked stuff usually tastes horrible) honey-dill-mustard sauce, herrings.. cooked eggs halves topped with peeled shrimps mixed with mayo and some shrimp on the top (the ones in their shell are way better so the peeling work is really worth it) could add some roe to make it super nice looking and tasting

I sometimes make for family those cooked egg halves with shrimp... (to help my dad not get Alzheimers as his mom ) The red or black roe on the top makes it really looking nice, you could put some dill on the top too, and put some lemon slices on the serving plate too
it is going to look very beautiful!

I am pretty sure everyone is going to want your food and not the pasta salad...

Another thing I do for preventing my dad from this horrible disease, and they all love it, is:

Baltic herring filets (strömming) about 1 kg
Salt them slightly and mix and roll and put side by side into a tin

Fry up some chopped onion (1-2) in butter and add organic chopped canned tomatoes. Add some black pepper too and some ACV and maybe 1/2 teaspoon honey and some chili and salt
it needs to have a nice sweet sour taste, I sometimes add some organic natural ketchup too

let it simmer for a while, maybe 10 minutes

pour it over the herring rolls

put into the oven at 170 degree C for maybe 15 minutes.

Take out and pour some great quality EVOO on the top, maybe 1/2 dl

This is nice eaten cold or warm. My parents often take some of it with them to work for lunch

### Inger — 2014-04-09T11:12:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120553>

If there are cheese lovers... you could bring a plate of nice raw milk cheeses...
Roquefort, Comte.... put it nicely on a plate and add some organic red pepper, cucumber stick, celery sticks...

### caroline — 2014-04-09T11:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120556>

everyone always loves gravlax - as Inger says ...That is what I always take....

### caroline — 2014-04-09T11:31:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120557>

do you know the website ....nom nom paleo ...she has lots of great recipes and uses a sous vide al lot too.

### Inger — 2014-04-09T12:18:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120564>

I overlooked the no dairy, Rerun, so no cheese, sorry

### caroline — 2014-04-09T12:38:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120566>

what about sushi????

### Lahelada — 2014-04-09T12:46:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120569>

Running off to work now but a lot of pasta eaters have been entirely convinced by a
_cauliflower and bacon salad_. The recipe does call for cheese but you could serve that as a sprinkle on ingredient on the side.
If you are feeling adventurous you could make a _ham on the bone_ beforehand. They could have bread .
Instead of chicken you could use pork steaklets. They with bread you with salad or all with salad with this recipe_.
[http://www.fatsecret.com/recipes/hot-and-spicy-garlic-chicken-wings/Default.aspx](http://www.fatsecret.com/recipes/hot-and-spicy-garlic-chicken-wings/Default.aspx)_

### sooperb — 2014-04-09T16:13:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120587>

I'd be so tempted to have two plates, one with cubes of lard and beef fat, fried squares of beef fat and fatty bacon cubes, cooked and chilled with any spare fat dribbled over it. You put that out first and invite anyone to try it. Then you bring out what you really eat, a plate covered in glorious food, avocado, mixed greens, juicy prawns, mayo, beetroot. Make it as colourful and all encompassing as you can. Then watch the realisation on their faces that what you eat isn't weird, it's fresh, tasty, healthy and can be exciting. I wish I'd saved the photos I took of some of my recent salads, they looked and tasted sensational.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-09T17:52:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-120627>

> [RobH said:](/goto/post?id=120624)
>
> Ceviche!
>
> robH

THIS. Shrimp Ceviche (easily made with pre-frozen raw shirmp. it needs a good 6hrs to "cook" in the lemon & lime juice in the fridge - so the timing is perfect!

Cut up shrimp
season with salt & pepper
zest 2 limes, and 1/2 a lemon
Add juice of 2-3 limes and one lemon (you want the lime flavor as the top note, and the lemon as the bottom - so you want more lime juice)
add minced red onion
Add jalapenos (if you eat night shades -)
add minced garlic
Add cilantro
add avocado oil or olive oil
stir it all up and pop into the fridge

Serve with jicama chips, guacamole, and a lovely salad of your choosing... nom nom nom nom nom

Rerun you can make gravlax without "sugar" too! Just use salt and dill and rosepepper... yummy! I do use a tiny amount of raw wild honey on my gravlax. It is so little I do not care. I make it mostly for the ex husband anyways. I prefer my food pretty plain and easy. I mostly eat salmon just sliced with some ACV and raw onion and salt... it is super fast and easy and yummy too!

The tomato herring will have a few carbs too. I do not make it for me, I prefer raw fish... but family loves it.

I usually do the Baltic herring filets I eat like this;

Marinate them raw over night in ACV (raw, organic and unfiltered), salt, raw onion slices and brown pepper (?) (brunpeppar, hela)

That is so super easy and I love it

You could also roll the mackerel filets in sushi sheets and cut into pieces. This is how I make it, did this 2 years ago.. (you could add avocado or cucumbers or anythings else in the roll of course..)

That looks delicious!
I have made something similar, a salmon tartar. I made up the recipe after I was taken out for dinner at the most exclusive sushi restaurant in Hamburg and ordered their salmon tartare. It was so yummy I had to made it at home. It also had onion and fresh coriander... on the top a thin layer of creme fraiche + topped with salmon roe, it looked like a pastry

> Can you get any North sea herring in Finland? I've got the impression that the Baltic sea is pretty polluted, with loads of heavy metals and toxins.
> As for "brown pepper", I think it's one other name for Sichuan pepper. Although I'm not completely sure.

I can get the North Sea herring but only salted.. not fresh

The Baltic Sea is polluted, but it is getting better I think. I eat the Baltic herring occasionally, not too often because of the pollution.. When I checked at the websites of the Finnish government about the degree of pollution in our fish, it said Baltic herring was polluted but the benefits of eating them in comparison to the pollutants was greater than 40 times it! So it really looks there are great benefits to eating the Baltic herring despite the pollution...

They are dirt cheap, so when I am short of money I eat them a little more often
I sometimes make smoothie out of their heads too, they are soft and nice for that

### Andrea — 2014-04-14T19:34:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-121449>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=120754)
>
> I usually do the Baltic herring filets I eat like this;
>
> Marinate them raw over night in ACV (raw, organic and unfiltered), salt, raw onion slices and brown pepper (?) (brunpeppar, hela)
>
> That is so super easy and I love it
>
> You could also roll the mackerel filets in sushi sheets and cut into pieces. This is how I make it, did this 2 years ago.. (you could add avocado or cucumbers or anythings else in the roll of course..)[](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RRLbiwuvWEU)

I played a little this weekend. I marinated some herring filets in ACV, salt, raw onion and coriander seeds (I don't have any brown pepper) for about an hour only. I didn't want them too "well done" and too acidic. I dried them off and removed the skin. Sprinkled meat glue on the meaty sides, put two filets on top of each other and packed firmly into rolls in plastic wrap. Left overnight in the fridge. This morning, for breakfast, I sliced the rolls in about 2 cm thick pieces and removed the plastic wrap. They were very pretty round pieces of fish! Tasted nice, too.

Still have some time to play and experiment... Next up will be something more complicated and impressive, and the Shrimp Ceviche that made me drool before...

When I checked at the websites of the Finnish government about the degree of pollution in our fish, it said Baltic herring was polluted but the benefits of eating them in comparison to the pollutants was greater than 40 times it! So it really looks there are great benefits to eating the Baltic herring despite the pollution...
>
> They are dirt cheap, so when I am short of money I eat them a little more often
> I sometimes make smoothie out of their heads too, they are soft and nice for that

i lobe herrings but only can get them frozen or marinated. As per my fish provider these are very seasonal and the season is about to come soon. Other thing he said, is that herring is the most "parazite loaded fish", that applies to the way it is being held already on the fishing boats. They process them on the boat already by freezing, this is done to finish the parazites. I wonder of your herrings are fresh. To my understanding the usual practice makes any hering reaching you to be frozen or thawed.

### Andrea — 2014-04-14T19:52:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/inspiration-for-bring-along-dinner-needed.10487/post-121454>

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=121452)
>
> i lobe herrings but only can get them frozen or marinated. As per my fish provider these are very seasonal and the season is about to come soon. Other thing he said, is that herring is the most "parazite loaded fish", that applies to the way it is being held already on the fishing boats. They process them on the boat already by freezing, this is done to finish the parazites. I wonder of your herrings are fresh. To my understanding the usual practice makes any hering reaching you to be frozen or thawed.

I thought they caught herring most of the year, and mackerel was more season bound. But I'm definitely not a fish expert. Just trying to learn, slowly...

But. Ever since I brought home fresh cod once, and when I cut into it and it was moving on the inside , I don't fancy fresh fish very much... Especially not for eating raw. I buy most of my fish frozen, because it's usually cheaper - and I suppose I would freeze it anyway. Fresh herring costs about twice as much as frozen here. And the frozen one usually comes from the North Sea or the Atlantic, which is a bit more open and, I suppose, less polluted then the local sea.
I think most people who buy fresh herring here fry it, so I guess they would kill most of the potential parasites.

# Thread: the best book to read this holiday!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-best-book-to-read-this-holiday.10488/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kovita — 2014-04-09T13:10:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-best-book-to-read-this-holiday.10488/post-120571>

friends, i am heading into what we call this year our holiday season. i will be on holiday on anf off for two months! I cannot wait because it will be finally proper outdoor holiday since the kids grew enough to withstand sone cold nights and ants. I would love to bring some nice, inspiring and positive book to read along.... If anyone can recommend it would be great!

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-09T21:50:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-best-book-to-read-this-holiday.10488/post-120663>

I'll recommend what I think is one of the best historical novels ever written.

It was recommended to me by a historical novelist -- a brave and disinterested thing for him to do, since few novelists would be able to take the comparison. I'd never heard of it.

The lady who wrote it was herself an Oxford University historian and I think probably has more knowledge of the period she writes about and a greater _feel_ for it than most people would be likely to have. She's writing about a key period in history and the disputes and struggles that accompanied that, but the characters aren't ciphers for the ideas floating around at the time: her characters strike me as utterly psychologically believable as individuals. She doesn't in any simple way take historical "sides" either, but nor is the book in any way merely nihilistic. The message in the end is inspiring and positive, as you asked for: it's that while life can be full of pain, this doesn't in the final analysis matter, because there's love.

The book is _Man on a Donkey_ by Hilda Francis Margaret Prescott and it comes in two volumes. It's actually slow to get going, and I think the author's genius doesn't become clear until the second volume, as she begins to work the different characters lives out:

[http://www.amazon.com/dp/0829426396/](http://www.amazon.com/dp/0829426396/)

[http://www.amazon.com/dp/0829427317/](http://www.amazon.com/dp/0829427317/)

### caroline — 2014-04-10T08:30:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-best-book-to-read-this-holiday.10488/post-120745>

Have you read Robert O. Becker's stuff?

Dr. Andrew Marino..... Going somewhere: truth about a life in science

Richard Feynman

Gerald H. Pollack

# Thread: Insects - Why do some people attract more insect bites?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insects-why-do-some-people-attract-more-insect-bites.10500/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2014-04-10T20:38:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insects-why-do-some-people-attract-more-insect-bites.10500/post-120826>

Something is different about me this year. It appears that my blood is like nectar to noseeums, mosquitos, flies, and sand fleas (I know sand fleas are really crustaceans but they still bite!).

The feasting started while I was on vacation and is ongoing. I got tore up last week but my husband wasn’t bitten at all.

Not into using DEET or other harmful bug repellents.We used this product before going outside it helped, but didn't last for long periods of time:
[http://www.amazon.com/Cactus-Juice-Skin-Outdoor-Protectant/dp/B0009DVP6Q/ref=pd\_sim\_sg\_1?ie=UTF8&refRID=1NR2SH5Z97EPBSRQKKWM](http://www.amazon.com/Cactus-Juice-Skin-Outdoor-Protectant/dp/B0009DVP6Q/ref=pd_sim_sg_1?ie=UTF8&refRID=1NR2SH5Z97EPBSRQKKWM)

Today I spent my usual lunchtime at the park. Came back with two more huge mosquito bites. Obviously, I have strong histamine reactions to insect bites. It's like they know this and want to torture me . I’m still scratching those I got last week.

_So what is it that makes some people more “tasty” to insects?_ (I'd really like to know)

### HoneyChild — 2014-04-10T21:00:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insects-why-do-some-people-attract-more-insect-bites.10500/post-120830>

Apparently ketones deter mozzies.

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/...t-smelling-sweat-keeps-mosquitoes-at-bay.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/science/science-news/6142961/Sweet-smelling-sweat-keeps-mosquitoes-at-bay.html)

### Lahelada — 2014-04-10T21:21:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insects-why-do-some-people-attract-more-insect-bites.10500/post-120836>

When I was a child I suffered badly and got the worst swellings from a simple bite . My pediatrician prescribed B12 supplement . It did help.Looking back that seems quite clairvoyant bearing in mind we are talking 40 years ago in the light of the things we are discussing today. Could you be short of B12?

### fitness@home — 2014-04-10T21:22:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insects-why-do-some-people-attract-more-insect-bites.10500/post-120837>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=120836)
>
> When I was a child I suffered badly and got the worst swellings from a simple bite . My pediatrician prescribed B12 supplement . It did help.Looking back that seems quite clairvoyant bearing in mind we are talking 40 years ago in the light of the things we are discussing today. Could you be short of B12?

I don't think so. Have been taking methyl and hydroxy B12 for months now. Will confirm with testing soon though.

### fitness@home — 2014-04-10T21:23:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insects-why-do-some-people-attract-more-insect-bites.10500/post-120839>

### prAna303 — 2014-04-12T11:45:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/younger-men.10502/post-121095>

For me this is about not your age but how you aged...

When we now add layers in every kind of life and how we feel life we also tend to look at, in my mind the wrong things in our partners. If we live by how the system has built all laws instead of looking at the laws of nature we must find financial security and other stuff.

This is me, i can not play anymore, i can not accept these things that is common truth today. I have laid all my static rules aside now and find more pure beauty in the laws of quantum, because they resonate with me and always had.

I am always naked in every kinf of sense, i look for the same in my partners, male or female. I do not put the regular rules at play anymore i try to accept the other laws... This means for me, the one in front of me should be alive no matter age or sex. It does not matter if the human up front is doing this or that, i look at the will to be alive. I have meet girls who are 19 and have a kind of wisdom of an ancient queen and on the other side, girls who are 45 and seems to stand on the edge of destruction. I look at the ones who kind of makes me tingle, makes me stop thinking about time.

Age, in the numbers or in the unit? If someone is holding on to life in a good way, time stands still for them, you age with pride. This means that when someone 70 of age is vital as a 50 years old, they are magic. Also the other way round. Age is to make us boxable, simple to put into systems when or where.

This means, i do not look at age, i look at how alive someone is. I have always done it and with laws of quantum physics i start to see why.

If i lock myself to thoughts, i loose the magic. When i let someone put their thoughts into me i go from quantum world to regular world, sometimes it is good but way to many bad.

Just a video to show that age does not matter... Aging does.

Michail Nikolajevitj Barysjnikov, 66 years
Ana Laguna, 59 years

### caroline — 2014-04-12T12:07:06+0300

### sooperb — 2014-04-12T12:14:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/younger-men.10502/post-121099>

I think some people live from inside themselves and others from outside. The inside livers radiate an acceptance that you live life as you meet it, accept it and live in harmony. The others see only superficially, they need embellishments, accoutrements and possessions to make them feel alive, inside they are hollow and empty.

I have to have a little laugh at the wealthy who "give it all up" and take themselves off to live simply, they never really give it all up, they can't surrender everything they can only pretend. They'll still be hollow living in a mud hut because they never understood about what makes us rich. I think that now there are very few who do, if you meet someone who does, you know straight away, they have a glow that never fades no matter what their circumstances.

Country of origin: Korea

>

> Also if the source is ok?

> Ingredients: Brown Seaweed
> Country of origin: Korea
>
> [http://www.hmart.com/shopnow/shopnow\_newsub.asp?p=8801052202020](http://www.hmart.com/shopnow/shopnow_newsub.asp?p=8801052202020)

Could it be possibly a hijiki seaweed?

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-13T18:14:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brown-seaweed.10506/post-121296>

> [Sammy said:](/goto/post?id=120965)
>
> I am pretty sure it's wakame.

Looks likely. It seems to be commonly used in Korea:

> _**Miyeok guk**_ is a Korean soup made from the sea vegetable known aswakame or "sea mustard" in English. It comprises two words: miyeok, the Korean word for wakame; and guk, meaning "soup"."
>
> Miyeok guk is typically consumed by women after giving birth as wakame contains a high content of calcium and iodine, nutrients that are important for nursing mothers.\[1\] Many women consume it during the pregnancy phase as well. It is also traditionally eaten on birthdays for this reason, a reminder of the first food that the mother has eaten and passed on to her newborn through her milk, thus bringing good fortune for the rest of the year.
>
> Because it is known as a highly nutritious soup, it is widely consumed in homes and restaurants in Korea.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miyeokguk](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miyeokguk)

> Seaweeds can be classified into three broad groups based on pigmentation: brown, red and green. Botanists refer to these broad groups as Phaeophyceae, Rhodophyceae and Chlorophyceae, respectively. …
>
> The Republic of Korea grows about 800 000 wet tonnes of three different species, and about 50 percent of this is for _wakame_, produced from a different brown seaweed, _Undaria pinnatifida_, grown in a similar fashion to _Laminaria_ in China. ...

[http://www.fao.org/docrep/006/y4765e/y4765e04.htm](http://www.fao.org/docrep/006/y4765e/y4765e04.htm)

I never knew it had an English name. Perhaps I should call it "sea mustard" from now on since I can't claim to be Japanese!

### sooperb — 2014-04-15T13:37:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brown-seaweed.10506/post-121615>

Whatever it is it's a lot cheaper than in the UK. A small pack of wakame cost me nearly £6 and some Irish "spaghetti" seaweed cost £3.50 for 50gs.

### kovita — 2014-04-15T14:22:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brown-seaweed.10506/post-121616>

from my experience wakame comes usually is long leaves. hijiki has form of tubular "spaghetti" with nodules. arame comes cut into long spaghetti, but if you hydrate it you see it is coming from big leaves. That is all i learned!

### Sue-UK — 2014-04-15T14:33:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brown-seaweed.10506/post-121618>

I don't like strands and sheets of seaweeds, I usually finish up putting them in the bath ... But sprinkles .... yum! I'm buying them from here [http://www.artisanbread-abo.com/Seaweed/Seaweed+Condiments+%2526+Supplements.htm](http://www.artisanbread-abo.com/Seaweed/Seaweed+Condiments+%252526+Supplements.htm). I've just ordered another £40 worth of different ones to get the free delivery.

### sooperb — 2014-04-23T13:20:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brown-seaweed.10506/post-123161>

Interesting site, those little bottles are about as expensive as Schwartz herbs and spices lol. I'm sure their bread will be good but as I don't eat it I didn't dare look.

# Thread: Plants, light cycles and electrons
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plants-light-cycles-and-electrons.10508/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2014-04-11T15:32:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plants-light-cycles-and-electrons.10508/post-120974>

While looking up the harvest time for ulluco or papalisa from the Andes I came across their long maduration cycle of 220 to 270 days in 10 -14 hour light periods.
From EE11
"Foods grown in longer light cycles have higher energized electrons and they are handled very differently than electrons from low light level foods because of where they enter the electron transport chain in our mitochondria."

Our foods we cultivate generally have a 3-4 months quick turnaround like potato,tomato,green beans etc. If we consider "Foods grown in longer light cycles have higher energized electrons" then "natural "plants with a long maduration cycle in long light days should be a superfood?

I considered the climate zones my genes are from moderate template climes Northern Europe and would think there is not a mismatch as these plants grow at altitudes of 2000- 4000 m template to cold in other words.

I for one will go to buy oca today ,another plant tuber that is being harvested around now.

Suppositions ,guesses,opinions all welcome.

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sjoshua — 2014-04-12T00:29:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/article-cherry-tree-seeds-that-lived-in-space-have-rapid-and-unique-growth.10517/post-121053>

[http://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/...ystery-baffles-Japan/articleshow/33592301.cms](http://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/home/science/Cherry-tree-from-space-mystery-baffles-Japan/articleshow/33592301.cms)

Seems our friend gravity is at play here.

### nonchalant — 2014-04-15T17:31:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/article-cherry-tree-seeds-that-lived-in-space-have-rapid-and-unique-growth.10517/post-121643>

> "A stone from the original tree had never sprouted before. We are very happy because it will succeed the old tree, which is said to be 1,250 years old."

So this 1250 year old cherry tree was not fertile anymore. And it only produces about 30 flowers per year. But they take its seed to another environment, and now one is fertile, and has unfamiliar characteristics. Sounds like the definition of epigenetics. Gravity, yes, different solar EMF, and less magnetism.

# Thread: Leafy greens
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leafy-greens.10530/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-13T18:43:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leafy-greens.10530/post-121306>

What to do with these …

I usually just steam or boil various things of this sort briefly -- or simply them "wilt" them by cooking them in a covered pot with very little water and a knob of butter.

However, perhaps it's worth doing a bit more with them. Also perhaps it would be good to cook them longer sometimes. I think there's been quite a vogue for cooking all vegetables very briefly, people muttering "vitamins" darkly while they do so. The texture is supposed to be superior with brief cooking, too. Some people even serve stuff that's no so much _al dente_ (as the phrase has it) as rock hard.

Terry Wahls follows this preserve-the-heat-labile-nutrients thinking and would rather we eat vegetables raw or very lightly cooked. However, I think I recall Sally Fallon saying somewhere that there's a reason why people in traditional cultures cooked some vegetables for a long time. (I think it was neutralising goitrogens that she was getting at.) Don't think you know better than all those old grandmas; you don't always, as the WAPF might say. And there's the Richard Wrangham point that cooking helps to break down foods and make some of the nutrients more easily available to us:

[http://www.amazon.com/Catching-Fire-Cooking-Made-Human/dp/184668286X/](http://www.amazon.com/Catching-Fire-Cooking-Made-Human/dp/184668286X/)

I do enjoy fairly lightly cooked vegetables, but why not longer-cooked sometimes? I did, in fact, used to enjoy a Delia Smith recipe for "Braised red cabbage with apples" which she recommends with sausages or cassoulet. I haven't cooked it for ages, though.

So here's a recipe for Southern U.S. style greens I found on a paleo site. Here they're stewed with pork --
a way of flavouring it (and perhaps, originally, of stretching the meat?). I did wonder, the South being heavily settled by Scots and Scotch-Irish people, if there was a Scottish origin to this dish, old Scotland being well-known for its Kailyards. But I guess some such dishes were probably common all over Europe. The Scots, I guess, would have used mutton rather than pork.

Here it is:

[http://www.liveto110.com/southern-collard-greens-recipe/](http://www.liveto110.com/southern-collard-greens-recipe/)

I used a bag of "spring greens" that looked pretty much like the leafy green vegetable she's using here. The bag was 200g, so I needed only about a quarter of everything else. I had some cold sausages -- Toulouse ones I'd cooked the day before, so I chopped one of those up and stirred it in at the end instead of the bacon. I used butter rather olive oil and didn't add any red pepper. Otherwise I followed the recipe. It was pretty good. I'm afraid I ate it all.

### Lahelada — 2014-04-13T18:49:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leafy-greens.10530/post-121310>

Good question.I still remember tge horrified face of my host family in England who askwd me to cook cabbage.I brought out the biggest pot in the house to give it a good boil. Indeed,vitamins they muttered and We are doctors we know..."

### nonchalant — 2014-04-15T18:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leafy-greens.10530/post-121676>

Yesterday I was giving some freshly cut young poke salat its first boiling, to just wilt the leaves, and was gratified at the strong asparagus scent it gave off. I drained the water, and would have boiled it a bit more to finish cooking it, but something came up, and I had to store it in the fridge. I've been waiting a year to taste this delicious green! Yes, I'm an asparagus fan.  I certainly wouldn't eat it raw, though I admit I haven't tried it. Not willing to risk the tummy drama. Tonight I'll see how it tastes after being stored.

DH knows very well not to mow off the patches of poke salat we have growing wild.

# Thread: Blackout shades for the bedroom?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hemming — 2014-04-13T21:34:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-121342>

I've been thinking about getting some new blackout shades for my bedroom which would quite effectively block out all the light from the surrounding buildings. But how will that affect my natural circadian rhythm where I wake up when it gets light outside? I'm just afraid that I'll block out so much light that I wont sense the sun rising as I do now.

Any thoughts on this issue?

### kovita — 2014-04-13T21:50:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-121344>

we are after them as well. I can give you just a small token from my experience using a sleeping mask. I so wake up normally now around 6:30-7 am, when I keep the mask thorough the whole night, I would sleep at least 1 hour more. So definitely it is changing my sense of normal light cycle. I have as well experience from visits of my familiars in Spain. In Spain, blackout shades are a norm (at least in the area wher my familiars live). I never liked it, because I immediately (and our kids as well) change the circadian rhytm to the local one (wake up very late, go to bed very late, all meals shifted by 2-3 hours). So it is a tough call, because light from buildings around are bothering my night, but the same time the blackout shades would not roll out when the sunrise comes.You could use some electronics with timer to roll them out, but electronics...

### Hemming — 2014-04-13T21:57:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-121345>

Thanks for sharing your experiences, that is exactly what I'm concerned about too. I want no light at night but in the morning and I would prefer to keep at least my bedroom free of all electronics.

On a side note, I also hate the Spanish circadian rhythm

### nonchalant — 2014-04-15T19:05:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-121683>

I have blackout shades on my bedroom windows, but I keep a door ajar that gets some morning sunlight from another room. So it is pretty dark at night, but enought light in the morning to signal the sunrise.

### Hemming — 2014-04-15T22:03:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-121712>

Good workaround the issue  Unfortunately I can't do that in my apartment.

### cinnamon — 2014-04-16T03:24:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-121765>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=121712)
>
> Good workaround the issue  Unfortunately I can't do that in my apartment.

I am very sensitive to light at night these days. If I am exposed to much that is bright, I get hunger pangs within a few minutes. I suppose that's a clue that I've successfully trained my body to think it is getting fed promptly come morning? LOL

I put up blackout curtains, but _I turn back one little corner of the curtain_ so there is a crack of light allowed in. I have also taken up wearing a sleep mask (though that is usually abandoned sometime in the middle of the night). The quality of daylight coming through that crack once dawn has arrived is vastly different from the artificial street lighting during the night and seems to work well to let me know it's time to get up.

### Marie — 2014-04-16T05:02:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-121774>

I use blackout curtains, blinds, and a sleep mask.
Since I started sleeping on a magnetico, I wake at dawn, whether I want to ... or not.

If you are going to start investing in yourself, I'd buy a sleep mask and a magnetico.

### Hemming — 2014-04-16T13:58:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-121813>

Thanks for the replies. I think I'll go for the blinds after all. They will be set up in a way that will enable some light to enter the room.

### Martha Ray — 2021-11-26T03:02:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-306677>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=121342)
>
> I've been thinking about getting some new blackout shades for my bedroom which would quite effectively block out all the light from the surrounding buildings. But how will that affect my natural circadian rhythm where I wake up when it gets light outside? I'm just afraid that I'll block out so much light that I wont sense the sun rising as I do now.
> Any thoughts on this issue?

I have your same concerns...

I have shades and curtains in bedrooms. All curtains are 100% cotton but they are Blue- I bought before I heard about avoiding blue colored materials...that also did away with 1/3 of my wardrobe. Anyway a new tower parking lot light was added this last year that shines right into a bedroom window and mulitiple community lights were added within the past 5 years that shine into our living room and am looking for how to black out more light at night...

Housewares are filled with toxins and all the blackout curtains I've seen have awful materials. I've not found healthy options for curtains ever ....the materials keep get worse and my wants for reasonally priced, simplistic, talored and healthy have increased. I travel with a backout mask but elastic bothers my ears so I put an old loose pair of blue blockers on to hold the mask in place. No healthy materials for masks either. I mouth tape too---
I so sleep better but .... not feeling so natural to me.

Had been sleeping with all doors open and that helped morning light come down the hall way.. but recently we had the fire department visit us to update our smoke detectors and they were really pushing the importance of sleeping with the bedroom doors closed as they told us most who leave their bedroom doors open, who suffer a fire, die from the smoke inhalation becasue it takes them before they wake up. Now just cracking the door so I still get the morning light.

What the world needs now is more builders and building biologists who are savvy about circadian rhythm biology and who have a interest in building healthy homes. I like bedroom windows facing east and bedrooms built for headboard to fit on a north wall...

I am looking for a healthy home southwest of where I am (38th latitude) and it is not an easy project. I have a boatload of criteria and a whole lot of questions. I want to remediate the home I am in to be better while we are here and for future owners and I hope someone is doing that for me as well....

### Sue-UK — 2021-11-26T12:19:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-306683>

> [Martha Ray said:](/goto/post?id=306677)
>
> I have your same concerns...
>
> I have shades and curtains in bedrooms. All curtains are 100% cotton but they are Blue- I bought before I heard about avoiding blue colored materials...that also did away with 1/3 of my wardrobe. Anyway a new tower parking lot light was added this last year that shines right into a bedroom window and mulitiple community lights were added within the past 5 years that shine into our living room and am looking for how to black out more light at night...
>
> Housewares are filled with toxins and all the blackout curtains I've seen have awful materials. I've not found healthy options for curtains ever ....the materials keep get worse and my wants for reasonally priced, simplistic, talored and healthy have increased. I travel with a backout mask but elastic bothers my ears so I put an old loose pair of blue blockers on to hold the mask in place. No healthy materials for masks either. I mouth tape too---
> I so sleep better but .... not feeling so natural to me.
>
> Had been sleeping with all doors open and that helped morning light come down the hall way.. but recently we had the fire department visit us to update our smoke detectors and they were really pushing the importance of sleeping with the bedroom doors closed as they told us most who leave their bedroom doors open, who suffer a fire, die from the smoke inhalation becasue it takes them before they wake up. Now just cracking the door so I still get the morning light.
>
> What the world needs now is more builders and building biologists who are savvy about circadian rhythm biology and who have a interest in building healthy homes. I like bedroom windows facing east and bedrooms built for headboard to fit on a north wall...
>
> I am looking for a healthy home southwest of where I am (38th latitude) and it is not an easy project. I have a boatload of criteria and a whole lot of questions. I want to remediate the home I am in to be better while we are here and for future owners and I hope someone is doing that for me as well....

I had the same problem when I moved here in April, it’s the one disadvantage about the physical location. The apartment has been fitted out to throw as much light in during the day as possible, the internal door to the living room is glazed, the living room has patio doors that take up most of one wall, and all the doors have glazed panels above them. The outside lights at night affect every room in the apartment. The positioning of them makes the small glazed area of the front door shining down the hall and into our bedroom worse than the patio doors in the living room, so the front door has a blackout blind, even though we close our bedroom door at night. If DH opens the door to go for a pee in the night, without the blackout blind it would be enough to wake me. The patio doors in the living room have thick lined curtains, and the placement of the outside light and the direction the light goes indoors makes that much less of a problem, even with a glazed door to the hall.

Our bedroom has horizontal blinds that were already there, and we added blackout blinds and blackout curtains. There is the tiniest bleed of light at the top, but it is so faint that it doesn’t bother me, if anything its better than it being pitch black with DH using a torch with a red filter on it if he needs to get up. It is possibly the very slight increase in light when the sun starts coming up that is enough to wake me, but I also use a yoga grounding mat as a runner at the bottom of the bed, which goes to the ground outside, and I think that’s what makes a difference. A mammal in a dark burrow or cave would be relying on timing signals from the changing geomagnetic field of the earth, not light.

I still follow the yoga grounding mat practice because of what Becker said about sensing the geomagnetic field, which I think would be changing the information in the electrons coming from the earth beneath the grounding rod. I mentally connect to that when I first put my feet on the mat at night, a sort of pay attention and add intention hack.

In Becker's book Cross Currents there is a discussion on the earth's rotation through the magnetosphere, and that any given spot on the earth's surface is in a constantly changing magnetic field. There's a diagram of the daily rise and fall in the strength of the magnetic field at one spot on the earth during a quiet period of solar activity. There's also a discussion on what is the magnetic organ, (separate from the pineal), but on the pineal, after acknowledging the retinal pathway, he says:

_"It appears that, over 2 billion years of evolution, living things have taken advantage of the two portions of the electromagnetic spectrum that could be depended upon to always be present: the geomagnetic field and visible light. In this view, it is no more surprising that life developed specific organs to sense the geomagnetic field and to derive timing information from it than that it developed specific organs to sense and derive information from light."

"More recently, it has been shown that the pineal is also sensitive to the daily cyclic pattern in the Earth's magnetic field. **Melatonin secretion in human subjects may be changed at will by exposure to steady magnetic fields of the same strength as the geomagnetic field.** Apparently, nature determined that biological cycle activity was too important to be left to one environmental signal alone. "_

_"Nature intended the pineal to simultaneously receive the same signals from the daily pattern of day-night and the same rise and fall in strength of the geomagnetic field. Obviously, when one or both signals are abnormal, the pineal does not respond in the normal fashion, and the body's biological cycles become disturbed - with important clinical consequences."_

It’s possible that cutting ourselves off from both signals makes it far more likely we will miss the early morning than if we just cut out light.

### Martha Ray — 2021-12-04T02:02:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-307034>

Thank you Sue-UK got _Cross Currents_ on my wish list. I appreciate your sensitivity as I am the same way....
Do you recall the **brands** of blackout blinds and blackout curtains you got or **what material they were?** Although they may be different for USA where I am. I can't find materials I like and concerned about polyesters, plastics, formaldehyde, and other chemicals.

Also wondering what dark colors are best burgundy might work for our decor. I have 6 windows all with cotton navy blue curtains to replace...oh yes and navy window shades under 3 of those windows.. they will need to be replaced too. A big project considering I'd like to move anyway.

Somewhat related:
**Is there an opinion on head direction for sleeping? I used to think it was NORTH** bit our King bed had to be set with head at South. Checking online, India and on the _Body Ecology_ site **thoughts favor head being on the East or 'if you must on the South' and not favoring West or North?
**
**Do you or others here have an option on that?

Thanks.**

### Sue-UK — 2021-12-04T10:47:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-307044>

> [Martha Ray said:](/goto/post?id=307034)
>
> Also wondering what dark colors are best burgundy might work for our decor. I have 6 windows all with cotton navy blue curtains to replace...oh yes and navy window shades under 3 of those windows.. they will need to be replaced too. A big project considering I'd like to move anyway.

If you are considering moving, I wouldn't worry about having navy blue curtains or shades. Navy is a saturated colour, which is like the blue contained in evening light, (Alexander Wunsch alert) and if its dark in the room photons won't bounce off the colour pigments anyway. My curtains and blinds are a neutral light colour, because that is better in the daytime. At night, without photons hitting them and then being perceived by my eyes, the effect is black. My burgundy bedding is black in a blacked out room. When we had a power cut in the night on the estate which switched off the security lights, the room was so dark when I woke up in the night I thought I'd gone blind.

### JanSz — 2021-12-04T15:14:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blackout-shades-for-the-bedroom.10531/post-307053>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=307044)
>
> My burgundy bedding is black in a blacked out room. When we had a power cut in the night on the estate which switched off the security lights, the room was so dark when I woke up in the night I thought I'd gone blind.

There is a trend toward solar and wind power.
Pretty soon expect to have electricity in a day time only.
Good idea to have a home electric generator handy, if possible, an automatic electric generator.

Note,
electricians are people too.
They try to sell you a system that will give them the most bang for their work.
They try to sell you the smallest possible generator and the maximum labor time to install the thing.

Go for the generator that would replace all energy that you could possibly need, even if only for a few seconds.
Those few seconds are usually AC compressors.
I have a 20 kW generator with an on/off switch (for the whole house).
Works when I am home or not. Takes about 30 seconds to bring back interrupted power.
That 30-second interruption screws electronics.
There is still another backup just for the 30 seconds.

A guy down the streets (for more of a total $$ than what I spent, got an 8 kW generator plus lots of electrical cablings that he have to operate manually).
Lucky for me, I learned from his story so you do not have to).

...

# Thread: Rerun's labs - what does all this mean?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reruns-labs-what-does-all-this-mean.10537/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Andrea — 2014-04-14T19:14:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reruns-labs-what-does-all-this-mean.10537/post-121445>

So, I've finally got some labs to flash with...
Not exactly what I would have picked if I could choose freely, but at least something...
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Yes, the names for everything are in Swedish, but with a little imagination I'm convinced you can guess what is what. But to make it a little easier:
_Järn = Iron
Kalium = Potassium
Natrium = Sodium_

I do have thyroid issues and I medicate with synthetic T4 and T3. My doc didn't really like my T4 and T3 values any more than I do, so in an effort to prove to me that I'm fine, they ordered another type of analysis which came out like this:
**
2014-04-01**
"Regular" analyses:
S-Cortisol (8:17 AM): 310 nmol/L (ref. 7 AM: 200-800 nmol/L)
S-T4: **48\*** nmol/L (ref. 66-181)
S-fT4: **8,2\*** pmol/L (ref. 12-22)
S-T3: **0.96\*** nmol/L (ref. 1.3-3.1)
S-fT3: **3.0\*** pmol/L (ref. 3.1-6.8)
S-TSH: 3.1 mIU/L (ref. 0.3-4,2)

Analyses on Architect i2000 (interference):
S-T4: **38\*** nmol/L (ref. 56-147)
S-fT4: **8,9\*** pmol/L (ref. 9-19)
S-T3: 1.0 nmol/L (ref. 0.9-2.4)
S-fT3: 3.2 pmol/L (ref. 2.6-5.7)
S-TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (ref. 0.2-4.0)

(As for the cortisol, I'm not sure what the value says, since I had been up since 4 AM that morning, stared at the sunrise, ate a BAB and ran to the tram on my way to the hospital...)

So, can one draw any conclusions whatsoever from these labs?
What does it mean that my T3 & T4 levels are so low on the regular test, even though I feel quite ok nowadays? And why would my T3 level turn out somewhat normal with the interference test?
Why would my TSH be jumping up and down from one test to another (it has been doing that for a couple years, between 0.06 and 4.0 or so)?
What does the high B12 level mean, by the way (other than that I eat a lot of seafood and liver)?

### kovita — 2014-04-14T19:26:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reruns-labs-what-does-all-this-mean.10537/post-121447>

just my ipinion. b12 is not so high and filat looks ok. in general high b12 means it is not getting into the cells, but your MCV is good and homocystein as well. To my understanding it does not look like methylation issue. I have no knowledge to decipher your thyroid.

### JanSz — 2014-04-14T22:11:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reruns-labs-what-does-all-this-mean.10537/post-121470>

State your sex.
If female, describe day of menstrual cycle when blood was drawn, or how many days after blood was drawn cycle started, and how long was that cycle, if possible ovulation day#.
All that to help find out where you were on this chart:
[http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Hormones\_estradiol,\_progesterone,\_LH\_and\_FSH\_during\_menstrual\_cycle.svg](http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Hormones_estradiol%2C_progesterone%2C_LH_and_FSH_during_menstrual_cycle.svg)

You using T3 & T4, how much?
Current dose seems insufficient, but to process thyroid hormone you need plenty of cortisol.
Your cortisol seems to be low (can't support increased thyroid hormone levels).

Preferably get saliva 4x cortisol and DHEA test.
If easier to get, serum cortisol (7AM, noon, 3PM) and DHEAs

Get DHEAs serum anyhow.

There is a likelihood that pregnenolone and progesterone are low, that may result in low cortisol.
Then supplementation with pregnenolone could raise all three.

Hgb is highish (ferritin also highish) may want to donate pint of blood.

///////////

5+5+5 mcg = total 15 mcg Liothyronine (T3) daily

I have been on 75 mcg Levothyroxine plus 20 mcg Liothyronine daily before, but my T4 & T3 (both total and free) values were practically the same as now. TSH was jumping up and down, although within a lower range than now. Decreased dose of T4 has made that I now have the ability to sweat (haven't been able to do that for years) and I feel I have generally more energy.

\--------------------------------

There is not much that can be done without labs.
First
follow dr Kruse's recomendations on how to live daily life
In respect to thyroid concentrate on maintaining and reinforcing Circadian Rhythm,
life according to available sunlight, no electric lights
fast when sunlight available, slow or sleep after sundown

Two
You may want to try my wild guessing (I am not a doctor).

1 tab/day of DHEA Micronized Lipid Matrix from Alergy Research 50mg tabs

Above should raise your available cortisol
so
you will be able to process more thyroid hormones

AFTER you have implemented above
increase your thyroid hormones.

Preferably use Natural Thyroid Hormone (regardless of what kind of brand it is).
Without much laboratory testing
watch your body temperature, preferably by mercury thermometer but not electronic. Deep in the mouth, under tongue.
Ideal temp =36.6C (reduce thyroid hormone dose if you are not sick but temperature and heart beat raises)

Watch your pulse, if increasing your thyroid hormone dose may be too large (today, tomorrow is another story).
Pulse up to 80-90 may be ok for a time, over 100 definitely reduce dose.

Natural Thyroid Hormone, is a pork, if you are Jew, it is still ok to eat it. Not sure about Muslems.
It is available over on ebay.com

If you must use synthetic thyroid hormones, attempt to use them in proportion similar to those in Natural Thyroid Hormone from pigs.
Table below will guide you.

Pregnenolone pills (as above), should improve your progesterone and specially your lagging cortisol.
It is not good practice to supplement cortisol directly (hydrocortisone, medrol, prednisolone).

your current dose:
25 mcg Levothyroxine (T4) daily
5+5+5 mcg = total 15 mcg Liothyronine (T3) daily
15+25/4= 21.25 T3
labs corresponding to current dose (below)
\--------
1Grain=(38-T4)+(9-T3)
(T3)=4(T4)

Code:

```
Grain    T4    T3    TotalT4    TotalT3
1    38    9    74    18.5
1.25    47.5    11.25    92.5    23.125
1.5    57    13.5    111    27.75
1.75    66.5    15.75    129.5    32.375

2.25    85.5    20.25    166.5    41.625
2.5    95    22.5    185    46.25

3    114    27    222    55.5

```

\================================================
2014-04-01
"Regular" analyses:
S-Cortisol (8:17 AM): 310 nmol/L (ref. 7 AM: 200-800 nmol/L)
S-T4: 48\* nmol/L (ref. 66-181)
S-fT4: 8,2\* pmol/L (ref. 12-22)
S-T3: 0.96\* nmol/L (ref. 1.3-3.1)
S-fT3: 3.0\* pmol/L (ref. 3.1-6.8)
S-TSH: 3.1 mIU/L (ref. 0.3-4,2)

When you are able to do blood tests, it would be interesting to find your
pregnenolone
progesterone
Estradiol
Total Estrogens

first on beginning of menstrual cycle, day 3-4
second on day 19-21
when you do 19-21, note also how many days it took till flow started for next cycle.

Also
cortisol,serum (7:30AM, noon, 3:00PM)
DHEAs,serum

is important to know.

### Andrea — 2014-04-19T09:39:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reruns-labs-what-does-all-this-mean.10537/post-122503>

Thank you so much, Jan!

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=121675)
>
> You have thyroid problems and at the same time have very low cortisol.

Is that necessarily a problem? My value was lowish, but it wasn’t a true morning cortisol test. I went up at 4 am that morning and I was subject to both artificial and natural light for several hours before the drew my blood at 8:something AM. I suppose my value could be compared to perhaps a 11-12 o’clock (noon) value, which ought to be lower than in the morning.

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=121675)
>
> follow dr Kruse's recomendations on how to live daily life
> In respect to thyroid concentrate on maintaining and reinforcing Circadian Rhythm,
> life according to available sunlight, no electric lights
> fast when sunlight available, slow or sleep after sundown

Yup. I’m doing my best in this respect. But it is a bit tricky, living in the north. It was relatively easy in the winter darkness, but it gets different in the summer, when there’s natural light (including the blue spectrum) most of the night.

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=121675)
>
> 2-3 tabs/day of pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix from Nutricology, 150mg tab
> 1 tab/day of DHEA Micronized Lipid Matrix from Alergy Research 50mg tabs
> Above should raise your available cortisol so you will be able to process more thyroid hormones

This is an interesting suggestion. Made my head spin when I tried to figure it out.
And it brought me back to this being a \[vitamin A recycling and\] light/dark issue...?

After even more thinking, I have now ordered one saliva test kit for DHEA, estradiol, testosterone, progesterone and 4x cortisol. Can’t afford more of them for the time being. I suppose I will do these tests on day 19-21 in my cycle.
The question is when to do the cortisol tests. At some fixed times according to a clock, or with respect to my own rhythm? Especially the “morning cortisol” is confusing.

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=121675)
>
> AFTER you have implemented above
> increase your thyroid hormones.

But do I really want to take more thyroid supplements? My thyroid isn’t completely dead. Well, at least it wasn’t when I started medicating...

Either way, this could only work if I’m free from inflammation. Otherwise, the hormones will be converted to rT3, which I suspect is one of my current problems. On a much higher dose of my T3/T4 supplements my T3/T4 (both free and total) were at the same level as now. I actually started feeling better when I had decreased the dose (less rT3?). My heart rate was at around 80-90 with the higher dose as well, now at comfortable 60-65.
A Natural Thyroid Hormone might be better for me, of course. Will look into that.

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=121675)
>
> Consider also using this:
> 1 dropperfull/day=48mg/day
> Lugol's Iodine Family Pack -- 6 (2 fl. oz.) bottles
> [http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW](http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW)
> recalculate dose if using other iodine/iodide source.

Ooops! I thought I was going in the right direction eating kelp and fish and shrimps, but I seem to have got my math wrong. To get that amount of iodine from seafood I’d need to eat much, much more. Will definitely get some Lugol’s.

### Andrea — 2014-05-22T19:27:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reruns-labs-what-does-all-this-mean.10537/post-127979>

An update:

I've done a saliva hormone test with these results:

The test was taken on day 19 (the cycle was totally 29 days).

Also, I suddenly got an appointment with a new endocrinologist, who ran their standard blood tests. Unfortunately, my blood was drawn on day 28 (of 29), so most of my sex hormones should be low...
_(Please note that the units are different in these two tests.)_

What surprises me most is my DHEA. The blood test says my level is quite ok, while the saliva test is way high. I thought it would be at the bottom of the scale, considering my crappy sleep. I don't get it.

Anyway, my cortisol is pretty low. Low sex hormones + low cortisol ought to mean low pregnenolone. Since my T3 isn't alarmingly low (which the interference test a month ago showed), this suggests I have a Vitamin A issue, doesn't it? A circadian mismatch?

What else does it say?
Obviously, very crappy Pg/E2 ratio. Which means....? (I need to get my memory fixed!) I need more CT, don't I?
By the way, I don't have PCOS.

### JanSz — 2014-05-23T22:24:42+0300

# Thread: Let's talk about Poop Baby...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2014-04-15T08:18:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121580>

Sorry to lower the tone but I wanna talk about poop...

Nutritionally I had a relatively good day yesterday although more food than I normally eat

8am Large bowl of leftover liver, bacon and sweet potato followed by scrambled eggs on gluten free toast plus bacon
2pm steamed mussels followed by greek yoghurt (hmm!) despite not feeling hungry
7pm Salmon, ratatouille, sweet potato with a glass of local red wine
8pm Whole bar of 70% dark chocolate
1am - Finally go to bed after finishing the wine bottle (always go to bed around 9/10pm)

This morning...slightly hung over but my poop is horendous, dark black, foul smelling (which also occurs when I eat cashews)

So, I have a blind spot, I dont get constipation or diarrhea and I go every morning but the foul smell is something I should be paying attention to and in that subconscious part of my mind where I imagine I am training on Dagobah
to be an epi-paleo [jedi master](http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=S5ZsMBLUsqU), the ghost voices of selectively ignored Amy Yasko inspired methylation posts whisper in the style of Yoda "[Address you must first CBS mutation](http://www.mthfrsupport.com/other-gene-mutations-that-must-be-addressed-before-starting-an-mthfr-protocol/)". "Bad is high protein Paleo diet", "sulphur and thiol foods dark side surely lead to" "Join us you must..True is the path for Vegan worrior"

But I don't want to throw out the baby with the bath water. And is that smell really ammonia or something else such as sulphur or hydrogen sulfide (if I were a chemist I could sniff these in the lab) or do I have [Helicobacter pylori](http://www.medfriendly.com/helicobacterpylori.html)

So, rather than bury my head in the sand I resolve to become a poop jedi so that I can talk sh\*t with true poop jedis such as [Sean Croxton](http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=0WQaqeC_wME)

as Jack says when you know better you do better...so [lets talk about poop baby](http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=ydrtF45-y-g) as we all do it even educated fleas do it.

**Poop resources**
[http://www.enzymestuff.com/rtstools.htm](http://www.enzymestuff.com/rtstools.htm)
[http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2013/02/14/normal-stool.aspx](http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2013/02/14/normal-stool.aspx)
[http://www.chirocolonics.com/stoolchart.html](http://www.chirocolonics.com/stoolchart.html)
[http://www.emilysstomach.com/2013/06/poop-what-different-colors-and-smells.html](http://www.emilysstomach.com/2013/06/poop-what-different-colors-and-smells.html)
[http://www.thenaturalrecoveryplan.com/articles/The-Scoop-on-Poop.html](http://www.thenaturalrecoveryplan.com/articles/The-Scoop-on-Poop.html)

**Toilet humour**

wow Paul .... you eat a lot!!! I know you are a big guy ....but holy smokes ........

Do you normally eat all those things? gluten free bread? yogurt? wine? Ratatouille? 70% chocolate?

### sooperb — 2014-04-15T13:18:01+0300

Chocolate, a whole bar, would be the likely culprit.

I see a lot of dark green spinach coloured stools, I assume it's the greens chugging through. Since I reintroduced the oxbile it's supercharged.

### kovita — 2014-04-15T14:28:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121617>

think of yoghurt as well. The usual store yoghurt is packed with cassein. Some people (my husband) tend to react dramatically to highfat cassein loaded dairy. I would not like to blame the wine, too good to be the truth, but most of the wines (specially the red ones) contain sulfites. To many people this is a garantee fro diarrhea. Last and but not least you have more stress last days, this always shows up in digestion.

### nicld — 2014-04-15T18:21:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-poop-baby.10549/post-121666>

Great thread

I have become way more aware of what is going on and now I might need to dig a little deeper into it. I am fairly regular, between 6:30 am and 8 am everyday, after I eat and get the beeeeeeep out of my way. I have gone from C to D now and as much as I dislike the D I would rather have that than the C.

Everything changed after I was treated for SIBO last year which has made me dig way deeper into gut issues. Now I am on Ox bile and a bunch of other stuff to help digest the fats and proteins since I have no gall bladder. Also being treated for microplasma biofilms and a gut infection so who knows what is making it wonky.

### kovita — 2014-04-15T11:09:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/th1-shift-anyone.10550/post-121595>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=121592)
>
> I don't understand?

Caroline, th1 shift in your immune system. Meaning that your body is producing mainly cytotoxic immune response. normally there is a longtemr bLance between the th1 (cytotoxic) and th2 (antibody) response of your immune system. Cytotoxic response is used to fight intracellular parazites (viruses, bugs like chlamydium, mycoplasma, borrelia etc.) and antobodies are used to attack cellular parazites (anything else starting by the prokaryota till visible parazites).

# Thread: The definition of the word quantum ......
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-definition-of-the-word-quantum.10551/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-04-15T13:14:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-definition-of-the-word-quantum.10551/post-121613>

**Definition of _quantum_ in English:**
**quantum**

**noun (plural quanta /-tə/)**

-   1 _Physics_ A discrete quantity of energy proportional in magnitude to the frequency of the radiation it represents.
    More example sentences
-   1.1An analogous discrete amount of any other physical quantity, such as momentum or electric charge.
    More example sentences
-   1.2 _Physiology_ The unit quantity of acetylcholine released at a neuromuscular junction by a single synaptic vesicle, contributing a discrete small voltage to the measured end-plate potential.
    More example sentences

-   2A required or allowed amount, especially an amount of money legally payable in damages: _the court must determine the quantum of compensation due_
    More example sentences
-   2.1A share or portion: _each man has only a quantum of compassion_

Are we sure we all understand the correct usage of the word?
