# Thread: Vampires
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-16T19:48:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-121909>

Not the Transylvanians with the teeth.

[http://i.telegraph.co.uk/multimedia/archive/01773/Leslie25\_1773332i.jpg](http://i.telegraph.co.uk/multimedia/archive/01773/Leslie25_1773332i.jpg)

… but people who "steal energy".

Now @[Albert83BCN](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5984/) broached this subject indirectly recently in a thread where he says:

> "Could hate be a survival reaction when you sense that someone else is stealing electrons from you at a very quick rate?"

I stumbled on an article recently that talks about this. It was on a site that's sometimes mentioned by people in the paleosphere, although it isn't a paleo site. (Evan Brand, among others, does some of this guy's stuff.) It's a site belonging to a Dr. Wilson, and there's an enormous number of articles on it on everything from seaweed to water to toxic metals to energy. Some of it seems fairly eccentric and some pretty interesting. Anyway, he has an article entitled "Energetic vampirism_".
_
I have to say although I'd read and remembered that post, and indirectly sentence, of Albert's I'd perhaps missed the literalism in it. (I also haven't returned to Albert's thread to see what's new in it since his original post.) But this is essentially what this article is about.

I don't know how seriously to take it all. As often happens in the U.S. both religion and politics get dragged in. Thus Obamacare -- whatever that is: I only know very vaguely -- appears as a form of vampirism. We also get some fairly esoteric and unverifiable claims, such as this one:

> Several types of vampirism are common with sex. … One of the partners causes the chakras of the other person to spin backwards.When this occurs, the person always gives up some etheric energy to the other partner.Some people who are very sensitive can feel this ...

Notwithstanding, it is interesting to suspend disbelief for a while and see some of the situations he talks about in _energetic_ terms, something I wouldn't really have thought of doing.

Clearly, some people do set out to "upset" others. It wouldn't have occurred to me that they do it in order to cause energy to flow from the victim to themselves -- I wouldn't really have thought in terms of energy doing that. Maybe he's right, though.

"Upset" has the sense here of putting someone, as it were, off balance. So it could be done by threats or violence or shouting but also by flattery or seductive behaviour. Sometimes, he says, it's sexual (or quasi-sexual?). So a woman wearing provocative clothing might be trying to put men off balance in order to steal their energy; a man wolf-whistling in the street might be trying to put women off balance in order to steal their energy.

The "religious" angle comes in, because Wilson says that energy that is "stolen" is impure and actually not good for the "vampire". The energy that is pure is that which comes from the ground of being, i.e. God.

( A side note that occurred to me in the religious context:

See the story in Luke 8:43-47 (it's in all the synoptics). There there seems to be some kind of movement of energy, although there's nothing sinister about it:

[http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+8:43-47&version=KJV](http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+8%3A43-47&version=KJV) )
​

Anyway, the article may need to be taken with a pinch of salt -- at any rate by those who are sceptical of energetic explanations or religion or chakras or who like Obamacare or agree or disagree with any number of other things in it -- but, FWIW, here it is:

[http://drlwilson.com/articles/vampirism.htm](http://drlwilson.com/articles/vampirism.htm)

### fitness@home — 2014-04-16T20:10:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-121927>

You come up with the most interesting finds! Reading artilce now...

> Women often better vampires than men. According to Roy Masters and some other authorities on this subject, women vampirize others more than men.
>
> This is a rather complex subject, however, because this perspective may be due to a quality of women that is discussed in other articles on this website. It is that many women tend to sort of latch on to men, even their fathers and brothers if they are unmarried, and to take a certain amount of energy from them. Most women then process this energy and then give it back to the men in their lives. This is a type of give and take, and is not what is meant by the term ‘vampirism’ in this article. Instead, this is a type of energy exchange that is usually very beneficial.

> The energy field and energy vampirism. Some people can see or feel the electrical energy field around each person. Everyone has an energy field around their body because we are electrical and frequency beings. This is sometimes called the aura, the chakras, the tubes and other names.
>
> If one can see the energy field, energy vampires generally have little tentacles that extend from their bodies to the bodies of others, particularly those closest to them such as family members and “friends”. They also may have little suction cups on the ends of the tentacles that literally suck energy from others. This topic is illustrated in a book called Hands of Light and other books by Barbara Brennan and Joseph A. Smith.
>
> Meanwhile vampire victims often have an energy field that is full of holes and often a number of people have their “tentacles” in the holes, sucking energy from the person. These people are usually quite unhealthy.

I admit to being an energy vampire with my DH. I love it when he puts a warm hand on my back or shoulder when it is aching... I just absorb the energy.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-04-17T09:27:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-122085>

Hey, I'm glad I inspired you with my thoughts hehe

Quite interesting article, if I were to read it several years ago I guess I'd have said that it's bullshit... but as of today... being aware of how much I don't know... I consider almost anything is possible (and on the contrary, I also question everything that is presented as true). I think the article is spot on in the description of what happens, but not so much on the mechanisms that cause it. Specially as of recently and influenced by the blogposts from this site, I think that this has totally something to do with the laws of thermodynamics. It's funny that this trimester I got to study biochemistry and biology in my degree and it's helping me to understand a lot of the topics presented on this site. It has not been so long since I've been presented with the term Entropy but since I have been, I've had a profound feeling that everything has to do something with it. Marrying it with these new organization series and what we know from the quantum perspective since 2013, I think that most things surrounding this vampirism behaviour has to do with the fact that when an exergonic or endergonic reaction takes place, that energy must be taken into account within the system and outside it. But here's the fun. Every single small thing in this universe is a system in itself, that inflicts a change to the external system. That makes almost impossible to able to guess what events will take place for sure, but from the physics laws we know or at least think that whataver happens it has to be a reaction that tends to bring stabilization to this world.

The idea of entropy is that everything will try to become stable, so that the entropy (organization) becomes lower. This is tied with some weird ideas that I can barely understand saying that time must always flow forwards but not backwards. Once a jar breaks It will never spontaneously flow to a state where it reconstructs, because that reaction would require a high level of reorganization that is very unlikely to happen. That means that being in a high state of organization (for us also) is a state of high entropy that is maintained by some kind of energy (being chemical, mechanical or in this case... spiritual?). So when the entropy on us is higher (and I guess that certain situations in life like the ones the article present make it happen) we need to balance ourselves so we flow to a more stable state (peace of mind?) and that will require to take energy from other place, most probably from the people that surround us. That would be according to the laws of thermodynamics if I understood them correctly. I'm pretty sure I've just got a glance of how they really work. BTW, this brings some other weird questions... if one person is what we would say an empowering one... where does he get all the positive energy? At home, I observed an interesting thing, one that I don't like very much though. As I try to improve myself, be more positive, and explore how the world works, I can feel it has had a profound impact on me, a positive one. I tried to inspire both my parents. My dad, after being on quite a few troublesome things, has adopted some of the principles of the paleo diet, I tried to rewrite his mind in a more positive manner, and I think it kind of worked. He's more thoughtful now than he once was. In fact, he tended to be the kind of man that would start shouting out loud when something irritates him, which happened quite often. Not so much as of recently. But, on the other side, I do have the feeling that my mom is getting to worse position than ever before. A typical doc would say that the menopause has settled a different scenario for her, and well, it's almost certain that it might have it's influence, probably she has a lower redox potential than before, but considering the laws of thermodynamics and the fact that energy must be conserved... I'm worried that maybe both I and my dad getting more positive is making her loss energy to compensate for the equilibrium at home?

That makes me wonder: is it even possible to get electrons without stealing them between us? Well, I guess we have an answer in the latest blogposts. If we need electrons and we don't want to steal them from others, were might we look for them? Optimal food, the photoelectric effect from the sun and being in contact with nature, that would be the greatest source of electrons "for free". Well, for free? Nothing is for free, I guess that's why we are wasting the planet resources so fast in our era. Might it be that the more insane we become, the more resources from nature we need?

### caroline — 2014-04-17T11:00:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-122092>

Jeremy told us in the Q&A with Dr. K. a bunch of stuff about this very topic. When there is 2 CF kids in a family - one will live and one will die......the healthier one will survive because they will steal electrons from the weaker one.

Jeremy said when he is in a group of people with CF - like recently they did a fund raiser and wanted to get a group pic........ Jeremy searched the group for the strongest to stand beside. He does this on intuition and it makes him feel bad - but he is protecting himself.

He also told us that they used to send CF kids to a camp in the summer ..... and they stopped doing it - you can guess why.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-04-17T12:42:59+0300

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-18T10:35:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vampires.10564/post-122332>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=122085)
>
> I think the article is spot on in the description of what happens, but not so much on the mechanisms that cause it.

This is an important point in general, isn't it? To turn it round the other way, sometimes the explanations people give you for phenomena are unconvincing, but it doesn't follow that the phenomena don't occur.

I like your conclusion where you suggest it doesn't have to be a zero-sum game. I have to think we don't live in the kind of universe where it is. To put this discussion in a larger context, one of the contemporary problems with our world might be that people do see life as a zero-sum game.

It seems to me that [fitness@home](mailto:fitness@home) (post #2 in this thread) is _not_ a vampire, although she jokingly says she is, because her husband _gives_ her the energy. It's not obtained through force or fraud. Of course, that kind of distinction seems to belong to the _Lebenswelt_ not the scientific frame of reference.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-19T10:49:52+0300

I've just got round to reading Clint Ober's book. I had some idea of what it was about, but actually reading him leisurely setting it out in order I found I had a much stronger sense of what he was talking about and how critical it was.

He mentions a Frenchman near the beginning of the book who was saying some of this stuff, albeit without the technical understanding Ober _et al_ have now, but in a broad ecological context quite some while ago.

I also found it interesting how fragments from Ober's past began to make sense to him taken retrospectively. For example, he says at school he had Indian (Native American) friends, and he recalled a boy's mother saying to him, "Now you're home take your shoes off, or you'll get ill."

### sooperb — 2014-04-16T22:41:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-121969>

I was interested to find out more about these and came across this website. I've no idea about the doctor whose site it is or how correct she is but it made a good read

[http://drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Detoxing\_-\_Far\_Infrared\_Sauna\_(FIRS)](http://drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Detoxing_-_Far_Infrared_Sauna_\(FIRS\))

I'd be happy for opinions, does an IR sauna seem like a positive option?

### Sue-UK — 2014-04-17T13:52:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122118>

I've used one before Soo, last time a couple of years ago. I was going to do a few sessions in March, but didn't because my FIL was ill (still is). This time I was going to do it to improve redox potential rather than detox, so wasn't going to go very hot with it. I felt the time was right because I'd been drinking lots of well water, and the weather was so horrible using the sun wasn't an option. When I found myself under a lot of stress I thought if I was dehydrated the FIR was a bad idea.... Now the sun's shining the water, grounding and sunshine is the better option for me, I'll probably rethink the FIR from October onwards.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-17T14:01:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122119>

Jack says in one podcast -- I think one he did with Evan Brand -- that he has infra-red bulbs built into his bathtub. I'm not sure how he uses them, though. One use seemed to be to get a heat gradient across his body -- heat it in one place and cool it with ice in another. But perhaps that's not the only thing he does with them.

What I hear is that the _far_ infra-red saunas, the ones they often have in spas, may be bad news. Far infra-red is getting nearer the wrong place on the electromagnetic spectrum. Apparently, you can get a pretty hefty dose of harmful EMF radiation in one of those. They have large panels and if you're leaning on a panel, it's going right in your head.

The bulbs are _near_ infra-red -- in fact, they go over into the visible spectrum and you can see red light from them. They use those bulbs for poultry houses and reptile tanks, which is why the bulbs are commercially available.

### cantweight — 2014-04-17T17:28:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122162>

I just started with FIR sauna use, my BUN/Creatinine is decent around 13. Initially I was feeling great using it, but have stopped recently due to really bad edema. After reading that article (thank Sue!) I realize I was staying in too long.

And the whole rinsing immediately after did not always happen. I let myself get a good sweat on a few times which I knew I shouldnt do from a previous thread...but it felt good...I succumbed...

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/mitochondria-produce-water-hydration-by-exercise.9543/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/mitochondria-produce-water-hydration-by-exercise.9543/)

### Justfourme — 2014-04-18T04:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122295>

I have a FAR infrared sauna and use daily. Jack seems to say they are ok for most things. Brand does matter. My integrative dr researched and narrowed down to 2 brands. She has one at her office you can purchase packages/minutes of use. I tried the beginner package and decided to buy my own unit. As far as effectiveness, prob need testing to determine heavy metal and toxins, then re-test to see changes. I have noticed a few changes. Toxins are excreted in sweat, urine and poop. Let's just say the restroom smelled like a landfill for several days! I had a bug bite on my arm that got infected and has been scaly/not healing , turned to some type of fungal infection for months. In 2 weeks time it was 90% healed. All skin seems to be feeling softer and looking better. With all the toxins in everything these days, if it helps to prevent disease it will be well worth it.
I had also been experiencing chronic shoulder pain for over a year - too much typing for extended periods. That too is 90% gone.
If nothing else, it's a quiet 30 min of meditation!
I bought from high tech health as it was the lowest in emf. Sunlighten is a close second. And no, I have no affiliation w either company. Beware of any made of cedar wood - unless you like adding another toxin to the mix.
and no higher than 130 degrees F, and no longer than 30 min. High temps and longer exposure should not be done. And you need to gradually work up to these over several sessions.

### Justfourme — 2014-04-18T04:42:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122297>

You also need to up mineral supplements while using or you will not feel well. Mag, vit c, sea salt, potassium, etc. I also drink prob 18 to 20 oz of water while in the sauna.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-20T12:41:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-122660>

There's a podcast specifically on these from Wendy Myers. Wendy seems to have connections with Evan Brand, who I'm sure you know. They're using these in accordance with Dr. Lawrence Wilson's protocol (crops here in the "Vampire" thread).

[http://www.liveto110.com/infrared-saunas-in-depth-with-nikki-moses/](http://www.liveto110.com/infrared-saunas-in-depth-with-nikki-moses/)

I have heard -- where I can't remember: possibly on this podcast -- that one study looked at the sweat of people leaving saunas (whether Finnish or Infra-red I can't remember) and found mercury in the sweat. This certainly suggests that saunas can help shift some substances out of the body.

The more hard-nosed people in Paleo would probably scoff at the notion of "toxins" and "detoxing". I've heard Rob Wolf do so on a podcast. To be sure people who talk vaguely of "detoxing" probably don't really know what they mean and the methods they propound don't necessarily work anyway. But these are real physiological processes. And maybe some of the methods Wendy uses do help our bodies' elimination systems.

I haven't tried infra-red saunas, but they do sound interesting. So many cultures use sweat-baths that I have to think there has to be _something_ in it for some purposes at any rate. (See Native American sweat lodges, for example.) They seem to have been almost the original form of bath. What seems to have driven them out in the modern world is, curiously enough, the spread of Christianity. The early Christians could be highly ascetic, and some even looked upon keeping onself clean as a form of luxury. There's no Biblical basis for this attitude at all, of course -- no more than for the New England Puritan view that was supposed to hold that "cleanliness is next to godliness" and had missionaries persuading Eskimos to use towels much to Stefansson's annoyance, since the towels spread eye-diseases from person to person -- but that's what they thought. Notice that the sauna comes from Finland, which was, as it were, on the fringes of the world and evangelised very late.

Anyway, if sweat lodges were good enough for the Apache, they're probably good enough for us -- though I'd be just as interested in any data that showed they were not.

### RobH — 2014-04-26T03:22:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-sauna-anyone.10567/post-123472>

Bought a SunLighten (got a great deal, got it up and running a few days ago.) Supposedly very low EMF per them (and I will test). Jack said in one podcast of the OR series (with Dr. Patel) that he thought they were great as long as one didn't suffer from chronic dehydration (and the sauna was low EMF). I am thinking anything I can do to add energy to my system will be helpful since energy loss is a huge issue. Naturally the folks who sell them cite host of benefits (detoxification, skin health, muscle tone, decreased BP, increased healing, weight loss, cardio tone, improve sleepetc.) Ours also has "chromotherapy" with changeable light (set to red) for additional anti-inflammatory effects.

My wife tried it the other night before bed. Said it stopped her late night carb cravings and she slept great. Turned off breakers too that night though.

So far have done 3 sessions in it < 125F. About 45 min each. Very nice. Really enjoyed it. Very relaxing. Have a few minor wounds / scrapes that seem to be healing faster than expected. Perhaps it's my imagination but my skin feels softer. Have been drinking lots of cold vortexed and magnetized RO water before and during.... goal is to store a whole bunch of extra energy in EZ of water!

I worry a bit about the FIR as postulated above by gagnrad but actually there are far worse sources of EMF I deal with every day. Ours is a "full-spectrum" model so theoretically I can customize the output and specify how much near / mid / far EMF to emit.

Very very happy so far.

Ask me in a month or so and I'll let you know...

RobH

To get full IR impact you should be very close to them, but mine has a total of 14 heaters including the floor so not too hard to get close. For stretching the heat is all that matters. I got mine after reading Pollack's book and listening to Jack and was amazed at the energy (electrons) it gave me. If you live in a northern climate, I'm in Canada, it really shows you how much we miss the sun in the winter.

# Thread: The Obesity Paradox?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-obesity-paradox.10570/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2014-04-17T02:35:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-obesity-paradox.10570/post-122025>

[http://www.abc.net.au/health/featur...hAndWellbeing|WhatIsTheObesityParadox\_FBP|abc](http://www.abc.net.au/health/features/stories/2014/04/14/3984799.htm?WT.mc_id=Innovation_Innovation-HealthAndWellbeing|WhatIsTheObesityParadox_FBP|abc)

> Being overweight or obese is known to increase your risk of a range of serious diseases. Yet researchers are finding you're more likely to survive a chronic illness if you carry extra weight.
> { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }
>
>
> \[Image source: iStockPhoto | jsheets19\]
> It's no secret Australians are getting heavier. More than [60 per cent of us are overweight or obese](http://www.aihw.gov.au/diabetes-indicators/overweight-and-obesity), and OECD data suggests when it comes to our weight we're the [fourth biggest nation in the world](http://www.oecd.org/els/health-systems/Health-at-a-Glance-2013.pdf)
> Being overweight or obese is known to increase your chances of developing serious illnesses such as [type 2 diabetes](http://www.abc.net.au/health/library/stories/2002/12/19/1829403.htm#d), [heart disease](http://www.abc.net.au/health/library/stories/2003/02/27/1831271.htm) and cancer, and maintaining a healthy BMI (Body Mass Index) is one of the best ways to prevent these health issues later in life.
> But a growing body of research suggests some people who are classed as "obese" have a better chance of surviving the chronic diseases that obesity causes in the first place. This phenomenon has been labelled the "obesity paradox" and it has researchers puzzled.
> **Breaking down the BMI**
> The BMI scale is used worldwide and is considered the best way to measure a person's body fat in relation to their height.
> It's easy to determine by plugging your [height and weight into an online calculator](http://www.abc.net.au/health/quizzestools/tools/2008/01/17/2114338.htm#bmi) and it will quickly let you know if you're carrying too much fat.
> CategoryBMI Range
> Very severely underweightLess than 15
> Severely underweight15-16
> Underweight16-18.5
> Normal (healthy weight)18.5-25
> Overweight25-30
> Obese class one (moderately)30-35
> Obese class two (severely)35-40
> Obese class three (very severely)Over 40
> But many experts, including Professor John Dixon, head of clinical obesity research at Baker IDI Heart and Diabetes Institute, warn BMI isn't the most accurate way of telling if someone is a healthy weight, because it will change depending on the person, their age and health issues.
> "Optimal weight for health, longevity and mortality isn't constant – and why should it be for all adults of any age and with any disease?" he says.
> **When bigger is better**
> The evidence for the obesity paradox has found those who are overweight or obese are more likely to survive renal failure, heart failure, diabetes and coronary heart disease, regardless of their age.
> Furthermore, one recent US study found being overweight or class-one obese can actually [protect people from _all causes_ of death](http://jama.jamanetwork.com/article.aspx?articleid=1555137).
> But despite all the recent research into the obesity paradox, experts still don't really know why it occurs.
> However, Dixon says, "it certainly is a very real thing". He argues it's easier to understand how older people in particular are protected by carrying extra weight, especially those who suffer serious wasting diseases.
> "It's been found that in people with all forms of cancer, the bigger they are, the better they do in treatment, because they have greater reserves of muscle and fat at a time when they are under stress," he explains.
> For example, a new review by Deakin University, which looked at research carried out on 20,000 people over the age of 65, found [those with a BMI between 23 and 33 actually live longer](http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2014/01/22/ajcn.113.068122.abstract) than people with a BMI in the lower end of the normal weight category (between 18.5 and 23).
> But what is harder to understand is why young people with lower BMIs have a smaller chance of surviving a sudden serious illness than those of a similar age who are obese, explains Joseph Proietto, professor of medicine at the University of Melbourne.
> "Let's say there's someone with a BMI of 19 who's fit and healthy and someone with a BMI of 34," he says.
> "Each has a heart attack because they've inherited high cholesterol. Who'll survive better? The evidence suggests the person with the BMI of 34 has a better chance."
> Proietto says it could be because people who are obese are often already being treated for conditions such as high blood pressure and cholesterol.
> People whose weight is not an issue on the other hand, probably aren't even aware that they have some of these health problems, giving them a disadvantage if they suddenly fall ill.
> **Cleaning up the confusion**
> The obesity paradox seems a huge contradiction to everything we've been told about maintaining a healthy weight. So it begs the question: "why bother?" when it comes to diet and exercise.
> It also doesn't help that researchers can't explain why obese people seem to fare better in illness. Until more research is done, they're really working on theories.
> But Dixon stresses the obesity paradox is definitely not an invitation to throw healthy eating and exercise habits out the window. Preventing these diseases should always be your priority and the best way to do that is by staying lean when you're young.
> "People under the age of 40 who have no illness should really be trying to keep their weight down, because there's no doubt that overweight and obesity is associated with disease: diabetes, heart disease, heart failure," he says.
> Up until middle age you should attempt to stay within a BMI of 20-25 and lead a healthy, active lifestyle, Proietto says.
> "But as you get older, you shouldn't fuss too much if you gain a little weight, because it's when you're older that you're going to have a stroke or heart attack or surgery," he says.
> "However, this doesn't apply if you're massively obese. Morbid obesity shortens your life, without a doubt."
> Once you reach middle age, he recommends focusing on keeping fit and eating highly nutritious foods, rather than losing weight.
> "If you stay fit, you're at least minimising your risk of having cardiovascular problems," he explains.
> But if you suffer from, say, diabetes and you're over 50, you're much better off asking yourself, "What's the ideal weight for me?" and seeing your doctor for advice.
> "We're not suggesting severely obese people shouldn't try to improve their diet and lifestyle, and try to lose weight," Dixon says.
> "If they can take off just three per cent of body weight, that has measurable benefits on their health.
> "But we can't ever expect people over the age of 60, for instance, to try to achieve a 'normal weight' range, which may not be good for healthy ageing.
> "I think the real concept here is that the normal weight range isn't necessarily the healthy weight range for a given individual."

# Thread: Tim Noakes Interview
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tim-noakes-interview.10575/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-17T11:58:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tim-noakes-interview.10575/post-122108>

Boy is Dr. Noakes in good form in this one:

[http://vinnietortorich.com/2014/04/angriest-trainer-256-dr-timothy-noakes/](http://vinnietortorich.com/2014/04/angriest-trainer-256-dr-timothy-noakes/)

I haven't heard it all yet. I'm still listening to it, but what I've heard so far is very good.

Among other things I like his very well-informed explanation of the history behind where we are now. He agrees that Ancel Keys is a key figure. What is interesting is that Dr. Noakes says that an Irish reseracher now living in South Africa found out that Keys had wanted to at least partially retract what he'd said later in his life but found he couldn't. Keys said he was blocked by industry, who'd found that the high-carb/low-fat paradigm was just too profitable.

This, of course, isn't a "conspiracy theory". They never devised the paradigm. People aren't as devilishly clever and far-seeing as that. What humans are smart enough and organised enough to do is to take advantage of situations when they see them. Unfortunately, people's self-interest -- or here group-interest -- is not always in the general interest (the fable of the bees nothwithstanding), and probably not even good for them in the long run. People think short-term, though, don't they?

Dr. Noakes is also good on the corruption of the medical profession by the pharmaceutical industry. He says that back when he trained doctors wanted to know what _caused_ problems; they didn't just throw a drug at a problem and push you out of the surgery (or "office" as they say in the U.S.). I think there's a kind of attitude in the "alternative" health world, and even in the paleosphere, where people will sneer on the grounds of not looking for causes at "Western medicine" -- as if anything "Eastern" is automatically superior! I think anyone who even knows a smidgin of the history of medicine will find this rather silly. If you're Chinese and your kid's face gets half burnt off, you don't put your trust in "traditional medicine"; you try to get her to the West (an actual case I know of). Modern medicine has its weaknesses, but it has also been corrupted by the pharmaceutical companies.

Anyway, Noakes is very good here on lots of things. It would be a good podcast to put in the way of anyone who still believes in the high-carb paradigm, since he explains things very well.

Oh, nearly forgot. Also on Keys. Dr. Noakes says that when Keys first trotted his theory out in a committee meeting of scientists, they were derisive and he was "humiliated". There's another moral to the story. Ancel Keys's pride was obviously hurt, and as a consequence he set himself to make his views dominant. Sometimes it's "interest" that is the problem; but often it's pride. Keys may have wished to retract later, but by then he'd done the damage.

> The evil that men do lives after them; the good is oft interred with their bones.

### sooperb — 2014-04-17T18:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tim-noakes-interview.10575/post-122185>

You could almost add "and the rest, as they say, is history". A useful illustration of the benefits/profits of commerce outweighing the good of the populace, there's nothing altruistic about big business is there!

I remember reading somewhere that the opponents of margarine endured some pretty awful heavy handed and threatening pressure when they started delivering lectures against it. In the end they gave up because they felt it wasn't worth the risk to either themselves or their futures.

### Gracelind — 2014-04-18T18:27:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tim-noakes-interview.10575/post-122388>

Re: margarine My dad used to talk about how the original margarine was an unappetizing gray. The butter industry prevented the margarine from being artificially colored at the plant. But it came with a packet of coloring that could be stirred in to make it yellow.

_By 1902, 32 states had passed margarine coloring bans. Butter is yellow; margarine, which at the time was produced with animal fat, was gray. Dairy producers opposed coloring margarine yellow. So margarine took on lots of colors, and, in some instances, margarine was sold with a home coloring kit._ [http://blogs.ancestry.com/cm/2013/03/11/a-margarine-of-error/](http://blogs.ancestry.com/cm/2013/03/11/a-margarine-of-error/)

# Thread: GM Crop to Produce Fish Oil
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gm-crop-to-produce-fish-oil.10576/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2014-04-17T14:40:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gm-crop-to-produce-fish-oil.10576/post-122126>

When I first read this I was a bit confused as to how plants could be modified to produce "fish oil" but then I realised that they are actually producing "oil for fish." They want to grow these plants so that they can be fed to farmed fish as currently "80% of the fish oil harvested from the oceans is fed to fish grown in farms."

[http://www.theguardian.com/environm...ified-plants-heart-disease-fish-oils-go-ahead](http://www.theguardian.com/environment/2014/apr/17/trial-gm-genetically-modified-plants-heart-disease-fish-oils-go-ahead)

### nonchalant — 2014-04-17T16:05:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gm-crop-to-produce-fish-oil.10576/post-122146>

> The particular oils that benefit the health of fish and humans, called EPA and DHA, are not in fact produced by fish themselves but instead accumulated by eating marine microbes. Napier's team took up to seven genes from algae that produce the fish oils and [transplanted them into oil seed plants called camelina](http://www.rothamsted.ac.uk/news/crop-plants-green-factories-fish-oils). It naturally produces short-chain oils and has been grown as a food crop for centuries in southern and eastern Europe and is used a biofuel crop in North America.

# Thread: Looking for resources on Brain/Gut/antibiotic connection.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-resources-on-brain-gut-antibiotic-connection.10599/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Marie — 2014-04-20T04:49:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-resources-on-brain-gut-antibiotic-connection.10599/post-122636>

A friend's 60 y/o hubby is having +40 seizures a day and the docs can't find anything wrong with his brain. I suggested that they look for a gut connection and asked if he'd had any heavy rounds of antibiotics. She said "Funny, yes. And the seizures dropped to 2 a day."

They've cleaned up his/their diet to the point that the hubby says "Life is better when you are eating close to the ground." His intuition is kicking in.

I'm guessing this is a Vit B issue and that the antibiotics are changing the gut profile for the better for a short while, but I'm looking for resources to send them to give to the docs.

(Oh, and yes... horrific circadian cycles with EMF exposure as he is a programmer.)

Marie

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-20T12:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-resources-on-brain-gut-antibiotic-connection.10599/post-122658>

> [Marie said:](/goto/post?id=122636)
>
> (Oh, and yes... horrific circadian cycles with EMF exposure as he is a programmer.)

Get him grounded. Send him a copy of this, perhaps:

[http://www.amazon.com/Earthing-Most-Important-Health-Discovery/dp/1591202833/](http://www.amazon.com/Earthing-Most-Important-Health-Discovery/dp/1591202833/)

Besides normalizing you electrically, it will, apparently, help your gut bacteria to normalize. Or perhaps I shouldn't say "besides" -- all these things seem to be linked.

I can recall some years ago reading in that marvellous book _[Lights Out](http://www.amazon.com/Lights-Out-Sleep-Sugar-Survival/dp/0671038680/)_ how harmful bacteria tend to get kept in check by benign ones during sleep; so one of the one of the important functions of sleep is allowing that to happen. Well, one of the most consistently reported effects of grounding/earthing is better sleep: some people who've only had a handful of hours a night and broken at that report going through solid for 7, 8, 9 or more the first time they sleep on a grounded sheet. If this man is a programmer, I'll bet his sleep is shot.

[http://www.amazon.com/Earthing-HSK-Half-Sheet-Kit/dp/B0071URSUW/](http://www.amazon.com/Earthing-HSK-Half-Sheet-Kit/dp/B0071URSUW/)

Not what you asked, however ...

I'd suggest trying pubmed:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed)

with some relevant search terms, e.g:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=brain+gut](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=brain+gut)

# Thread: Metabolomics? NIH
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metabolomics-nih.10612/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-04-21T19:29:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metabolomics-nih.10612/post-122836>

[http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2014/04/16/ajcn.113.078758.abstract](http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2014/04/16/ajcn.113.078758.abstract)

> Objectives: Our first aim was to identify metabolites that are biomarkers of usual dietary intake. Second, among serum metabolites correlated with diet, we evaluated metabolite reproducibility and required sample sizes to determine the potential for metabolomics in epidemiologic studies.
>
> Design: Baseline serum from 502 participants in the Prostate, Lung, Colorectal, and Ovarian (PLCO) Cancer Screening Trial was analyzed by using ultra-high-performance liquid-phase chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry. Usual intakes of 36 dietary groups were estimated by using a food-frequency questionnaire. Dietary biomarkers were identified by using partial Pearson's correlations with Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons. Intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) between samples collected 1 y apart in a subset of 30 individuals were calculated to evaluate intraindividual metabolite variability.
>
> Results: We detected 412 known metabolites. Citrus, green vegetables, red meat, shellfish, fish, peanuts, rice, butter, coffee, beer, liquor, total alcohol, and multivitamins were each correlated with at least one metabolite (_P_ < 1.093 × 10−6; _r_ = −0.312 to 0.398); in total, 39 dietary biomarkers were identified. Some correlations (citrus intake with stachydrine) replicated previous studies; others, such as peanuts and tryptophan betaine, were novel findings. Other strong associations included coffee (with trigonelline-_N_\-methylnicotinate and quinate) and alcohol (with ethyl glucuronide). Intraindividual variability in metabolite levels (1-y ICCs) ranged from 0.27 to 0.89. Large, but attainable, sample sizes are required to detect associations between metabolites and disease in epidemiologic studies, further emphasizing the usefulness of metabolomics in nutritional epidemiology.
>
> Conclusions: We identified dietary biomarkers by using metabolomics in an epidemiologic data set. Given the strength of the associations observed, we expect that some of these metabolites will be validated in future studies and later used as biomarkers in large cohorts to study diet-disease associations. The PLCO trial was registered at [clinicaltrials.gov](http://clinicaltrials.gov/) as [NCT00002540](http://ajcn.nutrition.org/external-ref?link_type=CLINTRIALGOV&access_num=NCT00002540).

[http://www.nutraingredients-usa.com...tter+Daily&c=T5DwZFxZmFHDKkAUN/jBxBvDZTrQryR6](http://www.nutraingredients-usa.com/Research/Advancing-nutritional-epidemiology-to-a-whole-new-level-NIH-scientists-publish-metabolomics-biomarkers-to-study-diet-disease-associations/?utm_source=newsletter_daily&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=Newsletter%2BDaily&c=T5DwZFxZmFHDKkAUN%2FjBxBvDZTrQryR6)

### nonchalant — 2014-04-21T22:34:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metabolomics-nih.10612/post-122863>

Wonder if those 39 biomarkers include ones for "vegetable" oil, high fructose corn syrup, soy, fluoride, wheat, A1 dairy, and MSG.

# Thread: 1830 Floor
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1830-floor.10625/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-22T22:07:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1830-floor.10625/post-123074>

I was out walking on Saturday and photographed this floor in the remains of an old cottage near the sea. This was a stationmaster's cottage in the 19th and early 20th century, but the building is older. It seems it went up in 1830 and, strangely enough, some of the bricks used in it were taken from the remains of a Roman villa a few miles away.

This now ruined village was a small settlement near a tide-mill -- grain was ground nearby using the tide.

What interested me about this particular building was this floor. I don't know whether it's made from bricks or whether these are flooring setts and perhaps somewhat thinner. I didn't get down and have a close look at the time. … Casting an eye over the floor now -- they don't look to me like regular sizes; possibly these are Roman.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Anyway, these would conduct in grounding terms. That's my point. I couldn't see any flooring in the other rooms. It's possible that those were floored with something else, including non-conductive material such as wood. But perhaps not. I think bricks and quarry tiles were probably used a fair bit back then. Indeed, I think earthen floors were probably still in use in the country. It would be interesting to ask an architectural historian.

I was thinking about all those podgy 18th-Century and Regency gentlemen again. AFAICT, it was the gentlemen who were overweight in those days, if anyone was, not the poor (as is frequently true now, as Gary Taubes has pointed out).

Of course, their diet was poorly chosen and they drank to excess, but I wonder if a more luxurious standard of interior decoration was part of the reason. You can't ground through thick carpeting, and I'll bet many of these people didn't go out much and didn't take their shoes off or lie in the grass.

This is perhaps why they did find some relief from sea bathing, which became a popular activity for the rich at this time:

> … bathing machine proprietors employed fishermen and their womenfolk to help the bathers into the sea. Tough, muscular fishermen and boatmen assisted the male swimmers and the fishermen's wives, sisters and daughters, if they were strong and sturdy enough for the job, helped to "dip" women and children. The male bathing attendants were generally known as "**bathers**" and the female attendants, who assisted women and children, were called "**dippers**".

[http://www.photohistory-sussex.co.uk/BTNCHAR\_MarthaGunn.htm](http://www.photohistory-sussex.co.uk/BTNCHAR_MarthaGunn.htm)

> I was thinking about all those podgy 18th-Century and Regency gentlemen again. AFAICT, it was the gentlemen who were overweight in those days, if anyone was, not the poor (as is frequently true now, as Gary Taubes has pointed out).

>
> Of course, their diet was poorly chosen and they drank to excess, but I wonder if a more luxurious standard of interior decoration was part of the reason. You can't ground through thick carpeting, and I'll bet many of these people didn't go out much and didn't take their shoes off or lie in the grass.

> [http://www.photohistory-sussex.co.uk/BTNCHAR\_MarthaGunn.htm](http://www.photohistory-sussex.co.uk/BTNCHAR_MarthaGunn.htm)

Maybe having servants, who were getting all that grounding, but the way, had something to do with that - very little activity level. When they were out and about, consider all the closing that was covering them - suntan had to be avoided at all cost, eating was their favorite pastime.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-23T07:56:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1830-floor.10625/post-123140>

Sure, women worried about their complexions.

I guess the other thing men did was sit after dinner with bottles -- probably often till pretty late. Alcohol and curtailed sleep: by candlelight to be sure, but still not a good idea.

### Gracelind — 2014-04-23T08:55:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1830-floor.10625/post-123144>

Love the photo of the brick floor--very cool. Think of all the generations of feet that trod those pavers. Think of the hands that crafted them however many years ago. The lives that were lived, the people who passed through that room.

In reading a US Federal Tax list from the 1860s I discovered that my gr-grandfather--emigrant from Glasgow to Santa Cruz--was a brickmaker. He was taxed on the number of bricks he had in his brickyard when the tax man came round. His brickmaking career was not part of the family lore until recently resurrected by old records filmed and displayed on the Internet.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-23T10:27:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1830-floor.10625/post-123146>

That's wonderful.

I took some photos a few years ago of the brickwork on some walling down at the same site. I'll look them out. The wall is quite beautiful in its way -- the different colours of the bricks, the patterns in how they'd laid them, the way they've weathered. I doubt the same could be said for much modern man-made construction.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-23T19:36:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1830-floor.10625/post-123218>

Here it is -- it's actually brickwork on a bridge there. It looks like some courses run side-on and some end-on.

### Gracelind — 2014-04-24T03:12:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1830-floor.10625/post-123258>

Very interesting switch in type of bricks there.

Could we call it a brick phase transition?

### nonchalant — 2014-04-24T04:21:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1830-floor.10625/post-123266>

# Thread: Hydration and movements.......

### nicld — 2014-04-23T03:18:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hydration-and-movements.10626/post-123109>

Ok, I know that how hydrated effects movements.

If someone gets a little dehydrated, how soon would that effect movements and how soon can things turn around?

I purposely drank way less water this weekend to see how in ketosis I was throughout the day. I was having a hard time getting the sticks to read anything in the late after noon after drinking tons of water throughout the day so I tried reducing the water.

I am starting to think that was a big mistake since I have not had a movement since Sunday morning when I use to be like clockwork, everyday by 8:30 am.

So I have learned a lesson to always make sure I drink my gallon of water a day.

### nicld — 2014-04-23T03:40:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hydration-and-movements.10626/post-123110>

Ok, nevermind. The answer is 2 days.

### caroline — 2014-04-23T04:40:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hydration-and-movements.10626/post-123126>

Some use a meter to check ketosis Nicole ....apparently it is way better.

My stix never read but if I am not in ketosis I dont want to know ....someone must be giving me magic mushroom then!

Whatever it is - I am loving it.

### nicld — 2014-04-23T16:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hydration-and-movements.10626/post-123180>

That was not fun. I always appreciated Jack for doing his own biohacks but after trying one myself for only a weekend I now have an even greater respect for what he does for us.

### sjoshua — 2014-04-23T21:20:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hydration-and-movements.10626/post-123227>

Ketostix are kinda silly, since they are only able to test for acetoacetate, which is a specific ketone body that is typically only produced during the initial phases of ketosis. I wouldn't rely on them for... anything really.

The meters typically test for Beta-Hydroxybutyrate, which is the ketone body that is much more prevailent after fully adapted to keto (and, is actually what your body converts acetoacetate into through beta-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase...).

@[caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) 's intuition here is spot on

EDIT: just stumbled on this, which dives in deeper than I have: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...etosis-are-we-actually-looking-for-nad.10598/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/best-measure-of-ketosis-are-we-actually-looking-for-nad.10598/)

### Tanya — 2014-04-24T02:45:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hydration-and-movements.10626/post-123254>

I need to get myself the meter then....

### cantweight — 2014-04-23T04:03:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123111>

These are my January labs. Drawn on CD8. Trying to figure out what I need to test at my next draw.

I am having horrendous issues with weight gain, skin dryness and acne, severe edema that ends in a day of constant urination with urine smelling strongly of ammonia. Something is wrong...docs are shaking their heads...like they just don't know.

Hoping you all can give some words of wisdom.

I really appreciate it.

### nicld — 2014-04-23T16:39:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123188>

One thing that caught my eye was your pregnenalone is in the gutter (so was mine). I am on cream now and it is getting better.

If you can swing it, I would start working with Dr. Tim if you are not already. The only issue with that might be is he in no longer doing single consults but the package year long deal. I think he said that if you were already a client you can still do the single ones (which I need to do soon) but new patients will be required to do the year package.

### cantweight — 2014-04-23T17:12:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123196>

That could definitely explain some of the cognitive issues I am having.....as well as inflammatory responses. I did a consult with Tim but after paying for it didnt have the funds for the 700 bucks worth of tests...so i kind of dropped that ball.

Might give the cream a go...thanks so much for the input. Really struggling to put my finger on stuff right now.

### cantweight — 2014-04-24T16:10:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123310>

@[nicld](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6689/) I took your advice and ran out and got some pregnenolone yesterday and I shit you not an hour after taking it I was dancing around and more clearheaded than I have been in months. Did not expect that! I also forced my ass into the tub to CT...it had been a while and added a new liver/GI detox supplement....I don't know which it was but I felt so much better by yesterday afternoon and my face is about 80% clear....drastic reduction in inflammation. I know oral preg can cause a rise in T but mine has a little wiggle room and I actually like a little T boost....gives me edge and my hubby likes my T boosted too

@[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) and @[Shijin13](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/969/) any words of wisdom? I am just not putting some pieces together. My field tests excellent, I stay at home so no work environment issues, I ground for 3-10 hours a week, I could use more sunlight for sure but that is a seasonal thing. My sleep is very good snoring and apnea under control with oral appliance, 3-4.5 litres of RO water daily. I have lightened my diet...breakfast was skyr, spinach, pineapple, and green banana smoothie and espresso with HWC. Added raw beef and seafood. I have lessened my fats and cooked proteins. I either feel good or feel horrible and the see sawing back and forth is exhausting. I am back to living life where I dont make plans to do things because i dont know how I will feel and I hate that.

I am considering adding metformin, modafinil, maybe even procrit to get my c4a down.

I have PCOS, hashi's, and am morbidly obese. My biggest concerns are rapid weight gain in the last year (40lbs), cognitive decline, edema, ammonia urine.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-24T17:45:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123319>

Christi

your comment the other day about not being able to even consider CO, and seeing the High levels of IgA and IgM for candida - have me thinking your gut microbia might be driving that aversion?

HbA1C is high.... at 5.6 and Insulin 21.7 are high need to bring them down. this coupled with your edema, and ammonia - possible dehydration, issues w/hormones, diabetes/metabolic syndrome or kidney issues.

Free T4 looks low, you're not making enough to support hormone cascade... and linked with elevated TSH; free T3 is low. I feel better when mine is above 3.5 and your FreeT3:rT3 ratio is 34. so that's good. I think you need more Armour. Have things changed with increased dose of Armour? ACTH - looks "normal" and indicates pituitary is working - though the question how much is actually working...

Pg Is low - but you knew that. coupled with your low pregnenolone and low thyroid = tanked hormone panel. Low pregnenolone = disconnected vitamin A cycle. which also impacts hormone cascade... which is verified by your vitamin A serum. Mag RBC is ok. Ideal is above 6. but if your dehydrated you're going to have issues with getting Mag RBC up while still being dehydrated (which you are given your Bun:Creatine ratio being 15)

SHBG isn't bad - but its above 30. so your Estrogen to Pg ratio is off... but then you know that!

let's see what @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) says.

### cantweight — 2014-04-24T18:28:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123324>

The candida numbers really surprised me...I have had zero symptoms which i used to have a terrible time with especially in the mouth. So definitely still a stressor, not sure why the sudden flare. My gut has taken a nosedive. Currently trying to support that.

Hoping the metformin will help to address the pre-diabetic/Blood glucose issue

Thyroid is a tricky one. Doc wants me back up at 180mg but I cant take it due to heart palps. So I am taking 90 AM and 45 PM and will restest next week.

Two months of oral pg after many months of creams and no response both rx and otc...will see where that has gotten me. Have also added lots of DIM and Ca D Glucarate.

BUN/Creat. was 13 in Oct and 15 in Jan....water consumption increased. Feeling badly in that time, redox low, ca efflux high, muscle fatigue and burning severe....mag driven out? water driven out? Trying to turn that ship around...not proving easy.

Thanks so much for the input it is greatly appreciated!!!

### JanSz — 2014-04-26T21:31:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123536>

> [Rylee said:](/goto/post?id=123358)
>
> The skin and acne issues could definitely be related to low vitamin A - I agree with JanSz to eat more A - liver is a good way to get it. Tanya has a thread going today with some liver recipes that sound totally good! I think your thyroid issues are behind the weight gain, and probably high estrogen/low Pg is behind the cognitive issues. Have you thought about supplementing progesterone? (Sorry if you already are - I'm new and don't know everyone's story yet!)

In my view when pregnenolone and progesterone are low and supplementation is contemplated,
it make sense (to me) to supplement with pregnenolone first.
With luck one can achieve number of goals with just one type of pill.

I figured that for my self and it works for me already for about three years.
I am man.
From pov of progestrone man=women in the first half of women's cycle.
[http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Hormones\_estradiol,\_progesterone,\_LH\_and\_FSH\_during\_menstrual\_cycle.svg](http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Hormones_estradiol%2C_progesterone%2C_LH_and_FSH_during_menstrual_cycle.svg)
In second half, specially around days 18-24 women need lots of progesterone,
so if need arise those are the days when on top of pregnenolone some progesterone may be need.

///

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123544>

I have added the pregnenolone and am already seeing improvenment. I thank you so much for your time and input!

### JanSz — 2014-04-26T23:52:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123545>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=123544)
>
> I have added the pregnenolone and am already seeing improvenment. I thank you so much for your time and input!

In my experience not all pregnenolones are equal. Either when in form of pills or transdermal.
Commonly doctors like more transdermal kind.
In my case I found the hard way that the only type that works for me is:

Pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix 150mg tabs, from Nutricology.

I am using currently two of those tabs per day, one at wakeup the other by noon.
But initially for many months I had to use 6 tabs (900mg/day)

Above still left me wanting on pregnenolone,
but my progesterone went high
and
my cortisol AM went up to top range

So really my pregnenolone dose is decided by my cortisol(AM)

This may not be your case. It all depends of status of enzymes facilitating conversion process.
You must do blood tests to monitor your status.

Initially I would not use any supplemental progesterone, that may cloud analysis of your progress.
But it is good idea to top off on DHEAs.

///

### Martin — 2014-04-27T00:53:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123548>

Bunch of tests! Cool!

Vit. D is toast-ish. I had to switch to water-based D cuz of VDR snp. Really helped!

I noticed your C4a was up... 5244 (0-650) Something's revved up.

Dr Shoemaker writes:
**C4a
Normal Range**: **0-2830 ng/ml

C4a has become the inflammatory marker of greatest significance looking at innate immune responses in those with exposure to [Water Damaged Buildings](http://www.survivingmold.com/diagnosis/what-is-a-water-damaged-building) (WDB).

The complement system is a group of proteins that move freely through your bloodstream. The proteins work with your immune system and play a role in the development of inflammation.

Each complement activates inflammatory responses, with spillover of effect from the innate immune response to acquired immune response and hematologic parameters.

These short-lived products are re-manufactured rapidly, such that an initial rise of plasma levels is seen within 12 hours of exposure to biotoxins, and sustained elevation is seen until definitive therapy is initiated.
**

# Thread: Modern domestic animal traits may have appeared far more recently than previously thought
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-domestic-animal-traits-may-have-appeared-far-more-recently-than-previously-thought.10629/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-23T07:50:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-domestic-animal-traits-may-have-appeared-far-more-recently-than-previously-thought.10629/post-123139>

> … "Looking today at our pets and livestock including dogs, cows, sheep, and chickens, there's been an assumption that if a particular trait is found in a majority of breeds, that it must have made its first appearance at the dawn of domestication thousands of years ago," explained Dr Greger Larson from Durham University's Department of Archaeology. "That makes sense since it is reasonable to assume a correlation between ubiquity and time – the more common a trait is, the longer it's likely to have been kicking around.
>
> "Our research, however, suggests that even some of the most common traits, like yellow legs in chickens, were extremely rare as recently as 500 years ago, and that humans can drive selection rapidly, making it look like the trait has a deep history. In this way, domestic animals evolve more like fashion and culture than like wild animals. Just like taking a 'selfie' on your smart phone is both recent and universal, chickens across the world possess traits their recent ancestors didn't have." ...

[http://phys.org/wire-news/159607267...raits-may-have-appeared-far-more-recentl.html](http://phys.org/wire-news/159607267/modern-domestic-animal-traits-may-have-appeared-far-more-recentl.html)

# Thread: What are the conditions fueling modern human brain?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-conditions-fueling-modern-human-brain.10630/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Melanie Procter — 2014-04-23T12:10:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-conditions-fueling-modern-human-brain.10630/post-123157>

**In Brain Gut 4, Jack wrote the following are the conditions that built an optimum human brain. This is an incredible approach to thinking about how to go optimal. I do wonder what the conditions are that have been created to create such a great leap forward in our cognitive evolution. Considering so many people are devoid of the nutritional components below, what precursors exist to fuel our cognitive upgrade? Is there something in the air? Are we in a more engergetic part of the universe? How will our cognitive upgrade be explained hundreds of years from now?**

**What else do we need to build a human brain?**

1.  Iodine
2.  Iron
3.  A placenta that was capable of creating a massive fat furnace to fuel the perfect mix of 03 DHA to 06 EPA to make the neural framework.
4.  Lots of oxygen to complete the desaturation converting shorter chain to long chain PUFA’s: DHA requires 6 O2 molecules. This is why previous to the Cambrian explosion we have no ‘big brained’ animals in the fossil record. We had no oxygen to fuel a brain’s growth. When O2 showed up life exploded.
5.  Efficient mitochondria carry out the oxidative metabolism and lots of them in the brain.
6.  Phospholipids and the special chemistry of lipids in aqueous milieu (brain and spinal cord sit in a bag of CSF fluid)
7.  Cell membranes respond to diet, temperature and to pressure changes epigenetically. Since humans are homeothermic, our neural lipid epigenetic variables are diet and environment dependent. These features allow biochemistry to specifically affect the acyl groups on our lipid layers in the brain for optimal cell signaling. You might be a great biochemist or organic chemist but if you do not know neural biochemistry you might not be aware of just how much you do not know.
8.  Proteins receptors are inserted in these special lipids to signal cells chemically and electrically. This means lipids and proteins have to be multidimensional in configuration and must thermodynamically matched to the lipids in our membranes. DHA is that “**magic lipid**” that does all of this. You don’t find that information in fossils or in bones on the land.

# Thread: Anyone with a good beef liver recipe?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-04-23T18:41:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123209>

Yesterday I finally bought grassed beef liver and now I am looking for a recipe to use it. Absolutely, 100% fully cooked!!!! Still not sure why I even bought it in the first place because I hate liver with passion. ....

Hope that this time it would be different somehow - I never thought that I would eat any seafood either, but now it is a different story....

### Andrea — 2014-04-23T18:48:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123211>

I like Heston Blumenthal's "Meat fruit": [http://cumbriafoodie.wordpress.com/2011/02/27/meat-fruit-making-a-heston-favourite-at-home/](http://cumbriafoodie.wordpress.com/2011/02/27/meat-fruit-making-a-heston-favourite-at-home/)
The recipe says chicken liver, but it works just as fine with any mammalian liver.

I suppose a more simple liver pâté could work too...

### Tanya — 2014-04-23T19:31:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123217>

Thanks, Rerun. It really looks good, but I don't think I am quite ready to incorporate all the ingredients listed there (eggs, vine, brandy), and without them, I am afraid it will not be the same....

### Andrea — 2014-04-23T20:50:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123222>

A more simple suggestion - if you're ok with dairy.

A plain, simple liver mousse/pâté
400g chicken/lamb/beef liver
butter
200 ml heavy cream
spices/herbs

Cut the liver in small pieces. Sear it in plenty of butter until it's done. Add salt & pepper and other herbs or spices (I used to add thyme and garlic when I did this).
Mix the liver in a mixer, together with the cream and some extra butter.
Pour the mixture into a sealable mold - it will be runny at this point, but will set in the fridge - and let set.

### raysblue — 2014-04-23T21:02:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123223>

This is a simple! From Nigel Slater: Warm some butter in a shallow pan. Lay 4 slices of liver in the pan. Fry for a couple of minutes on each side (toasty brown edges are fine , but inside should still be rose pink) . Remove to a warm plate and pour a small glass of malbec red wine into the pan. Bubble stir and scrape then reduce by half over a high heat. Stir in 4 tablespoons of course paleo apple and onion chutney. Slide the liver in and serve. Enjoy!

### Tanya — 2014-04-23T22:13:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123232>

Fry for a couple of minutes on each side (toasty brown edges are fine , but inside should still be rose pink) . Remove to a warm plate and pour a small glass of malbec red wine into the pan. Bubble stir and scrape then reduce by half over a high heat. Stir in 4 tablespoons of course paleo apple and onion chutney. Slide the liver in and serve. Enjoy!

Thank you, raysblue

### Tanya — 2014-04-23T22:15:41+0300

This is more doable, I think... If I substitute butter with ghee and heavy cream with coconut milk, I hope that it still might resemble the original version of it. Thanks again, Rerun

### nicld — 2014-04-24T03:58:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123263>

I make liver tacos. Take the liver and slice it thin, fry it in some oil and lots of chipotle and jalepenos. Makes it fairly tolerable and the DH LOVES it and he loves liver. I just use lettuce as the wraps.

I have also cooked it, chopped it up fairly fine and added it to my "earthy" soup. It's made with bone broth, cabbage, rutabaga, carrots and onions. Also add some ground beef to it.

Oh and my hats off to those who can eat it raw, tried it this weekend and not for me.

### Tanya — 2014-04-24T17:26:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123317>

>

>
> Oh and my hats off to those who can eat it raw, tried it this weekend and not for me.

I like the soup idea - I can probably disguise just about anything in it...lol I still must avoid, however, chipotle and jalapeños - It must be frustrating to try to help someone with all these food limitations.... Thank you so much for the recipes, Nicole

### Marie — 2014-04-24T18:31:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123325>

Soak 1 lb of calf liver overnight in milk.

Saute covered on low heat:
4-6 cups (or more) of chopped white onions
1-2 TBS chopped garlic
A dash of salt
1 TBS cinnamon (Yes this sounds like a lot)

Rinse liver removing traces of milk
Chop the liver into 1 inch cubes

When the onions are glassy, add the liver cubes.
Cover and simmer on low heat for 5-7 mins, stirring occasionally.

The result should be "sweet" with a subtle liver flavor.

### Andrea — 2014-04-24T18:48:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123328>

> [Marie said:](/goto/post?id=123325)
>
> Soak 1 lb of calf liver overnight in milk.

I suppose the milk will remove some of the flavor. Good one!
Cinnamon with liver? Interesting! Will try that next week.

Tanya, in case you still would feel that you just can't eat the liver, there's one "last resort".
Run it through a food processor and blend it with minced meat (or run some meat through the food processor as well) - 50/50 ought to do. Then make hamburgers or meat balls of the mix, adding an egg if necessary to keep the mix together. You could add some onion and spices to the mince as well.

### Tanya — 2014-04-24T22:05:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123343>

Thank you for all the ideas, Rerun and Marie. At this point, I should be able to whip up something.....However, without using any dairy, nightshades or eggs, it will probably resemble more of a mad scientist project then a gourmet cooking

### Marie — 2014-04-25T01:14:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123368>

> [Rerun said:](/goto/post?id=123328)
>
> I suppose the milk will remove some of the flavor. Good one!
> Cinnamon with liver? Interesting! Will try that next week.

Yes, the milk soak seems to kill 80% of the offensive "liver" flavor. It does take quite a bit of rinsing to get rid of the milk, so be forewarned. The cinnamon gives it a Mediterranean spin.

This is the only way I've been able to eat liver.

### JMO — 2014-04-25T03:39:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123369>

Here's a good recipe for liver - [http://thehealthyfoodie.com/beef-liver-with-fig-bacon-and-caramelized-onion-compote/](http://thehealthyfoodie.com/beef-liver-with-fig-bacon-and-caramelized-onion-compote/). I use to gag when I first ate liver, now it's not bad. I use bubbies horeseradish in a little whipped cream (no sugar--just whip the cream) or sour cream if I cook just liver with onions--all you can taste is horseradish--which I love! I soak it in some lemon juice before cooking---don't cook too long or it gets rubbery.

Here's a tip for eating raw liver. I get some grassfed liver which comes frozen. I let it unthaw slightly until I can cut it in bite size pieces. Then I refreeze all the pieces then put them in a bowl in my freezer so I can take out one piece at a time. I let it slightly unthaw then I bite down on it a couple times and swallow followed by a big drink of water. Dont let it unthaw too much or you have that gross liver texture. Just a bite a day, by the end of the week I prob had an entire piece of raw liver.

### sooperb — 2014-04-28T21:16:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123744>

Liver works well with oranges. Slice the oranges and fry with onions, garlic etc. add the liver and a little oj and cook till it's as you like it. The original recipe calls for the orange to be dipped in sugar so either use a little or substitute using honey or similar or just leave out.

### Tanya — 2014-04-28T22:47:11+0300

Thanks Sue - I'll give it a try... I actually made a pate this morning - used lots of spices and garlic.... It came out pretty good, if I may say so myself. I will not hesitate to make it again...

# Thread: St. George's Day
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/st-georges-day.10633/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-23T19:30:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/st-georges-day.10633/post-123216>

Here's something for St. George's Day.

St. George is the patron saint of England, and also, according to Wikipedia, of

> Georgia, Egypt, Bulgaria, Aragon, Catalonia, Romania, Ethiopia, Greece, India, Iraq, Lebanon, Lithuania, Palestine, Portugal, Serbia, Macedonia, Ukraine, Russia and Syria, as well as the cities of Genoa, Amersfoort, Beirut, Botoşani, Drobeta Turnu-Severin, Timişoara, Fakiha, Bteghrine, Cáceres, Ferrara, Freiburg im Breisgau, Kragujevac, Kumanovo, Ljubljana, Pérouges, Pomorie, Preston, Qormi, Rio de Janeiro, Lydda, Lviv, Barcelona, Moscow and Victoria, as well as of the Scout Movement and a wide range of professions, organizations and disease sufferers.

Wow! He's got his work cut out.

The day's not much celebrated over here, perhaps because English people tend to take the view that if you're enthusiastic about something it's proof that you're not really sincere. But … well … we all have dragons to kill, so maybe the day is worth celebrating.

Here's a icon of St. George for the Russians:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

And how about some music, too? Here's William Blake's immortal poem _Jerusalem_ set to music by Sir Hubert Parry.

The subject of the poem is "Did Christ come to Glastonbury?" as the legend had it. The poem is also a kind of protest against the ugliness and inhumanity of early industrialisation in England -- "dark Satanic mills". (I believe Blake was thinking of an actual factory building in a part of London he knew.) Perhaps we might think of those currently building on Greenbelt land and applying to uproot Ancient Woodland or polluting the soil.

Here's the text:

> And did those feet in ancient time
> Walk upon England's mountains green?
> And was the holy Lamb of God
> On England's pleasant pastures seen?
>
> And did the Countenance Divine
> Shine forth upon our clouded hills?
> And was Jerusalem builded here
> Among these dark Satanic mills?
>
> Bring me my bow of burning gold:
> Bring me my arrows of desire:
> Bring me my spear: O clouds unfold!
> Bring me my chariot of fire.
>
> I will not cease from mental fight,
> Nor shall my sword sleep in my hand
> Till we have built Jerusalem
> In England's green and pleasant land.

[http://rpo.library.utoronto.ca/poems/milton-and-did-those-feet-ancient-time](http://rpo.library.utoronto.ca/poems/milton-and-did-those-feet-ancient-time)
