# Thread: A look back
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-look-back.10831/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nicld — 2014-05-14T16:48:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-look-back.10831/post-126891>

So, I have been talking a lot about weight issues so I thought it would be good to reflect on the positive changes that have happened with the health the past year.

1\. Sleep has improved dramatically. It no longer takes me hours to fall asleep and I no longer wake at 2 am and cannot fall back asleep.
2\. Don’t have to pee 3x during the middle of the night.
3\. Movements have become so regular. Every morning between 7-8 am and no more C. A little loose but Dr. T is not concerned.
4\. Skin looks amazeballs. I do have to say that I think I do not look almost 41.
5\. My nails and hair are also fabulous. The nails are getting so long (as long as I don’t break them on something), even the toe nails.
6\. I think my eyesight is improving. I HATE wearing my glasses now.
7\. I am no longer a freeze baby.
8\. The brain fog is getting better but still not where is was 10 years ago, but getting there.
9\. Actually waking up and being hungry. I was never one to be hungry in the morning.
10\. Cycles are getting better. Still shorter but I am no longer doubled over in pain for the first day.
11\. My food choices have expanded. Who would have thought that I would be eating oysters and raw beef.
12\. My negative thoughts about my body have got better, still there a bit, but not all consuming anymore.
13\. The sex life has improves 500%. Not sure if the hubby is all for this as he says that he is no longer 20. I keep telling him that we can fix that but he just won’t listen.

I still have things to work on, the gut first, then weight. Sleep can still be better and I can always improve my EMF situations. But overall, I am not too bad.

A huge thanks to Dr. Kruse for shining the light.

### kovita — 2014-05-14T19:28:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-look-back.10831/post-126913>

Amazing, Nicld! This is a lot! Many of your points I can copy and paste to my journal, including the eye sight and night trips to the toilet! Keep up the good work! Eventually evrything in your body settles in to the right place. It is just matter of time and trust in yourself! I truly believe it!

### Inger — 2014-05-14T19:33:15+0300

Great stuff - i am with Kovita! Many of your improvements are mine too

### caroline — 2014-05-15T02:33:20+0300

# Thread: CT and TCM, some thoughts
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-tcm-some-thoughts.10856/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-05-17T03:01:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-tcm-some-thoughts.10856/post-127280>

Q: What are your thoughts about CT and using it year round and its effects on yang etc from a TCM perspective? Would TCM thought say CT is too much cold?

Me: This is not a small subject as we need to consider how whoever observed and codified this in Oriental Medicine arrived at their conclusions and under what COE's? Dr. Kruse has ostensibly gone further back to uncover a counter intuitive mechanism that is buried in our genome....the difference between what is codified and what may be or have been practiced by adepts in Taoism and Buddhism is another story....Tumo is one example of this. Consider that no one had heat in Asia and many still do not. Cold is a natural part of many days depending upon where you live. I have lived in a mountain village in India, so I have firsthand experience.

We need to start with considering which functions "yang" represents and how and if they are degraded by a given dose of CT. For example, the Zeltiq therapy kills the fat cells at the right dose and will destroy all of the tissue and potentially kill the person at a higher dose....everything is N=1 and dose dependent.....This is why Oriental medicine is essentially and N=1 system with tools to diff/dx and treat accordingly...

So how the medicine tailored to COE's 300-1500 years or more ago translates into modern COE's is not as simple as it is sold.....

Consider whether CT at the right dose optimizes "Yang" as it optimizes cell volume, collagen and electron/qi(?) flow and preservation.....

Q: Yes very good point about the mechanism in our genome, I hadn't considered that. Do you find using TCM (or any other alternative approach) has become way more effective with minimizing exposure to an altered field?

Me: TCM is a fairly complete model based upon an agglomeration of schools and systems based on similar but not identical concepts. A lot was left out in the codification as well. It is very useful for assessing where and how someone is out of balance and affected by stressors. It is perhaps the most complete alternative model and it requires a good deal of skill and understanding to apply it effectively to all cases. As always, the stronger people with good qi/redox respond well and it gets more challenging as one moves away from this population. The simple answer is the better your redox/qi is, the better one maintains metastability in the face of resonant/energtetic/magnetic/EMF disturbances....

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-05-17T13:04:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-tcm-some-thoughts.10856/post-127324>

this is probably why in ancient china one would fire his physician if he got sick.
the redox was good so they responded accordingly. i believe when one is very sick TCM would be less effective, or at least take much more work, as you have laid out here.

# Thread: How DHA reaches the brain (study)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-dha-reaches-the-brain-study.10860/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2014-05-17T14:04:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-dha-reaches-the-brain-study.10860/post-127329>

[http://m.medicalxpress.com/news/2014-05-dha-omega-fatty-acid-brain.html](http://m.medicalxpress.com/news/2014-05-dha-omega-fatty-acid-brain.html)

### Josh — 2014-05-17T21:58:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-dha-reaches-the-brain-study.10860/post-127391>

> Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) is an omega-3 fatty acid that is essential for normal brain growth and cognitive function[1](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature13241.html#ref1), [2](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature13241.html#ref2), [3](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature13241.html#ref3), [4](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature13241.html#ref4). Consistent with its importance in the brain, DHA is highly enriched in brain phospholipids[5](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature13241.html#ref5), [6](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature13241.html#ref6), [7](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature13241.html#ref7). Despite being an abundant fatty acid in brain phospholipids, DHA cannot be _de novo_ synthesized in brain and must be imported across the blood–brain barrier, but mechanisms for DHA uptake in brain have remained enigmatic. Here we identify a member of the major facilitator superfamily—Mfsd2a (previously an orphan transporter)—as the major transporter for DHA uptake into brain. Mfsd2a is found to be expressed exclusively in endothelium of the blood–brain barrier of micro-vessels. Lipidomic analysis indicates that _Mfsd2a_\-deficient (_Mfsd2a_\-knockout) mice show markedly reduced levels of DHA in brain accompanied by neuronal cell loss in hippocampus and cerebellum, as well as cognitive deficits and severe anxiety, and microcephaly. Unexpectedly, cell-based studies indicate that Mfsd2a transports DHA in the form of lysophosphatidylcholine (LPC), but not unesterified fatty acid, in a sodium-dependent manner. Notably, Mfsd2a transports common plasma LPCs carrying long-chain fatty acids such LPC oleate and LPC palmitate, but not LPCs with less than a 14-carbon acyl chain. Moreover, we determine that the phosphor-zwitterionic headgroup of LPC is critical for transport. Importantly, _Mfsd2a_\-knockout mice have markedly reduced uptake of labelled LPC DHA, and other LPCs, from plasma into brain, demonstrating that Mfsd2a is required for brain uptake of DHA. Our findings reveal an unexpected essential physiological role of plasma-derived LPCs in brain growth and function.

[http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature13241.html](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature13241.html)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Kinky Transhuman Biohacking...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kinky-transhuman-biohacking.10865/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-05-17T21:22:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kinky-transhuman-biohacking.10865/post-127385>

I wonder if he knows about CT?...

Rowland’s latest project, however, does not rely on training, prosthetic additions, or physical modification. Instead, it could be described as dietary bio-hacking. By cutting [retinal](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retinal) (part of the Vitamin A complex) out of his diet, he is hoping to force his body to[develop near-infrared vision](http://scienceforthemasses.org/infrared-project/).

It is — if it works — a dietary anti-supplement that bestows sensory superpowers.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

IMAGE: Electromagnetic spectrum via the [Universities Space Research Association.](http://www.lpi.usra.edu/education/fieldtrips/2005/activities/ir_spectrum/)

The strategy is inspired by tilapia (and other freshwater fish), which use a different form of Vitamin A, [dehydroretinal](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dehydroretinal), to bind with their[opsins](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opsin) (the retina-based proteins that turn light into an electrochemical signal). In humans, opsins preferentially bind with retinal to form rhodopsin; in freshwater fish, it’s more common for dehydroretinal and opsin combine to form porphyropsin. The difference between the two light-sensitive visual pigments is small at the blue end of the spectrum, but porphyropsin is progressively more sensitive than rhodopsin at longer wavelengths.

The result is that freshwater fish can spot tasty infrared-reflective shrimp in murky lakes and streams, and humans cannot.

Because the human body is capable of metabolising and using dehydroretinal, if forced, [Peyton Rowlands and his Science for the Masses colleagues](https://experiment.com/projects/can-we-biologically-extend-the-range-of-human-vision-into-the-near-infrared) have reasoned that a diet that cuts out all retinal but contains dehydroretinal will enable them to see a tiny bit beyond normal human vision, into the near edge of the infrared band.".....

Not everyone is so sure that dietary superpowers can be so easily achieved, [arguing](http://hplusbiopolitics.wordpress.com/2008/09/15/therma-vision/) (both persuasively and poetically) that the eye will only see itself — that a device built of warm-blooded cells will blind itself with its own thermal signature. There’s also a concern that, whether or not humans can develop the physical ability to detect IR in our environment, we can’t evolve the neural structures to translate it into a comprehensible visual signal quickly enough.

On the other hand, Tibbets points out that he and his bio-hacking colleagues are merely following in the footsteps of the United States Navy, which apparently already successfully tried this experiment on sailors during World War II.

They fed their sailor guinea-pigs with a diet low in normal sources of Vitamin A but supplemented with the livers of walleyed pikes, and, according to [_Perception_](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0072887605/ref=as_li_qf_sp_asin_il_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=0072887605&linkCode=as2&tag=ediblgeogr-20), a psychology textbook, “over several months, the volunteers’ vision changed, giving them greater sensitivity to light of longer wavelengths.” "

[http://www.ediblegeography.com/dietary-superpowers/](http://www.ediblegeography.com/dietary-superpowers/)

### nonchalant — 2014-05-18T00:45:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kinky-transhuman-biohacking.10865/post-127407>

But Josh, we're just trying to stave off age-related macular degeneration...
Actually it's an interesting idea. But considering the variety of uses for vitamin A for in the brain and body, sounds risky. Sure, you might be able to see infrared now, but how's your sleep?

1) What is the best price-performance device for measuring EMF? I found two devices on Amazon under $100/each, but I have no idea if they are good enough:

[http://www.amazon.com/Lutron-822-A-Fully-Digital-Resolution/dp/B0015ENO6W](http://www.amazon.com/Lutron-822-A-Fully-Digital-Resolution/dp/B0015ENO6W)
[http://www.amazon.com/Extech-480823-Electromagnetic-Extremely-Frequency/dp/B00023RXDC](http://www.amazon.com/Extech-480823-Electromagnetic-Extremely-Frequency/dp/B00023RXDC)

Some meters include in addition to a magnetism / gauss meter, and RF power meter. How important is it to have both functions?

2) In terms of measuring grounding, what kind of instruments can measure a voltage differential from a surface to true ground? Obviously you could put a voltmeter on the surface of a bed sheet, and the other end of the voltmeter to a true ground. That isn't accurate however. For such a reading to be accurate you want a device that can measure the electrical potential over a broad surface area, not a spot measurement at one point on the bed. I think this is the same issue that is encountered when you build a laboratory and need to electrically ground the environment - including flooring - to prevent electrostatic shock. The devices they use to establish the floor is at ground measure broad surface areas and are not spot measurements.

If I haven't totally lost people here, does anyone make such a device for measuring surface to ground potential at a price that humans can afford? Any referrals to specific products are appreciated.

### nicld — 2014-05-19T03:24:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instruments-for-measuring-emf.10869/post-127496>

You can rent a package from Michael Neuart. He did the EMF bootcamp here and it was really helpful and informative.

[http://emfcenter.com/metrsale.htm](http://emfcenter.com/metrsale.htm)

### nonchalant — 2014-05-19T04:18:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instruments-for-measuring-emf.10869/post-127511>

Persistence, magnetism appears to be more of a problem to people than voltage. And a meter that does not measure RF completely misses problems with those higher frequencies, such as microwaves.

A body voltage meter, made from a regular voltage meter, would measure grounding over a large area.

### Arborescence — 2015-04-26T21:41:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/instruments-for-measuring-emf.10869/post-162482>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=127511)
>
> Persistence, magnetism appears to be more of a problem to people than voltage. And a meter that does not measure RF completely misses problems with those higher frequencies, such as microwaves.
>
> A body voltage meter, made from a regular voltage meter, would measure grounding over a large area.

What is RF? @[nonchalant](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/191/)

### Penny — 2015-04-27T06:35:21+0300

Best not to panic. Only an issue if they are expressing.

Look back at your medical history. Do you have any recent labs with results for homocysteine, vitamin D level, progesterone/estrogen ratio, phase 1 or two detox, etc?

Drink good water, eat epipaleo, do the best in your environment to limit emf, embrace cold, and ground outside in sunshine (everthing we've learned here).

You can always do a Dr. Tim consult, but he likes to review other labs and history in conjunction with the 23andme.

Bottom line is that although 23andme is helpful there are many other factors to consider...

### JasonB — 2014-07-07T19:55:41+0300

I've been fascinate about it for a long time but only made a couple of pieces. What I didn't realize is that they should be recharged periodically. Until I get the hang of making it will I know one way or another.

Where I think orgonite and what JK crossover is with tensegrity. The resin one uses for the orgonite compresses on the metal and crystal in the resin to create a piezo-electrical charge and that is what's being "gifted" to neutralize the positive ions in the vicinity. This reminds me of Tensegrity 1, I think. The blog that talks about the matrix within bone and the piezo signal within.

Or I could be stretching the analogy a bit, but it seems to make sense down at the micro-quanta level.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-11T11:20:13+0300

### JasonB — 2014-07-11T16:10:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orgonite.10876/post-133933>

One thing that's made me skeptical about orgonite is thing whole thing about por (positive orgonite) and nor (negative orgonite). In this case negative being the bad energy (can we assume unnatural EMPs?). In addition if you're buying commercially, avoid Don Croft as he's supposedly a shyster. Then you have the people who say all orgonite is bad for you eventually and need an orgone generator (plugs in the wall or is magnet based) which always generates positive orgone. I've not tried it so can't say one way or another if those claims are true.

It's hard to tell with the orgonite community because some people come across as plain kooky so it takes away from the valuable information. I think Reich was on to something with orgonite. Unfortunately the USFDA deemed him a fraud, banned his orgone accumulators and burned six tons of his books and research.

I need to attempt to make more. My first attempt was really ugly and I likely did it wrong. I took some copper pan scrubbers, cut them up to put in the resin, but did the pieces too big and put too much in so there are bits of copper sticking out from the bottom of the puck. Oops. I call a re-do.

### Debntx — 2017-03-19T00:01:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orgonite.10876/post-210620>

i went through a phase of being really interested in orgonite (the objects) for some reason - i did buy some jewelry pieces and pyramids and some little "pucks" to put around the house - mainly i thought the jewelry was "pretty" and i still have it and love wearing it mainly because someone made it and its like art to me - it is an art - one which i never studied but love the results (art) - i love the idea of wearing crystal minerals metals all together - or putting stuff on a pretty charge plate to collect "good" before i send it out to the world - dreamy right?

the idea of changing energy by thought - in goes bad / out comes good - very unscientific , dreamy but that was what my thinking was at the time - a transmutation of sorts

# Thread: NOSH Aspirin?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosh-aspirin.10881/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-05-21T00:27:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosh-aspirin.10881/post-127731>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23918470](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23918470)

> Hydrogen sulfide (H2 S) and nitric oxide (NO) have been described as gasotransmitters. Anti-inflammatory activity in the central and peripheral nervous systems may be one of their functions. Previously we demonstrated that several SH(-) donors including H2 S-releasing aspirin (S-ASA) exhibited anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective activity in vitro against toxins released by activated microglia and astrocytes. Here we report that NOSH-ASA, an NO- and H2 S-releasing hybrid of aspirin, has a significantly greater anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effect than S-ASA or NO-ASA. When activated by LPS/IFNγ, human microglia and THP-1 cells release materials that are toxic to differentiated SH-SY5Y cells. These phenomena also occur with IFNγ-stimulated human astroglia and U373 cells. When the cells were treated with the S-ASA or NO-ASA, there was a significant enhancement of neuroprotection. However, NOSH-ASA had significantly more potent protection properties than NO-ASA or S-ASA. The effect was concentration-dependent, as well as incubation time-dependent. Such treatment not only reduced the release of the TNFα and IL-6, but also attenuated activation of P38 MAPK and NFκB proteins. All the compounds tested were not harmful when applied directly to SH-SY5Y cells. These data suggest that NOSH-ASA has significant anti-inflammatory properties and may be a new candidate for treating neurodegenerative disorders that have a prominent neuroinflammatory component such as Alzheimer disease and Parkinson disease.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-22T02:37:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ofs-3.10884/post-127898>

This quote from the blog was a good summary for me, and the bolded portion is a good terse explanation for why it's water and proteins that matter, and how proteins exhibit all sorts of different behaviour based upon the contingent conditions that they find themselves in.

It's also a statement that is pretty optimistic about the our pursuit toward optimal health

> **The second law of thermodynamics is not an absolute law, but a statistical one that we can improve upon or destroy**. It is akin to the the space between 0 and 1 in a quantum computer. The end points are well defined, but the space in the middle of them can keep being subdivided into smaller segments until infinity is reached. This is why there is Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle. Nothing in the universe can ever reach pure hell or heaven……we can just approach it. It confused Einstein for all his life, but it is firmly quantum and parsimonious when you understand what I have written here.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/succinic-acid-and-lactic-acid-metabolic-analysis-profile.10886/post-127802>

I had a Metabolic Analysis profile done and my succinic and were shown to be very low. No value was given as it was so low, so I guess it was almost undetectable. Any idea what this might mean practically and what clinical significance it may have?

# Thread: Clams?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.10902/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AnnieW — 2014-05-22T22:06:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.10902/post-127994>

Hey guys, I picked up some chopped clams to have something quick to eat. The only recipes I can find for frying are ones that include a ton of breading.

Can someone give me advice on cook time and type of fat to use? I've never cooked clams at home so this will be a (hopefully tasty) adventure.

### fitness@home — 2014-05-22T22:24:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.10902/post-127995>

I have tossed them in coconut flour and then fried in bacon fat. Even better is you add crumbled bacon to the bowl before eating.

### AnnieW — 2014-05-22T22:29:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.10902/post-127996>

Thanks! Do you know about how long you cook them? Is that on high heat or medium? I am notorious for over-cooking seafood so I don't want to mess these up.

### Lahelada — 2014-05-22T22:31:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.10902/post-127998>

Chop garlic and a hot chili,fry it in oil until translucent add clams and cook until done. If you have no chile add spicy paprik or hot sauce just before end of cooking

### AnnieW — 2014-05-22T22:46:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.10902/post-128001>

Dumb question...how do I know they are done?

### Lahelada — 2014-05-22T23:33:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.10902/post-128004>

No idea but this man knows
[http://www.manandmollusc.net/molluscan\_food\_files/clam-preparation.html](http://www.manandmollusc.net/molluscan_food_files/clam-preparation.html)

### AnnieW — 2014-05-22T23:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.10902/post-128007>

Thanks for the link!

### Lahelada — 2014-05-22T23:59:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.10902/post-128009>

Welcome! It was a useful find for me too. Clams and mussels are on my shopping list tomorrow.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-film-education.10906/post-128076>

In my last job a young lad of twenty something admitted to only having watched a dozen or so films...So we spent much time making fun him and his lack of film 'exposure' but we also recommended our favourite films and must see films before we die..

I have watched hundreds of films over the years and am off to bed now but some off the top of my head as being personal favourites are below. I have also watched many non english films that I have found the most rewarding. The other night I watched the Warrior but perhaps my favourite film is the Swedish film Everlasting moments

I am like Audrey.... I practically never watch movies anymore....... since i found Jack, even less..... and i never was a big movie watcher. But now.. I dread them!

I need to live my life live t be happy...

how can people have time to watch many movies... I do not understand.. I am sorry

I have become incredibly busy with life somehow  even if it is only sitting on the grass on the pasture drinking a glass of wine

about 2 movies / year is as far as I go - then my movie tank is filled  That said, I have lately have had years with not one single movie fit in...  i now read Jacks blogs over any movie any day. How weird. They are far more interesting. Maybe I have gone nuts

or maybe it is because my brain is so damaged it takes so much effort to read his blogs so there is nothing left for movies...

# Thread: cheese, butter, and Kefir from Bas Taurus cow
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheese-butter-and-kefir-from-bas-taurus-cow.10909/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2014-05-23T21:56:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheese-butter-and-kefir-from-bas-taurus-cow.10909/post-128123>

Hello, it has been kind of stressful to find Bas Indicus cow milk, butter, etc.

I was wondering........ cheese, butter, and Kefir made from the Bas Taurus cow that are grass fed.....the processing of the milk to create the cheese, etc...will that destroy the negative stuff that is associated with the milk that is from the Bas Taurus cow?

### x_danny_x — 2014-05-23T22:30:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheese-butter-and-kefir-from-bas-taurus-cow.10909/post-128126>

Nevermind I figure out the answer....seems butter is okay to get from the Bos Taurus cow but everything else is not since the A1 Casein Protein BCM7 will still be present in Kefir, and even cheese. Butter contains little protein.

### Hope — 2014-05-24T00:32:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheese-butter-and-kefir-from-bas-taurus-cow.10909/post-128137>

Have you tried goat kefir?

# Thread: Grounding/Earthing Doesn't Work......
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-earthing-doesnt-work.10924/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HuntIII — 2014-05-26T02:57:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-earthing-doesnt-work.10924/post-128370>

Made you look

I do think grounding or earthing works. BUT....I recently read an article in which the physicist who is interview says any electrons picked up from the earth will REMAIN ON THE SKIN making a positive health
effect UNLIKELY.

Here's the article: [http://online.wsj.com/news/articles/SB10001424052702304652804579572072977644460](http://online.wsj.com/news/articles/SB10001424052702304652804579572072977644460)

Is this true that the electrons just stay on your skin or do they actually go deeper?

Does anyone know of a physicist who says earthing has benefits?

### nicld — 2014-05-26T17:09:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-earthing-doesnt-work.10924/post-128408>

I can't say anything about if it scientifically work but all I know is that I feel way better when I get to spend the day outside digging in the dirt barefoot. I would be surprised if there is a physicist who believes in it since Jack has said that very few physicist make the connection to biology.

### nonchalant — 2014-05-26T19:31:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-earthing-doesnt-work.10924/post-128418>

I seem to recall Jack saying that once a threshold is reached, the large charges will move. Either from the earth to the body, or, perhaps more often, from the body to the earth. Probably the tickling feeling we often get.

For the small negative charge from the earth, your tissues have to be ready to move the charge. Semiconductors charged and ready. Redox potential up.

### sjoshua — 2014-06-25T03:37:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-earthing-doesnt-work.10924/post-132110>

Thought experiment:

If you try to plug an American lamp into a UK outlet, you will run into problems. First off, the plug is a different shape, and will not interface. Second, the frequency of AC currents varies throughout the world, so the 60Hz American lamp may not appreciate the 50Hz UK frequency. Third, the voltage output varies as well, so the 120v American lamp would not operate on the 230v UK circuits.

Hopefully this mismatch provides you with a rough example of why humans have difficulty with the concept of grounding. When you do not match the environment you are in, then you cannot expect the power source to interface properly. So when humans don't match the environment of the earth, then even if the ability to ground is there, its like trying to plug the American lamp into the UK socket... Interface properly, and you can recharge at will.

# Thread: Tiny Bubbles
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-bubbles.10926/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2014-05-26T21:43:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-bubbles.10926/post-128426>

Tiny bubbles everywhere! Thousands of them! And more are forming by the minute! What to do??
Sorry, getting ahead of myself. Here's the story:

Thanks to DH, and the oversight of the previous owner, I now have an outdoor CT tank out in the country. It is thick metal, round, and looks to be a cover for some sort of machinery. We were told it was originally intended to be a watering trough, but nothing ever happened with it. DH pulled it out of the ground, where it was mostly buried, upside down. He brushed off the rust, and painted it. It makes a perfect tub, large enough for two. It has a nice lip on the edge, great for resting the head, or sitting. The depth is perfect for me.

We put it in a spot that was half shade, half sun. Filled it with water from our wonderful deep well. So nice for CT. The water wasn't real cold like for regular CT, but it still gave me goosebumps. Felt fabulous. I lay there, soaking up the sun for a bit, then I noticed lots of bubbles on my arms. Or the hairs on my arms, actually. Some hairs had a string of bubbles on them; I could count 5 to 8 bubbles on some hairs. After more time, looking at my shoulder and upper arm, there was a forest of bubbles. So cool! And they clung fairly tightly. I could swish an arm or leg gently in the water, and most would still cling, though others let go and floated to the surface. Bubbles also clung down the walls of the tank.

Of course I thought of Pollack & Kruse & Ling. All those EZ droplets, sending me energy. No, I couldn't feel anything specific. The next day I bathed in the same water from the day before. The water was definitely warmer. Goosebumps were subdued. The sunshine was about the same, partly cloudy. The bubbles were diminished. But since the hairs weren't clogged with bubbles, I could see that yes, there were bubbles on my skin as well. Minuscule ones! Made my skin look shiny. With patience, I did grow some strings of bubbles on some hairs. Bubbles on the tank walls again.

The third day, I kept the same water again. Partly cloudy again, same temp as the day before, as far as I could tell, and lots more bugs and debris, lol. Goosebumps subdued again. Bubbles were even slower forming than the day before. Finally got some sheen to the skin, which were bubbles upon close inspection. Only a few bubbles on hairs. Bubbles only on a couple of inches of tank wall just below the surface.

My first guess is that it isn't the water temperature that is the variable, but the "age" of the water. When it is drawn fresh from the well, I think it is more energetic. But I think, in the interests of science, I need to float away more time to figure this all out. Who knows, maybe its the tiny bugs?

Also, if we are able to get energy in this manner, through the hairs, is that a reason why the arm and leg hair is generally thicker the further it is from the brain? Maybe our hair serves as a DC voltage amplifier?

I think pebbles would add more nucleation sites, to allow bubbles to stick to their surfaces. I'm also thinking shipwrecks and other ocean debris also provides hydrophilic surfaces, to attract structured water, which is what initially draws life. Not just protection or a place to attach to.

### nonchalant — 2014-05-27T04:48:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-bubbles.10926/post-128483>

I'm trying to link The Body Electric (DC current) and The Fourth Phase of Water (EZ battery). I've lent out my Becker book, but Pollack's is back in my possession. I know the DC current comes from the brain, but can it be helped along? CT, EZ water, sunlight, skin, hair, collagen...

In OSF3, Jack said

> The continued flow of this DC electron current induces the water hydration shell that surrounds collagen. This electronic induction maximizes collagen’s water binding sites on its triple helix simultaneously without any use of exogenous energy. It uses the electric power in the quanta of the electron to make collagen hydrophilic so water binds to it maximally. It does this by making collagen more negatively charged so it gets along better with water. This allows water to naturally bind to collagen everywhere it is found in a cell. When water meets collagen, its charge naturally separates, as shown by Gerald Pollack’s recent work. This natural charge separation of water occurs with no energy used.

If nothing else, perhaps the tiny bubbles are busy charging up collagen, at least in the skin and hair follicles. Tighten up those triple helixes! Go water go!

### nonchalant — 2014-05-27T15:33:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-bubbles.10926/post-128560>

> [ssj3 said:](/goto/post?id=128554)
>
> Thought process... gases captured in the water form a hydrophobic surface with the water (bubble) and the cooled water increases the area of stability and structure. And since the tub since it is connected to the earth it is collecting negative electrons further increasing the structured EZ.

It's impossible to visually tell the difference between a bubble and an underwater droplet. Pollack's book tells of the ways they used to determine that the "vesicles" were not bubbles. They punctured them with a sharp probe, and usually it had no effect on the droplets. They also squeezed and pulled them, but the droplets regained their original shape quickly. They even cut some in half, but the droplet instantly reformed.

He said both bubbles and droplets have EZ 'membranes'. And he said that the structured water inside droplets can undergo a phase transition as pressure changes (like when I move my leg from the bottom of the tank to near the surface) or as they absorb more sunshine (or body heat?), and become a gas bubble. Phase transition -- Jack's favorite term! I do think the metal tank grounding may have something to do with all the bubbles. And water straight from the well definitely could have dissolved gasses.

You have given me an idea, ssj3. Next time we go out to the country, I should bring a needle with me to the tank. Do my own experiment with the larger bubbles.

### nonchalant — 2014-06-10T19:51:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-bubbles.10926/post-130383>

This weekend I got a chance to play with bubbles again. This time I took my needle!
When the bubbles got big enough, I tried puncturing them with the needle. Not as easy as it sounds. They evaded the needle, moving to the side, or releasing from the skin to float to the surface (lower density). Tough membrane. I found that approaching them from above was the way to go. I could not pop any of the bubbles. The needle just went inside with little reaction from the droplet. Removing the needle also had no effect. I also managed to slice into them, using the side of the needle. No popping, no dividing into two.

This time I also tried smashing some that were attached the inside of my elbow using quite a bit of force. Tough little buggers.
DH joined me for a while. When I pointed out all these neat features of the bubbles, he just looked at me and said "You must not have much to do while soaking in the tank."

### nonchalant — 2014-06-10T23:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-bubbles.10926/post-130416>

### nonchalant — 2014-07-01T04:53:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-bubbles.10926/post-132796>

A few days ago I held a hair underwater in my tank. It was about a foot long. Eventually bubbles formed along its entire length, and they grew in size as time passed. Looked like a string of pearls.  I swished the hair around, being careful not to let it touch itself, and the bubbles clung tightly. Then I let the end of the hair (an inch or two) float up to the surface. The bubbles (approximately 10 of them) stayed where they were, just below the surface, still attached to the hair. Then I pulled the hair back down, and those bubbles let go and stayed under the surface, and soon popped. Finally I stretched both ends of the hair, and the bubbles still clung, though they pulled apart from each other. When I let go, and the hair snapped back, every single bubble let go.

### nonchalant — 2014-07-16T00:54:39+0300

Are there tiny bubbles in the ocean? Salt water diminishes the EZ. The sodium ion invades the lattice. But there is EZ left still? Pollack's book p97 says the "balance point will depend on how much energy enters the system: more intense incident energy yields larger exclusion zones". So there should be EZs in seawater, at least near volcanic vents or where sunlight is strong. Is that why artificial reefs are attractive? A shipwreck lands in relatively shallow water, and sea life is quickly attracted. I thought it was the anchoring points, but perhaps the ship's hydrophyllic surfaces structures sea water? Life would be attracted to the energy.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wellnessfx.10927/post-128442>

Hey everybody,

I'm planning on getting my initial labs done very soon and am considering WellnessFX. Anybody have any experience with them or suggestions for a better option? I'm in Los Angeles.

I've been eating "paleo" or "bulletproof" for about a year and a half now, but I have yet to get any labs done. Excited to see what's really going on in there! Thanks.

Jason

### kyrakitty — 2014-06-18T09:29:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wellnessfx.10927/post-131239>

If I hadn't found a physician to do labs for cheaper I would have done them through WellnessFX. But I was feeling so crummy I had to find a physician to help me.

If you feel relatively healthy and are simply trying to tweak yourself so that your labs are in the optimal range then WellnessFX is a good start. But at some point you might require hormones or other labs that can only be ordered by having a functional medical doctor.

I live 40 min from WellnessFX so everyone around here is always talking about it.

In response to my post in the Meet and Greet about my health issues I was told to post my labs here so here it goes. I've had a LOT though so I'm only going to post the ones that are out of range or ones that may be pertinent. Im happy to post up anything else though.

**LAB** ........................................ **MY NUMBERS** .................. **RANGE
**

Sex Hormone Binding Globulin ..........90 (H) ........................16.5-55.9

Ferritin, Serum .................................. 426 (H) ............ ..............30-400

Lp-PLA2 ............................................. 203 (H) .............. ...........131-199

Calcitriol (1,25 di-OH Vit D) ............. 87.2 (H) ......................... 10-75

Complement C3a ............................. <40 (L) ........................... 54-202

Manganese, RBC ................................ 4.9 (L) ........................... 11-23

Monocytes, Absolute ......................... 120 (L) .......................... 200-950

RBC .................................................... 5.2 (H) .......................... 4.2-5.1

Hemoglobin ....................................... 16.6 (H) ......................... 13.2-15.5

Bilirubin, Total .................................... 2.3 (H) .......................... 0.2-1.2

Bilirubin, Direct ................................. 0.3 (H) ......................... <0.2

Bilirubin, Indirect ............................... 2.0 (H) ......................... 0.2-1.2

Sum Total Cholesterol ........................ 285 (H) ........................ <200

\*\*All of my cholesterol and Lipid panels are high

cP Coproporphyrin ................................ 151 (H) ........................ 60-120

T3, Total ................................................. 75 (L) .......................... 76-181

T3, Free, Serum .................................... 2.2 ............................. 2.0-4.4

T4, Free, Direct ...................................... 1.38 ............................ 0.82-1.77
..
Reverse T3, Serum ................................... 23 ............................. 9.2-24.1

TSH ......................................................... 1.62 ............................. 0.45-4.5

\*\* Even though my thyroid is generally in range I was told my ratios are way off

Lyme, Ab IgG Western Blot, P41 Ab................... Positive

M. Pneumoniae, Ab, IgG, EIA ........................ >5.00 (H) .................... <0.90

Parvovirus B19 Ab, IgG ...................................... 5.6 (H) ................... 0-0.89

HHV-6, Ab, IgG, IFA ......................................... 1.160 (H) .............. none given

EBV Nuclear AG (EBNA) IgG ............................ 1.83 (H) ................. <0.91

Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate ......................... 3 ................... none given, told this is low

8-OXO- guanosine ..............................................normal

\*\*Vitamin D was 22 3 years ago but I have been taking D3 every day now and its back up

DHEA ................................................................... 238 ........................ 38-523

Pregnenolone ....................................................... 176 ......................... >151

Bun/Creatinine Ratio ...........................Within normal limits ............. 6-22

Creatinine, Serum ...............................................0.9 ........................... 0.8-1.3

C-Reactive Protein ...............................................0.1 ............................< 0.9

Methylmalonic Acid ............................................146 ........................... 87-318

**BRAIN TESTS** Brain symptoms are by far my worst

MRI and MRA- normal

Brain SPECT with Picker 3000-
At rest, reduced tracer activity seen in:

-   Left and Right Inferior Prefrontal Cortex
-   Left and Right Temporal Lobe
-   Parietal Lobe
-   Left and Right Lateral Prefrontal Lobe
-   Left and Right Prefrontal Cortex Pole
-   Left and Right Occipital Lobe
-   Dorsal Prefrontal Cortex

EEG- Mildly low voltage awake only with probably diffuse slowing.

LUMBAR PUNCTURE- Negative for malignant cells, Rare small Lymphocytes present

Well, I guess thats everything. Like I said, Im happy to post any more tests that anyone wants to see, I have plenty!

### Melanie Procter — 2014-05-27T05:09:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emfs-and-the-star-trek-teleporter.10931/post-128488>

When I think of how EMF's make me feel, I often get this image of how the people on Star Trek looked when they were teleporting.

[https://www.google.co.th/search?q=s...quantum-teleportation-successful.html;500;340](https://www.google.co.th/search?q=star+trek+teleporter&es_sm=91&source=lnms&tbm=isch&sa=X&ei=2fKDU-jeJIfJkQX7oIDABQ&ved=0CAgQ_AUoAQ&biw=1422&bih=733&dpr=0.9#facrc=_&imgdii=_&imgrc=VLnkmevCr9X1NM%253A%3B5zL3CSksc-ZL8M%3Bhttp%253A%252F%252F4.bp.blogspot.com%252F-QD9566AGsQ8%252FTarR9PATWpI%252FAAAAAAAABNg%252FZfvB-FRzMus%252Fs1600%252Fstar%25252Btrek%25252Bbeam.jpg%3Bhttp%253A%252F%252Frealityviews.blogspot.com%252F2011%252F04%252Fquantum-teleportation-successful.html%3B500%3B340)

They looked like they were disintegrating energetically. I don't know what scientific principle on which the teleporter worked. Do you guys think this is a good image that is representative of the effects of EMF's on us?

Mel

### nonchalant — 2014-05-27T05:38:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emfs-and-the-star-trek-teleporter.10931/post-128494>

NN EMF would interfere with order inside the cells, so you would have more Brownian motion, entropy, chaos, static. Yeah, I would agree that the transporter might be a good visual. But without the happy ending of being down on the planet in one piece, mental faculties intact.

Melanie, what are you working on? I'd love to see what you come up with. Always good to have pics.

### Melanie Procter — 2014-05-27T05:45:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emfs-and-the-star-trek-teleporter.10931/post-128498>

I am trying to create visuals to get my head around all the concepts here. I believe that it is the best way to ground the information. I actually used to be a bit of a famous educator in the career from which I retired. As I educate myself, I use the same techniques of using visuals or creating visuals in my mind to grasp the concepts. Sometimes Jack gives us some great visuals when he talks. My favorite was the DHA being like a semi conducting
magnet.

[https://www.google.co.th/search?q=s...dpr=0.9#q=levitating+magnet&tbm=isch&imgdii=\_](https://www.google.co.th/search?q=semi+conducting+magnet&es_sm=91&source=lnms&tbm=isch&sa=X&ei=s_uDU6W1HYWokQWN54CIAg&ved=0CAgQ_AUoAQ&biw=1422&bih=733&dpr=0.9#q=levitating+magnet&tbm=isch&imgdii=_)

I would like to now get a good image for understanding iodine.

### nonchalant — 2014-05-27T06:07:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emfs-and-the-star-trek-teleporter.10931/post-128504>

Richard Feynman also would construct a visual in his head when trying to understand difficult mathematical concepts. Even if it was a ridiculous, fantasy object, like a hairy, green ball thing.

Visuals tend to go well with holographic memories. A lot of information in just one visual.

For iodine, how about a spacesuit for EVA on the space station?

### Melanie Procter — 2014-05-27T06:17:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emfs-and-the-star-trek-teleporter.10931/post-128507>

As they say, "Success leaves clues." Since we're trying to think like Jack, I am trying to decipher how he is holding onto information. We have seen signs of what some would call a photographic memory, as he will tell you the names of journals, the months and even roughly the page numbers. But then I heard him screw up the title of one of his favorite books. He turned the words of the title around. It could have been because he was being blasted with EMF's as he was talking to us in a live webinar on his computer. But then he downloads information with such incredible detail. So, I am now thinking he is seeing all the cogs in the wheel as an engineer would. He seems to be taking a walk through the body's electrical system and narrating to us what is happening. Based on watch how he learns and retains information, I am trying to create my own such visuals through metaphorical visuals I can grab off the net so sometimes I can take my own walking tour through the body. I share with you guys all along the way as I create this visual adventure as the real underlying theme of this website that makes it so powerful is "generosity with information". It is the glue that holds the forum together along with Jack's unrelentless expectations for us. We're the luckiest people in medical history.

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-05-28T20:43:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detox-summit-functional-medicine-postioning.10941/post-128736>

> **Detox Summit set for August**
> May 27, 2014Food & Spirit | Engredea News & Analysis
>
> -   -   [EMAIL](http://newhope360.com/forward?path=node%2F750591)
> -   [COMMENTS](http://newhope360.com/business/detox-summit-set-august?NL=NH-07&Issue=NH-07_20140528_NH-07_322&YM_RID=%27email%27&YM_MID=%27mmid%27&sfvc4enews=42&cl=article_3#comments) [0](http://newhope360.com/business/detox-summit-set-august?NL=NH-07&Issue=NH-07_20140528_NH-07_322&YM_RID=%27email%27&YM_MID=%27mmid%27&sfvc4enews=42&cl=article_3#comments)
>
>     Online Detox Summit, presented by nutritionist and author Deanna Minich and the Institute for Functional Medicine, will feature experts on the role toxins in health, followed by a 21-day Detox Challenge.
>
>     **More About:**
>
>     -   [Herbs and Botanicals](http://newhope360.com/herbs-and-botanicals)
>
>
>     Functional Medicine nutritionist and author, Deanna Minich, PhD, in collaboration with The Institute for Functional Medicine (IFM), is pleased to announce an international, online Detox Summit featuring experts on the role of toxins in health that will take place during the first week of August of 2014, followed by a 21-day Detox Challenge that everyone can participate in during the month of September.
>
>     The word “detox” is a catch-all term that means many things to people: drug and alcohol detoxification, nutritional detoxification, or cleanses using food, smoothies, and juices. During this informational event, 30 experts will discuss all aspects of detoxification. These experts include physicians, other health professionals, and researchers who will provide the latest clinical approaches for identifying and encouraging the elimination of environmental toxins. In addition to treatment of the body’s toxin burden, the Detox Summit will address emotional/mental toxins accumulated in the course of relationships, stressful interactions, and life experiences. Experts in self-help will discuss their tried-and-true techniques for transformation.
>
>     This combination of experts from various areas of medicine, nutrition, and personal growth makes this Summit unique, while offering a well-rounded, credible approach to a much-debated issue. Summit spokesperson Deanna Minich, PhD, stated, “‘Detox’ is a buzz word, and it brings with it much confusion and misinterpretation, which is why I conceived The Detox Summit—to investigate the science and self-help behind this movement of health. The Detox Summit, with 30 experts like functional medicine physician and best-selling author, Mark Hyman, MD, nutrigenomics pioneer and founder of Functional Medicine, Jeffrey Bland, PhD, and neurologist and best-selling author, David Perlmutter, MD, plus many others, will help guide and inform the public on topics like endocrine disruptors and fertility, dental amalgams, laboratory tests for determining whether we are toxic, and why we should watch out for gluten and genetically modified foods.”
>
>     Participants will be able to follow up the Detox Summit with the Detox Challenge—working with IFM Faculty and IFM Certified Practitioners to put their knowledge and enthusiasm for detoxification into practice in their own lives.
>
>     Laurie Hofmann, CEO of IFM, stated, “The Institute for Functional Medicine is pleased to collaborate with Deanna Minich, PhD, on the upcoming Detox Summit and Detox Challenge planned for August and September 2014. Deanna has served as a passionate, creative, and deeply knowledgeable member of our Functional Nutrition development team and faculty. We are thrilled to collaborate with Deanna on the Detox Summit and Detox Challenge and look forward to the opportunity of unveiling some of IFM’s personalized nutrition and food plan suites as part of these two very important and exciting online events. We hope that clinicians and patients alike will seize this unique opportunity to participate in both the education-oriented Summit and the experiential/interactive Challenge.”
>
>     The Institute for Functional Medicine Medical Director, Patrick Hanaway, MD, comments, “Using food and nutrition to assist the body in detoxification may seem new to some, but has been a well-established practice in traditional healing systems and functional medicine practices. Increasing evidence in mainstream medical journals indicates the burden of toxicity on health. For example, new research indicates that chronic conditions like type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and obesity are all associated with environmental toxins in the body. We need to start looking at the root of chronic diseases rather than simply suppressing symptoms. Identifying the root cause of disease and treating with a personalized approach is what we embrace in Functional Medicine. Detoxification is one way to treat an important root cause of many diseases.”
>
>     The Detox Summit is an online event. Interested persons can sign up via detoxsummit.com. During the week of August 4 to 10, 2014, people who have registered for the event will have free access to audio recordings of cutting-edge interviews with 30 experts in the area of toxicity on everything from environmental toxins in food, air, and water to internally-generated toxins such as thoughts, emotions, and behaviors. Listeners will not only be informed of the perils of these poisons, but, more importantly, will be taught about breakthrough solutions to reduce and transform them for healthier living. More information on The Detox Challenge will be released this summer. Join The Detox Summit movement now to become informed by the world’s brightest minds on toxicity and what you can do to become healthy! Find us at detoxsummit.com and Facebook at facebook.com/TheDetoxSummit.
>

[http://thedetoxsummit.com/](http://thedetoxsummit.com/)

# Thread: Aerobic and Anaerobic Respiration
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-05-28T22:55:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128743>

Is there a decent two to three page reference that would give an overview of how the body transitions through different forms of energy use? I've read about anaerobic and aerobic respiration (glycolysis, krebs cycle, electron transport), but I have problems seeing the larger picture.

Which of these are we using when at rest?

Which of these are we using when we walk around?

Which of these are we using during a brisk 30 minute walk?

How does energy utilization change during a 30 minute jog?

If you are doing some anerobic sprinting, how does that transition to aerobic?

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-28T23:21:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128746>

I don't have any reference at hand but if you do a search on the net focusing on endurance running you'll find a lot of good articles that, at least, cover the basics about all that questions regarding how the metabolism changes between fuels. It's something that is quite important in endurance sports so there's a ton of info regarding that subject. I've found this book that is not a quick reference but seems pretty detailed yet more or less easy to understand (at least has colours, with me that helps )

[http://books.google.es/books?id=RWP3JQfvHGEC&lpg=PA49&dq=endurance metabolism&hl=es&pg=PA39#v=onepage&q=endurance metabolism&f=false](http://books.google.es/books?id=RWP3JQfvHGEC&lpg=PA49&dq=endurance%20metabolism&hl=es&pg=PA39#v=onepage&q=endurance%20metabolism&f=false)

Basically, at rest you use fat and also when walking, considering that you walk slowly or at least not with much effort. As you start using more oxygen, feel the effort and your heart rate goes up you start using more glucose and less fat at the same time, the point where you use more glucose than fat, which is when you get to your aerobic threshold that, if I call remember correctly is when you're running at 70% Vo2Max or so that, for me, equals 160 heart rate or so. If you go even higher intensity then you end up using almost glucose and cannot satisfy the oxygen demand and then that's where you start building up lactate (which causes that burning feeling, well in fact is not lactate but their H+ ions). The threshold seems to be at the intensity where you could run up to 1h at that even effort level so to speak.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

When you do anaerobic sprinting... it depends, if it's less than 10s short then you basically use the creatine-phosphate ATP system which does not use any glucose just your already available short-term energy, but if it lasts a little bit more then you get again into the lactic glycolithic pathway, and you get aerobic once you ease until you can work again with more oxygen to use the beta-oxidation from fats.

More or less that's it with a lot of oversimplification.

Good reference, but much more technical than I want to be for a high level of understanding....

> Basically, at rest you use fat and also when walking, considering that you walk slowly or at least not with much effort. As you start using more oxygen, feel the effort and your heart rate goes up you start using more glucose and less fat at the same time, the point where you use more glucose than fat, which is when you get to your aerobic threshold that, if I call remember correctly is when you're running at 70% Vo2Max or so that, for me, equals 160 heart rate or so. If you go even higher intensity then you end up using almost glucose and cannot satisfy the oxygen demand and then that's where you start building up lactate (which causes that burning feeling, well in fact is not lactate but their H+ ions). The threshold seems to be at the intensity where you could run up to 1h at that even effort level so to speak.
>
>
>
>
> When you do anaerobic sprinting... it depends, if it's less than 10s short then you basically use the creatine-phosphate ATP system which does not use any glucose just your already available short-term energy, but if it lasts a little bit more then you get again into the lactic glycolithic pathway, and you get aerobic once you ease until you can work again with more oxygen to use the beta-oxidation from fats.
>
>
>
>
>
> More or less that's it with a lot of oversimplification.

So how do the three stages of aerobic respiration map to this? Those are glycolysis, krebs cycle, and electron transport.

Basically what your diagram calls "Glycogen-lactic acid system" is the glycolysis stage of aerobic respiration? And what you are labeling as "aerobic respiration" is where krebs cycle begins?

Why I care about all of this: it turns out that I may have classic "chronic fatigue syndrome". The key characteristic of this disease is mitochondrial collapse in energy production after aerobic exercise. The exciting new development in diagnosing this disease is a \*TWO\* day VO2 max test. In people with this disease, on day two of the test their aerobic capacity during testing collapses by more than 10%. Google Workwell Foundation in Rincon California for details.

It's very much a research question still why the mitochondria stop producing energy, and at what stage this happens. But I very much want to start understanding the cycle.

Another exciting discovery from research is that people with CFS appear to be able to do anaerobic exercise without creating an energy collapse. So doing weight workouts with less than six reps per set makes sense. I'm going to try experimenting with sprint-swimming single laps followed by long rests to recovery.

Definitely as soon as I transition to any kind of aerobic exercise, I get a deep muscular weakness (which is probably either carbonic acid / CO2 or H+ from conversion of lactic acid to lactate). I have discovered some ways to clear the lactic acid, but it doesn't treat the mitochondrial loss of energy production.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-29T00:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128753>

Uhmm... I'm not an expert so I'll give you my understanding and maybe Barry or anyone else can chime in to clarify. The krebs cycle is always working and producing ATP for you, for that, it uses AcetylCoA mainly as a substrate. You can get AcetylCoa both via the beta-oxidation from fats or the conversion of glucose to pyruvate or other steps like the gluconeogenesis if you get it from aminoacids (last resort), etc. When you are doing very low intensity activity you can get to oxidize fats via the beta-oxidation that your mitochondria use with oxygen to get the Acetyl-CoA that is a fuel that can enter the Krebs Cycle. Once it gets in, you get some ATP and enzimes to generate NADH and FADH2 from that via some steps that take place that then fuel the Electron Transport Chain to get even more ATP. I guess Jack would tell you that this is bullshit and what counts here is electron flow and not ATP to get the energy... but I'll let that for him!

So, in this case that your mitochondria can use oxygen to fuel all your demands, we're using beta-oxidation, get AcetylCoa and fuel the Krebs Cycle that fuels the ECT and that allows you to get up to 146 or so ATP, which is quite much indeed. Not a problem with slow activity like working on the PC, walking or even running at very mild pace. That's what I understand as "aerobic respiration". But, there are too many steps on the cycle to get so much ATP by this way and this cannot always happen fast enough to burn the fat with respiration. That's when the glycogen-lactic thing gets in, the "anaerobic process".

In this case you start to get AcetylCoA from pyruvate which comes from glycolysis mainly, breakdown of glycogen to glucose, as we said once you get the AcetylCoA via one route or another you can fuel the Krebs cycle and then the ECT. Both sustrates end powering the Krebd Cycle but in this case you don't use oxygen, is anaerobic and the process is quite faster than using fat, but you also get only 36 ATP or so, and lactate as a by-product, which is good because it can be reused to get even more ATP but if it builds up faster than you can recycle it via other mechanisms then your fatigue starts to show up via de H+ ions that acidify the muscles and then you have to ease down the pace.

Not sure if it makes it any clear than before?

BTW, I'm not an expert on that (although I've had some degree of fatigue myself in the past and looked at the subject, and in fact that's why I don't engage in endurance running anymore at least at this moment...) but I suspect that Jack would tell you that it's not a disease per se but a state where your HPA is off and probably your thyroid is suffering and this cannot get enough free t3 to power the mitochondria... but again, not sure... maybe he can chime in and clarify.

Uhmm. for that weakness feeling... have you tested for RBC, iron, folate and Vit B12? any methylation issue? I also had a lot of weak leg feeling when I was training a lot, even on the rest days just by walking on the street, and turned out it correlated with a slightly low haematocrite and iron deficiency... solved it once I stopped it and gave myself some inner peace and verified these values where in range, but of course every case is different and similar issues may come from very different triggers...

Well, hope something might help!!

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-29T01:02:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128754>

The CFS and anaerobic exercise capability makes sense to me if you're doing short term high intensity efforts with ample recovery times as you mainly use the ATP-CP system (alactic) because it relies on your immediate pool which is filled with aminoacids from the diet and creatine phosphorilation, its ready to go at anytime as long as you only do short bursts, but once you have to get the 'burning furnaces' to get started is when the loss of efficiency might be noticeable the most, that's just my guess I might be missing something.

In any case, the leptin reset protocol is pretty clear that until someone is not leptin sensitive (2011 vision of the mitochondrial efficiency perspective, now in 2014 seems to be an electron loss issue due to poor redox potential, but all gets down to the same principles) is better to focus on getting that first solvend and then restarting any time of exercise. I think only walking is advised as a form of exercise.

### persistence — 2014-05-29T01:08:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128755>

I also had a lot of weak leg feeling when I was training a lot, even on the rest days just by walking on the street, and turned out it correlated with a slightly low haematocrite and iron deficiency... solved it once I stopped it and gave myself some inner peace and verified these values where in range, but of course every case is different and similar issues may come from very different triggers...
>
> Well, hope something might help!!

My labs so far are good. There may be a methylation problem because my homocysteine is 14 and my other inflammatory markers are near zero. I will get genetic testing.

The feeling here is not a weak leg feeling. It is a catastrophic collapse in energy. At its worst, I would go out for an hour of sprinting and then lay awake that night drinking 1/2 litre of water every hour, desperately dehydrated, breathing heavily and abnormally (compensatory respiratory alkalosis to metabolic acidosis), and then wake feeling like the muscle had no ability to generate any energy for even simple movement.

I went from 14 hour workdays to three hour workdays, quickly. It's not a fine-tuning thing. It's massive and overwhelming.

### nonchalant — 2014-05-29T16:06:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-shirts.10945/post-128824>

I'm having difficulties reconciling what Jack said long ago about not wanting to compress the breasts (especially at night), with the goals of a compression shirt. I guess not wearing one all the time is what would be important.

I was previously using a long-sleeved compression shirt by Under Armour. Much cheaper. But I'd like to get a sleeveless one now.

# Thread: Sleep and Obesity Study
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-05-30T14:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-128912>

[http://www.bbc.com/news/health-27617615](http://www.bbc.com/news/health-27617615)

> Sleeping in a room with too much light has been linked to an increased risk of piling on the pounds, a study shows.
>
> A team at the Institute of Cancer Research in London found women had larger waistlines if their bedroom was "light enough to see across" at night.
>
> However, they caution there is not enough evidence to advise people to buy thicker curtains or turn off lights.
>
> The study of 113,000 women was [published in the American Journal of Epidemiology](http://aje.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/05/29/aje.kwu117.short?rss=1).
>
> The women were asked to rate the amount of light in their bedrooms at night as:
>
>
> -   Light enough to read
> -   Light enough to see across the room, but not read
> -   Light enough to see your hand in front of you, but not across the room
> -   Too dark to see your hand or you wear a mask
>
> Their answers were compared to several measures of obesity. Body Mass Index, waist-to-hip ratio and waist circumference were all higher in women with lighter rooms.

[http://aje.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/05/29/aje.kwu117.short?rss=1](http://aje.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/05/29/aje.kwu117.short?rss=1)

> **Abstract**
> There has been a worldwide epidemic of obesity in recent decades. In animal studies, there is convincing evidence that light exposure causes weight gain, even when calorie intake and physical activity are held constant. Disruption of sleep and circadian rhythms by exposure to light at night (LAN) might be one mechanism contributing to the rise in obesity, but it has not been well-investigated in humans. Using multinomial logistic regression, we examined the association between exposure to LAN and obesity in questionnaire data from over 100,000 women in the Breakthrough Generations Study, a cohort study of women aged 16 years or older who were living in the United Kingdom and recruited during 2003–2012. The odds of obesity, measured using body mass index, waist:hip ratio, waist:height ratio, and waist circumference, increased with increasing levels of LAN exposure (_P_ < 0.001), even after adjustment for potential confounders such as sleep duration, alcohol intake, physical activity, and current smoking. We found a significant association between LAN exposure and obesity which was not explained by potential confounders we could measure. While the possibility of residual confounding cannot be excluded, the pattern is intriguing, accords with the results of animal experiments, and warrants further investigation.

### PaulG — 2014-05-30T15:30:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-128919>

> However, they caution there is not enough evidence to advise people to buy thicker curtains or turn off lights.

No repeat prescriptions for these..

### nicld — 2014-05-30T16:31:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-128927>

### kathylu — 2014-05-30T17:11:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-128932>

I know that since I have been using blackout drapes, an eyemask and keeping lights low in the evening, I sleep much, much better. And my weight is trending down...

### sooperb — 2014-05-30T17:44:28+0300

### nonchalant — 2014-05-30T18:08:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-128935>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=128927)
>
> That is why the next house I get or build I want my bedroom in the basement with one window. I want it like a cave and thankfully so does the DH.

That sounds ideal, Nicole. You don't spend much time there in the daytime anyway.

### Josh — 2014-05-31T04:30:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-128994>

Melatonin Research

> **Abstract**
> Melatonin is an old and ubiquitous molecule in nature showing multiple mechanisms of action and functions in practically every living organism. In mammals, pineal melatonin functions as a hormone and a chronobiotic, playing a major role in the regulation of the circadian temporal internal order. The anti-obesogen and the weight-reducing effects of melatonin depend on several mechanisms and actions. Experimental evidence demonstrates that melatonin is necessary for the proper synthesis, secretion, and action of insulin. Melatonin acts by regulating GLUT4 expression and/or triggering, via its G-protein-coupled membrane receptors, the phosphorylation of the insulin receptor and its intracellular substrates mobilizing the insulin-signaling pathway. Melatonin is a powerful chronobiotic being responsible, in part, by the daily distribution of metabolic processes so that the activity/feeding phase of the day is associated with high insulin sensitivity, and the rest/fasting is synchronized to the insulin-resistant metabolic phase of the day. Furthermore, melatonin is responsible for the establishment of an adequate energy balance mainly by regulating energy flow to and from the stores and directly regulating the energy expenditure through the activation of brown adipose tissue and participating in the browning process of white adipose tissue. The reduction in melatonin production, as during aging, shift-work or illuminated environments during the night, induces insulin resistance, glucose intolerance, sleep disturbance, and metabolic circadian disorganization characterizing a state of chronodisruption leading to obesity. The available evidence supports the suggestion that melatonin replacement therapy might contribute to restore a more healthy state of the organism.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/24654916/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/24654916/)

### Hope — 2014-05-31T23:23:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-129078>

The best information - well, easiest to assimilate - was in the early EMF blogs...I think one or two...in the Beings of Resonance video or what it was called that Jack shared there. Amazing. Totally.

For my n=1, my aMSH is super low, one more reason I don't make much melatonin among others. But I too sleep way better following the circadian stuff.......despite a tanked aMSH (which is slowly rising yaye).

# Thread: CO2 and Plant Nutrient Density
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/co2-and-plant-nutrient-density.11001/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-06-05T20:04:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/co2-and-plant-nutrient-density.11001/post-129836>

[http://www.boulderweekly.com/articl...els-to-cut-key-nutrients-in-global-crops.html](http://www.boulderweekly.com/article-12866-rising-co2-levels-to-cut-key-nutrients-in-global-crops.html)

> New research studying 41 strains of six different crops on three continents that was published May 7 in the scientific journal,Nature, shows that as concentrations of atmospheric carbon dioxide increase, the nutritional content of key food crops will drop, exacerbating hunger woes for millions of people worldwide. In some crops, concentrations of nutrients could drop by as much as 10 percent when CO2 levels rise to 500 parts per million by mid-century.
>
> Harvard University public health researcher Samuel Myers says that the new research once and for all resolves questions about the impacts of CO2 on major food crops.
>
> “Already, two billion people are suffering from zinc and iron deficiencies. That equals 63 million life years lost,” Myers says, explaining that the grains most affected by increased greenhouse gas emissions are exactly the ones that provide those nutrients.
>
> “This is a huge public health problem today ... These are grains and legumes that people rely on for nutrition,” he says.
>
> “Humanity is conducting a global experiment by rapidly altering the environmental conditions on the only habitable planet we know. As this experiment unfolds, there will undoubtedly be many surprises. Finding out that rising CO2 threatens human nutrition is one such surprise,” he says........

"Finding out that rising CO2 threatens human nutrition is one such surprise,”

"Surprise"....Really!?!?!?
