### Josh — 2014-10-11T21:04:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-effects-calcium-efflux-and-more-in-images-and-maps.11878/post-143536>

Cilia:

> Cilia are microtubule-based organelles that project like antennae from the surface of most cells in the body. Motile cilia move fluid past cells, for example mucus in the airway. Non-motile primary cilia, however, transduce a multitude of sensory stimuli, including chemical concentrations of growth factors, hormones, odorants, and developmental morphogens, as well as osmolarity, light intensity, and fluid flow. Cilia have evolved a complex ultrastructure to accommodate these diverse functions, and an extensive molecular machinery has developed to support the assembly of these organelles. Defects in the cilia themselves, or the machinery required to assemble them, lead to a broad spectrum of human disease symptoms, including polycystic kidney disease, nephronophthisis, hydrocephalus, polydactyly, situs inversus, retinal degeneration, and obesity. While these diseases highlight the pivotal roles of cilia in physiology and development, the mechanistic link between cilia, physiology, and disease remains unclear.

[http://www.cell.com/current-biology/abstract/S0960-9822(06)01831-8](http://www.cell.com/current-biology/abstract/S0960-9822\(06\)01831-8)

### Josh — 2014-10-11T21:08:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-effects-calcium-efflux-and-more-in-images-and-maps.11878/post-143539>

> Figure 2. Role of cilia in left–right symmetry
> breaking.
> Three models for how rotational movement
> of cilia leads to leftward fluid flow.
> (The cartoon represents a ventral view of
> the animal with the left side of the animal
> being to the right of the cone.) (A) Tilted
> rotation leading to a leftward flow layer
> above the surface and a rightward flow
> layer near the surface, as proposed by
> Cartwright and co-workers \[84\]. The rightward
> flow predicted by this model is not
> observed experimentally. (B) No-slip
> boundary condition at surface (depicted by the hand) causes the rightward phase of rotation to be ineffective in moving fluid, while
> the leftward phase, which is less constrained by the surface boundary layer, is able to move fluid productively towards the left \[85\].
> The ability of this model to generate flow has been verified with a mechanical model \[85\]. (C) Intrinsic asymmetry in beating cycle,
> so that the leftward velocity is higher than the rightward velocity, which requires prior symmetry breaking \[87\].

[http://www.cell.com/current-biology/abstract/S0960-9822(06)01831-8](http://www.cell.com/current-biology/abstract/S0960-9822\(06\)01831-8)

### Josh — 2014-10-11T21:10:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-effects-calcium-efflux-and-more-in-images-and-maps.11878/post-143540>

> Figure 3. Models for nodal flow sensing.
> (A) Classic morphogen accumulation model. A secreted molecule (X) makes a concentration gradient
> by leftward flow (red arrow). Okada et al. \[86\] estimated the molecular weight of X to be 15–50
> kDa con- sidering effects of diffusion (green arrows) and returning flow (blue arrow). (B) Original
> two-cilia model proposed by McGrath et al. \[88\]. Perinodal cilia are immotile and act as
> PKD2-bearing mechanosensors. They are bent at the left side to cause cal- cium influx (Ca). (C) A
> modified form of the two-cilia model that is more hydrodynami- cally feasible. Mechanosensory cilia
> are di- rectionally oriented so as to cause calcium influx only in response to lateral bending away
> from the midline of the embryo. (D) Nodal vesicular parcel model. Multivesicu- lar parcels are
> pinched off from node cells, blown leftward, and smashed against the left wall of the node to
> release inner vesi- cles containing signal molecules, Sonic hedgehog and retinoic acid \[90\].

[http://www.cell.com/current-biology/abstract/S0960-9822(06)01831-8](http://www.cell.com/current-biology/abstract/S0960-9822\(06\)01831-8)

### Alex Fergus — 2015-09-03T10:21:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bodybuilding.11883/post-176301>

Hey.

I know this is an old post, but I wanted to say hi.

I'm an ex Bodybuilder, I have also done a lot of powerlifting.

I still strength train, but my focus in health now (saying that I'm probably in better shape than my BBer days!).

I dieted down for my shows using a cyclic keto diet. It wasn't anything like it is today, but I was eating bacon and eggs for breakfast during my 12 week diet. I got down to ~5% and I won a national title.

I would be curious doing another comp using the tips and tricks (such as CT) i have learnt over the years. But I think BBing really messed me up. Years of overtraining, poor recovery, too many stimulants (I was all natural, but was taking far too much caffiene for example) and too much protein.
Anyway, I still train and coach Bodybuilders, so if you have any questions hit me up.

Cheers

### ssj3 — 2015-09-03T11:25:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bodybuilding.11883/post-176302>

Hi Alex,

Sometimes I think that our environment changed so much over the years that the constant beating we put on our bodies and the constant shovelling in of 6 meals a day along with out-of-season food intake that only a few would get through without health problems or injuries. I never made it to the stage, but I always think of how to make it happen combining what I learn on here, but I would have to completely overhaul my current life to balance that health tradeoff I now know comes with it.

My current plan which I am finding to work well is to run with a strength based program during the winter months (JoeD program) and then mountaindog during the months when I get amble sunshine to support increased carb intake and some intra-workout.

I am in Adelaide... do you run your own facility over in Sydney??

Rob

My goal is to add size and strength in a good environmental context. I'm 6-5 235 but am carrying a lot of fat right now. I was conscious this summer to add lots of calories, like the deer I plan to hunt here in a few weeks. I even did GOMAD (Gallon of Milk a Day) for 25 days and gained 15 lbs during that time. I took advantage of the long holiday weekend to go strict keto so I'm on day 3 now. Next August I'm moving to Hawaii so I'll have lots of carbs available and will of course eat more of them in Hawaii. Now I'm focused on mostly leafy greens for my carbs. I'm thinking I might want to match that 25 day GOMAD with 25 days keto and then see what my body composition is like.

Hi Jack,
Sounds good, Im still very new in regards to JK protocols so I don't really have much advice to give (I'm still learning!). But I can definitely help on the strength side of things. Heres my advice - don't overcomplicate things, use a basic strength program, focus on strength, size will come (with the help of the extra calories). And don't over do it. Personally I think 3 strength sessions a week, with a 4th HIIT conditioning day is all you need to put on size. Focus on recovery, sleep, and quality food while minimising stressors and you'll do great.

Be sure to keep us updated with your progress

### jackware — 2015-09-08T04:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bodybuilding.11883/post-176706>

I appreciate the reply, Alex. Where are you located? I've always handled stress well; introvert, laid back, easygoing, etc. Always slept well too, and drank a lot of water. I've just never really been serious about weight training. I've been working a body part as soon as it's not sore from the workout before. Today was biceps, calves, and abs. Tomorrow is leg day. Day 3 of Keto went well. A few cravings but I drowned them in fat. I'm only working out at home, doing bodyweight exercises and lifting heavy things. I also installed a pullup bar. I've always loved to eat and it's crossed my mind a few times in my life that bodybuilding would make sure those calories were being used instead of deposited. Now I'm all in!

### Alex Fergus — 2015-09-08T04:36:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bodybuilding.11883/post-176709>

Not a problem

I'm based in Sydney Australia.

Sounds like you have things all sorted then! Regarding your program, I would recommend doing more compound multi joint lifts (ie, squats, rows, chins, presses, deadlifts) and less isolation single joint lifts (biceps, calves, shoulder isolation exercises etc). And also look at doing these 2-3x a week (ie, theres no reason why you can't do pullups - which are great btw, I have them included in all of my clients strength/size programs - 3x a week, just make sure you have a day or two rest in between and you're good to go).

I say this to a lot of new lifters - don't train like a bodybuilder until you've been a powerlifter for a year or two. In essence what I'm saying is even if your goals are size, you are better off building a strength base before doing any advanced split routines. In fact, even guys that have a solid strength base still do better on a strength program if their goals are size. Maybe doing a few more reps/sets than a true powerlifting program, but I hope you get my jist. In summary - don't get caught up with fancy programs, just do the basics.

Bodybuilding is a great sport in the sense that you are so focused on achieving a great body - symmetrical, balanced, proportionate etc. And a lot of people associate health and bodybuilding, but this is not necessarily the case. Competitive BB'ing (whether natural or not) is horrible for your health. I'm still paying the price of pushing to hard for my years BBing. There's also the selfishness of the sport, it's all about you. It can be great for someone who is a horrible eater, and has no respect for their body, I've seen fat lazy kids change big time when they stumbled upon bodybuilding, but unfortunately it has a lot of costs. Excuse my profanity, but its a constant mindf&\*k. You are always doubting yourself, comparing yourself to others, never happy with what you have. But this is at a competitive level. Recreational bodybuilding can be great!

Anyway, that was a bit of a rant! Let me know if you have any more questions, especially in regards to training.

### ssj3 — 2015-09-08T10:54:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bodybuilding.11883/post-176722>

> [jackware said:](/goto/post?id=176676)
>
> My goal is to add size and strength in a good environmental context. I'm 6-5 235 but am carrying a lot of fat right now. I was conscious this summer to add lots of calories, like the deer I plan to hunt here in a few weeks. I even did GOMAD (Gallon of Milk a Day) for 25 days and gained 15 lbs during that time. I took advantage of the long holiday weekend to go strict keto so I'm on day 3 now. Next August I'm moving to Hawaii so I'll have lots of carbs available and will of course eat more of them in Hawaii. Now I'm focused on mostly leafy greens for my carbs. I'm thinking I might want to match that 25 day GOMAD with 25 days keto and then see what my body composition is like.

My concern is pushing the weight gain if you are already carrying extra fat. I feel that a conscious approach that incorporates the key aspects taught by JK coupled with a simple consistent program and supportive food intake to add lean body mass and reduce body fat would be a good way to go.

### jackware — 2015-09-08T16:22:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bodybuilding.11883/post-176733>

I fully expect the keto will burn off the fat while at least maintaining or slightly gaining the muscle I already have. I love carbs and as soon as my bodyfat gets back under control I will add back carbs, targeted after workouts, to build muscle. I agree with the powerlifting approach first. My core exercises are pushups and pullups and bodyweight squats (I'm still working out at home). The mental aspect of it is what I love most. If it is to be, it's up to me!

### Danco3636 — 2015-09-09T03:22:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bodybuilding.11883/post-176825>

I lift and maintain good muscle tone and build using Jack's models and Quilt. Right now I am doing a hack so taking over 4 weeks off lifting and most fitness activity. I am still maintaining my leanness and muscle tone.... Must be the morning CT shivering that keeps me looking like I workout daily.... We will see as things change for me in 3-4 weeks.

### Danco3636 — 2015-09-09T03:35:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bodybuilding.11883/post-176826>

A few things of note.... Last week I did a medical exam/ surgery
Resting heart rate 42-45 - kept setting off the alarm on the machine.
Blood pressure was normal to low....
They took my temperature and thought the machine was broken, they got another and just figured mine was on the low side. I had CT'ed a few hours before going there but felt warm.
They started an IV and asked if I had been dehydrated. Nope I was around a 20 hour fast but had some water in the AM (I wasn't suppose to). My blood looked dark red and they said normally it is a bit clearer on people. I just said "I had rich blood".
After a 3-4 hour surgery and when the anesthesia wore off I had two things to say....... "Are we done?" They said "Yes". I then sad "Time for some salmon! Yep went home had some salmon and then a cool soak....
I opt out of any pain meds and antibiotics.... Just wasn't needed.

Just sharing and experience. - Can't wait to get back to lifting.....

### JoeBranca — 2015-09-09T20:31:22+0300

I might be confused on what's defined as bodybuilding... My understanding is its a specific concentration on building muscle mass for an ideal physique that would do well in competition? Different than movement literacy, or even strength training per se.

### Danco3636 — 2015-09-09T21:00:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-bodybuilding.11883/post-176870>

> [JoeBranca said:](/goto/post?id=176868)

> I might be confused on what's defined as bodybuilding... My understanding is its a specific concentration on building muscle mass for an ideal physique that would do well in competition? Different than movement literacy, or even strength training per se.

I agree. Physique for me is a byproduct of healthy movement, fitness and healthy living.
Bodybuilding in its self can be considered a sport that often times comes with unhealthy consequences.
Now perhaps chasing a good looking body/ build through a healthy approach can also be bodybuilding but not in the context of a sport.
Good point above. Context matters which may equal long term effects, consequences or results.

### Robert Haas — 2015-09-10T04:07:10+0300

# Thread: Help with Deciphering Thyroid Study
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-deciphering-thyroid-study.11888/>
**Subforum:** Heal Your Hormones
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### River — 2014-10-14T00:03:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-deciphering-thyroid-study.11888/post-143742>

I read this article with interest a few times and was hoping that some of you with more understanding of the terminology, content and context of the article could help extract the nuggets and boom moments (if any).

I know the studies were done on animal thyroid but from what Jack has told us regarding the evolution process much of this could apply to us as well.

As someone with thyroid issues for many years who monitors lab values of TSH, T3, T4, et.al., one of my take-aways (which may be wrong) was that the article seems to imply that TSH may be increased or decreased depending upon whether we are in a circadian long photoperiod or short photoperiod. The same with T3, and conversion from T4 to RT4 and from T3 to T2. I think they are saying their studies show it may change with the seasons.

For those taking NDT hormones to decrease TSH, this may put us in a permanent state of Fall/Autumn, even if we are honoring the natural light protocol per JK (minimal artificial light after sunset, and no blue light at all after dusk). Same with T3 and T4 levels.

I know that modern people today are living the majority of the day under unnatural light, but I'm thinking more about those of us here who are honoring length of day. I wonder if we should re-think thyroid labs. And I wonder if the labs should re-think the thyroid lab ranges, since they wouldn't apply to humans who live in the ancient pathway.

Sorry for my limited knowledge, but my inquisitiveness is genuine along with my desire to share.

Here is the link to the study:

[http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/fendo.2014.00019/full](http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/fendo.2014.00019/full)

### Grmoline — 2014-10-15T13:03:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-deciphering-thyroid-study.11888/post-143910>

This is a really interesting study, and will take me another read to completely digest it all. But it does demonstrate seasonal photoperiod-related changes in T3 and revT3 production in a number of vertebrate species, linked to melatonin and separate from the usual hypothalamic signalling. Two things that I found interesting. One is the statement about how genetic/molecular circadian clocks (peripheral clocks) are found in virtually every tissue, and that they rapidly become desynchronised and phase-shifted without regular resetting cues from the SCN (something JK has been telling us for years). And the other is how refractory states can be artificially induced (i.e., artificially inducing a particular season by manipulating light) and the effect it has on T3 production or repression. You've raised some really interesting questions for me also, as one who is treating Hashimoto's.

### Josh — 2014-10-15T21:05:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-deciphering-thyroid-study.11888/post-143966>

I posted this related article:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4104039/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4104039/)

Here:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/tensegrity-5-and-thyroid-hashi-question.11842/page-2](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/tensegrity-5-and-thyroid-hashi-question.11842/page-2)

### Josh — 2014-10-15T21:09:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-deciphering-thyroid-study.11888/post-143967>

As I recall Jack's summary is that if we are LS and Cold-adapted, the brain bypasses the thyroid. Regardless, these thyroid producing areas within the brain have circannual and circadian functions as well. I am still clarifying how it all ties to the actual thyroid organ and thyroid hormone supplementation.

### Josh — 2014-10-15T21:32:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-deciphering-thyroid-study.11888/post-143968>

> -   [Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi)I told people in CT 6 that even without a thyroid you can use cold to use T3 to help you.......now you see why.
>     [2 hrs](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/882734145072814/?comment_id=882734428406119&offset=0&total_comments=5) · [Like](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/#) · [3](https://www.facebook.com/browse/likes?id=882734428406119)
> -   [
>     Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi)photoperiodic response = the Vitamin A cycle in brain and skin. The photonic power of the seasons excites electrons in the skin arterioles that vasodialate to raise the electrons to a higher energy level to unfold protein polymers more than we see in winter. This is codified and tied to the Vitamin D cycle in brain and skin.
>     [2 hrs](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/882734145072814/?comment_id=882735025072726&offset=0&total_comments=5) · [Edited](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/#) · [Like](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/#) · [3](https://www.facebook.com/browse/likes?id=882735025072726)
> -   [
>     Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi)across all species the highest density of melatonin-binding sites was found in the pars tuberalis (PT), a region of the pituitary stalk apposed to the median eminence. The median eminence is one of 7 places in the brain where no blood brain barrier exists so these neurons can sense the information coded for in the electrons in the blood plasma.
>     [2 hrs](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/882734145072814/?comment_id=882736461739249&offset=0&total_comments=5) · [Like](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/#) · [2](https://www.facebook.com/browse/likes?id=882736461739249)
> -   [
>     Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi)This line made me laugh: "Although cold exposure activates thyroid activity" Consider what Masterjohn and Kresser told people in a podcast about Inuits that they need to eat Moose thyroids to have children because they have no carbs for fertility to stimulate t3. I laughed then and I am really laughing now.

[https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/)

### River — 2014-10-16T17:37:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-deciphering-thyroid-study.11888/post-144013>

Thanks for your comments and insights. I was happy to see Jack re-posted the article on the FB page and commented there. It validated the study for me somewhat and my question as to whether we should be so quick to quiet a rising TSH with medication without considering the current season.

The other interesting thing she talked about was Visible Light Transmission - using light instead of wireless... but then she wondered if you had to take into account the blue/green light that was hitting people's eyes... hmm... would that have an effect? She didn't know...

Why can't they just used wires? Or fiber optics?

Lots of info on the above webinar folks - well worth the hour spent - take notes on the names of scientists she gives - like Barry Trower who worked at MI5 - and she talks about the EMF spectrum and what it can do to a person psychologically - you can't believe how your government is wasting your $$$...

# Thread: Hair Loss
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair-loss.11910/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Melanie Procter — 2014-10-16T11:53:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair-loss.11910/post-143996>

I am seeing so much hair loss in women these days. I just spent some time in Dubai and was doing work for the royal family. One of the princesses is 24 years old and is losing her hair. You can imagine the money she has spent trying to get to the bottom of it. I started searching the forum for a blog for her. I just stumbled across his recent blog about men's hair loss. I found this quote absolutely radical and thought I would repost it here.

"My reading is now focusing in on teleromase shortening of the hair follicles and early cell death.......and lack of replacement stemcells in the hair follicle......and its the MSH stem cells particularly of the hair follicle. It appears teleromase inhibitors can make hair grow and turn grey hair back to what it was in youth! The effect on Mice is off the chain. Bald Mice becoem shaggadelic pretty fast. But mice have long telomeres to begin with......we dont.

I have on older man (80) who is taking TA 65 and he has peach fuzz all over his head now and his hair is no longer grey. He went completely bald at 55. He is the one that got me thinking about a lot of stuff. Not sure this answer is helping you.....but the standard answers of hair docs is all about DHT modulation. After speaking to Bauman.....I am not a buyer of his science. Its not up to date.

I think guys who go bald early is because they are genetically limited in their MSH cells in the hair follicle and they undergo senescense earlier because their mitochondria have a faulty telomerase......or their is a signalling issue with it. It appears DHT brings the effect on quicker because DHT stimulates hair follicles to divide quicker so if you got genetically faulty mitochondria....and DHT makes them divide quicker.......well you exhaust your Hayflick number faster and you go bald as a young man. I think the whole blocking the 5 alpha reductase pathways partially with Propecia or totally with Avodart is playing Russian roulette with your pituitary and your sexual performance and ability to get a stiffy. There was just an article in one of the sex journals that completely under cut Bauman assertion that he has never seen anyones' pituitary gets french fried for a long time and never recover. Well there is a ton of them out there......I know cause I see them. Me, Id rather have my Johnson working and be bald. We know that when you screw with reproductive ability the body senses this and tries to take you out........its a signal for apoptosis. Cell death via suicide and cancer rates go up because of mTor and activation of NF kappa beta......my advice is stay bald and get a transplant........based on all I know currently based upon the latest science. But I have a feeling this story is going to evolve with telomerase drugs now hitting the mainstream anti aging scene."

### Shijin13 — 2014-10-16T13:33:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hair-loss.11910/post-144003>

Melanie

I have seasonal hair loss. mostly in the fall. I've linked mine to seasonal changes. Its finally starting to slow down....

### NeilBB — 2014-10-18T01:04:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joan-rivers-cause-of-death-released.11920/post-144134>

Use of propofol anesthesia for surgical procedures, ICU sedation, and to stop intractible seizures is very standard in all hospitals today. What was unusual about MJ was that he was using the drug at home just to sleep. Anesthesia does carry risks, but what happened to Rivers and what happened to Jackson are far from same thing IMO...

I crock pickles in summer with raw vinegar and sea salt so may try a version of this. Anyone tried this or have more ideas for raw fish? Ceviche is good too.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-10-19T01:11:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/herring.11931/post-144224>

And BTW, don't need to obsess over raw vs cooked. So long as you're not using super high heat methods of cooking like deep frying, you'll still be getting lots of DHA (thanks to iodine for protecting it). Quantity would trump quality in many cases.

I'd think eating 200g of cooked mackerel would still deliver more DHA over 100g raw. (though my favourite method of cooking is definitely briefly seared on the outside while leaving the inside raw).

>

Followed a link over the weekend that took me to a Q&A on DHA ...it stated that 40% of DHA was lost if it was frozen. I thgt it also had some cooking methods also lost DHA. Does anyone know the link....can't find it now?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-10-23T06:27:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/herring.11931/post-144804>

> [JMO said:](/goto/post?id=144785)
>
> Followed a link over the weekend that took me to a Q&A on DHA ...it stated that 40% of DHA was lost if it was frozen. I thgt it also had some cooking methods also lost DHA. Does anyone know the link....can't find it now?

This paper shows that pan frying salmon to 70C  reduced the DHA content by 40% -- [http://www.researchgate.net/publica...\_EPA\_and\_DHA\_loss/file/9fcfd50046720d9a70.pdf](http://www.researchgate.net/publication/223442449_Pan-frying_salmon_in_an_eicosapentaenoic_acid_\(EPA\)_and_docosahexaenoic_acid_\(DHA\)_enriched_margarine_prevents_EPA_and_DHA_loss/file/9fcfd50046720d9a70.pdf)

Specifically, the DHA concentration dropped from 581mg/100g when raw, to 351mg/100g when pan fried (to 70C/158F).

Firstly, salmon is better raw anyway , and even if you cook it thoroughly, I doubt you would want to cook it to 70C anyway (tastes lousy). Even most paranoid people would only cook it to 60-62C (145F). Or you can do a salmon aburi, and only sear the outside while leaving the inside raw, creating a tasty yet mostly raw piece of fish.

The C-O covalent bond between the glycerol backbone an the fatty acid is pretty strong, and is not likely to dissociate by cooking your fish to 70C. This just means that DHA that is in the sn-2 position stays in that position despite cooking.

So if you could only stomach 100g raw, but 200g cooked, you'll be getting more DHA by going the cooked route. (at least with salmon)

\---

As for freezing, you shouldn't freeze fish for that long, but I wouldn't be too concerned if you keep the freezing period at 3 months or under.

See this paper on Caspian white fish fillets being frozen at -18C -- [http://www.davidpublishing.com/davidpublishing/Upfile/5/8/2012/2012050812006534.pdf](http://www.davidpublishing.com/davidpublishing/Upfile/5/8/2012/2012050812006534.pdf) . This species of fish is pretty low fat, so that may skew the results, but still, with an original 15.07g/100g fat of DHA content in the fresh fillets, we see that drop to 14.73g/100g fat in 1 month, and then 14.05g/100g fat at 3 months, and then 6.58g/100g fat at 6 months. Even at 3 months freezing, the fish had only lost 8% of its DHA.

Another similar study from caspian sea fish being frozen at -24C -- [http://en.journals.sid.ir/ViewPaper.aspx?ID=179943](http://en.journals.sid.ir/ViewPaper.aspx?ID=179943) . We see that the rate of DHA loss differs based on the fish. Pike lost 21% of its DHA in 3 months, and Kilka lost 22% of its DHA in 3 months.

Another study on refrigeration of Caspian Kutum roe, which has a very high concentration of DHA -- [http://www.idosi.org/wjfms/wjfms4(5)12/19.pdf](http://www.idosi.org/wjfms/wjfms4\(5\)12/19.pdf) . Vacuum seal + refrigeration => 7.26g of DHA at 5 days, 5.81g of DHA at 120days (4 months). So in 4 months, the DHA content decreased by 20%.

Again, I don't view this as significant. If being able to buy in bulk and easily access twice the amount of fish, you are ahead in the DHA game. Just eat as much as you can

### Inger — 2014-10-30T21:24:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/herring.11931/post-145676>

I make my herring like this
slice the herring, put in a bowl. Slice onion, thin, add. Grate fresh ginger, salt, ACV, brown pepper, garlic... and if doing carbs... a tiny bit of raw honey... just a touch.
Mix it all and let stay over night. Just made a huge bowl of it... I eat from the bowl several days in a row. So convenient and yummy! I never get tired of it

### Inger — 2015-01-21T19:02:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/herring.11931/post-153541>

Haaa... I did it    I spilled tea on my screen and now 30% from the right is blacked out.. the screen has a weird pale color all over
and i cant reply cause the button for
that is on the right side of the screen... but i just clicked all over in the dark and then i hit it  I was at the internet cafe today....
but now i can reply to you with this damaged computer yay
to read your posts i need to copy it into my word...lol or i can see only 70% of it..... haha... very complicted but i have figured out a way
there is always a way     and i bet a bit hard work helps to heal a broken heart

Ok... so.. to your questions.....

I buy my herrings Fhursday or Friday... make the "chevice" on Saturday normally.... i prefer whole herring they taste better
and i get the innards and heads too. Fillets tend to oxidize faster..
so i eat from the bowl many days until.. normally Wednesday. Today I ate the rest of the marinated herring. Tasted great still!

That said... I am not picky... when it tastes a tiny bit old i just eat it anyways. But I try to keep the fish on the collest place in my fridge.. it helps.
If you take out the innards and the herring is whole you could easily keep it for a couple of days.... if it is fresh.
My herring is not always that fresh when i buy it...... you can see it on the eyes if they are clear... if red, and not clear the fish is not so fresh
and also the older the fish... the softer it gets. Hard herring with clear eyes is very fresh. Same goes for mackerel.
I think mackerel and herring keeps about the same way... mackerel mis maybe a bit faster spoiled.
I have eaten pretty old herring and mackerel too.. no problem.. even if this is not ideal for sure. The fresher the better.. and especially
the taste!
You can freeze it immediately after taking out the innards. That way you will have it freshest. Mackerel and herring, both.

I usually take the fish milk (the white male stuff) and the roe (female) and the heads, and make small bags with a few heads, some roe, some fish milk.
That is for one fish head smoothie. And i make a few of these bags and put them in my freezer.
Then i leave a few heads and fishmilk and roe in a bowl in my fridge for a few days supply of fish head smoothie.
It will stink a little after a few days but if I have forgot to use them i use it anyways...hehe.... I just hold my nose and get it down....
Then i freeze bags with a few herring in each, usually 4 whole, that is a meal for me........

now let see if i can post this... darn have to blind search for the button again.....

### Inger — 2015-01-25T14:11:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/herring.11931/post-153805>

Veg, that is so cool.. I like it better when it sit a bit longer in the fridge too

I made it the other day and measured what i put in. That is what came out;

0,9 liters of loosely packed herring fillet pieces (readily cut into slices)
1 dl of ACV
1 teaspoon of seasalt
1 big onion, sliced
1 spoonful of Piment (whole brown pepper)

That was it, about  This is a small amount, I usually make about the double, so double all the amounts and you get what is in the herring bowl I eat from a few days / week

# Thread: Pregnancy testing - Ancient Egyptian style
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-testing-ancient-egyptian-style.11938/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2014-10-19T14:42:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-testing-ancient-egyptian-style.11938/post-144271>

One of the modules I'm studying is Analytical Sciences and I came across this interesting and thought-provoking article...

> **Cameo 2.1 Pregnancy testing in ancient Egypt**
> In the early 1800s a papyrus (thick paper document produced from the papyrus plant) was discovered in Saqqara, the sacred burial ground of the ancient Egyptian capital of Memphis. Dated to approximately 1350 BC, this ancient Egyptian document (known as the Berlin Papyrus because of its storage in the Berlin Museum) was found to contain detailed mathematical and medical knowledge including the first record of an analytical pregnancy test:
>
> "Another test for a woman who will bear or a woman that will not bear. Wheat and spelt; let the woman water them daily with her urine, like dates and like Sh’at seeds in two bags. If they both grow, she will bear: if the wheat grows it will be a boy; if the spelt grows, it will be a girl. If neither grow she will not bear."
> (Verse 2, 1–2)
>
> Similar non-invasive tests have been recorded in other papyri, including the Kahun Papyrus which was discovered by the famous British Egyptologist Flinders Petrie (1853–1942). They are collectively known as Medical Papyri because of their content. While translations of these ancient texts have revealed many mystical and magical diagnostic tests, including spells and incantations, could there be any basis of truth in this ancient pregnancy test?
>
> In the 1940s, American gynaecologist Erle Henriksen tested this ancient Egyptian theory on the urine of 50 women, some of whom were known to be pregnant. He found that 75% of the diagnoses made using the ancient Egyptian test were correctly positive, and 85% were correctly negative (Forbes, 1957). At that time this was as good as, if not better than, many of the pregnancy tests available. But what was the scientific basis for this test? Although modern tests use the presence of hCG to indicate pregnancy, there are other significant hormonal changes during pregnancy. While controversial and currently unproven, it has been suggested that some of these hormones may have the potential to stimulate plant growth.
>
> By the end of the 20th century, almost 4000 years after the Berlin Papyrus was written, researchers in India and Sri Lanka harnessed this ancient knowledge to develop an analytical test, known as the Punyakoti test, to determine pregnancy in cattle (Dilrukshi and Perera, 2009). They tested the urine of cows and buffaloes on barley, wheat and mung beans and found that after five days, urine from pregnant cattle was found to suppress germination, unlike urine from non-pregnant cattle. They believed that the suppression of growth was due to the presence of excess abscisic acid in the pregnant cattle’s urine; abscisic acid is a natural plant hormone produced to slow down bud and seed growth before the winter months.
>
> While these results were in conflict with those from the Egyptian analytical test, it shows that there is, potentially, a scientific basis for the simple analytical test. The Punyakoti test is being adopted in areas of Asia which rely heavily on a thriving dairy crop for self-sufficiency and income but where transporting cattle to veterinary practices for pregnancy testing is impossible.

It's interesting how the human and cattle urine appeared to have different effects, all I can think is that we're omnivores and cattle are herbivores. I remember reading in Survival of the Sickest about how clover grown in Australia started producing lots of phytoestrogens which acted as birth control for the sheep. I'm not sure why a herbivore would want to slow down the growth of plants though. Perhaps they only want to slow the growth down during pregnancy, then when the calves are born the grains can start to germinate thus providing them with lots of good nutrition.

### Melanie Procter — 2014-11-03T12:36:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-testing-ancient-egyptian-style.11938/post-146021>

I found this amusing. Thanks for sharing this.

### Josh — 2014-12-09T18:52:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-testing-ancient-egyptian-style.11938/post-149611>

> [HoneyChild said:](/goto/post?id=144271)
>
> One of the modules I'm studying is Analytical Sciences and I came across this interesting and thought-provoking article...
>
>
>
> It's interesting how the human and cattle urine appeared to have different effects, all I can think is that we're omnivores and cattle are herbivores. I remember reading in Survival of the Sickest about how clover grown in Australia started producing lots of phytoestrogens which acted as birth control for the sheep. I'm not sure why a herbivore would want to slow down the growth of plants though. Perhaps they only want to slow the growth down during pregnancy, then when the calves are born the grains can start to germinate thus providing them with lots of good nutrition.

Look at Steiner's work on the preparations for agriculture in the Biodynamic literature....

### Jonathin — 2014-12-09T21:36:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnancy-testing-ancient-egyptian-style.11938/post-149625>

So I finally got around to getting my grounding rod in the basement and the copper wires strung up the stairs. I checked with the voltage meter and the basement reading were really good. Got a little higher upstairs but not horrible. I put my grounding strap on and the reading went way way down. Then I crawled on the magnetico and it went to almost 0 millivolts. The bedroom even not on the Magentico was not bad either.

I just wish I could do this at work but the only way to get grounded there is with an outlet and just can't bring myself to try that yet. I will be hooking myself up to the grounding strap at home when awake now.

I think I'm in a bit of a 'detox' stage since I have woken up with headaches almost everyday this week. Had to take two days off of work. Not a big deal since my area is slow right now anyway.
It's just very frustrating when I have a couple of immature co- workers that love to bug me about not making it in to work. Because when you have nothing that's obviously wrong with you it must not exist. (Kind of like conventional medicine)

It's interesting - When my brain is functioning well, they seem to not poke fun, nor does it bother me if they do.

### kovita — 2015-04-25T08:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounded.11942/post-162374>

Mike, emotional and mood swings are VERY common among anyone with chronic borrelia infection. I am inclined to believe it is not due to the direct actions of that bloody bacterias but rather a result of altered neurochemistry and hormonal dysbalance. I used to be impossible, lots of anger...now with some time distance it is difficult to say which part of my anger was organic result of the lyme disease and which my own anger on the world, God (if there is any) and everyone who was not sick (and desreved to be from my perspective back than). I am out of it now, so will you, I am sure. I experienced again something similar but much more "congested" when forced to take amoxicilin some months back. I was ready to jump out of a window. As your capacity to deal with endotoxins and other daily "system clenaing and maintance" improves, your control over your mind improves with it. I found a lot of help in nature and sort of external stress management, which I did according to the common man's sense. Basically I was over excited, stressed and nervous person for almost whole my life and now people ask me to teach them how to remain calm and avoid stress. So much have I changed. All that "thanks" to one tick bite which I am realizing slowly worked for me just like the "viral market" Jack talks about frequently. Whatever terrible it sounds (I hate lyme as much as you) this disease gave me a new start and new life.

# Thread: The Law of One
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-law-of-one.11948/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sjoshua — 2014-10-20T20:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-law-of-one.11948/post-144433>

Anyone hear of this before? I've found it to be quite an interesting read... I'm working my way through the 4th book now. I offer it here since the JK forums folks tend to be a bit different, which may make this type of information more likely to strike a chord or two.

Depending on your beliefs or lack thereof related, a brief background is: this group of researchers channeled an entity that refers to itself as Ra, an humble messenger of the Law of One, for over 100 sessions in the early 80's, asking questions related to life, human existence, purpose, etc.

To me it reads as an unadulterated summary of all Earth religion's ideas; perse if you could catch them at their source moment, or original intents, still unaffected by human interpretations.

They have the series of 5 books in PDF form available for free from their website: [http://www.llresearch.org/library/the\_law\_of\_one\_pdf/the\_law\_of\_one\_pdf.aspx](http://www.llresearch.org/library/the_law_of_one_pdf/the_law_of_one_pdf.aspx)

# Thread: Ebola Airborne
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ebola-airborne.11959/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BTA — 2014-10-21T18:16:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ebola-airborne.11959/post-144604>

Interesting to see more than 25 years ago the Military discovered Ebola can be transmitted by air if it's cold. Cold temperatures cause viruses to be more virulent. Must be a tie into how it helps humans and has an effect on viruses?

**Ebola can spread by air in cold, dry weather common to the U.S. but not West Africa, presenting a “possible, serious threat” to the public, according to two studies by U.S. Army scientists.**

After successfully exposing monkeys to airborne Ebola, which “caused a rapidly fatal disease in 4-5 days,” scientists with the _U.S. Army Medical Research Institute of Infectious Diseases_ (USAMRIID) concluded Ebola can spread through air but likely hasn’t in Equatorial Africa because the region is too warm, with temperatures rarely dropping below 65°F.
“We… demonstrated aerosol transmission of Ebola virus at lower temperature and humidity than that normally present in sub-Saharan Africa,” the 1995 study entitled [_Lethal Experimental Infections of Rhesus Monkeys by Aerosolized Ebola Virus_](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1997182/) reported. “Ebola virus sensitivity to the high temperatures and humidity in the thatched, mud, and wattle huts shared by infected family members in southern Sudan and northern Zaire may have been a factor limiting aerosol transmission of Ebola virus in the African epidemics.”
“Both elevated temperature and relative humidity have been shown to reduce the aerosol stability of viruses.”

The study also referred to the 1989 Ebola outbreak at a primate quarantine facility in Reston, Va., in which the virus rapidly spread between unconnected rooms.
“While infections in adjacent cages may have occurred by droplet contact, infections in distant cages suggests aerosol transmission, as evidence of direct physical contact with an infected source could not be established,” the study added.
It is interesting to note this outbreak occurred in [December 1989](http://www.foxnews.com/science/2014/09/19/how-virginia-suburb-became-ebola-epicenter/), when [temperatures in Reston were usually below freezing](http://www.wunderground.com/history/airport/KIAD/1989/12/15/MonthlyHistory.html), and it’s unlikely the indoor temperature in the vast quarantine facility was much higher.

A 2012 study also by the USAMRIID, which exposed monkeys to an airborne filovirus similar to Ebola, reached a similar conclusion to the 1995 study.
“There is no strong evidence of secondary transmission by the aerosol route in African filovirus outbreaks; however, aerosol transmission is thought to be possible and may occur in conditions of lower temperature and humidity which may not have been factors in outbreaks in warmer climates,” the study entitled [_A Characterization of Aerosolized Sudan Virus Infection in African Green Monkeys, Cynomologus Macaques and Rhesus Macaques_](http://www.mdpi.com/1999-4915/4/10/2115/pdf) stated.
The study pointed out that filoviruses, which include Ebola and the Sudan virus used in this particular study, have stability in aerosol form comparable to influenza.
“Filoviruses in aerosol form are therefore considered a possible, serious threat to the health and safety of the public,” it added.

And the Pentagon took this threat of airborne filoviruses so seriously that it organized a [_Filovirus Medical Countermeasures Workshop_](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4113787/) with the Department of Health and Human Services in 2013.
“The DoD seeks a trivalent filovirus vaccine that is effective against aerosol exposure and protective against filovirus disease for at least one year,” the [executive summary of the workshop](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4113787/) stated.
The Pentagon’s concern with airborne Ebola runs contrary to health officials who claim the disease can’t spread through coughing and sneezing, but according to the Army studies, that may only be true in tropical climates.
“How much airborne transmission will occur will be a function of how well Ebola induces coughing and sneezing in its victims in cold weather climates,” the web site [potlblog.com](http://pissinontheroses.blogspot.com/2014/09/us-army-says-ebola-flu-in-airborne.html) suggested. “Coughing and nasal bleeding are both reported symptoms in Africa, so the worst should be expected.”

# Thread: 21 cm Hydrogen line?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/21-cm-hydrogen-line.11963/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2014-10-22T12:48:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/21-cm-hydrogen-line.11963/post-144705>

I was watching [Brian Cox's Human Universe](http://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episode/p0276pxp/human-universe-3-are-we-alone) last night and he was talking about searching for life on other planets. He said something about electromagnetic frequencies being emitted 21 cm from hydrogen atoms. Is this something we should be thinking about in biology?

> The **hydrogen line**, **21 centimeter line** or **HI line** refers to the [electromagnetic radiation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation) [spectral line](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spectral_line) that is created by a change in the energy state of neutral [hydrogen](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen) atoms. This electromagnetic radiation is at the precise frequency of 1420.40575177 [MHz](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Megahertz), which is equivalent to the [vacuum wavelength](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wavelength#in_non-vacuum_media) of 21.10611405413 [cm](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Centimeter) in [free space](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Free_space). This wavelength or frequency falls within the [microwave](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microwave) [radio](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radio) region of the [electromagnetic spectrum](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum), and it is observed frequently in [radio astronomy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radio_astronomy), since those [radio waves](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radio_wave) can penetrate the large clouds of interstellar [cosmic dust](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmic_dust) that are [opaque](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opacity_%28optics%29) to [visible light](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visible_light).
>
> The microwaves of the hydrogen line come from the atomic transition between the two [hyperfine levels](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyperfine_levels) of the hydrogen [1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_quantum_number)[s](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Azimuthal_quantum_number) [ground state](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ground_state) with an energy difference of 5.87433 µeV.[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen_line#cite_note-Hyperphysics-1) The frequency of the [quanta](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum) that are emitted by this transition between two different energy levels is given by [Planck's equation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Planck%27s_constant).
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen\_line](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen_line)

# Thread: Ebola & Non-native EMF
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ebola-non-native-emf.11967/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BTA — 2014-10-23T00:02:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ebola-non-native-emf.11967/post-144766>

Interesting to see the research on Ebola & genetic snips. Humans use to make a certain enzyme that would destroy viruses like Ebola, AIDS, ect... However, genetic snips have been found which means humans stop making the enzyme. The research doesn't point to non-native EMF but I'm thinking the environment has allowed viruses to run loose?

**High-Dose Mannose-Binding Lectin Therapy for Ebola Virus Infection**

**[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3071052/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3071052/)**

Persons with mutations of both MBL2 alleles constitute between 1 and 65 percent (median 5 percent) of the population, depending upon the ethnic group. Heterozygosity, or the presence of mutations in one MBL2 allele, is found in more than 30 percent of the population in most ethnic groups evaluated. Thus, up to 40 percent of most populations may have mutations in one or both MBL2 alleles (See ["Mannose-binding lectin", section on 'The MBL gene'](http://forum.jackkruse.com/contents/mannose-binding-lectin?source=see_link&anchor=H2#H2).)

Several common mutations of the MBL2 gene can lead to a condition called mannose-binding lectin deficiency. People with this condition have low levels of mannose-binding lectin and may be susceptible to recurrent infections. Several of the disease-associated mutations occur in a region of the MBL2 gene known as exon 1 and result in a change to single protein building blocks (amino acids) in the mannose-binding lectin subunit. Other mutations occur in an area of DNA near the MBL2 gene called the promoter region, which helps control the production of the mannose-binding lectin subunit.

Functional mannose-binding lectins are made up of two to six protein groups called trimers, which are each composed of three of the protein pieces (subunits) produced from the MBL2 gene. This protein complex plays an important role in the immune system's response to foreign invaders (pathogens).

Mannose-binding lectin recognizes and attaches (binds) to sugars, such as mannose, fucose, and glucose, that are found on the surface of bacteria, viruses, and yeast. This binding turns on (activates) the complement system, which is a group of immune system proteins that work together to destroy pathogens, trigger inflammation, and remove debris from cells and tissues. Attachment of mannose-binding lectin also targets the pathogen to be engulfed and broken down by special immune cells. Recognition of foreign invaders by mannose-binding lectin provides one of the body's first lines of defense against infection.

Cytogenetic Location: 10q11.2
Molecular Location on chromosome 10: base pairs 52,760,859 to 52,772,817

### Butters — 2014-10-25T19:46:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-mode-on-smarthphone-safe.11990/post-145116>

Hello,

I have one short question. I am sleeping with my smarthphone aside and always have the flight-mode on. Is it enough or should I switch it off completely/plant it far away because any electronic device sends non native EMF?

### Danco3636 — 2014-10-25T19:51:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-mode-on-smarthphone-safe.11990/post-145117>

Based on my own n=1 mine goes into airplane mode early evening and gets plugged in two rooms away from bedrooms.
I sometimes even cover it with a EMF protective case.

Why chance it when we can make the choice of eliminating any risk?

### Rain — 2014-10-26T08:01:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-antibiotic-resistant-skin-infection.11993/post-145194>

Jack if you see this, or anybody else want to chime in.... i need help fast

here's what happened, i had such bad candida that i ordered threelac, i ended up in bed for days, i could do nothing but sleep, with sporadic waking for the needful things

i broke out in a HORRIBLE skin rash, first itching, then BAD BURNING, then PAIN, then all three

Penny wrote me the following in my other thread to point out that it is a bad skin infection, but i have been flox'd and can't take strong antibiotics, and i don't know what to do? JACK, QUANTUM LEVEL TREATMENT QUICKLY PLEEEEEASE????

ok Penny wrote

**Verified Purchase**([What's this?](http://www.amazon.com/gp/community-help/amazon-verified-purchase/175-5491908-7031623))
**This review is from: Threelac Probiotic Dietary Supplement, Natural Lemon Flavor, Includes 60 .053-Ounce Packets (Health and Beauty)**
I just stopped taking ThreeLac because I developed a severe skin infection with a burning rash. At first I did not connect the ThreeLac to the rash. After several days it became worse and I saw a doctor who prescribed anti-biotics for the skin infection. I stopped taking the ThreeLac since I was now taking anti-biotics and decided to do an internet search for other probiotics that I could take while on the antibiotic. I came across a Holistic health care provider's website where she described her reaction to ThreeLac and many other people left comments about their experiences...most negative. I was surprised since I had read so many positive reviews and I thought that probiotics could do no harm - they would either help or do nothing so I thought. After reading through those experiences I felt weak and panicky because I realized that all my current symptoms were due to the release of a pathogenic bacteria into my bloodstream which is the last ingredient in ThreeLac: enterococcus faecalis. It is a bacteria that normally resides in our intestines, but if you have too much, of have leaky gut syndrome (as most Candida sufferers do), it can enter you bloodstream and cause all kinds of infections. Many people reported getting urinary tract infections which led to kidney infections, and most people reported feeling weak, tired, dizzy, and having other related problems. I too feel sick and weak and horrible with this rash - all my symptoms meet the those of an infection with enterococcus faecalis.

I am not sure if ThreeLac is a complete scam or just a very dangerous "nutritional" supplement that affects people in extreme ways - either very good or very bad. Either way, please do your research before trying it because it's better to be safe than sorry. I am sorry. I believed all the positive reviews and let down my "scam sniffing" guard because I did not think a pro-biotic could cause me harm. I have read that Australia and Canada do not allow this bacteria into food products because it is extremely resistant to antibiotics and pathogenic to some people. Most ironic that the packages say "Pure Natural Food" when it feels like i just unknowingly poisoned myself and may have to spend lots of money getting treatment since I am uninsured and may be waiting too long get proper medical help. Please do not give this to your children without trying it for yourself first. I read some sad experiences of well-intentioned parents giving it to kids with awful side effects.

I am heading back to the tried-and-true natural candida remedies that I used over ten years ago and worked: Caprylic acid, GOOD probiotics (that are legal in other countries with better oversight than our FDA), and and a Candida diet.

From Penny:

You need to get to a doctor pronto and get some vancomycin - if you add ginger juice when you take the vancomycin, it will help the resistance factor of the microbe to the vancomycin... this is from Steven Buhner's Herbal Antibiotics book... he gives a list of tinctures you could use, but screw that, get to a doctor - this bug can affect your heart...

Do not \*ever\* use Tylenide as it can deplete your glutathione... your body's main antioxidant - baking soda or calamine lotion might be helpful - any kind of clay might help also - according to the reviewer, you might go see your doctor and get some antibiotics...

I know it costs $100.00 but if you gave a stool sample to American Gut, they would give you the DNA of your gut flora and you could see what is actually killing you - at least bacteria-wise... the little buggers never travel alone...

[http://americangut.org/](http://americangut.org/)

But please, take the package of threelac to a doctor and have him test your blood for e. faecalis... this is quite serious...

### Lahelada — 2014-10-26T11:26:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-antibiotic-resistant-skin-infection.11993/post-145205>

Rain, don't panic. I am not Dr K but I did read a bit about Herxheimer ,iodine supplementation etc.
What you are experiencing could be any of the above including the bactericaemia but it does not have to be that.

It seems to me that Threelac is a quite aggressive approach.Discontjnue,alsodiscontinue the iodine. Iodine needs cofactors like selenium and vit b in specific amounts. I have taken it but never as a daily routine but as a very judicial extra . N=1 but I get the feeling that you need to evaluate or reevaluate what you are doing,what you are taking and whether you should still be taking it and what would happen if you did not.
Have you heeded the docs advice of some sun? I know it is Vancouver and cloudy but you may still see some benefit.
Hugs,hope you are beter today.

### Linz — 2014-10-26T12:59:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-antibiotic-resistant-skin-infection.11993/post-145210>

Rain,
Some years ago I had a very bad rash/blistering skin that eventually required intravenous antibiotics for an infection and it was a frightening experience where none of the medics seemed to know what to do (seven of them!) It was before I modified my diet and I already had severe leaky gut/difficult to control auto-immunity and almost certain Vit A/D problem. I think a strong (medically approved and trialled) probiotic was probably the final trigger for the skin problem. The hospital treatment stopped the infection quickly but not the rash. At that point, feeling abandoned by the medical profession, I discovered a grain-free. sugar-free, high fat diet and the rash evaporated in days although hot itchy spots returned at the slightest provocation for some time. I still take very great care with manufactured probiotics (not so much natural sauerkraut,, kefir etc) and think even the best of them can cause imbalances in the wrong circumstances.

Some years later with greatly improved health and understanding of what I am doing ( and a heartfelt thanks to Dr K) , I have tried a few probiotics very cautiously - Mutaflor, Lactobacillus Plantarum and another ill-fated trial of VSL3 (rapid nausea and itchiness) - but I haven't seen much if any improvement and I'm very suspicious that they can sometimes cause more harm than good. Penny's explanation of the risks with leaky gut sounds plausible to me. Also good advice from Lah on the iodine. You will get plenty on an epi-paleo diet especially if you include seaweeds in your bone/ fishbroths and there are many warnings about the dangers of supplementing here.

Don't delay dealing with an infection, I'm sure one of the amazing people here will give you help with the immediate advice you may need on infection and you have already discovered that this is the most amazing place to deal with sorting out the underlying chaos.

Not helpful for your immediate crisis but I am beginning to think the only really effective way to use probiotics is faecal transplant which I think needs extensive testing of existing gut mo's and using an anaerobic process. Dr K does talk about re-engineering our own gut using raw fish, chitin, not over-chewing food and careful light exposure but I have no idea what would be the best approach for you with your digestive system problems. One supplement that might be worth looking at is vitamin K (esp mark IV). Poor supply in modern diets and otherwise obtained in the gut by good mo's. It helps put calcium in the right place - not sure if it helps prevent efflux though.

Really hope you start feeling better soon

### Justfourme — 2014-10-26T18:51:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-antibiotic-resistant-skin-infection.11993/post-145220>

With the severity of your rash, sounds like you may need to see a doc. I would advise against taking a probiotic with a compromised immune system and leaky gut while on an antibiotic. Antibiotics kill both good and bad bacteria in the gut. Instead of using a probiotic, use a prebiotic - that supports the good bacteria when taking meds. Once you are better and your leaky gut begins to heal, you can switch over to a probiotic and continue to take them.

# Thread: Protocoll for NST/BAT activation more cold restistance
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protocoll-for-nst-bat-activation-more-cold-restistance.11994/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Butters — 2014-10-26T12:26:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protocoll-for-nst-bat-activation-more-cold-restistance.11994/post-145208>

I'm trying to improve my tolerance to cold for a while since end of summer now. Everyday I do sessions with cold packs on my body for min. 1hr at shiver-intensity as well as using cold showers in the morning, sometimes cold baths but not regularly and dressing like summer. I know the CT-protocoll from Jacks site.
It seems to work slowly but how I know if I am cold adapted and what could I else do for massive BAT formation and activation? What shows me I am radiating heat? Jack says it doesn't take so long to cold adapt, but what does it really mean?

I often feel cold and shiver at 6 to 12°C when wearing shorts and T-shirt. I know it is maybe not a must but I want the peak of cold adaptation min. one time in my life to know how it is to not fear the cold and radiating heat in a massive amount. Or is it simply unrealistic? From a evolutionary standpoint I think its possible because somehow mankind had to survive in extrem temperatures without massive insulation gear
I also read studies about BAT-formation but they are only talking about minimal lower temperatures like 15-17°C and not as low as I want it.

I try to eat a high protein/fat diet direction keto with massive fish and DHA. What supplements could I also take to improve my BAT formation/activation?

### Da-mo — 2014-10-26T18:56:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protocoll-for-nst-bat-activation-more-cold-restistance.11994/post-145223>

Bitter melon extract is mentioned in the protocol.

> In the beginning of my adaptation, I also used [Bitter Melon extract](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B000RE4CSK/ref=as_li_qf_sp_asin_il_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=B000RE4CSK&linkCode=as2&tag=jackru-20) to cold adapt. The reason is that bitter melon appears to be quite effective at causing formation of BAT from WAT especially in T2D or those with metabolic syndrome. No one knows why it really works. However, I believe it is has to do with the simultaneous loss of adiponectin and leptin from fat cells with the simultaneous induction of [Irisin](http://blogs.scientificamerican.com/observations/2012/01/11/newly-discovered-hormone-boosts-effects-of-exercise-could-help-fend-off-diabetes/) from the cold stimulus on the skin and subcutaneous fat.

[http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/)

### Butters — 2014-10-27T09:55:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protocoll-for-nst-bat-activation-more-cold-restistance.11994/post-145267>

Yeah I didn't mention that. I am pretty lean I think. Is it working despite of that?
Last evening I added some chilli and pepper to the meal and got pretty warm face/ears, my girlfriend mentioned I was radiating heat. Is that a good idea as well or could it be a coincidence? It wasn't so much, because not hot at all.
I ordered the cool fat burner too and plan to to multiple sessions everyday when it arrives.

# Thread: Something interesting for singers!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-interesting-for-singers.12004/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hansen Kenimer — 2014-10-27T19:26:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-interesting-for-singers.12004/post-145320>

On Saturday and Sunday mornings I sit out on my porch in the sunshine, drinking my coffee and singing. For the past two weeks I've noticed that when I sing looking directly into the morning sun (I'm usually out there from about 7:30 to 9 or 9:30) the ability to hit very high notes becomes absolutely effortless, as does vibrato. Not just sitting in the full sun, but looking directly into it. Just fascinating. Try it!

### ColdKnight — 2014-10-27T21:17:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-interesting-for-singers.12004/post-145329>

### Hansen Kenimer — 2014-10-27T21:29:14+0200

Something about the sun at that time of day is different. It's easy to look at, and doesn't hurt my eyes.

# Thread: Why is moving air an electron stealer?

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Penny — 2014-10-27T20:19:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-moving-air-an-electron-stealer.12005/post-145323>

So, I was telling the kids that when they run the casablanca fan, it is an "electron stealer" - but then I realized I actually had no idea why that was... but even more interestingly though, my neighbor with Sjogrens, Raynaud's and Fibromyalgia can't go out in the wind - regardless of the temperature without pain - don't remember if it was in her head or just her ears, but am thinking she is in really bad shape... if anyone can explain that to me, I would appreciate it thanks!

### JMO — 2014-10-28T00:15:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-moving-air-an-electron-stealer.12005/post-145350>

### Penny — 2014-10-28T00:32:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-moving-air-an-electron-stealer.12005/post-145352>

### shah78 — 2014-10-28T15:54:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-moving-air-an-electron-stealer.12005/post-145424>

Wow!.I used to experience cold as pain. I now embrace much of of the "cold experience". But i always experienced most wind as pain as well. Hot air was fine though. Ayurveda explained it as VATA imbalance. I lived with that explanation for twenty years. This "electron loss stuff "is a little clearer and much easier to employ to actually fix the problem. The Ayurveda was very poetic, but didn't really solve much. "KRUSURVEDA" is a real step up. Now I have to wait for a windy day to see if I embrace the wind as well as I embrace the cold. I imagine I will.....Thanks for the post and getting me to think about this.

### Sue-UK — 2014-10-28T17:43:27+0200

### Penny — 2014-10-28T23:48:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-moving-air-an-electron-stealer.12005/post-145459>

So, I was thinking about the wind at o'dark 30 this morning and thought about how a waterfall is invigorating because of the negative ions and thought maybe someone with almost zero extra electrons would be affected by wind because it is positively charged... So, I found this:

**IONS CREATED BY WINDS MAY PROMPT CHANGES IN EMOTIONAL STATES**
**By WALTER SULLIVAN
Published: October 6, 1981**

-   EMAIL
-   [PRINT](http://www.nytimes.com/1981/10/06/science/ions-created-by-winds-may-prompt-changes-in-emotional-states.html?pagewanted=print)
-   REPRINTS

FOR many years it has been suspected that certain weather conditions, notably such hot, dry winds as the foehn in Europe, the sharav in Israel and the Santa Ana in southern California, affect people's mood and health, even precipitating suicides, crimes and accidents.

One controversial explanation -electrification of the atmosphere by positive ions - has now acquired greater plausibility through a series of experiments on volunteers at New York University.

The findings, the researchers said, ''are potentially quite important, as atmospheric charge is a fundamental part of the air we breathe.''

''Since altered ion concentrations can result not only from changes in weather conditions but also from the presence of pollutants, highvoltage lines, and radioactivity,'' they said, ''the possibility of far-reaching implications emerges.''

Normally molecules of oxygen, nitrogen and other elements in the air carry their full complement of electrons and are therefore electrically neutral. In various ways, however, an electron may be knocked off such a molecule, leaving it with a positive charge. It is then a ''positive ion.''

The liberated electron may attach itself to a molecule that already has a full complement of electrons, whereupon the molecule becomes a ''negative ion.''

Positive ions may be generated by hot winds, radio and television transmitters, ventilators or direct current power lines. For a number of years some people, to neutralize the allegedly deleterious effects of such ions, have installed negative ion generators in their homes or offices.

When, in the 1950's, ion generators were intensively marketed as effective against a variety of diseases, the Food and Drug Administration forced the salesmen to claim only that the generators help clean the air.

Ever since then the scientific community, with some exceptions, has looked down its nose at suggestions that ions affect human health and behavior. Now, however, researchers believe they have documented some psychological effects of positive ions.

The experiments are described by Dr. Jonathan M. Charry of Rockefeller University and Dr. Frank B.W. Hawkinshire 5th of N.Y.U. in a recent issue of the Journal of Personality and Social Psychology.

The apparent effects of positive ions included increased tension and irritability as well as a slowing of reaction times. The reaction times were measured in terms of the lag between appearance of a red light and the pressing of a button by the subject.

These effects, however, were not obvious when findings from all 85 subjects were lumped together. They were evident chiefly when results from the 34 subjects with a less resilient autonomic nervous system were separately examined. The autonomic system controls reflexes, heart performance and the other involuntary functions. Its resilience can be seen, for example, in the return of heart rate to normal after a fright.

In an interview last week Dr. Charry pointed out that the study was the first conducted in this manner and its findings must be considered tentative until replicated elsewhere.

Much of the early research on this subject was conducted in Israel, where the sharav has a marked effect. It has been reported that 30 percent of the population becomes ill with migraine, nausea, vomiting, irritability, dimness of vision, respiratory symptoms and other effects.

The symptoms are said to appear a day or two before heat and dryness become severe, but when air blowing in from the desert is already laden with positive ions. Such winds in other parts of the world have also been associated with increased crime, suicide and accidents. These winds, in addition to the foehn, sharav, and Santa Ana, include the chinook of the Pacific Northwest, the sirroco of Italy, the ghibli of Libya and the zonda of the Argentine Andes.

The results of the current research derive some support from mouse experiments conducted for over two decades by Dr. Albert Paul Krueger and his colleagues at the University of Californ ia in Berkeley.

They have reported that exposure to high levels of positive ions raises blood levels of the hormone serotonin, a substance that has generated much recent research in a number of fields as scientists increasingly appreciate the hormone's importance in behavior. Negative ions have the opposite effect on serotonin in the blood. Both types of ion, however, depleted brain levels of the hormone. As noted by Dr. Charry, analysis of brain t issue of suicides has shown reduced levels of the hormone, as has c erebrospinal fluid from depressed people. In Israel, during a sharav, excess amounts of serotonin have been found in the urine of tho se classed ''weathersensitive.''

The discovery in mice that negative, as well as positive, ions depress brain levels of the hormone, Dr. Charry said, suggests that long exposure to artificially produced ions might be harmful. He and his colleagues are now conducting tests with rats to determine the effects on brain and behavior of various ion doses and lengths of exposure. Later they hope to continue the human tests.

The new results, according to Dr. Charry, suggest that much more needs to be known of ion effects on the body before the usefulness of devices generating negative ions can be assessed.

In the N.Y.U. experiments the volunteers were told that, while details regarding the purpose of the tests would be withheld until afterwards, lest they bias the results, the goal concerned factors that lead pilots and drivers to commit fatal errors. Each person was tested twice in the experimental chamber: once with the ion generators on and once with them off. The volunteers were unaware of the generators' role.

Whether each person was first tested with the generators on or off was randomly determined. The two sessions were spaced three days apart. The test for resilience of the autonomic nervous system was one widely used by heart specialists since that system controls heart performance and blood pressure.

It involves plunging one hand into cold water while electrodes attached to the palm of the other hand record changes in its electrical conductivity. The response was interpreted to identify those whose autonomic nervous system is less resilient.

It was those who proved most ion-sensitive. For members of this category the lag between display of a red light and the buttonpushing response increased after ion exposure.

Also used was a standard psychological test, known as the Nowlis Mood Adjective Checklist. Each subject was given an alphabetized list of 100 adjectives and asked to indicate those nearest his or her state of mind - from adaptable, adventurous, aggressive and so on to worried.

Scoring depended on the clustering of responses in various categories. The subjects were also rated on such subjective symptoms as headache, dizziness, blurred vision and drowsiness. The results, after ion exposure, showed reduced scores in the categories labeled sociability, surgency (elation) and task involvement.

Scores showed an increase in tension, inattention and fatigue. Males appeared more susceptible than females, but it was suggested that the women might be vulnerable to long-term exposure. The tests lasted only 90 minutes.

Those whose autonomic nervous systems appeared highly resilient - two-thirds of the total - showed no ion effects. ''It appears that consideration of individual differences in ion sensitivity,'' wrote the researchers, ''is essential for assessing performance effects of ions.''
